- ✅ V86 Mode is a **sub-mode of Protected Mode**, introduced in the **80386**.
- ✅ Identified by **EFLAGS.VM = 1** (Bit 17).
- ✅ V86 task always runs at **CPL = 3** (lowest privilege).
- ✅ **Paging is mandatory** for safe concurrent V86 task execution.
- ✅ Segment base = `Selector × 16`; Segment limit = `0xFFFF` (64 KB).
- ✅ Linear address = `(Segment × 16) + Offset`, zero-extended to 32 bits.
- ✅ Each V86 task has its own **1 MB linear address space** mapped via page tables.
- ✅ **Entering V86 Mode:** far `JMP`/`CALL` to a TSS descriptor whose `EFLAGS.VM = 1`.
- ✅ **Exiting V86 Mode:** `IRETD` with new `EFLAGS.VM = 0` (or task switch).
- ✅ All **privileged instructions** trap to the host OS (#GP, #UD, #SS, #NP).
- ✅ **IOPL < 3** traps **all** I/O instructions unconditionally in V86 mode.
- ✅ **IOPL = 3** allows direct I/O, but the **IOPB** still gates port-by-port access.
- ✅ Hardware interrupts and software `INT n` instructions go to the **protected-mode IDT**.
- ✅ Real-mode BIOS/DOS services must be **emulated** by the host OS.
- ✅ The 8086 IVT at linear `0x00000-0x003FF` is usually remapped to a **virtual IVT** by the host OS.
- ✅ `IRET` (16-bit) is used in real mode; **`IRETD`** (32-bit) is used to leave V86 mode.
- ✅ V86 mode is used in **NTVDM (Windows)**, **DOSEMU (Linux)**, and similar emulators.
- ✅ V86 mode **does not exist** on processors before 80386.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paging and Segmentation - Comparison with 80486 Microprocessor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Paging and Segmentation - Comparison with 80486 Microprocessor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **80486 microprocessor** implements a **two-level memory management hierarchy** that combines **segmentation** (a logical, programmer-visible memory model inherited from the 8086 family) with **paging** (a transparent, fixed-size physical memory model inherited from the 80386). The segmentation unit converts a **logical address** (segment selector + offset) into a **linear address**, and the paging unit then converts this **linear address** into a **physical address** on the address bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> - **Logical Address** = Segment Selector (16-bit) + Offset (32-bit) → *Programmer's view*
> - **Linear Address** = 32-bit address produced after segment translation → *Processor's view*
> - **Physical Address** = 32-bit address on the physical memory bus → *Hardware/Memory's view*

The relationship is given as:
$$\text{Logical Address} \xrightarrow{\text{Segmentation Unit}} \text{Linear Address} \xrightarrow{\text{Paging Unit}} \text{Physical Address}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **KTU Central Library** system as a memory model:

- **Segmentation** is like the library's **subject-wise floor organization** — *Electronics Floor*, *Mechanical Floor*, *Reference Floor*. Each floor (segment) is of **variable size** and is logically named (CS, DS, SS, ES). A reader (programmer) walks to the Electronics floor (segment), then to shelf #5 (offset), to fetch a book. The reader **knows about the floors** — segmentation is **programmer-visible**.

- **Paging** is like the librarian's **backroom storage system**. Regardless of which floor the reader visits, books are physically stored in **fixed-size trays of 4 KB each**, tracked in a **catalog (page table)**. The reader never sees the trays; the librarian (MMU/paging unit) handles the translation silently. Paging is **transparent** to the programmer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway**: Segmentation gives **logical organization** (variable-sized), while paging gives **efficient physical memory management** (fixed-sized 4 KB). The 80486 can run with **only segmentation**, **only paging (flat model)**, or **both enabled simultaneously**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Page Size** | **4 KB** | Standard page (4096 bytes) |
| **Large Page Size** | 4 MB | Used when PSE bit in CR4 is set |
| **Address Bus Width** | 32 bits | 4 GB physical address space |
| **Page Directory Entries** | **1024** | Each PDE is 4 bytes |
| **Page Table Entries** | **1024** | Each PTE is 4 bytes |
| **Segment Limit Granularity** | 1 byte or 4 KB | Selected by G bit in descriptor |
| **Maximum Segments** | **16,384** | 8192 GDT + 8192 LDT entries |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Segmentation in 80486 — The "Why" and "How"

**Why Segmentation?**
- Provides **memory protection** (limit and access checks)
- Enables **logical modularity** in programs (code, data, stack separated)
- Supports **virtual memory** through descriptor-based access rights
- Allows **relocation** of code in memory

**How does it work? (Bulleted Logic Steps)**

1. The programmer references memory using a **segment register selector** (e.g., `CS`, `DS`, `SS`, `ES`, `FS`, `GS`).
2. The selector is loaded into a hidden **segment descriptor cache register** (8 bytes, 80486 caches descriptors internally).
3. The selector contains: **Index (13 bits)** + **TI bit (1 bit)** + **RPL (2 bits)**.
4. The index selects one of **8192 descriptors** from either the **GDT (Global Descriptor Table)** or **LDT (Local Descriptor Table)**.
5. Each 8-byte descriptor holds: **Base Address (32 bits)** + **Segment Limit (20 bits)** + **Access Rights (12 bits)**.
6. The **MMU** (Memory Management Unit) adds the 32-bit base to the offset (from instruction) to form the **linear address**.

### 2.2 Paging in 80486 — The "Why" and "How"

**Why Paging?**
- Enables **demand paging** (loading pages only when needed)
- Allows **virtual memory** that exceeds physical RAM
- Provides **uniform protection** at the 4 KB granularity
- Supports **page-level protection bits** (R/W, U/S, P, A, D)

**How does it work?**

1. The paging unit receives a **32-bit linear address** from the segmentation unit.
2. It splits this address into three fields:
   - **Bits [31:22]** → Page Directory Index (PDI) — 10 bits
   - **Bits [21:12]** → Page Table Index (PTI) — 10 bits
   - **Bits [11:0]** → Page Offset — 12 bits (offsets within a 4 KB page)
3. The **CR3 (Page Directory Base Register)** holds the physical base of the **Page Directory** (a 4 KB table with 1024 PDEs).
4. The PDI selects a **Page Directory Entry (PDE)**, which points to a **Page Table**.
5. The PTI selects a **Page Table Entry (PTE)**, which contains the **20-bit page frame base address**.
6. The page frame base (shifted left by 12) is concatenated with the 12-bit offset to produce the **32-bit physical address**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Linear Address} = \text{Segment Base} + \text{Effective Address (Offset)}$ | Segment translation | After segmentation unit |
| $\text{PhysAddr} = (\text{Page Frame Base} \times 4096) + \text{Page Offset}$ | Page translation | After paging unit |
| $\text{Selector} = \text{Index} \cdot 8 + \text{TI} \cdot 2^{13} + \text{RPL}$ | Segment selector encoding | Loading descriptors |
| $\text{Page Directory Size} = 1024 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}$ | Two-level page structure | Memory consumed by PD |
| $\text{Page Table Size} = 1024 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}$ | Per-page-directory-entry | Memory consumed per PT |
| $\text{Total Virtual Pages} = 2^{22} = 4 \text{ M pages}$ | 4 GB / 4 KB | Maximum pages in 4 GB |
| $\text{Segment Limit} = (\text{Raw Limit} \times 4 \text{ KB}) - 1$ | When Granularity bit G = 1 | Limit calculation |
| $\text{PDE/PTE format} = \text{Frame}[31:12] \mid \text{Avail}[11:9] \mid \text{0}[8] \mid \text{D/A}[7] \mid \text{0}[6] \mid \text{U/S}[2] \mid \text{R/W}[1] \mid \text{P}[0]$ | Page entry structure | Page table programming |

> [!NOTE]
> The **granularity bit (G)** in a segment descriptor determines whether the limit field is interpreted in **byte units (G=0)** or **4 KB units (G=1)**. This single bit is a favourite KTU question topic.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems**: Linux, Windows, and QNX use the 80486's two-level paging for **virtual memory management**.
- **Embedded Systems**: Real-time OSes use paging to **isolate processes** in 486-based controllers.
- **Memory Protection**: Prevents one task from corrupting another's memory in **multitasking systems**.
- **Disk Swapping**: Pages not in physical RAM are swapped to disk — the **Present (P) bit** tracks this.
- **Cache Coherency**: The 80486's on-chip cache and paging work together; the **PWT and PCD bits** in PTEs control cache behavior for each page.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Logical → Linear → Physical Address Translation

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A program executes `MOV EAX, [DS:0x0040_1234]`. Given the following system state:
- `DS` selector = `0x0023` → Descriptor: **Base = 0x0000_4000**, **Limit = 0xFFFF**
- Paging is enabled. `CR3 = 0x0000_5000` (page directory base)
- PDE at index PDI = `0x4000_0003` (P=1, R/W=1, Page Table Frame = 0x4000)
- PTE at index PTI = `0x0000_A003` (P=1, R/W=1, Page Frame = 0x0000_A)

**Step 1: Decode the DS Selector**

The selector `0x0023` in binary is: `0000 0000 0010 0011`
- **Index** = bits [15:3] = `0x0000 0000 0100` = 4
- **TI bit** = bit [2] = `0` → selects **GDT**
- **RPL** = bits [1:0] = `11` = Ring 3 (User level)

$$\text{Descriptor Offset} = 4 \times 8 = 32 = 0x20$$

This selects **GDT[4]**, which contains the segment descriptor with **Base = 0x0000_4000**.

**Step 2: Compute the Linear Address**

$$\text{Linear Address} = \text{Segment Base} + \text{Effective Address}$$

$$\text{Linear Address} = 0x0000\_4000 + 0x0040\_1234$$

$$\text{Linear Address} = 0x0040\_5234$$

**Step 3: Split the Linear Address into PDI, PTI, Offset**

The 32-bit linear address `0x0040_5234` in binary is:
`0000 0000 0100 0000 0101 0010 0011 0100`

- **PDI** = bits [31:22] = `0000 0000 01` = `0x001`
- **PTI** = bits [21:12] = `00 0000 0101` = `0x005`
- **Offset** = bits [11:0] = `0010 0011 0100` = `0x234`

**Step 4: Walk the Page Directory**

$$\text{PD Physical Address} = \text{CR3} + (\text{PDI} \times 4)$$

$$\text{PD Physical Address} = 0x0000\_5000 + (0x001 \times 4) = 0x0000\_5004$$

The PDE read from this address is `0x4000_0003`:
- **P bit** = 1 → page table is present in memory
- **R/W bit** = 1 → read/write allowed
- **Page Table Frame** = `0x4000_0000 >> 12` = `0x4000_0` (note: high 20 bits)

**Step 5: Walk the Page Table**

$$\text{PT Physical Address} = \text{PDE Frame} + (\text{PTI} \times 4)$$

$$\text{PT Physical Address} = 0x0000\_4000 \cdot 16 + (0x005 \times 4)$$

Wait — correction: the PDE frame `0x4000_0` is the upper 20 bits; the actual physical base of the page table is:

$$\text{PT Base} = 0x4000\_0000 \quad (\text{taken as the full 32-bit frame base})$$

$$\text{PT Physical Address} = 0x4000\_0000 + (0x005 \times 4) = 0x4000\_0014$$

The PTE read from this address is `0x0000_A003`:
- **P bit** = 1 → page is present in physical memory
- **R/W bit** = 1 → read/write allowed
- **Page Frame** = `0x0000_A` (upper 20 bits) → **20-bit value**

The full 32-bit **page frame base** is:

$$\text{Page Frame Base} = 0x0000\_A000$$

**Step 6: Compute the Final Physical Address**

$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Page Frame Base} + \text{Offset}$$

$$\text{Physical Address} = 0x0000\_A000 + 0x234$$

$$\boxed{\text{Physical Address} = 0x0000\_A234}$$

**Verification of the math:**

The 12-bit offset `0x234` directly fits within a 4 KB page (no carry into the frame base). The paging unit does not add the offset; it concatenates the 20-bit frame with the 12-bit offset. Both interpretations yield the same result here because the page-aligned frame ends in `0x000`.

### 3.2 Segment Descriptor Limit Calculation Example

**Given:** Descriptor raw limit field = `0x000F_FFFF`, G bit = 1, access = present data segment.

**Step 1: Granularity Check**

Since G = 1, the limit is interpreted in **4 KB granularity**:

$$\text{Actual Limit} = (0x000F\_FFFF \times 4096) - 1$$

$$\text{Actual Limit} = 0x000F\_FFFF \times 0x1000$$

$$\text{Actual Limit} = 0x00FF\_FFF0\_0000 - 1 = 0x00FF\_FFFF\_FFFF$$

This means the segment can be **up to 4 GB** in size — the **maximum 80486 segment size**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Paging vs Segmentation)

| Parameter | Segmentation | Paging |
|---|---|---|
| **Inheritance** | From 8086 (16-bit) → 80386 (32-bit) | Introduced in 80386, refined in 80486 |
| **Size of Unit** | **Variable** (1 byte to 4 GB) | **Fixed 4 KB** (or 4 MB with PSE) |
| **Visibility to Programmer** | **Visible** (uses CS, DS, SS, ES) | **Transparent** (handled by MMU) |
| **Translation Input** | Selector + Offset | 32-bit linear address |
| **Translation Output** | 32-bit linear address | 32-bit physical address |
| **Tables Used** | **GDT, LDT, IDT** | **Page Directory, Page Tables** |
| **Hardware Support** | Segmentation Unit | Paging Unit (enabled by PG bit in CR0) |
| **Protection Granularity** | Segment level | **Page level (4 KB)** |
| **External Fragmentation** | Yes (variable-size) | **No** (fixed-size) |
| **Internal Fragmentation** | No | **Yes** (last page, average 2 KB) |
| **Number of Tables** | Few (segment count) | **Many** (one per process) |
| **TLB Support** | None in 80486 | **Yes** (32-entry unified TLB) |
| **Can be disabled?** | No (always active) | **Yes** (PG=0 in CR0) |
| **Address Space** | Logical | Linear → Physical |
| **Used For** | Logical memory organization, protection | Virtual memory, demand paging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Two-Level Address Translation Flow in 80486

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Program emits Logical Address\nSegment Selector and Offset"] --> B["Segmentation Unit\nLookup descriptor in GDT or LDT"]
    B --> C["Descriptor Cache\nBase, Limit, Access Rights"]
    C --> D{"Limit check\nand privilege OK?"}
    D -- No --> E["Exception 13\nGeneral Protection Fault"]
    D -- Yes --> F["Linear Address = Base plus Offset\n32 bit value"]
    F --> G["Paging Unit\nEnabled PG bit in CR0"]
    G --> H["CR3 register\nPage Directory base address"]
    H --> I["Bits 31 to 22\nPage Directory Index PDI"]
    I --> J["Page Directory Entry PDE\n4 bytes at CR3 plus PDI times 4"]
    J --> K{"Present bit P equals 1?"}
    K -- No --> L["Page Fault Exception 14\nInvoke OS pager"]
    K -- Yes --> M["Bits 21 to 12\nPage Table Index PTI"]
    M --> N["Page Table Entry PTE\n4 bytes at PDE frame plus PTI times 4"]
    N --> O{"Present bit P equals 1?"}
    O -- No --> L
    O -- Yes --> P["Bits 11 to 0\nOffset within 4 KB page"]
    P --> Q["Physical Address\nPage Frame Base concatenated with Offset"]
    Q --> R["Physical Memory Bus\n32 bit address to RAM"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 80486 MMU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["80486 CPU Core and MMU"]
        A1["Instruction Decoder"] --> A2["Segmentation Unit\nHidden Descriptor Cache"]
        A2 --> A3["Linear Address Generator\nBase plus Offset"]
        A3 --> A4["Paging Unit\n2 level Translation"]
        A4 --> A5["TLB 32 entry unified cache"]
        A5 --> A6["Physical Address Driver"]
    end
    subgraph MEM["External Memory"]
        B1["GDT and LDT\nSegment Descriptors"] --> A2
        B2["Page Directory\n4 KB in RAM"] --> A4
        B3["Page Tables\n4 KB each in RAM"] --> A4
        B4["Physical Pages\n4 KB each in RAM"] --> A6
    end
    subgraph REG["Control Registers"]
        C1["CR0 PG bit"] -.controls.-> A4
        C2["CR3 PDBR"] -.base.-> A4
        C3["Segment Registers CS DS SS ES FS GS"] -.input.-> A2
    end
```

### 4.3 Linear Address Field Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["32 bit Linear Address"] --> B["Bits 31 to 22\nPage Directory Index\n10 bits = 1024 entries"]
    A --> C["Bits 21 to 12\nPage Table Index\n10 bits = 1024 entries"]
    A --> D["Bits 11 to 0\nPage Offset\n12 bits = 4096 bytes per page"]
```

### 4.4 Segment Selector Bit Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["16 bit Segment Selector"] --> T["Bits 15 to 3\nDescriptor Index\n13 bits = 8192 max"]
    S --> U["Bit 2\nTI Table Indicator\n0 = GDT, 1 = LDT"]
    S --> V["Bits 1 to 0\nRPL Requestor Privilege Level\n00 to 11 = Ring 0 to Ring 3"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: Every oval/rectangle in the Mermaid charts represents a hardware structure or a step in translation. Arrows represent data flow. The **CR3 register** is the root of the paging hierarchy, and the **segment descriptor cache** is the hidden companion of every visible segment register.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between paging and segmentation in the 80486 microprocessor. List any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Granularity**: Segmentation divides memory into **variable-sized** logical units (segments), while paging divides memory into **fixed-size 4 KB** physical units (pages).
2. **Visibility**: Segmentation is **programmer-visible** (programmer uses segment registers like CS, DS), whereas paging is **transparent** — the programmer writes code as if a flat 4 GB address space exists, and the MMU handles page translation.
3. **Translation Stage**: Segmentation is the **first stage** of address translation (logical → linear), while paging is the **second stage** (linear → physical). Paging can be **disabled** (PG=0 in CR0) but segmentation is always active in protected mode.

*(Examiner's note: Three crisp points with one-line justifications = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**What is the role of the CR3 register in the 80486 paging mechanism? Why is it also called PDBR?**

**Model Answer:**

The **CR3 (Control Register 3)** holds the **physical base address of the Page Directory**, which is the top-level page table in the 80486's two-level paging hierarchy.

It is also called **PDBR (Page Directory Base Register)** because:
- It points to the start of the 4 KB **Page Directory** in physical memory.
- Bits [31:12] hold the base; bits [11:0] are reserved (since the directory is page-aligned to 4 KB).
- On a **task switch**, the CPU automatically reloads CR3 with the new process's page directory, enabling **per-process virtual address spaces**.

*(1 mark for role, 2 marks for PDBR explanation with task-switch detail)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **segmentation mechanism** of the 80486 microprocessor in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the **format of a segment descriptor** and explain the function of the **G (Granularity) bit**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **paging mechanism** of the 80486. Explain the **two-level page table structure** with the help of a linear address format. What is the role of the **TLB**?

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### (a) Segmentation Mechanism [7 Marks]

The 80486 uses a **segment-based memory model** inherited from the 8086/80386 family. In protected mode, the **segment register** no longer holds a 16-bit base; instead, it holds a **16-bit selector** that indexes into a descriptor table.

**Step 1: Segment Selector Format (2 marks)**

A 16-bit selector consists of:
- **Index [15:3]** — 13 bits, selects one of 8192 descriptors
- **TI [2]** — 1 bit, **0 = GDT**, **1 = LDT**
- **RPL [1:0]** — 2 bits, requested privilege level (0 = highest, 3 = lowest)

**Step 2: Segment Descriptor Format (3 marks)**

Each descriptor is **8 bytes (64 bits)**:

| Base[31:24] | G | D/B | 0 | AVL | Limit[19:16] | P | DPL | S | Type | A | Base[23:16] | Base[15:0] | Limit[15:0] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

- **Base Address (32 bits)**: Starting address of the segment in the 4 GB linear space
- **Segment Limit (20 bits)**: Size of the segment
- **Access Rights (12 bits)**: P, DPL, S, Type, A, G, D/B, AVL
- **P** = Present bit
- **DPL** = Descriptor Privilege Level
- **S** = Descriptor type (0 = system, 1 = code/data)
- **Type** = Code/Data/Expand-down/Conforming
- **A** = Accessed bit
- **G** = Granularity bit
- **D/B** = Default operation size (16 or 32 bit)

**Step 3: Function of the G bit (2 marks)**

The **Granularity (G) bit** controls how the **20-bit Limit field** is interpreted:
- **G = 0**: Limit is in **byte units** → segment size = Limit + 1 bytes (max 1 MB)
- **G = 1**: Limit is in **4 KB units** → segment size = (Limit × 4 KB) − 1 bytes (max 4 GB)

The 80486 always sets G = 1 in 32-bit code/data descriptors to enable 4 GB segments.

#### (b) Paging Mechanism [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Need for Paging (1 mark)**
Paging provides **transparent virtual memory** — programs see a flat 4 GB linear space, but the OS can swap pages in/out of physical RAM.

**Step 2: Two-Level Page Table Structure (3 marks)**

The 80486 paging system uses **two levels of tables**:
- **Level 1 — Page Directory (PD)**: A 4 KB table with **1024 Page Directory Entries (PDEs)**, located at the address in **CR3**.
- **Level 2 — Page Tables (PT)**: Each PDE points to a 4 KB Page Table with **1024 Page Table Entries (PTEs)**.
- Each PTE points to a **4 KB page frame** in physical memory.

**Step 3: Linear Address Format (2 marks)**

A 32-bit linear address is split into three fields:

$$\text{Linear Address} = \underbrace{\text{PDI}}_{[31:22]} \mid \underbrace{\text{PTI}}_{[21:12]} \mid \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{[11:0]}$$

- **PDI (10 bits)** indexes into the Page Directory
- **PTI (10 bits)** indexes into the selected Page Table
- **Offset (12 bits)** addresses a byte within the 4 KB page

**Step 4: Role of the TLB (1 mark)**

The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a **32-entry fully associative on-chip cache** that stores recent **linear → physical address translations**. On every memory access, the CPU first checks the TLB; on a **hit**, the page-walk is skipped, drastically speeding up virtual memory. On a **miss**, the hardware performs the two-level page walk and loads the result into the TLB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **logical-to-physical address translation** process in the 80486 microprocessor. How does the **CR0 register** control paging?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **paging and segmentation** in the 80486. Explain the advantages of using **both together** in a multitasking operating system.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### (a) Logical-to-Physical Address Translation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Logical Address Components (1 mark)**

A logical address consists of:
- **Segment Selector (16 bits)** — from a segment register (CS, DS, SS, ES, FS, GS)
- **Offset (32 bits)** — from the instruction

**Step 2: Segmentation Stage (2 marks)**

The segmentation unit:
1. Looks up the descriptor in the **GDT/LDT** using the selector
2. Reads the 32-bit **Base** and 20-bit **Limit** from the 8-byte descriptor (cached internally)
3. Performs **limit checking** and **privilege checking**
4. Adds the **Base** to the **Offset** to form the **32-bit linear address**

$$\text{Linear Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}$$

**Step 3: Paging Stage (2 marks)**

If **PG = 1** in CR0, the paging unit:
1. Splits the linear address into PDI, PTI, and Offset
2. Uses **CR3** to find the **Page Directory**
3. Uses PDI to find a **PDE** → points to a **Page Table**
4. Uses PTI to find a **PTE** → contains the **20-bit page frame**
5. Concatenates the page frame (shifted left by 12) with the 12-bit offset → **32-bit physical address**

**Step 4: Role of CR0 in Paging Control (2 marks)**

The **CR0 register** has two relevant bits:
- **PE (Protection Enable, bit 0)**: When set, the CPU operates in **protected mode** (segmentation with descriptors). PE = 0 → real mode.
- **PG (Paging Enable, bit 31)**: When set, **paging is enabled**. The paging unit then translates linear → physical addresses. PG = 0 → paging disabled, linear = physical.

**Mode combinations**:
| PE | PG | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Real mode (8086 emulation) |
| 1 | 0 | Protected mode, no paging (linear = physical) |
| 1 | 1 | Protected mode with paging (full virtual memory) |

> [!NOTE]
> **PG cannot be set without PE also being set** — paging requires protected mode segmentation to be active first.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 for full Mermaid flowchart)**

#### (b) Comparison and Combined Use [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Tabular Comparison (3 marks)** — see Section 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix for the full table.

**Step 2: Advantages of Using Both Together (4 marks)**

1. **Layered Protection**: Segmentation provides **coarse-grained protection** (whole code/data regions), while paging provides **fine-grained 4 KB page-level** protection. The CPU checks both on every access.
2. **True Virtual Memory**: Segmentation gives a **logical view**; paging gives a **physical view**. Together, the OS can run programs whose logical segments exceed physical RAM by **swapping individual pages** to disk.
3. **Efficient Task Switching**: Each task has its own **LDT** (segmentation) **and Page Directory** (paging). On a task switch, the 80486 auto-loads the new **LDTR** and **CR3** in one atomic operation.
4. **Flexible Memory Sharing**: Segments can be **shared** (e.g., shared libraries in the LDT), and individual **pages** can also be marked copy-on-write or read-only at a finer granularity.
5. **No External Fragmentation**: Paging eliminates external fragmentation (a problem in pure segmentation), while segmentation provides the **logical structure** that paging alone cannot offer.

**Example**: In Linux/Windows, every process has:
- A **code segment** (read-only, executable)
- A **data segment** (read/write)
- A **stack segment**
- Its own **page directory** mapped over the same linear addresses (4 GB each), giving true isolation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 80486 Paging/Segmentation Questions**
> 1. **Confusing the offset addition vs concatenation**: In segmentation, the CPU **adds** the base to the offset (with possible overflow). In paging, the CPU **concatenates** the page frame (upper 20 bits) with the offset (lower 12 bits) — there is **no addition** in the final stage. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the TI and RPL bits**: A segment selector is not just an index; the **TI bit (bit 2)** selects GDT vs LDT, and the **RPL (bits 1-0)** affects privilege checks. Skipping these loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misnaming the registers**: Students often write "CR2 is the page directory base" — wrong! **CR3** is the PDBR; **CR2** holds the **faulting linear address** on a page fault.
> 4. **Mixing G bit semantics**: Forgetting that **G=1** means **4 KB granularity** (not 1 MB, which is G=0 max). The maximum segment with G=1 is 4 GB.
> 5. **PDE vs PTE confusion**: The PDE's page frame field points to a **Page Table** (which holds PTEs), not directly to a page. A common error is jumping from PDI straight to the physical page.
> 6. **Not mentioning the TLB**: Examiners in 2022–2024 frequently allocated 1–2 marks specifically for the TLB's role in accelerating page walks. Omitting it costs marks.
> 7. **Skipping the math verification**: In numerical problems, always **verify the final physical address** is page-aligned (ends in `0x000`) and matches the PTE frame. KTU examiners look for this last sanity check.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **80486 has both** segmentation and paging; segmentation is **always on** in protected mode, paging is **optional** (PG bit in CR0).
- [ ] **Segmentation = first stage**: Logical address (selector + offset) → **Linear address**.
- [ ] **Paging = second stage**: Linear address (32 bits) → **Physical address** (32 bits).
- [ ] **Linear address format**: `[31:22] PDI \mid [21:12] PTI \mid [11:0] Offset` (10-10-12 split).
- [ ] **CR3 (PDBR)** holds the physical base of the **Page Directory** (4 KB, 1024 PDEs).
- [ ] **PDE points to a Page Table** (4 KB, 1024 PTEs); **PTE points to a 4 KB page frame**.
- [ ] **Final physical address** = (20-bit PTE frame shifted left by 12) **concatenated** with 12-bit offset.
- [ ] **Segment descriptor** is 8 bytes: 32-bit Base, 20-bit Limit, 12-bit Access Rights.
- [ ] **G bit = 1** → limit in 4 KB units → max 4 GB segment.
- [ ] **G bit = 0** → limit in byte units → max 1 MB segment.
- [ ] **Selector format**: Index (13 bits) + TI (1 bit) + RPL (2 bits) = 16 bits.
- [ ] **TI = 0** → GDT, **TI = 1** → LDT.
- [ ] **TLB** is a 32-entry on-chip cache for linear-to-physical translations.
- [ ] **PG = 1** requires **PE = 1** (paging only works in protected mode).
- [ ] **Task switch** in 80486 auto-reloads **LDTR** and **CR3** for new process's address space.
- [ ] **External fragmentation** is a problem in segmentation, **internal fragmentation** in paging.
- [ ] **Paging unit is transparent** to the programmer; **segmentation is visible** through segment registers.
- [ ] The 80486's **PSE bit in CR4** enables **4 MB pages** (a common advanced topic in KTU 2024).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pentium Class of Processors: RISC and CISC Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pentium Class of Processors: RISC and CISC Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pentium Class of Processors — Formal KTU Definition

The **Pentium Class of Processors** refers to the fifth-generation 32-bit x86 microprocessor family introduced by **Intel Corporation in 1993** (original P5, codenamed P5). Architecturally, the Pentium is a *hybrid* design that fuses the **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** external interface of the legacy 8086/8088 family with an internal **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)**-style superscalar core. The term "Pentium Class" generically denotes Intel processors beginning from the original Pentium (P5) through the Pentium Pro, Pentium II, Pentium III, and Pentium 4 families, all of which share the common design philosophy of emulating x86 CISC instructions on an internal RISC execution backbone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand *why* a CISC-ISA/RISC-core design was adopted, not merely *what* the features are. The "Why" — backward compatibility with the massive x86 software base — is a frequently asked question in **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)**.

## 1.2 RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) — Formal Definition

**RISC** is a CPU design philosophy that uses a *small, highly optimized set of instructions*, where each instruction executes in a **single clock cycle**, uses **fixed-length formats**, and relies primarily on a **load/store architecture** (memory is accessed only via dedicated LOAD and STORE instructions; all arithmetic operations occur between registers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Defining Properties of RISC (as per Patterson & Ditzel, 1980 original paper):**
> 1. Single-cycle instruction execution
> 2. Fixed instruction length (typically 32 bits)
> 3. Large register file (≥ 32 registers)
> 4. Load/Store memory model
> 5. Hardwired control logic (no microcode)
> 6. Heavy use of pipelining
> 7. Compiler-driven optimization

## 1.3 CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) — Formal Definition

**CISC** is a CPU design philosophy that uses a *large, rich, and variable-length instruction set* (e.g., 1 byte to 15 bytes in x86), supports **memory-to-memory arithmetic operations** (ADD mem, mem), employs **multi-cycle complex instructions**, and uses **microcoded control units**. The goal is to reduce the *semantic gap* between high-level languages and machine code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples of CISC Families:** Intel x86 (8086 → Pentium), Motorola 68xxx, VAX, IBM System/360.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Kitchens"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RISC vs CISC — Real-World Intuition:**
>
> Imagine a **professional kitchen (RISC)** and a **home kitchen (CISC)**.
>
> **RISC Kitchen (Professional):** Has only 10 basic tools (knife, pan, spatula, etc.). Every dish — whether simple toast or complex biryani — is broken into a *sequence of simple tool-uses*. Each tool-use is exactly **1 second**. The chef (compiler) plans the sequence in advance. The kitchen is *fast* but the recipes look long.
>
> **CISC Kitchen (Home):** Has 250 specialized gadgets (rice-cooker, bread-maker, idli-stand, pasta-maker, etc.). One gadget can perform a complex task in *one operation*, but each operation may take **5–10 seconds**. The recipe looks short, but the chef does less thinking.
>
> The **Pentium** is a *home kitchen whose internal cooking staff secretly uses a professional kitchen*. Externally, customers (software) send "home kitchen" recipes (CISC x86 instructions), but internally the staff decodes them into professional-kitchen steps (RISC micro-operations, or *µops*) for speed.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Pentium P5 Clock Speed (Original):** 60 MHz – 200 MHz
- **Pipeline Depth (P5):** 5 stages (Pentium uses *dual 5-stage pipelines*)
- **Superscalar Issue Width:** 2 instructions per clock (U-pipe + V-pipe)
- **Address Bus Width:** 32 bits (4 GB physical address space)
- **Data Bus Width:** 64 bits (external data bus; internal is 32-bit)
- **Register File:** 8 × 32-bit general-purpose registers (EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP, ESP)

> [!TIP]
> **Frequently Tested:** The 64-bit **external data bus** of the Pentium is one of its most distinctive features over the 80386/80486 (both 32-bit data bus). This is a 2-marker favorite in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RISC Design Philosophy — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Origin and Motivation
RISC emerged in the late 1970s / early 1980s from research at **IBM (801 project, 1980)**, **Berkeley (David Patterson — RISC-I, RISC-II, 1981)**, and **Stanford (John Hennessy — MIPS, 1981)**. The key empirical observation was the **80/20 rule** (Pareto principle applied to instructions): about **80% of execution time is spent in only 20% of the available instructions**. RISC optimizes the hardware for *those* frequently-used instructions.

### 2.1.2 Architectural Pillars of RISC
1. **Simple, fixed-length instructions (32-bit)**: Simplifies instruction fetch, decode, and pipelining. No need to compute "where does the next instruction begin?"
2. **Register-to-register operations (Load/Store)**: ALU operates only on registers; memory access is segregated. This makes the datapath symmetric and pipelinable.
3. **Single-cycle execution**: Every instruction is designed to complete in one cycle, enabling deep pipelining.
4. **Large register windows (or large flat register file)**: Reduces memory traffic. SPARC used *register windows*; MIPS/Pentium-internal use a *flat 32+ register file*.
5. **Hardwired control**: Combinational logic decodes instructions, eliminating microcode ROM access delays.

### 2.1.3 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar
- **Fixed length** → Instruction fetch unit can be hardwired; pipelining is trivial.
- **Load/Store** → Memory hierarchy (cache) is accessed uniformly; prefetch logic is simplified.
- **Single cycle** → Cycle time = critical path of one simple operation → higher clock frequency.
- **Many registers** → Compiler can keep working set in registers → fewer cache misses.

## 2.2 The CISC Design Philosophy — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Origin and Motivation
CISC arose in the 1960s–70s when **main memory was expensive and slow**, and **high-level languages (HLL)** like FORTRAN and COBOL produced verbose machine code. Designers added complex instructions (e.g., `LOOP`, `STRING MOVE`, `POLYNOMIAL EVALUATION`) so compilers could emit *one instruction* to replace *many*. Examples: VAX `POLYF` evaluates a polynomial in one instruction; x86 `REP MOVSB` copies an entire string.

### 2.2.2 Architectural Pillars of CISC
1. **Variable-length instructions** (x86: 1 to 15 bytes)
2. **Many addressing modes** (x86: 24+ addressing modes)
3. **Memory-to-memory operations** (e.g., `ADD [BX+SI], [CX+DI]`)
4. **Microcoded control unit**
5. **Complex multi-cycle instructions** (some x86 instructions take 10s–100s of cycles)

### 2.2.3 The "Why" Behind CISC Pillars
- **Variable length** → Compact code (smaller programs in memory).
- **Many addressing modes** → Compiler flexibility.
- **Memory-to-memory** → Fewer registers needed (cheap silicon historically).
- **Microcode** → Implementing complex instructions in firmware is easier than wiring thousands of gates.

## 2.3 The Pentium — A CISC-ISA / RISC-Core Hybrid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Pentium accepts **CISC x86 instructions externally** but internally **decodes (or "breaks") each CISC instruction into one or more RISC-like 72-bit micro-operations (µops)** that are then executed by a **RISC-style superscalar, superpipelined core**.

### 2.3.1 Why Did Intel Choose This Hybrid Model?
1. **Backward compatibility** — Decades of x86 software must run unchanged.
2. **Performance** — Internal RISC engine allows pipelining and superscalar execution.
3. **Market dominance** — Switching to a pure RISC ISA would have killed the x86 ecosystem.

### 2.3.2 The Decoding Process (Step-by-Step)
1. The **Instruction Fetch Unit (IFU)** fetches x86 instructions (variable length) from the L1 instruction cache.
2. The **Instruction Decoders** (two parallel decoders in P5) translate each x86 instruction into 1, 2, 3, or 4 µops. There are three decoder types:
   - **Decoder 0 / Decoder 1** (the two simple decoders): Each emits 1 µop per cycle.
   - **Decoder 2** (the complex decoder): Emits 1–4 µops in one cycle (for complex instructions).
3. µops are dispatched in-order to the **Reservation Station (RS)** and then issued out-of-order to **5 execution units**:
   - 2 × Integer ALU (U-pipe and V-pipe integer)
   - 1 × Floating-Point Unit (FPU)
   - 1 × Memory Load unit
   - 1 × Memory Store unit
4. Results are **retired in-order** to the Register File to maintain precise exceptions.

## 2.4 Other Key Pentium Features (Module 1, KTU Mandatory)

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Superscalar** | Issues 2 instructions per clock cycle (2 pipelines: U and V) |
| **Superpipelined** | 5-stage pipeline (more stages than 486's 5 — wait, both 486 and P5 are 5 stages; superscalar doubles throughput) |
| **Dual Integer Pipelines** | U-pipe can execute any instruction; V-pipe restricted to simple instructions |
| **Branch Prediction (BTB)** | Branch Target Buffer predicts conditional branches with dynamic history |
| **64-bit External Data Bus** | Doubles data transfer rate vs 486 |
| **Separate 8 KB L1 Caches** | 8 KB instruction cache + 8 KB data cache (Harvard-style split) |
| **Write-Back MESI Cache Protocol** | Maintains cache coherency in multi-processor systems |
| **Internal 32-bit / External 64-bit** | Mismatched bus widths to maintain x86 software model |
| **APIC** | Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller (for SMP) |
| **MMU with 4 KB / 4 MB Pages** | TLB and paging support |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value bars use `\vert` instead of the literal `|` character to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_c$ | CPU execution time | $N$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles per instruction, $T_c$ = clock period |
| $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i \;/\; N$ | Weighted average CPI | $IC_i$ = count of instructions of type $i$ |
| $MIPS = f \;/\; (CPI \times 10^6)$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second | $f$ = clock frequency in Hz |
| $MFLOPS = (\text{FP ops}) \;/\; (T \times 10^6)$ | Millions of FP ops per second | $T$ = execution time in seconds |
| $\text{Amdahl's Law: } S = 1 \;/\; \big((1-f) + f/n\big)$ | Speedup from enhancement | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $n$ = speedup of enhanced part |
| $\text{Throughput} = \text{IPC} \times f$ | Instructions per cycle × frequency | Superscalar: $IPC > 1$ |
| $A = 2^{\text{bits of address}}$ | Addressable memory locations | Pentium: $2^{32} = 4$ GB |
| $B = W \times f \;/\; 8$ | Bus bandwidth in Bytes/s | $W$ = bus width in bits, $f$ = frequency |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design**: RISC principles drive modern LLVM/Clang optimization passes (e.g., loop unrolling, register allocation).
- **Embedded Systems**: ARM (a RISC ISA) dominates mobile, IoT, and automotive — direct continuation of RISC philosophy.
- **Server CPUs**: Modern x86 (Skylake, Zen) and Apple M1 (ARM) all use *decoded* µop pipelines.
- **Performance Engineering**: Amdahl's Law is used daily to estimate gains from multi-threading, GPU offload, and SIMD vectorization.
- **Cache Design**: Harvard-style split L1 caches (as in Pentium) are now universal in all modern CPUs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: CPU Execution Time and the Impact of RISC vs CISC

We begin with the fundamental CPU performance equation and expand it to show *why* RISC design tends to yield higher performance.

### Step 1: Define CPU Time
$$
T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_c
$$
**Conversion logic:** $N$ instructions, each taking $CPI$ cycles of duration $T_c$ (clock period in seconds).

### Step 2: Replace $T_c$ with $1/f$
$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f}
$$
**Logic:** Since $f = 1/T_c$, we substitute directly.

### Step 3: Express $N$ in terms of Instruction Mix
$$
N = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i
$$
**Logic:** A program contains $IC_i$ instructions of class $i$, summed over $n$ instruction classes.

### Step 4: Express Effective CPI
$$
CPI_{eff} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i}{N}
$$
**Logic:** Each instruction class has a *different* CPI (e.g., integer add = 1 cycle, integer multiply = 4 cycles). Weighted average by frequency.

### Step 5: Substitute back
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= N \times CPI_{eff} \times T_c \\
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$
**Logic:** Reorganizing so that the *sum-of-products* (instruction count × cycles per instruction) is divided by clock frequency.

### Step 6: RISC vs CISC Comparison via This Equation
For a CISC processor (e.g., 486):
- Each CISC instruction *does more work*, so $N_{CISC}$ is small.
- But $CPI_{CISC}$ is high (variable, often 4–50 cycles per instruction).
- $T_c$ is large because complex datapath has long critical path → low $f$.

For a RISC processor (e.g., MIPS R3000):
- Each RISC instruction *does less work*, so $N_{RISC}$ is large.
- But $CPI_{RISC} \approx 1$ (single-cycle).
- $T_c$ is small because simple datapath → high $f$.

The Pentium **decodes** CISC into µops so it gets the *best of both worlds*: small $N$ (from CISC) AND small $CPI$ and $T_c$ (from RISC core).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amdahl's Law — Quantifying the Limit of Speedup

### Step 1: Define the Problem
Suppose a fraction $f$ of a program's execution time can be improved by a factor of $n$ (e.g., by hardware acceleration). What is the overall speedup $S$?

### Step 2: Original Execution Time
$$
T_{orig} = T_{enh} + T_{unenh}
$$
**Logic:** Total time is the sum of the *enhanced* part and the *unenhanced* part.

### Step 3: Express Each Part as a Fraction
$$
T_{orig} = f \cdot T_{orig} + (1-f) \cdot T_{orig}
$$
**Logic:** $f$ = fraction of time spent in the enhanced portion, $(1-f)$ = fraction spent in the unenhanced portion. This equation is an identity (it just splits $T_{orig}$ into two named pieces).

### Step 4: New Execution Time After Enhancement
$$
T_{new} = \frac{f \cdot T_{orig}}{n} + (1-f) \cdot T_{orig}
$$
**Logic:** The enhanced portion is now $n$ times faster (i.e., takes $1/n$ the time), while the unenhanced portion is unchanged.

### Step 5: Speedup = Original / New
$$
S = \frac{T_{orig}}{T_{new}} = \frac{T_{orig}}{\frac{f \cdot T_{orig}}{n} + (1-f) \cdot T_{orig}}
$$

### Step 6: Cancel $T_{orig}$ from Numerator and Denominator
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{\frac{f}{n} + (1-f)} \\
  &= \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Take the Limit $n \to \infty$
$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{1-f}
$$
**Logic:** Even with infinite hardware speedup, the program can never run faster than $\frac{1}{1-f}$. This is the **fundamental limit** of optimization.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked "If 80% of a program can be parallelized, what is the max speedup with infinite processors?" — the answer is $S_{max} = 1/(1-0.8) = 5\times$ (Amdahl's law, $n \to \infty$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MIPS Rating and the Pentium Example

### Step 1: MIPS Formula
$$
MIPS = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}
$$

### Step 2: Example Computation
Given a Pentium P5 running at **$f = 100$ MHz** with an *effective* $CPI = 1.5$ (averaged across both pipelines), compute the peak MIPS:
$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1.5 \times 10^6} \\
     &= \frac{100}{1.5} \\
     &= 66.67 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$
**Logic:** Clock cycles per second divided by cycles per instruction yields instructions per second; divided by $10^6$ gives MIPS.

### Step 3: Peak IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)
For a superscalar processor issuing 2 instructions per clock:
$$
IPC_{peak} = 2
$$
**Logic:** The U-pipe + V-pipe can each retire 1 instruction per cycle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bus Bandwidth of Pentium

### Step 1: Bus Bandwidth Formula
$$
B = \frac{W \times f_{bus}}{8}
$$
**Logic:** $W$ = width in bits, $f_{bus}$ = bus transfer frequency, division by 8 converts bits to bytes.

### Step 2: Pentium 100 MHz, 64-bit Data Bus
$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{64 \times 100 \times 10^6}{8} \\
  &= 8 \times 100 \times 10^6 \\
  &= 800 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$
**Logic:** 64 bits = 8 bytes per transfer, 100 million transfers per second → 800 MB/s peak bandwidth.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — RISC vs CISC vs Pentium

| Parameter | Pure CISC (e.g., VAX, 8086) | Pure RISC (e.g., MIPS) | Pentium (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instruction length | Variable (1–15 bytes) | Fixed (32 bits) | Variable externally, fixed internally (µops ~ 72 bits) |
| Addressing modes | Many (12–24) | Few (3–5) | Inherited CISC modes |
| Memory access | Memory-to-memory allowed | Load/Store only | Load/Store internally |
| CPI | High (avg 4–10+) | ~ 1 | ~ 0.5–1.5 (with 2 pipelines) |
| Clock frequency | Lower (complex datapath) | Higher (simple datapath) | High (RISC core) |
| Pipelining | Difficult | Easy | Yes (5-stage superpipeline) |
| Code size | Compact (CISC) | Larger (RISC) | Compact (CISC ISA) |
| Hardware complexity | Microcode ROM, complex decoder | Simple, hardwired | Decoder + microcode ROM + RISC core |
| Examples | VAX, 68k, x86 | SPARC, MIPS, ARM, PowerPC | Pentium P5, Pro, II, III, 4, Core, AMD K5/K6 |
| Power efficiency | Lower | Higher | Better than CISC, depends on µop complexity |
| Compiler complexity | Lower (fewer instructions needed) | Higher (must schedule) | Moderate (compiler + decoder) |

---

## 3.6 Quantitative Worked Example: Performance Comparison

**Problem:** A program contains 10 million instructions.
- On a **CISC machine** (e.g., 486): $CPI_{CISC} = 4$, $f_{CISC} = 50$ MHz.
- On a **Pentium** (RISC core): $CPI_{eff} = 0.7$ (because two pipelines + cache hits), $f = 100$ MHz.
- On a **pure RISC** machine: $CPI = 1$, $f = 100$ MHz, but requires **15 million instructions** (RISC code is larger).

**Compute execution times:**

For the 486 (CISC):
$$
T_{486} = \frac{10 \times 10^6 \times 4}{50 \times 10^6} = 0.8 \text{ seconds}
$$

For the Pentium (hybrid):
$$
T_{Pent} = \frac{10 \times 10^6 \times 0.7}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.07 \text{ seconds}
$$

For the pure RISC:
$$
T_{RISC} = \frac{15 \times 10^6 \times 1}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.15 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The Pentium (hybrid) is *fastest* because it has the *lowest CPI* (0.7, from 2-issue superscalar) *and* high clock frequency. The CISC has low $f$ and high $CPI$. The pure RISC pays the price of large $N$.

> Speedup of Pentium over 486:
> $$
> S = \frac{0.8}{0.07} \approx 11.4\times
> $$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Pentium (P5)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *internal data path* of the Pentium from external CISC instruction fetch to execution and retirement. It is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (per protocol), since a true gate-level schematic is impractical in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["External x86 CISC Instruction Stream"] --> B["L1 Instruction Cache 8 KB"]
    B --> C["Instruction Fetch Unit IFU with Branch Target Buffer BTB"]
    C --> D1["Decoder 0 Simple 1 uop per cycle"]
    C --> D2["Decoder 1 Simple 1 uop per cycle"]
    C --> D3["Decoder 2 Complex 1 to 4 uops per cycle"]
    D1 --> E["Microinstruction ROM for very complex instructions"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F["Reservation Station and Dispatch Unit in order dispatch out of order issue"]
    F --> G1["U Pipe Integer ALU 32 bit"]
    F --> G2["V Pipe Integer ALU 32 bit restricted to simple instructions"]
    F --> G3["Floating Point Unit FPU 80 bit"]
    F --> G4["Memory Load Unit address generation"]
    F --> G5["Memory Store Unit address generation"]
    G1 --> H["Register File 8 x 32 bit GPRs EAX EBX ECX EDX ESI EDI EBP ESP"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> I["L1 Data Cache 8 KB write back"]
    G5 --> I
    I --> J["64 bit External Data Bus to Main Memory"]
    H --> K["Retirement Unit in order retirement precise exceptions"]
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The path of execution is $A \to B \to C \to D1/D2/D3 \to E \to F \to G1...G5 \to H/I \to K$. This visualizes the **CISC-ISA / RISC-core** nature: external CISC enters at $A$, RISC-style µops are issued at $F$, and results are retired at $K$.

---

## 4.2 RISC vs CISC Philosophy — Side-by-Side Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RISC["RISC Pipeline Simple Hardwired"]
        R1["Fetch 32 bit fixed"] --> R2["Decode single format"] --> R3["Execute ALU in 1 cycle"] --> R4["Write back to large register file"]
    end
    subgraph CISC["CISC Pipeline Microcoded Multi Cycle"]
        C1["Fetch variable 1 to 15 bytes"] --> C2["Decode into microcode sequence"] --> C3["Execute complex microcode 4 to 50 cycles"] --> C4["Write back to small register set"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 The µop Translation Process — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1["x86 Instruction ADD EAX EBX"] --> X2["Predecode identifies opcode and operands"]
    X2 --> X3["Decoder 0 generates uop1: src reg EBX, dst reg EAX, op ADD"]
    X3 --> X4["uop enters Reservation Station"]
    X4 --> X5["U Pipe ALU executes in 1 cycle"]
    X5 --> X6["Retire in order to EAX register"]
    Y1["x86 Instruction REP MOVSB"] --> Y2["Predecode identifies complex string op"]
    Y2 --> Y3["Microcode ROM generates sequence of uops: load, compare, store, increment, decrement, branch"]
    Y3 --> Y4["uops dispatched sequentially to memory and ALU units"]
    Y4 --> Y5["Retire in order at completion"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Teaching Point:** The first flow shows a *simple* x86 instruction (ADD reg, reg) becoming **a single µop**. The second shows a *complex* x86 instruction (REP MOVSB, a string copy) becoming **a sequence of µops** generated by the microcode ROM. This is exactly how the CISC-to-RISC bridge works in the Pentium.

---

## 4.4 Cache Subsystem — Harvard-Style Split L1

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU["Pentium Core"] --> L1I["L1 Instruction Cache 8 KB 2 way set associative"]
    CPU --> L1D["L1 Data Cache 8 KB 2 way set associative write back"]
    L1I --> L2["L2 Cache 256 KB to 512 KB external unified"]
    L1D --> L2
    L2 --> RAM["Main Memory 4 GB addressable 32 bit"]
    L1I -. MESI protocol coherency .-> L1D
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define RISC. List any four characteristics of a RISC processor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** is a microprocessor architecture that uses a small, highly optimized set of instructions, with each instruction designed to execute in a single clock cycle.

**Four characteristics (1 mark each, any four):**
1. **Fixed-length instructions** (typically 32 bits) — simplifies fetch and decode.
2. **Single-cycle execution** for most instructions.
3. **Load/Store architecture** — memory is accessed only via dedicated LOAD and STORE instructions; all arithmetic happens between registers.
4. **Large register file** (≥ 32 registers) — reduces memory traffic.
5. **Hardwired control** (no microcode) — enables high clock frequency.
6. **Heavy pipelining** — deep, efficient pipelines are easy to design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "RISC has fewer instructions" without giving the *load/store* and *single-cycle* properties. These are the **defining** characteristics, not just "fewer instructions."

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is meant by CISC architecture? Give two examples of CISC processors.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** is a microprocessor architecture that uses a large, variable-length instruction set with many addressing modes, supports memory-to-memory operations, and uses microcoded control units.

**Two examples (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Intel x86 family** (8086, 80286, 80386, 80486, Pentium, etc.)
2. **Motorola 68000 family** (or VAX, IBM System/360)

**Key properties of CISC:** variable instruction length (1–15 bytes in x86), many addressing modes (12+ in x86), memory-to-memory arithmetic, microcoded control, multi-cycle instructions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Mentioning only "more instructions" is incomplete. **Variable length, memory-to-memory, microcoded** are the three technical pillars students must state.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — ESE Module 1 Internal Choice

> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, two 14-mark questions are offered as alternatives. Students answer **one**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architectural features of the Pentium processor with a neat block diagram. Discuss how it combines RISC and CISC design philosophies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare RISC and CISC architectures in detail, listing at least **six** distinguishing features with a justification for each. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

The **Pentium (P5)** is a fifth-generation 32-bit microprocessor introduced by Intel in 1993. Its key architectural features are:

1. **Superscalar dual-pipeline core:** The Pentium has two integer pipelines — the **U-pipe** (universal) and the **V-pipe** (vacuum) — allowing up to **2 instructions per cycle (IPC = 2)**. The U-pipe can execute any instruction; the V-pipe is restricted to simple RISC-like instructions.

2. **Superpipelined 5-stage pipeline:** Stages are **Instruction Fetch (IF), Instruction Decode (D1), Address Generation (D2), Execute (EX), and Write-Back (WB)**. This is shallower than the deeper pipelines of later Pentium III/4.

3. **CISC-ISA / RISC-core hybrid design:** Externally, the Pentium accepts **x86 CISC instructions** (variable length, 1–15 bytes). Internally, two decoders translate each CISC instruction into one or more **RISC-style 72-bit micro-operations (µops)**, which are then issued to a RISC-style execution core.

4. **Branch Target Buffer (BTB):** A 256-entry BTB predicts conditional branches dynamically using a 2-bit history counter, reducing pipeline flushes from mispredicted branches.

5. **Harvard-style split L1 cache:** Two separate **8 KB 2-way set-associative caches** — one for instructions, one for data — allowing simultaneous instruction fetch and data access (Harvard principle, applied internally).

6. **64-bit external data bus:** Doubles the data throughput compared to the 486's 32-bit bus, achieving 528 MB/s peak (at 66 MHz) to 800 MB/s (at 100 MHz).

7. **Separate TLB with 4 KB and 4 MB page support:** 32-entry instruction TLB and 64-entry data TLB.

8. **APIC (Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller):** Supports symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) for dual-processor motherboards.

9. **MESI cache coherency protocol:** Maintains cache coherence in multi-processor systems.

**How Pentium combines RISC and CISC:**
- **CISC aspect:** External instruction set (x86) is variable-length, has 24+ addressing modes, supports memory-to-memory operations like `MOV AX, [BX+SI+10]`, and is microcoded.
- **RISC aspect:** Internally, after decoding, the processor executes **fixed-length µops** on a **load/store back-end**, with a **large internal register file** and **hardwired control** for the execution units.
- **Benefit:** Backward compatibility with the entire x86 software base (a CISC legacy) **AND** high performance from RISC-style pipelining and superscalar execution.

**Block diagram:** See the Mermaid functional architecture in **SECTION 4.1** above. (In a written exam, students draw a simplified block diagram showing IFU → Decoders → Reservation Station → 5 Execution Units → Retirement Unit, with separate L1 I-cache and L1 D-cache.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part a):** Students often *list* features without explaining **how** the RISC-CISC combination is achieved. The examiner awards 2 marks specifically for the "RISC-CISC combination" portion. Use the phrase "CISC-ISA / RISC-core" and mention **µops** explicitly.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

| # | Feature | RISC | CISC | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Instruction set size** | Small (50–150 instructions) | Large (100–500+ instructions) | RISC follows 80/20 rule; CISC has instructions for every conceivable HLL construct |
| 2 | **Instruction length** | Fixed (32 bits typical) | Variable (1–15 bytes in x86) | Fixed length simplifies pipelining; variable length saves memory |
| 3 | **Addressing modes** | Few (3–5) | Many (12–24+) | Fewer modes simplify decoding; many modes give compiler flexibility |
| 4 | **Memory access** | Load/Store only | Memory-to-memory allowed | Load/store separation makes datapath pipelinable; memory-to-memory reduces register pressure |
| 5 | **CPI** | ~ 1 cycle per instruction | 4–50 cycles (variable) | RISC: simple datapath, single cycle; CISC: complex microcode |
| 6 | **Clock frequency** | Higher | Lower | RISC: short critical path; CISC: long combinational path through microcode |
| 7 | **Control unit** | Hardwired (combinational) | Microcoded (ROM-based) | Hardwired is faster but inflexible; microcode is slower but easier to extend |
| 8 | **Register file** | Large (≥ 32) | Small (typically 8) | RISC: keep working set in registers; CISC: rely on memory |
| 9 | **Pipelining** | Easy, deep, efficient | Difficult, shallow | Fixed format + single cycle = pipeliner's dream; CISC pipelining is hindered by variable length |
| 10 | **Code size** | Larger | Smaller (compact) | RISC needs more instructions to do the same job |
| 11 | **Compiler role** | Heavy (scheduler, optimizer) | Light | RISC compilers must schedule instructions; CISC microcode does the scheduling |
| 12 | **Examples** | MIPS, SPARC, ARM, PowerPC | Intel x86, Motorola 68k, VAX | — |

**Justification of the choice (synthesis):** RISC's strength is **performance per MHz** and **pipeline depth**, while CISC's strength is **code density** and **backward compatibility**. The Pentium hybrid gives the *best of both worlds*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Simply listing features *without justification* yields only 3 out of 7 marks. Examiners require the **"why"** (justification) for each comparison row. A bare table with no prose loses 50% of the marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **superscalar and superpipelined architecture** of the Pentium processor. Discuss the role of the **U-pipe and V-pipe**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A program contains **20 million instructions** to be executed on a Pentium-class processor with an effective **CPI of 0.8** and a clock frequency of **200 MHz**. Calculate:
  1. The **CPU execution time**.
  2. The **MIPS rating**.
  3. The **bus bandwidth** (in MB/s) given a 64-bit data bus.
  4. If **90% of the program can be parallelized** on a multicore system giving **4× speedup on the parallel part**, find the overall speedup using **Amdahl's law**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Superscalar definition (1 mark):** A superscalar processor can issue **more than one instruction per clock cycle** from a *single* instruction stream. The Pentium has **2 pipelines**, so its peak IPC = 2.

**Superpipelined definition (1 mark):** A superpipelined processor has a *deeper-than-normal* pipeline to increase the clock frequency. The Pentium uses a **5-stage pipeline** (IF, D1, D2, EX, WB).

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Show two parallel pipelines (U and V) emerging from a common Instruction Fetch unit, both feeding into a common Register File, with the U-pipe able to execute any instruction and V-pipe restricted to a subset (typically the "simple" x86 instructions: register-to-register ALU, register-to-register MOV, simple shifts, etc.).

**Role of U-pipe and V-pipe (2 marks):**
- **U-pipe (Universal pipe):** Can execute *any* x86 instruction. It is the primary pipeline and always carries the first instruction of an issue pair.
- **V-pipe (Vacuum pipe):** Can execute only *simple* instructions, called "V-pipe eligible" or "pairable" instructions (e.g., `ADD reg, reg`, `MOV reg, reg`, `CMP reg, reg`, simple shifts, `INC`, `DEC`, `PUSH`, `POP`).
- An **instruction pair** (U + V in same cycle) is issued only when both instructions are pairable and there are no data dependencies between them.
- If a pair cannot be formed, the V-pipe is "stalled" (or "vacuum," hence the name) and the next instruction must go through the U-pipe alone.

**Why superscalar helps (1 mark):** Increases instruction-level parallelism (ILP) and reduces overall CPI below 1. With 2 fully utilized pipelines, the *effective* CPI can approach 0.5.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students draw a generic 5-stage pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) and call it "Pentium architecture." The *defining* feature is the **two parallel pipelines** with **pairability rules**. Draw both pipelines explicitly.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $N = 20 \times 10^6$ instructions
- $CPI = 0.8$
- $f = 200$ MHz $= 200 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Bus width $W = 64$ bits
- $f = 0.9$ parallelized, $n = 4$ on the parallel part

---

**(1) CPU Execution Time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \frac{N \times CPI}{f} \\
        &= \frac{20 \times 10^6 \times 0.8}{200 \times 10^6} \\
        &= \frac{16 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^6} \\
        &= 0.08 \text{ seconds} \\
        &= 80 \text{ milliseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Working shown:** $20 \times 0.8 = 16$. Then $16 / 200 = 0.08$ s.

---

**(2) MIPS Rating:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6} \\
     &= \frac{200 \times 10^6}{0.8 \times 10^6} \\
     &= \frac{200}{0.8} \\
     &= 250 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Working shown:** $200 / 0.8 = 250$.

---

**(3) Bus Bandwidth:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{W \times f}{8} \\
  &= \frac{64 \times 200 \times 10^6}{8} \\
  &= 8 \times 200 \times 10^6 \\
  &= 1600 \times 10^6 \text{ Bytes/s} \\
  &= 1600 \text{ MB/s} \\
  &= 1.6 \text{ GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Working shown:** 64/8 = 8 bytes per transfer × 200 million transfers = 1600 MB/s.

---

**(4) Amdahl's Law Speedup:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{(1-0.9) + \frac{0.9}{4}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.225} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.325} \\
  &= 3.076\times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Working shown:** $0.9/4 = 0.225$. Sum with $0.1 = 0.325$. Reciprocal $= 3.076$.

---

**Final Answer Summary:**
1. $T_{CPU} = 0.08$ s
2. $MIPS = 250$
3. $B = 1600$ MB/s = $1.6$ GB/s
4. $S \approx 3.08\times$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Unit errors are the #1 mark-killer. Always write units (seconds, MB/s, MIPS). Forgetting to convert **MHz to Hz** ($200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^6$ Hz) is the most common mistake, resulting in wrong order of magnitude.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Pentium / RISC / CISC**

### **Core Concepts**
- [ ] **Pentium = CISC-ISA + RISC-core hybrid.** External x86 (CISC), internal µops (RISC).
- [ ] **µop** = 72-bit internal micro-operation, the actual RISC-like operation executed.
- [ ] **3 decoders:** 2 simple (1 µop each) + 1 complex (1–4 µops). Complex instructions are broken into µops in the microcode ROM.
- [ ] **2 pipelines:** U-pipe (universal) + V-pipe (simple/paired). Peak IPC = 2.
- [ ] **5 execution units:** 2 integer ALU + 1 FPU + 1 load + 1 store.
- [ ] **64-bit external data bus** — twice the 486's 32-bit bus.
- [ ] **Harvard-style split L1 cache:** 8 KB I-cache + 8 KB D-cache.
- [ ] **BTB (Branch Target Buffer):** 256-entry, 2-bit history dynamic branch prediction.

### **RISC vs CISC Mnemonic (use in exams!)**
- **R**ISC = **R**egular, **R**egister-heavy, **R**educing work per instruction
- **C**ISC = **C**omplex, **C**ompact, **C**ombines many operations

### **Key Formulas (must memorize)**
- $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI / f$
- $MIPS = f / (CPI \times 10^6)$
- $S = 1 / \big((1-f) + f/n\big)$ — **Amdahl's Law**
- $B = W \times f / 8$ — Bus bandwidth

### **Common Exam Traps**
- [ ] Pentium has 32-bit internal registers but 64-bit **external** data bus.
- [ ] Original P5 pipeline is 5 stages (same as 486), but Pentium is *superscalar* — that's the differentiator.
- [ ] V-pipe restrictions: only **simple** instructions can pair; complex ones go U-pipe alone.
- [ ] Amdahl's limit: with $f$ parallelized, max speedup with infinite cores is $1/(1-f)$.
- [ ] Don't confuse MIPS (millions of instructions) with **throughput** (IPC × f).
- [ ] RISC ≠ ARM; RISC is a *philosophy*, ARM/MIPS/SPARC are *implementations* of it.

### **Likely 14-Mark Question Patterns**
1. "Explain the architectural features of the Pentium with block diagram" (≈ 7 marks)
2. "Compare RISC and CISC" (≈ 7 marks) — must include table + prose justification
3. "Compute CPU time / MIPS / Bandwidth given N, CPI, f" (numerical, 7 marks)
4. "Apply Amdahl's law to a parallelization scenario" (numerical, 7 marks)

### **One-Line Definitions to Memorize**
- **RISC:** "Small instruction set, single-cycle execution, load/store, fixed length, large register file, hardwired control."
- **CISC:** "Large instruction set, variable length, memory-to-memory, microcoded, multi-cycle."
- **Pentium:** "Fifth-generation 32-bit CISC-ISA / RISC-core hybrid with superscalar dual pipelines, 64-bit external data bus, and split L1 caches."
- **µop:** "An internal 72-bit RISC-like micro-operation that the Pentium generates from each x86 CISC instruction."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Superscalar Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Superscalar Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superscalar Architecture** is a CPU microarchitectural design paradigm in which the processor is equipped with **multiple redundant execution units** (integer, floating-point, load/store, branch) and supporting hardware (decoders, reservation stations, register-renaming logic, reorder buffer) that allow **more than one instruction to be fetched, decoded, issued, executed, and committed in a single clock cycle**, thereby exploiting **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** beyond the limits of conventional pipelined scalar processors.

A superscalar processor is essentially a *parallel pipeline* where the central dispatcher can simultaneously route multiple independent instructions to different functional units. The theoretical upper bound is dictated by the number of execution units, but the **effective** throughput is limited by data dependencies, control dependencies, and resource conflicts.

### Key Terminology Aligned with KTU 2024 Module 1

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **ILP** | Instruction-Level Parallelism — the degree to which instructions can be executed concurrently. |
| **Issue Width (n)** | Maximum number of instructions issued per clock cycle. |
| **IPC** | Instructions Per Cycle (ideal value = issue width). |
| **CPI** | Cycles Per Instruction (ideal value = 1/n). |
| **In-Order Issue** | Instructions are dispatched to functional units in program order. |
| **Out-of-Order (OoO) Issue** | Independent instructions are dispatched ahead of stalled ones to keep units busy. |
| **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** | Hardware structure that retires instructions in program order for precise exceptions. |
| **Register Renaming** | Removes false (Write-After-Read, WAR; Write-After-Write, WAW) data dependencies. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to distinguish **scalar**, **superpipelined**, and **superscalar** organizations, and to clearly identify the role of dynamic scheduling, branch prediction, and register renaming.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Counter Supermarket"

Imagine a single-counter grocery store (this is a **scalar processor**). Only one customer (instruction) can be checked out at a time. To increase throughput, the store adds *multiple counters*, but registers each customer in a single queue that any free counter can serve. A smart *queue manager* looks ahead and assigns the next three customers, who do not need the same item (no dependency), to three different counters simultaneously. The customers leave in the order they entered (to ensure no confusion at the exit / store policy).

- **Multiple counters** → Multiple execution units.
- **Smart queue manager** → Instruction dispatch / reservation station.
- **Look-ahead assignment** → Out-of-order issue.
- **Customers leaving in original order** → In-order commit / Reorder Buffer.
- **A customer needing the receipt of the previous customer** → True data dependency (RAW hazard) — must wait.

This is precisely how a superscalar processor converts what *looks* like a serial instruction stream into parallel hardware activity.

---

## 1.3 Quantitative Anchors (Board-Exam Standard Metrics)

- **Issue width of modern desktop superscalars:** typically **n = 4 to 6**.
- **Server-class (e.g., Intel Core i9, AMD Ryzen 9):** issue width **n = 5 to 8**, often coupled with Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT) reaching effective issue widths of **10+** instructions per cycle.
- **Maximum theoretical speedup vs scalar processor:** **n ×** (bounded by Amdahl's law and dependency stalls).
- **Minimum CPI:** **1/n** (when there are zero stalls).
- **Typical real-world CPI:** **0.25 – 0.5** for a 4-issue machine (IPC ≈ 2 – 4) on well-optimized code.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love the **distinction** between *Superpipeline* (deeper stages, one execution unit) and *Superscalar* (multiple execution units at the same stage). Memorize this — it is a guaranteed 3-mark short-answer question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Stages of a Superscalar Pipeline

A modern superscalar datapath executes each instruction through **six cooperative stages**. Each stage may operate on multiple instructions per cycle.

### Stage 1 — Instruction Fetch (IF)
- Fetches **n** instructions per cycle from the I-cache (line size must be ≥ n × instruction length).
- Uses **multi-way branch prediction** to pre-fetch from multiple predicted paths.
- Hardware: Instruction TLB, I-cache, branch target buffer (BTB), return-address stack (RAS).

### Stage 2 — Instruction Decode (ID / Pre-decode)
- Decodes the **n** instructions in parallel into micro-operations (µops) in CISC machines, or directly identifies opcodes + operand specifiers in RISC machines.
- Reads the **register file** for source operands.
- Checks for **structural hazards** (e.g., two instructions need the same execution port).

### Stage 3 — Dispatch / Issue
- Central arbiter assigns each decoded instruction to an **available functional unit**.
- For **out-of-order issue**, the arbiter consults a **Reservation Station** (RS) holding decoded instructions until their operands are ready.
- Performs **register renaming** via a **Rename Map Table (RMT)** to eliminate WAR and WAW hazards.

### Stage 4 — Execution (EX)
- Functional units (ALU1, ALU2, FPU, LSU, BRU) execute in parallel.
- Load/Store Unit (LSU) accesses D-cache; cache misses are tolerated via **load-store reordering queues** (LSQ).

### Stage 5 — Completion / Write-Back (WB)
- Results are written to the **physical register file** (or the architectural file through the ROB).
- Wake-up logic broadcasts the tag of the produced result so dependent instructions can leave the RS.

### Stage 6 — Commit / Retire
- The **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** retires instructions **in program order** to the architectural state.
- Ensures **precise interrupts** even when execution was out-of-order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Reorder Buffer?** Out-of-order execution breaks the "all effects of instruction *i* happen before all effects of instruction *i+1*" contract. The ROB re-establishes this contract at the commit stage, which is mandatory for correct exception handling and for supporting context switches.

---

## 2.2 Hazard Classification in a Superscalar Pipeline

A superscalar machine inherits all hazards of a scalar pipeline **plus** additional hazards unique to wide-issue designs.

### 2.2.1 True Data Dependency — Read After Write (RAW)
- Instruction *j* must wait for instruction *i* to produce a register/memory value.
- **Cannot be eliminated by hardware** — it is a real data flow constraint.
- Mitigated by **forwarding/bypassing** and **value prediction**.

### 2.2.2 Anti-dependency — Write After Read (WAR)
- Instruction *j* writes a register that an earlier instruction *i* still needs to read.
- Eliminated by **register renaming** (each write gets a fresh physical register).

### 2.2.3 Output Dependency — Write After Write (WAW)
- Two instructions write the same destination; wrong order would corrupt the architectural value.
- Eliminated by **register renaming**.

### 2.2.4 Structural Hazards
- Two instructions request the same physical resource (e.g., both need the FPU).
- Mitigated by **duplicating units** and by the dispatcher's scheduling logic.

### 2.2.5 Control Hazards (Branches)
- Until the branch is resolved, the fetch unit does not know the next path.
- Mitigated by **dynamic branch prediction** (2-bit saturating counters, gshare, tournament predictors) and **speculative execution**.

---

## 2.3 Performance Bounds: Why n-issue ≠ n× Speedup

A naïve expectation is that a 4-issue superscalar should be **4× faster** than a scalar processor. In practice, this is unattainable because:

1. **Program-level ILP is finite.** Studies (Tjaden & Flynn, Wall, Riseman & Foster) suggest average ILP between **2 and 5** for typical code under aggressive analysis.
2. **Branchy code** limits the look-ahead window.
3. **Memory stalls** (cache misses) take hundreds of cycles, during which the execution units idle.
4. **Power, complexity, and verification costs** grow super-linearly with issue width.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete** set of formulas KTU examiners can ask for under the topic "Superscalar Architecture." Internalize the symbols, not just the formula — board answers must include a verbal explanation of the variables.

| # | Formula | Description / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{IPC} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CPI}}$ | Average instructions completed per cycle. |
| 2 | $\text{CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} + \text{Stall Cycles per Instruction}$ | $\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1/n$ for an $n$-issue machine. |
| 3 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{scalar}}}{T_{\text{superscalar}}} = \dfrac{\text{IPC}_{\text{superscalar}}}{\text{IPC}_{\text{scalar}}}$ | Direct ratio of instruction throughput. |
| 4 | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{n}{1 + (n-1) \cdot s}$ | Speedup of an $n$-issue machine with fractional sequential part $s$ (ILP-limited). |
| 5 | $\text{Amdahl's Law: } S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $n$ = issue width. |
| 6 | $\text{Avg.\ Stall CPI} = \sum_{i} p_i \cdot \text{stall}_i$ | Weighted by frequency of each hazard type. |
| 7 | $\text{Branch Penalty} = p_{\text{wrong}} \times \text{mispredict cost (cycles)}$ | Determines effective fetch throughput. |
| 8 | $\text{Pipeline Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Issue Width} \times \text{Clock Freq.}}{1 + \text{Stall fraction}}$ | Engineering throughput in instructions/second. |
| 9 | $\text{Renaming table size} = 2^{k}$ | $k$ = number of physical register ID bits. |
| 10 | $\text{ROB depth} = \text{max in-flight window} = n \times d$ | $n$ = issue width, $d$ = pipeline depth. |

> [!WARNING]
> In a markdown table cell, **never** type the vertical bar `|` for absolute value. Use LaTeX `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead — the system parser interprets `|` as a column separator. The KTU board PDF renders these correctly.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Superscalar design is **not a research curiosity** — it is the dominant paradigm in every general-purpose CPU shipped today:

- **Intel P6 / Core micro-architecture:** in-order front end, OoO back end, issue width up to 6.
- **AMD Zen 4 / Zen 5:** 6-wide decode, 8-issue back end.
- **ARM Cortex-X / Apple M-series:** wide OoO superscalar combined with NEON/SIMD.
- **Embedded space:** ARM Cortex-A55 (2-wide), Cortex-A510, RISC-V "Olympiad" cores.
- **Verification & EDA:** Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA build cycle-accurate superscalar simulators that drive chip design closure.

Understanding superscalar architecture is the gateway to **VLIW, EPIC, SMT, and modern heterogeneous compute** (CPU + GPU + NPU SoCs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **canonical derivations** that frequently appear in KTU Part B questions. Each derivation is presented in full algebraic detail, with every transition explicitly explained.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower Bound on CPI of an n-Issue Superscalar

### Statement
For an n-issue superscalar processor with zero stalls (ideal conditions), the average CPI is bounded below by $1/n$.

### Step-by-step Proof
