Let the machine issue $n$ instructions per cycle in the ideal case. Therefore the number of cycles required to execute $N$ instructions is

$$
C_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{N}{n}.
$$

Average cycles per instruction:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{C_{\text{ideal}}}{N} = \frac{N/n}{N} = \frac{1}{n}.
$$

For a real machine, every stall adds cycles:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1}{n} + \text{stall CPI}.
$$

Taking the lower bound $\text{stall CPI} \geq 0$ yields

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} \geq \frac{1}{n}}.
$$

The corresponding upper bound on instructions per cycle is

$$
\text{IPC} \leq n.
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Even a *perfect* superscalar cannot exceed $n$ instructions per cycle; any real program executes below this limit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Superscalar Speedup

### Setup
A fraction $f$ of a program's instructions can benefit from the n-issue parallel execution. The remaining $(1 - f)$ portion is strictly sequential (e.g., instruction fetch, exception handling, unavoidable stalls).

### Derivation
Execution time of the scalar baseline (1 instruction / cycle):

$$
T_{\text{base}} = T_{s} + T_{p},
$$

where $T_{s} = (1-f)\,T_{\text{base}}$ and $T_{p} = f\,T_{\text{base}}$.

Execution time on the n-issue machine (parallel portion accelerated by $n$):

$$
T_{n} = (1-f)\,T_{\text{base}} + \frac{f}{n}\,T_{\text{base}}.
$$

$$
T_{n} = T_{\text{base}}\left[(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}\right].
$$

Speedup:

$$
S_{n} = \frac{T_{\text{base}}}{T_{n}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}.
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{n} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}}.
$$

### Asymptote
As $n \rightarrow \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S_{n} = \frac{1}{1-f}.
$$

This is the **fundamental ceiling** of any superscalar design — no amount of issue-width engineering can break it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stall-CPI from Hazards

### Setup
A 4-issue superscalar has the following stall distribution on a benchmark:

- 20% of cycles: branch mispredict (cost = 12 cycles per mispredict, occurring 8% of the time).
- 10% of cycles: L1 D-cache miss (cost = 30 cycles per miss, occurring 2% of the time).
- 5% of cycles: structural hazard (1 stall cycle).

### Derivation
Compute the average stall cycles per instruction:

$$
\text{Stall CPI} = p_{\text{branch}} \times \text{cost}_{\text{branch}} + p_{\text{cache}} \times \text{cost}_{\text{cache}} + p_{\text{struct}} \times \text{cost}_{\text{struct}}.
$$

$$
\text{Stall CPI} = 0.08 \times 12 + 0.02 \times 30 + 0.05 \times 1.
$$

$$
\text{Stall CPI} = 0.96 + 0.60 + 0.05.
$$

$$
\text{Stall CPI} = 1.61 \text{ cycles per instruction}.
$$

Total CPI:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{4} + 1.61 = 0.25 + 1.61 = 1.86.
$$

IPC:

$$
\text{IPC} = \frac{1}{1.86} \approx 0.538.
$$

**Critical Insight for the Board:** Notice that the **branch mispredict** and **cache miss** dominate the stall CPI. The 4-issue hardware is being **grossly underutilized** — only ~0.54 of 4 possible instructions complete per cycle. This is why high-end designs invest so heavily in branch predictors and prefetchers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (typical 7-mark sub-part):** A 4-issue superscalar processor runs at 3 GHz. The average IPC measured on a workload is 2.4. The dynamic instruction count is $2.5 \times 10^{9}$ instructions. Calculate (a) the total execution time, (b) the effective CPI, (c) the CPI lost to stalls, and (d) the percentage of *peak* IPC achieved.

### (a) Total Execution Time
Cycles executed:

$$
C = \frac{N}{\text{IPC}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{2.4} = 1.0417 \times 10^{9} \text{ cycles}.
$$

Clock period:

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 0.333 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 0.333 \text{ ns}.
$$

Total time:

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = C \times T_{\text{clk}} = 1.0417 \times 10^{9} \times 0.333 \times 10^{-9}.
$$

$$
T_{\text{exec}} \approx 0.347 \text{ s}.
$$

### (b) Effective CPI

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\text{IPC}} = \frac{1}{2.4} = 0.4167.
$$

### (c) CPI Lost to Stalls

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{stall}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} - \frac{1}{n} = 0.4167 - 0.25 = 0.1667.
$$

### (d) Percentage of Peak IPC

$$
\% \text{ Peak} = \frac{\text{IPC}_{\text{actual}}}{n} \times 100\% = \frac{2.4}{4} \times 100\% = 60\%.
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{exec}} = 0.347 \text{ s},\quad \text{CPI} = 0.4167,\quad \text{Stall CPI} = 0.1667,\quad \text{Utilization} = 60\%.}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Examiners reward students who show the unit conversion explicitly. Always carry units through, and always box the final answer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

The following matrix summarises the **interlocking effects** of design choices. Memorise it for essay-type 14-mark questions.

| Design Lever | Effect on ILP | Effect on Clock | Effect on Power | Effect on Verification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Increase issue width $n$ | ↑ (theoretically) | ↓ (longer combinational paths) | ↑↑ super-linear | ↑↑ combinational explosion |
| Deeper pipeline (superpipeline) | ↑ (modest) | ↑ (shorter stages) | ↑ (more latches) | ↑ (more pipeline regs) |
| OoO execution + renaming | ↑↑ (eliminates false deps) | ≈ neutral | ↑↑ (CAM tag broadcast) | ↑ (complex wakeup) |
| Better branch predictor | ↑↑ (fewer flushes) | ≈ neutral | ↑ (BTB storage) | ≈ neutral |
| Larger ROB / RS window | ↑ (more in-flight) | ≈ neutral | ↑↑ (CAM/RAM size) | ↑ (corner cases) |
| SMT (2 threads) | ↑ IPC, may ↓ per-thread | ≈ neutral | ↑ modest | ↑ (deadlock analysis) |
| Larger caches / prefetch | ↑ (hides memory wall) | ≈ neutral | ↑ (SRAM leakage) | ↓ (less stall) |
| Simpler (in-order) | ↓ ILP | ↑ (short clock) | ↓ | ↓ |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix shows why **all** modern CPUs use a *combination* — wide issue + OoO + deep speculation + aggressive prefetch + SMT — because each lever attacks a different bottleneck.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic n-Issue Superscalar Datapath

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the data-flow and control-flow of a typical out-of-order superscalar CPU. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to satisfy Mermaid v10 syntax safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ICache] --> B1[Fetch Unit n-wide]
    B1 --> C1[Branch Predictor and BTB]
    C1 --> D1[Decode Unit n-wide]
    D1 --> E1[Register Rename Map Table]
    E1 --> F1[Dispatch and Reservation Station]
    F1 --> G1A[ALU Integer 1]
    F1 --> G1B[ALU Integer 2]
    F1 --> G1C[FPU]
    F1 --> G1D[Load Store Unit LSU]
    F1 --> G1E[Branch Unit BRU]
    G1A --> H1[Write Back Bus]
    G1B --> H1
    G1C --> H1
    G1D --> H1
    G1E --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Reorder Buffer ROB]
    I1 --> J1[Architectural Register File]
    I1 --> K1[Retire to Memory and PC]
    F1 -. wakeup tags .-> F1
    H1 -. result broadcast .-> F1
    C1 -. redirect flush .-> B1
    I1 -. mispredict repair .-> C1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Solid arrows = primary data flow.
- Dotted arrows = control/feedback (wakeup, broadcast, misprediction repair).
- The **Reservation Station** acts as the *heart* — it absorbs decoded instructions, monitors operand readiness, and dispatches them to the appropriate functional unit as soon as both operands and the unit are free.
- The **ROB** is the *guardian* of program order — it commits results in fetch order regardless of completion order.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Instruction Lifecycle in a Superscalar CPU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Instruction Fetched from ICache] --> S2{Decoded Successfully?}
    S2 -- No --> S2A[Raise Illegal Opcode Exception]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Read Source Operands from Rename Table]
    S3 --> S4[Allocate ROB Entry and RS Slot]
    S4 --> S5{Operands Ready?}
    S5 -- No --> S5A[Wait in RS and Listen for Wakeup Tags]
    S5A --> S5
    S5 -- Yes --> S6{Functional Unit Free?}
    S6 -- No --> S6A[Wait in RS for Free Unit]
    S6A --> S6
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Dispatch to Functional Unit]
    S7 --> S8[Execute in Functional Unit]
    S8 --> S9[Write Result to Physical Register]
    S9 --> S10[Broadcast Wakeup Tag to RS]
    S10 --> S11{Head of ROB?}
    S11 -- No --> S11A[Remain in ROB until Head Reached]
    S11A --> S11
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Commit to Architectural State]
    S12 --> S13[Free ROB Entry and Advance Head Pointer]
```

---

## 4.3 Hazard Resolution Schematic (Register Renaming Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Architectural Registers R0..R31] --> RM[Rename Map Table]
    RM --> PR[Physical Registers P0..P127]
    PR --> RS[Reservation Station Entries]
    RS --> FU[Functional Units]
    FU --> WB[Write Back to Physical Register]
    WB --> RM
    RM -. updates map .-> PR
```

**How register renaming eliminates WAR/WAW hazards:**

Consider the sequence:

1. `I1: R1 = R2 + R3` → assigned physical `P10`
2. `I2: R4 = R1 * 5`  → reads `P10`, writes a fresh `P22`
3. `I3: R1 = R7 - R8` → writes a fresh `P33` (R1's architectural map now points to `P33`)

Even though `I3` writes `R1` after `I1` reads it (WAR), the rename map ensures `I2` reads the correct `P10` value, not the to-be-overwritten `P33`. WAW is also resolved — there is no architectural write conflict because each writer updates a distinct physical register.

---

## 4.4 Instruction Window & In-Flight Tracking

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Fetch Buffer
        FB1[Slot 0]
        FB2[Slot 1]
        FB3[Slot 2]
        FB4[Slot 3]
    end
    subgraph Reservation Station
        RS0[RS Entry 0]
        RS1[RS Entry 1]
        RS2[RS Entry 2]
        RS3[RS Entry 3]
        RS4[RS Entry 4]
        RS5[RS Entry 5]
    end
    subgraph Reorder Buffer
        ROB0[ROB 0]
        ROB1[ROB 1]
        ROB2[ROB 2]
        ROB3[ROB 3]
        ROB4[ROB 4]
        ROB5[ROB 5]
        ROB6[ROB 6]
        ROB7[ROB 7]
    end
    FB1 --> RS0
    FB2 --> RS1
    FB3 --> RS2
    FB4 --> RS3
    RS0 --> ROB0
    RS1 --> ROB1
    RS2 --> ROB2
    RS3 --> ROB3
    ROB0 --> Retire0[Retired]
    ROB1 --> Retire0
    ROB2 --> Retire0
    ROB3 --> Retire0
```

This diagram illustrates the **in-flight instruction window** — a key concept in KTU exam answers. The window consists of all instructions that have been fetched but not yet retired. The wider this window, the more ILP the processor can expose, but the more hardware and energy it consumes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between a scalar, a superpipelined, and a superscalar processor. Mention the typical issue width of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Scalar | Superpipelined | Superscalar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Issue width | 1 | 1 | $n \geq 2$ |
| Pipeline depth | Normal | Very deep (many short stages) | Normal or moderate |
| Throughput strategy | Increase clock | Increase clock | Increase parallelism per cycle |
| Issue width (typical) | 1 | 1 | 4 – 6 |

A scalar processor issues one instruction per cycle. A superpipelined processor (e.g., MIPS R4000) increases clock by subdividing stages but still issues one instruction per cycle. A superscalar processor (e.g., Intel Core i9, AMD Zen 5) issues **multiple instructions per cycle** by replicating execution units and dispatching independent instructions in parallel. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)? State two hardware techniques used to exploit ILP in a superscalar processor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ILP (Definition — 1 mark):** ILP is the property of a program that expresses the degree to which its instructions can be executed concurrently, independent of data and control flow. Studies by Riseman and Foster (1972) and Wall (1991) measured the average ILP in general-purpose code to be between **2 and 5** under ideal analysis.

**Two hardware techniques (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Pipelining + multiple execution units** — multiple functional units operate in parallel, each on a different instruction.
2. **Out-of-order execution with register renaming** — instructions are dispatched to execution units as soon as their operands are ready, regardless of program order; false dependencies are eliminated by mapping architectural registers to a larger pool of physical registers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]
**(a)** Explain the **organization of a superscalar processor** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Instruction Dispatch Unit, Reservation Station, Reorder Buffer, and Register Renaming Unit** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, derive the **performance bounds** of an n-issue superscalar processor. Comment on why the speedup is always less than $n$ in real systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

The organization of a superscalar processor is illustrated in the block diagram below (textual representation since the question requires a hand-drawn diagram in the answer script):

1. **Instruction Fetch Unit** — fetches $n$ instructions per cycle from the I-cache; uses a Branch Target Buffer (BTB) and a Return Address Stack (RAS) to predict branches. **(1 mark)**
2. **Decode Unit** — decodes the $n$ instructions in parallel, identifying the opcode, source/destination registers, and immediate values. **(1 mark)**
3. **Register Rename Unit** — maps each architectural register to a fresh physical register from the pool (P0, P1, ..., P127 in a typical machine), eliminating WAR and WAW hazards. **(1 mark)**
4. **Instruction Dispatch Unit** — assigns each decoded instruction to a Reservation Station slot, monitors operand readiness, and dispatches to a free functional unit. **(1 mark)**
5. **Reservation Station (RS)** — holds instructions until both source operands are available and a matching functional unit is free; uses **content-addressable memory (CAM)** for tag-based wakeup. **(1 mark)**
6. **Functional Units** — multiple ALUs, an FPU, a Load-Store Unit (LSU), and a Branch Unit (BRU) execute instructions in parallel. **(1 mark)**
7. **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** — a circular buffer holding in-flight instructions; retires them in program order to ensure precise exceptions and to maintain architectural state. **(1 mark)**

> **Conclusion (1 mark, included in 7):** The cooperative action of these units allows the superscalar processor to look ahead in the instruction stream, identify independent instructions, and execute them in parallel, achieving IPC approaching the issue width $n$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

Let $N$ be the total dynamic instruction count. For an $n$-issue superscalar:

**Ideal cycles (no stalls):**
$$
C_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{N}{n}.
$$
$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{n}. \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Real cycles with stalls:**
$$
C_{\text{real}} = \frac{N}{n} + \text{Stall Cycles}.
$$
$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{real}} = \frac{1}{n} + \text{Stall CPI}. \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Amdahl's speedup** for fraction $f$ of parallelizable work:
$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}. \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Why $S < n$ in practice (1 mark):**
- Finite ILP in real programs ($f < 1$).
- Branch mispredictions cause flushes of in-flight work.
- Cache misses stall the pipeline for tens to hundreds of cycles.
- Structural hazards force some units to idle.
- Power, complexity, and verification cost grow super-linearly with $n$, limiting how wide a design can be made cost-effective.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $S = n$ as the "speedup" of an $n$-issue processor. This is **incorrect** — the board explicitly deducts 2 marks for this oversimplification. Always derive the bound using Amdahl's Law and explain why real systems fall short of $n$.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative for the same 14 marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Restyled]
**(a)** What are the **different types of hazards** encountered in a superscalar pipeline? Explain how each is resolved with modern hardware techniques. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-issue superscalar processor has the following statistics on a benchmark: branches form 18% of all instructions, the branch predictor accuracy is 92%, and each misprediction costs 14 cycles. L1 data cache miss rate is 4%, and each miss costs 28 cycles. Structural hazards cause an average of 0.06 stall cycles per instruction. Compute the **average CPI** and **IPC**, and comment on the dominant bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. **RAW — Read After Write (True Dependency).** *(1 mark definition + 1 mark resolution)*
   - **Definition:** Instruction $j$ reads a register/memory location that is written by a prior instruction $i$. This is a real data-flow constraint and **cannot be removed by hardware alone**.
   - **Resolution:** Data forwarding (bypassing) paths route the result from the producing functional unit directly to the consumer; Load-Store Queue (LSQ) forwarding handles memory RAW. Out-of-order execution allows independent instructions to bypass the stalled one.

2. **WAR — Write After Read (Anti-dependency).** *(1 mark definition + 1 mark resolution)*
   - **Definition:** Instruction $j$ writes a register that an earlier instruction $i$ still needs to read.
   - **Resolution:** Register renaming — each write is directed to a fresh physical register, so the earlier read operates on the correct older physical value.

3. **WAW — Write After Write (Output dependency).** *(1 mark definition + 1 mark resolution)*
   - **Definition:** Two instructions write the same architectural register; the later write must happen last in program order.
   - **Resolution:** Register renaming — each write gets a distinct physical register, and the Reorder Buffer commits them in order.

4. **Structural Hazard.** *(1 mark for resolution)*
   - **Definition:** Two instructions simultaneously demand the same hardware resource.
   - **Resolution:** Duplicate the resource (e.g., 2 ALUs, 2 FPUs) and have the dispatcher arbitrate.

5. **Control Hazard (Branch).** *(1 mark for resolution)*
   - **Definition:** Until a branch is resolved, the next instructions to fetch are unknown.
   - **Resolution:** Dynamic branch prediction (2-bit saturating counters, gshare, TAGE), speculative execution, and Branch Target Buffer (BTB). Mispredictions trigger a pipeline flush and redirect fetch.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to confuse **WAR** and **WAW** definitions. Memorize the mnemonics: **W**rite-After-**R**ead (WAR) means the *Write* is the *second* event; **W**rite-After-**W**rite (WAW) means two *Writes* to the same location. Reverse the order in your answer and you'll lose the full 2 marks allocated to that hazard.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Branch misprediction contribution.** *(2 marks)*

Misprediction rate $= 1 - 0.92 = 0.08$.
Branch frequency $= 0.18$.
Branch mispredicts per instruction:

$$
p_{\text{bm}} = 0.18 \times 0.08 = 0.0144.
$$

Branch stall cycles per instruction:

$$
\text{stall}_{\text{branch}} = 0.0144 \times 14 = 0.2016.
$$

**Step 2 — Cache miss contribution.** *(2 marks)*

Cache miss rate $= 0.04$.
Cost per miss $= 28$ cycles.

$$
\text{stall}_{\text{cache}} = 0.04 \times 28 = 1.12.
$$

**Step 3 — Structural hazard contribution.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{stall}_{\text{struct}} = 0.06.
$$

**Step 4 — Total Stall CPI.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{Stall CPI} = 0.2016 + 1.12 + 0.06 = 1.3816.
$$

**Step 5 — Base + Stall CPI.** *(1 mark)*

For a 4-issue machine, $\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1/4 = 0.25$.

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{total}} = 0.25 + 1.3816 = 1.6316.
$$

**Step 6 — IPC.** *(bonus 0.5 mark for completeness)*

$$
\text{IPC} = \frac{1}{1.6316} = 0.613.
$$

**Step 7 — Dominant Bottleneck Comment.** *(0.5 mark)*

The **L1 data cache miss** is the dominant contributor (1.12 of 1.3816 stall CPI ≈ 81%). Even with a wide 4-issue front end, the design is **memory-bound**, not compute-bound. The remedy is a larger L1, prefetching, or a non-blocking cache.

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI} = 1.63,\quad \text{IPC} = 0.61,\quad \text{Dominant bottleneck: L1 data cache misses}.}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings (Consolidated Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing Superpipeline with Superscalar.**
> A superpipeline has *one* execution unit but many pipeline stages. A superscalar has *many* execution units. Examiners repeatedly test this distinction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Stating speedup = n.**
> Always derive via Amdahl's Law. The ceiling is $1/(1-f)$, not $n$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Forgetting the Reorder Buffer.**
> OoO execution is **impossible** to retire correctly without the ROB (or equivalent). Mention the ROB in every OoO-related answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — Skipping units in the block diagram.**
> The fetch → decode → rename → dispatch → execute → commit chain must be in that order. Skipping the rename stage in a diagram is a 2-mark deduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 — Mixing CPI and IPC arithmetic.**
> CPI is the **average cycles per instruction**; IPC is its reciprocal. Sloppy conversion loses marks in numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Glance at this list the night before the exam — it covers ~90% of the marks available on this topic.**

- **Definition:** Superscalar = multiple instructions per cycle using replicated execution units. Goal: exploit ILP.
- **Issue width $n$** = max instructions dispatched per cycle. Ideal CPI = $1/n$.
- **Six logical stages:** Fetch → Decode → Rename → Dispatch/RS → Execute → Retire (ROB).
- **Hazards:** RAW (true — cannot eliminate), WAR (anti — eliminate by renaming), WAW (output — eliminate by renaming), Structural (duplicate units), Control (branch prediction).
- **Register Renaming** = map $N$ architectural registers to $M > N$ physical registers via a **Rename Map Table (RMT)**. Removes WAR + WAW.
- **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** = commits in program order; enables **precise exceptions** despite OoO execution.
- **Reservation Station (RS)** = holds decoded instructions + operand tags; performs **tag-based wakeup**.
- **Branch prediction** = dynamic hardware (BTB, 2-bit counter, gshare, TAGE). Mispredict cost = pipeline-flush penalty in cycles.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/n]$. Limit as $n \to \infty$ is $1/(1-f)$.
- **Performance metrics:** CPI, IPC, speedup, stall cycles, branch mispredict rate, cache miss rate.
- **Distinguish from:** Scalar (1 issue, 1 unit), Superpipelined (1 issue, many stages), VLIW (compiler-driven, no dynamic hardware scheduling).
- **Real-world examples:** Intel Core (6-wide OoO), AMD Zen 5 (8-issue), Apple M-series (8-wide + NEON).
- **Design trade-offs:** Wider issue → more ILP but more power, more verification complexity, longer critical path.
- **Common numerical problems:** Compute CPI given stall contributions; compute speedup via Amdahl; identify dominant bottleneck from a stall breakdown.
- **Memory ordering note:** Loads and stores go through the **Load-Store Queue (LSQ)** to preserve memory consistency.
- **Why OoO matters:** Without OoO, a single cache miss freezes the entire front end; with OoO, independent instructions fly through.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam-Day Mantra:** "Fetch wide → Decode wide → Rename → Reserve → Dispatch → Execute in parallel → Retire in order. Hazards are RAW, WAR, WAW, Structural, Control. The bottleneck is usually memory, not compute. Amdahl's Law is the ultimate truth."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MMX Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MMX Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **MMX Technology** stands for **MultiMedia eXtensions**. It is an **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)** instruction set extension introduced by **Intel Corporation in 1996** with the **Pentium MMX (P55C)** processor. MMX adds **57 new instructions**, **8 new 64-bit registers (MM0–MM7)**, and **four new packed data types** to the x86 instruction set architecture to accelerate multimedia, communications, and graphics workloads.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, MMX Technology is a **hardware-level parallel processing enhancement** that enables the CPU to perform a single arithmetic or logical operation on multiple data elements simultaneously — a foundational concept for modern vector and SIMD extensions (SSE, AVX, NEON).

> [!NOTE] > **Key Architectural Identity:** The 8 MMX registers (MM0–MM7) are **not** physically new registers. They are **aliased onto the existing 80-bit FPU (Floating-Point Unit) registers ST(0)–ST(7)**. The low-order 64 bits of each FPU register are reused as an MMX register.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a chef in a kitchen who can only cut **one carrot at a time** with a single knife. This is how a normal CPU works — one instruction, one piece of data. 

Now imagine giving that chef a special tool that chops **8 carrots in a single downward motion**. The chef makes the same gesture (one instruction), but now processes 8 carrots (8 pieces of data) at the same time. **That is exactly what MMX does to the processor.**

| Scenario | CPU Action | Analogy |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Normal x86 | `ADD AL, BL` → adds **1 byte** | Cut 1 carrot |
| MMX | `PADDB MM0, MM1` → adds **8 bytes** in parallel | Cut 8 carrots at once |

### 1.3 Why MMX Was Needed (The Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **The Multimedia Bottleneck (Pre-1996):** Multimedia applications (audio/video codecs, image processing, 2D/3D graphics) consume enormous CPU cycles because they repeatedly apply the **same operation** (e.g., add, multiply) to **large streams of small data items** (pixels, audio samples). The classical ALU processes only 8, 16, or 32 bits per instruction — leaving most of the 64-bit datapath **idle**.

**MMX fixes this by:**
- Packing many small integers into one 64-bit register.
- Performing one parallel operation on all of them simultaneously.
- Reducing instruction count by up to **8×** for byte-level operations.

### 1.4 Physical / Architectural Constants to Remember

| Constant | Value | Significance |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| New Instructions | **57** | Number of MMX opcodes added |
| New Registers | **8** | MM0 – MM7, each **64 bits** |
| Register Aliasing | FPU ST(0)–ST(7) | Low 64 bits of FPU reused |
| Max Throughput Gain | **8×** (byte) / **4×** (word) / **2×** (dword) | Parallel SIMD factor |
| Datatypes | 4 packed types | Byte, Word, Doubleword, Quadword |
| Introduced In | **Pentium P55C (1996)** | First consumer MMX CPU |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The SIMD Principle Behind MMX

**SIMD = Single Instruction, Multiple Data.** A standard (scalar) operation looks like:

$$ \text{Result}[i] = A[i] + B[i] \quad \text{— executed N times} $$

An MMX packed operation looks like:

$$ \text{Result}[0..k] = A[0..k] + B[0..k] \quad \text{— executed ONCE} $$

where $k$ is the number of packed elements (8, 4, 2, or 1 depending on datatype).

### 2.2 The Four MMX Packed Data Types

> [!NOTE] > A 64-bit MMX register can be subdivided into smaller elements. The **same 64 bits** are interpreted differently by different instructions.

| Data Type | Size | Elements per Register | Typical Use |
|-----------|------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Packed Byte** | 8 bits | **8** bytes | 8-bit pixel/audio data |
| **Packed Word** | 16 bits | **4** words | 16-bit audio samples |
| **Packed Doubleword** | 32 bits | **2** dwords | 32-bit graphics/coordinates |
| **Packed Quadword** | 64 bits | **1** qword | 64-bit scalar integer |

**Pictorial Representation (Packed Bytes in MM0):**

```
 MM0 (64 bits)
 ┌──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┐
 │ B7   │ B6   │ B5   │ B4   │ B3   │ B2   │ B1   │ B0   │
 │ 8bit │ 8bit │ 8bit │ 8bit │ 8bit │ 8bit │ 8bit │ 8bit │
 └──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┘
   Add Packed Bytes (PADDB) → 8 parallel 8-bit additions
```

### 2.3 Saturation Arithmetic — A Critical KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Saturation Arithmetic** prevents **wrap-around overflow** by clamping the result to the maximum (or minimum) representable value. This is vital for multimedia (e.g., pixel values must stay in **0–255**, audio samples must stay in valid signed ranges).

| Mode | Behaviour on Overflow | Formula |
|------|----------------------|---------|
| **Wrap-around (default)** | Result rolls over (modular) | $R = (A+B) \bmod 2^n$ |
| **Unsigned Saturate** | Clamps to $2^n - 1$ | $R = \min(A+B,\ 2^n-1)$ |
| **Signed Saturate** | Clamps to $\pm(2^{n-1}-1)$ | $R = \text{clamp}(A+B)$ |

**Packed saturation example** (8-bit unsigned saturation, `PADDUSB`):

$$ R_i = \text{clamp}_8(A_i + B_i) = \begin{cases} 0, & A_i + B_i < 0 \\ 255, & A_i + B_i > 255 \\ A_i + B_i, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

This is computed **in parallel for all 8 bytes** in a single instruction.

### 2.4 Categories of the 57 MMX Instructions

> [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner almost always asks to **list and explain the categories of MMX instructions**. Memorize the **7 groups** below.

| # | Category | Count | Example Mnemonics | Purpose |
|---|----------|-------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Data Transfer** | 4 | `MOVD`, `MOVQ` | Move between MM/INT/MEM |
| 2 | **Arithmetic** | 17 | `PADDx`, `PSUBx`, `PMULx`, `PMADDWD` | Parallel add/sub/mul |
| 3 | **Comparison** | 6 | `PCMPEQx`, `PCMPGTx` | Parallel compare → mask |
| 4 | **Conversion** | 4 | `PACKSSxx`, `PUNPCKHxx`, `PUNPCKLxx` | Pack/unpack/change width |
| 5 | **Logical** | 4 | `PAND`, `PANDN`, `POR`, `PXOR` | Bitwise AND/OR/XOR |
| 6 | **Shift** | 4 | `PSLLx`, `PSRLx`, `PSRAx` | Parallel logical/arith shifts |
| 7 | **EMMS** | 1 | `EMMS` | Empty MMX State (exit MMX) |
| 8 | **Cache/Misc** | ~17 | `MASKMOVQ`, `MOVNTQ`, `PSHIMW` | Streaming, prefetch |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **EMMS (Empty MM State)** is the **only mandatory instruction** to be executed **after finishing MMX code** and **before executing any FPU instruction**. It clears the *tag word* of the FPU so the FPU stack is correctly recognized as empty. Forgetting EMMS causes **catastrophic FPU corruption** — a classic KTU pitfall.

### 2.5 Multiply-Add Instruction (`PMADDWD`)

`PMADDWD` is the **most powerful** MMX instruction. It performs:
1. **4 parallel 16-bit × 16-bit signed multiplications**.
2. **Adjacent pair-wise 32-bit additions** of the four products.

This single instruction executes **4 multiplies + 2 adds in one cycle**, ideal for **FIR filters, dot products, and convolution kernels** used in DSP and image processing.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
| Parallel Speedup (byte) | $S_{\text{byte}} = 8\times$ | 8-bit pixel operations |
| Parallel Speedup (word) | $S_{\text{word}} = 4\times$ | 16-bit audio samples |
| Parallel Speedup (dword) | $S_{\text{dword}} = 2\times$ | 32-bit coordinates |
| Unsigned Saturation (n-bit) | $R = \min(A+B,\ 2^n - 1)$ | Pixel clamping (0–255) |
| Signed Saturation (n-bit) | $R = \text{clamp}(A+B,\ -2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1)$ | Audio sample safety |
| Wrap-around (n-bit) | $R = (A+B) \bmod 2^n$ | Default MMX arithmetic |
| PMADDWD products | $P_i = A_{2i} \cdot B_{2i} + A_{2i+1} \cdot B_{2i+1}$ | FIR filter taps |
| Total Instructions Added | $N_{\text{MMX}} = 57$ | New ISA extensions |
| Register Count | $N_{\text{reg}} = 8$ | MM0–MM7 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Saturation Arithmetic Boundary Conditions

> [!NOTE] > Below is the **complete algebraic derivation** of saturation clamping for an **8-bit unsigned** MMX addition. This is the most-likely KTU numerical problem.

**Problem Context:** Add two unsigned 8-bit bytes $A = 250$ and $B = 200$ using `PADDUSB` (Packed Add Unsigned with Saturation, Byte). Compute the result.

**Step 1 — Identify the representation range for unsigned 8-bit.**

$$ \text{Range}_{u8} = [0,\ 2^8 - 1] = [0,\ 255] $$

**Step 2 — Perform the raw addition (ignoring saturation).**

$$ A + B = 250 + 200 = 450 $$

**Step 3 — Check for overflow (whether the sum exceeds 255).**

$$ A + B = 450 > 255 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{OVERFLOW DETECTED} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the saturation rule.**

$$ R = \text{clamp}_{u8}(A + B) = \min(450,\ 255) = 255 $$

**Step 5 — Compare with wrap-around (default) result.**

$$ R_{\text{wrap}} = 450 \bmod 256 = 194 $$

**Conclusion:**

$$ \boxed{R_{\text{saturate}} = 255 \quad \text{vs.} \quad R_{\text{wrap-around}} = 194} $$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** In a video frame, a wrap-around to 194 would cause a sudden dark pixel flash. Saturation to 255 keeps the pixel cleanly bright-white — **physically correct behaviour for image blending.**

### 3.2 Derivation: PMADDWD (Multiply-Add) Step-by-Step

**Problem:** Compute `PMADDWD MM0, MM1` where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MM0 (4 packed 16-bit signed words)} &= [a_3, a_2, a_1, a_0] \\
\text{MM1 (4 packed 16-bit signed words)} &= [b_3, b_2, b_1, b_0]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Perform 4 parallel 16-bit signed multiplications.**

$$ p_0 = a_0 \cdot b_0,\quad p_1 = a_1 \cdot b_1,\quad p_2 = a_2 \cdot b_2,\quad p_3 = a_3 \cdot b_3 $$

**Step 2 — Each 16×16 multiplication produces a 32-bit signed result (still packed).**

$$ \text{Intermediate} = [p_3,\ p_2,\ p_1,\ p_0] \quad \text{(as 4 packed 32-bit signed values)} $$

**Step 3 — Perform pair-wise 32-bit additions of adjacent products.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_0 &= p_0 + p_1 = a_0 b_0 + a_1 b_1 \\
R_1 &= p_2 + p_3 = a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final 64-bit result layout (2 packed 32-bit signed dwords).**

$$ \text{MM0 (after PMADDWD)} = [R_1,\ R_0] \quad \text{(high dword} = R_1,\ \text{low dword} = R_0) $$

**Workload Analysis:**

$$ \text{Total scalar ops in PMADDWD} = 4\ \text{muls} + 2\ \text{adds} = 6\ \text{operations in 1 instruction} $$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Speedup vs. Datatype

> [!NOTE] > A frequently asked KTU question: *"How much speedup does MMX provide for an operation on 256 bytes of data?"*

| Datatype | Elements in 64-bit Reg | Registers Needed for 256 bytes | Scalar Instructions | MMX Instructions | Speedup $S$ |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------------|---------------------|------------------|--------------|
| Byte | 8 | $256/8 = 32$ | 256 | 32 | $8\times$ |
| Word | 4 | $256/4 = 64$ | 128 | 32 | $4\times$ |
| Dword | 2 | $256/2 = 128$ | 64 | 32 | $2\times$ |
| Qword | 1 | $256/1 = 256$ | 32 | 32 | $1\times$ (no gain) |

**Generic Speedup Formula (proved):**

$$ S = \frac{N_{\text{scalar}}}{N_{\text{MMX}}} = \frac{N_{\text{bytes}}}{N_{\text{elements/reg}}} \cdot \frac{N_{\text{elements/reg}}}{N_{\text{bytes/iter}}} = \frac{\text{register width in bits}}{\text{element width in bits}} $$

For 8-bit elements in a 64-bit register:

$$ S_{\text{byte}} = \frac{64}{8} = 8 \quad \checkmark $$

### 3.4 Register Aliasing — Engineering Implication

> [!IMPORTANT] > MM0–MM7 share physical storage with FPU ST(0)–ST(7). The FPU register is **80 bits**; MMX uses only the **low 64 bits** and ignores the upper 16 exponent/sign bits. After using MMX, if you do not call `EMMS`, the FPU tag word will incorrectly assume valid 80-bit floats are present — leading to **floating-point exceptions** in subsequent FPU code.

```
 80-bit FPU Register ST(i)
 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┐
 │          16-bit Exponent / Sign          │   64-bit Mantissa  │
 │         (overlapped by MMX tags)         │  ← USED AS MM(i)   │
 └──────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────┘
                                              ↑ Aliased as MM(i)
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MMX System-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application: Audio Video Codec] --> B[x86 Instruction Stream]
    B --> C{Opcode Decode}
    C -->|0x0F prefix| D[MMX Instruction Detected]
    C -->|Standard| E[Conventional ALU Execution]
    D --> F[Read MM0 MM7 Registers]
    F --> G[64 bit Packed Datapath]
    G --> H{Packed Operation}
    H --> I[PADD PSUB PMUL PMADDWD]
    H --> J[PCMP PCMPEQ PCMPGT]
    H --> K[PAND POR PXOR PANDN]
    H --> L[PSLL PSRL PSRA Shift]
    H --> M[PACKSS PUNPCKH PUNPCKL]
    I --> N[Result written to MM0 MM7]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    E --> O[Continue Scalar Pipeline]
    N --> P{EMMS Issued?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Clear FPU Tag Word - Safe FPU Reuse]
    P -->|No| R[Warning: FPU Corruption Risk]
```

### 4.2 MMX Register File — Aliasing with FPU Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FPU ["FPU Register Stack (80 bits each)"]
        ST0["ST(0) 80-bit"]
        ST1["ST(1) 80-bit"]
        ST2["ST(2) 80-bit"]
        ST3["ST(3) 80-bit"]
        ST4["ST(4) 80-bit"]
        ST5["ST(5) 80-bit"]
        ST6["ST(6) 80-bit"]
        ST7["ST(7) 80-bit"]
    end
    subgraph MMX ["MMX Register File (64-bit visible)"]
        MM0["MM0 64-bit"]
        MM1["MM1 64-bit"]
        MM2["MM2 64-bit"]
        MM3["MM3 64-bit"]
        MM4["MM4 64-bit"]
        MM5["MM5 64-bit"]
        MM6["MM6 64-bit"]
        MM7["MM7 64-bit"]
    end
    ST0 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM0
    ST1 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM1
    ST2 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM2
    ST3 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM3
    ST4 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM4
    ST5 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM5
    ST6 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM6
    ST7 -.->|alias low 64 bits| MM7
```

### 4.3 Packed Datatype Layout Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R["64-bit MMX Register"]
    R --> P1["Packed Byte 8 x 8-bit"]
    R --> P2["Packed Word 4 x 16-bit"]
    R --> P3["Packed Doubleword 2 x 32-bit"]
    R --> P4["Packed Quadword 1 x 64-bit"]
    P1 --> U1["Use: 8-bit RGB pixel arithmetic"]
    P2 --> U2["Use: 16-bit PCM audio samples"]
    P3 --> U3["Use: 32-bit X Y coordinates"]
    P4 --> U4["Use: 64-bit scalar integers"]
```

### 4.4 MMX Instruction Execution Pipeline (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Fetch Opcode with 0x0F Prefix] --> S2[Step 2: Decode to Identify MMX Class]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Read Operands from MM0 MM7 or Memory]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Parallel Operation on All Sub-Elements]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5: Saturation Required?}
    S5 -->|Yes PADDUSB etc| S6[Clamp Result to Range]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Store Wrap-Around Result]
    S6 --> S8[Step 6: Write Back to Destination MM Register]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Step 7: Check for EMMS Before Next FPU Op]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define MMX Technology. List the new data types introduced by MMX.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

MMX (MultiMedia eXtensions) is an **SIMD instruction set extension** added by Intel in 1996 to accelerate multimedia processing. It introduces **57 new instructions** and **8 new 64-bit registers (MM0–MM7)** aliased onto the FPU stack.

The four new packed data types are:
1. **Packed Byte** — 8 bytes per register.
2. **Packed Word** — 4 words per register.
3. **Packed Doubleword** — 2 dwords per register.
4. **Packed Quadword** — 1 qword per register.

*(Award full marks for listing all 4 types with the correct count.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q2. What is the purpose of the `EMMS` instruction in MMX programming?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `EMMS` (Empty MM State) instruction is used to **clear the FPU tag word** after MMX operations, indicating that the FPU registers no longer contain valid floating-point data. This is essential because the **MMX registers MM0–MM7 are physically aliased to the FPU stack ST(0)–ST(7)**. If `EMMS` is not issued before a subsequent FPU instruction, the processor may misinterpret the MMX integer data as floating-point operands, leading to **catastrophic floating-point exceptions and incorrect program behaviour**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **ONE** of the following questions (either Q.A or Q.B). Each has sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sample]*

**(a)** Explain the architecture of MMX technology in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the SIMD concept and the four packed data types supported by MMX registers. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain **Saturation Arithmetic** and **Wrap-around Arithmetic**. Compute the result of `PADDUSB MM0, MM1` for the following input bytes, demonstrating both modes: (7 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MM0 (bytes)} &= [200,\ 100,\ 50,\ 10,\ 0,\ 240,\ 180,\ 90] \\
\text{MM1 (bytes)} &= [100,\ 50,\ 200,\ 10,\ 0,\ 30,\ 100,\ 50]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MMX Architecture (7 marks):**
- **Definition & Year:** MMX (MultiMedia eXtensions) introduced by Intel in **1996** with the **Pentium P55C** processor. (1 mark)
- **SIMD Principle:** Single Instruction, Multiple Data — one operation applied in parallel to multiple data elements packed inside a 64-bit register. (1 mark)
- **Register File:** 8 new 64-bit registers **MM0–MM7**, physically aliased to the low 64 bits of FPU stack ST(0)–ST(7). (1 mark)
- **Packed Data Types** (with diagram): (3 marks)
  - Packed Byte (8 × 8-bit)
  - Packed Word (4 × 16-bit)
  - Packed Doubleword (2 × 32-bit)
  - Packed Quadword (1 × 64-bit)
- **57 new instructions** spanning arithmetic, comparison, conversion, logical, shift, data transfer, and EMMS. (1 mark)

**(b) Saturation vs. Wrap-around (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
- **Wrap-around:** Overflow bits are discarded; result computed modulo $2^n$. Can cause sudden dark flashes in pixel data.
- **Saturation:** On overflow, result is **clamped** to the maximum (or minimum) representable value. Saturation range for **unsigned 8-bit** is **[0, 255]**.

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

For each of the 8 byte-pairs $(A_i, B_i)$, compute $S_i = A_i + B_i$:

| $i$ | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | Raw Sum | Wrap-around ($S \bmod 256$) | Saturation ($\min(S, 255)$) |
|-----|-------|-------|---------|-----------------------------|------------------------------|
| 0 | 200 | 100 | 300 | 44 | **255** |
| 1 | 100 | 50  | 150 | 150 | 150 |
| 2 | 50  | 200 | 250 | 250 | 250 |
| 3 | 10  | 10  | 20  | 20  | 20  |
| 4 | 0   | 0   | 0   | 0   | 0   |
| 5 | 240 | 30  | 270 | 14  | **255** |
| 6 | 180 | 100 | 280 | 24  | **255** |
| 7 | 90  | 50  | 140 | 140 | 140 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MM0 (wrap-around)} &= [44,\ 150,\ 250,\ 20,\ 0,\ 14,\ 24,\ 140] \\
\text{MM0 (saturated)}   &= [255,\ 150,\ 250,\ 20,\ 0,\ 255,\ 255,\ 140]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget to perform modulo 256** in wrap-around mode. The formula is $R = (A_i + B_i) \bmod 2^8 = (A_i + B_i) \bmod 256$. Marks are deducted if the wrap-around column is not computed correctly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]*

**(a)** List and explain the **seven categories of MMX instructions** with at least **two example mnemonics** from each category. Why is `EMMS` considered mandatory in MMX code? (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **aliasing of MMX registers with the FPU register stack**. Discuss the engineering consequences of this aliasing and explain the role of `EMMS` in preventing register-state corruption. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Categories of MMX Instructions (7 marks):**

| # | Category | Examples (2 each) | Purpose (1 mark) |
|---|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Data Transfer** | `MOVD`, `MOVQ` | Move 32/64-bit data between MM regs and memory/GP regs |
| 2 | **Arithmetic** | `PADDB`, `PADDW`, `PSUBB`, `PMULLW`, `PMADDWD` | Parallel add, subtract, multiply, multiply-add |
| 3 | **Comparison** | `PCMPEQB`, `PCMPGTW` | Compare elements, produce all-1s (true) or all-0s (false) mask |
| 4 | **Conversion** | `PACKSSWB`, `PACKUSWB`, `PUNPCKLBW` | Pack narrower elements into wider (saturated), or unpack |
| 5 | **Logical** | `PAND`, `POR`, `PXOR`, `PANDN` | Bitwise AND, OR, XOR, AND-NOT |
| 6 | **Shift** | `PSLLW`, `PSRLD`, `PSRAD`, `PSLLQ` | Parallel logical/arithmetic shift of all elements |
| 7 | **State Management** | `EMMS` | Clear FPU tag word after MMX work |

**Why EMMS is mandatory (1 mark):** Because MMX registers are aliased onto the FPU stack, the FPU's tag word is left in an invalid state after MMX operations. If a subsequent FPU instruction executes without `EMMS`, the CPU may either (a) incorrectly treat integer data as a valid 80-bit float, or (b) raise a floating-point exception. `EMMS` resets the tag word to indicate an empty FPU stack.

**(b) Register Aliasing Diagram & Engineering Consequences (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
    Physical Hardware (80-bit FPU Registers)
    ┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
    │  ST(0)   │  ST(1)   │  ST(2)   │  ST(3)   │  ST(4)   │  ST(5)   │  ST(6)   │  ST(7)   │
    │  80-bit  │  80-bit  │  80-bit  │  80-bit  │  80-bit  │  80-bit  │  80-bit  │  80-bit  │
    └────┬─────┴────┬─────┴────┬─────┴────┬─────┴────┬─────┴────┬─────┴────┬─────┴────┬─────┘
         │          │          │          │          │          │          │          │
         │ Low 64 bits aliased as ↓        │          │          │          │          │
         ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼
    ┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
    │   MM0    │   MM1    │   MM2    │   MM3    │   MM4    │   MM5    │   MM6    │   MM7    │
    │  64-bit  │  64-bit  │  64-bit  │  64-bit  │  64-bit  │  64-bit  │  64-bit  │  64-bit  │
    └──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┘
```

**Engineering Consequences (3 marks):**
1. **No new physical registers** → die size and cost are minimized.
2. **Cannot mix MMX and FPU concurrently** → programmer must issue `EMMS` before transitioning back to floating-point code (e.g., a 3D engine that uses MMX for vertex math and FPU for matrix transforms).
3. **Context switch overhead** → saving/restoring FPU state in OS automatically saves/restores MMX state since they share storage. This is efficient.

**Role of `EMMS` (1 mark):** Resets the FPU tag word to `0b11` (empty) for all 8 registers, signalling that no valid 80-bit floats are present, thus safely allowing the next FPU instruction to begin with a clean stack.

> [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common KTU mistake is stating that **MMX and FPU instructions can be freely interleaved**. They cannot. Because of the register aliasing, the two units **must be used in strictly separate code regions**, with `EMMS` acting as a barrier. Mixing them without `EMMS` is **undefined behaviour** and is a guaranteed mark-loser.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):**

- **MMX** = **MultiMedia eXtensions**, introduced by **Intel in 1996** with the **Pentium P55C** processor.
- **MMX is SIMD** (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) — one operation on many packed data elements.
- **57 new instructions** and **8 new 64-bit registers (MM0–MM7)** were added.
- **Register aliasing:** MM0–MM7 share physical storage with the **low 64 bits of FPU ST(0)–ST(7)**.
- **Four packed data types:** Packed Byte (8), Packed Word (4), Packed Doubleword (2), Packed Quadword (1).
- **Speedup factor** = 64 / element width in bits → 8×, 4×, 2×, 1× respectively.
- **Saturation arithmetic** clamps results to the max/min representable value; prevents wrap-around artefacts in pixel/audio data.
- **Wrap-around** is the default MMX arithmetic mode.
- **Seven instruction categories:** Arithmetic, Comparison, Conversion, Logical, Shift, Data Transfer, State (EMMS).
- **`PMADDWD`** performs 4 multiplies + 2 adds in one instruction — used in FIR filters and dot products.
- **`EMMS` is mandatory** before transitioning back to FPU code — failure causes FPU tag word corruption.
- **Applications:** MPEG video decode/encode, audio codecs (MP3), speech recognition, 2D/3D graphics, image processing, communications DSP.
- **Limitations of MMX:** No new physical registers, no separate MMX/FPU context, no floating-point MMX (introduced later in SSE), no privileged control over cache.
- **Successor to MMX:** **SSE (Streaming SIMD Extensions)** in Pentium III (1999) added 8 new 128-bit XMM registers and floating-point SIMD.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pipelining** is an implementation technique in advanced microprocessors whereby multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by dividing the instruction processing into a series of independent stages. Each stage performs a specific micro-operation and hands its result to the next stage, while a new instruction enters the previously vacated stage. This is analogous to an **assembly line** in manufacturing, where the total work is partitioned into sequential substations.

Mathematically, if a non-pipelined processor requires **T** clock cycles to execute one instruction, and the pipeline has **k** stages, then after an initial fill-up latency, the processor completes (ideally) one instruction every clock cycle, achieving a maximum theoretical **speedup of k**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Pipelining in the PEBMT741 syllabus spans the 5-stage MIPS pipeline, pipeline hazards, data forwarding, control hazard mitigation (branch prediction, delayed branch), and quantitative performance evaluation (speedup, throughput, efficiency). Mastery of the *speedup formula* and *stall cycle analysis* is mandatory for the ESE.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Shop

Imagine a laundromat with **3 washing machines (stages)**: Washer → Dryer → Folder.

- **Non-Pipelined (Serial):** One person loads the washer, waits, then loads the dryer, waits, then loads the folder. Total time per load = sum of all stages.
- **Pipelined (Overlap):** Person 1 in Washer, Person 2 enters as soon as Person 1 moves to Dryer, Person 3 enters Washer when Person 1 moves to Folder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Pipelining **does not reduce the latency of a single instruction**; it increases the **throughput** — the number of instructions completed per unit time. The dryer still takes 30 minutes for one load; we just keep it busy continuously.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Definitions)

- **Clock Cycle Time (T_clk):** The time period of one clock pulse driving the pipeline. Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**.
- **Pipeline Depth (k):** Number of stages in the pipeline.
- **Throughput (TP):** Number of instructions completed per clock cycle. *Ideal TP = 1 instruction/cycle*.
- **Speedup (S):** Ratio of execution time of non-pipelined to pipelined implementation.
- **Efficiency (η):** Ratio of actual speedup to maximum speedup, expressed as a percentage.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** Average number of clock cycles per instruction; *ideal CPI = 1*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5-Stage MIPS Pipeline (Reference Architecture)

The classic **MIPS (Microprocessor without Interlocked Pipeline Stages)** RISC pipeline divides instruction execution into five stages:

| Stage | Mnemonic | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IF** — Instruction Fetch | Reads instruction from Instruction Memory using PC |
| 2 | **ID** — Instruction Decode / Register Fetch | Decodes opcode, reads source registers from Register File |
| 3 | **EX** — Execute / Address Calculation | ALU operation, computes effective address, branch decision |
| 4 | **MEM** — Memory Access | Loads from or stores to Data Memory (for LW/SW) |
| 5 | **WB** — Write Back | Writes result back to Register File |

> [!NOTE]
> Each stage uses dedicated hardware (separate latches/buffers between stages called **pipeline registers**) so all five stages can operate in parallel on *different* instructions.

## 2.2 Types of Pipelining

1. **Instruction Pipelining** — Overlapping execution of *machine instructions* (most common in CPUs).
2. **Arithmetic Pipelining** — Overlapping the sub-operations of a *single arithmetic operation* (e.g., floating-point multiplication: exponent addition, mantissa multiplication, normalization).
3. **Processor Pipelining** — Macro-level pipelining of entire processors (used in vector/supercomputers).

## 2.3 Pipeline Hazards — The "Why It Fails" Section

A **hazard** is any condition that prevents the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle, forcing the pipeline to **stall**.

### (a) Structural Hazards
- **Cause:** Hardware resource conflict (e.g., single memory port used by both IF and MEM stages).
- **Solution:** Duplicate the hardware (Harvard architecture: separate instruction & data caches).

### (b) Data Hazards
- **Cause:** An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline.
- **Sub-types:** RAW (Read After Write — true dependency), WAR (Write After Read — anti-dependency), WAW (Write After Write — output dependency).
- **Solutions:**
  1. **Data Forwarding (Bypassing):** Route the ALU result from EX/MEM or MEM/WB register directly to the ALU input of the dependent instruction — *no stall needed*.
  2. **Pipeline Interlock / Stalling:** Insert **NOP (No Operation) bubbles** (1-cycle stall) when forwarding is impossible (e.g., LW-use hazard).
  3. **Compiler Scheduling (Software):** Reorder instructions to avoid dependencies.

### (c) Control Hazards
- **Cause:** Branch and jump instructions — the next PC is not known until EX stage.
- **Solutions:**
  1. **Branch Prediction (Static/Dynamic):** Guess taken/not-taken.
  2. **Delayed Branch:** Compiler fills the slot after the branch with a useful, independent instruction.
  3. **Speculative Execution:** Execute predicted path, squash on misprediction.
  4. **Branch Target Buffer (BTB):** Cache of recent branch target addresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** LW-use hazard (load followed immediately by an instruction using the loaded value) is **NOT fully resolvable by forwarding alone** — it requires **1 stall cycle**. This is a frequently tested edge case.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Speedup | $S_{ideal}$ | $S_{ideal} = k$ | dimensionless |
| Actual Speedup | $S_{actual}$ | $S_{actual} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + (n-1)}$ | dimensionless |
| Speedup w/ stalls | $S$ | $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + (n-1) + \text{stall cycles}}$ | dimensionless |
| Throughput | $TP$ | $TP = \dfrac{n}{k + (n-1) + \text{stalls}}$ | instr/cycle |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + (n-1) + \text{stalls}}$ | (0 to 1) |
| CPI (pipelined) | $CPI$ | $CPI = 1 + \text{stalls per instruction}$ | cycles/instr |
| Execution Time | $T_{exec}$ | $T_{exec} = (n-1+k) \cdot T_{clk}$ | seconds |
| Amdahl's Limit | $S_{max}$ | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ | dimensionless |
| Branch Penalty (n-cycle) | $P_b$ | $P_b = n - 1$ | cycles |
| Pipeline Throughput (max) | $TP_{max}$ | $TP_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | instr/sec |

> Where:
> - **n** = total number of instructions
> - **k** = number of pipeline stages
> - **f** = fraction of execution time enhanced by the improvement

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Speedup of a k-Stage Pipeline

**Premise:** A non-pipelined processor executes *n* instructions in *n·k* clock cycles (each instruction takes *k* cycles serially). A k-stage pipelined processor takes *k* cycles to fill the pipeline plus *(n-1)* cycles to drain and complete the remaining instructions.

$$
T_{\text{non-pipe}} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}
$$

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{clk}
$$

Therefore, the ideal speedup *S* is:

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{non-pipe}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_{clk}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}
$$

**Limit Analysis (as n → ∞):**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k}{\frac{k}{n} + 1 - \frac{1}{n}} = \frac{k}{0 + 1 - 0} = k
$$

> **Conclusion:** For an infinitely long instruction stream, the maximum speedup equals the number of pipeline stages *k*. This is the **theoretical upper bound** (Amdahl's Law applied to pipelining).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speedup With Pipeline Stalls

**Premise:** Suppose *s* stall cycles are inserted per instruction (on average) due to hazards. The new cycle count per instruction becomes $(1 + s)$, and the total time for *n* instructions becomes:

$$
T_{\text{pipe,stall}} = n \cdot (1 + s) \cdot T_{clk}
$$

But more accurately, accounting for fill-up latency:

$$
T_{\text{pipe,stall}} = (k + (n-1) + n \cdot s) \cdot T_{clk}
$$

Speedup becomes:

$$
S = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}}{(k + (n-1) + n \cdot s) \cdot T_{clk}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + (n-1) + n \cdot s}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 4-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. Each stage takes 20 ns. Branch instructions (20% of total) incur a 2-cycle penalty. Calculate: (a) Execution time, (b) Throughput, (c) Speedup, (d) Efficiency.

### Given Data
- $k = 4$ stages
- $n = 100$ instructions
- $T_{clk} = 20$ ns
- Branch fraction = 0.20
- Branch penalty = 2 cycles

### Step 1 — Calculate Total Stall Cycles
$$
\text{Stall cycles} = n \cdot f_{\text{branch}} \cdot P_b
$$
$$
\text{Stall cycles} = 100 \times 0.20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ cycles}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Clock Cycles
$$
\text{Total cycles} = k + (n - 1) + \text{stall cycles}
$$
$$
\text{Total cycles} = 4 + (100 - 1) + 40 = 4 + 99 + 40 = 143 \text{ cycles}
$$

### Step 3 — Execution Time
$$
T_{exec} = 143 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 2860 \text{ ns} = 2.86 \;\mu s
$$

### Step 4 — Throughput
$$
TP = \frac{n}{T_{exec}} = \frac{100}{2860 \times 10^{-9}} = 34.97 \times 10^{6} \text{ instr/sec}
$$

### Step 5 — Speedup
$$
S = \frac{n \cdot k}{\text{Total cycles}} = \frac{100 \times 4}{143} = \frac{400}{143} \approx 2.797
$$

### Step 6 — Efficiency
$$
\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{2.797}{4} \approx 0.6993 = 69.93\%
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Mitigation Comparison

| Hazard Type | Detection Point | Hardware Cost | Performance Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structural | Resource allocator | High (duplicate HW) | 0 cycles |
| Data (RAW) — ALU-ALU | ID stage | Low (forwarding mux) | 0 cycles |
| Data (LW-Use) | ID stage | Medium (interlock) | **1 cycle stall** |
| Control (Static Predict NT) | EX stage | Low | 0–2 cycles |
| Control (2-bit Dyn. Pred.) | IF stage | High (BTB) | ~0.5 cycle avg |
| Control (Delayed Branch) | Compiler | None (software) | 0 cycles (if slot filled) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 5-Stage MIPS Pipeline Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pcReg[PC Register] --> ifStage
    ifStage[IF Stage Instruction Fetch] --> ifidReg[IF ID Pipeline Register]
    ifidReg --> idStage[ID Stage Decode and Register Read]
    idStage --> idexReg[ID EX Pipeline Register]
    idexReg --> exStage[EX Stage ALU Execute]
    exStage --> exmemReg[EX MEM Pipeline Register]
    exmemReg --> memStage[MEM Stage Memory Access]
    memStage --> memwbReg[MEM WB Pipeline Register]
    memwbReg --> wbStage[WB Stage Write Back]
    wbStage --> regFile[Register File]
    regFile --> idStage
    ifStage --> pcPlus4[PC Plus 4 Adder]
    pcPlus4 --> pcReg
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pipeline Timing Diagram (Instruction Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cc1["Clock Cycle 1"]
        i1if["I1 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cc2["Clock Cycle 2"]
        i1id["I1 ID"]
        i2if["I2 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cc3["Clock Cycle 3"]
        i1ex["I1 EX"]
        i2id["I2 ID"]
        i3if["I3 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cc4["Clock Cycle 4"]
        i1mem["I1 MEM"]
        i2ex["I2 EX"]
        i3id["I3 ID"]
        i4if["I4 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cc5["Clock Cycle 5"]
        i1wb["I1 WB"]
        i2mem["I2 MEM"]
        i3ex["I3 EX"]
        i4id["I4 ID"]
        i5if["I5 IF"]
    end
    cc1 --> cc2 --> cc3 --> cc4 --> cc5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Data Forwarding Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    exmemRegA[EX MEM Register] --> muxA[Forward Mux A]
    memwbRegA[MEM WB Register] --> muxA
    idexRegA[ID EX Register] --> muxA
    muxA --> aluA[ALU Input A]
    exmemRegB[EX MEM Register] --> muxB[Forward Mux B]
    memwbRegB[MEM WB Register] --> muxB
    idexRegB[ID EX Register] --> muxB
    muxB --> aluB[ALU Input B]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Hazard Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hazards[Pipeline Hazards] --> structural[Structural Hazards]
    hazards --> data[Data Hazards]
    hazards --> control[Control Hazards]
    structural --> structSol[Solution Duplicate Hardware e.g. Harvard]
    data --> raw[RAW Read After Write]
    data --> war[WAR Write After Read]
    data --> waw[WAW Write After Write]
    raw --> rawSol[Solution Forwarding plus Interlock]
    war --> warSol[Solution Register Renaming]
    waw --> wawSol[Solution Register Renaming]
    control --> ctrlSol1[Static Branch Prediction]
    control --> ctrlSol2[Dynamic Branch Prediction]
    control --> ctrlSol3[Delayed Branch Slot]
    control --> ctrlSol4[Branch Target Buffer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define pipelining. List the three types of pipeline hazards.

**Model Answer:**

**Pipelining** is a technique of overlapping the execution of multiple instructions by dividing the instruction processing into a series of independent stages, each performing a specific sub-operation, such that multiple instructions can be in different stages of execution simultaneously.

The three types of pipeline hazards are:
1. **Structural Hazards** — hardware resource conflicts.
2. **Data Hazards** — dependencies between instructions (RAW, WAR, WAW).
3. **Control Hazards** — caused by branches and jumps that alter the instruction flow.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** What is data forwarding in a pipelined processor? Why is it insufficient for the load-use hazard?

**Model Answer:**

**Data forwarding (bypassing)** is a hardware technique that routes the result of an ALU operation from a later pipeline stage (EX/MEM or MEM/WB registers) directly to the ALU input of a dependent instruction in the EX stage, thereby eliminating the need to wait for the result to be written back to the register file.

**Why insufficient for load-use hazard:** In a `LW $t0, 0($s0)` followed immediately by `ADD $t1, $t0, $s1`, the loaded value from `LW` is only available at the **end of the MEM stage**, but the `ADD` instruction needs it at the **start of its EX stage** — which is one cycle earlier. Since forwarding cannot go *backward in time*, a **1-cycle stall (NOP bubble)** must be inserted.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**Explain the 5-stage MIPS pipeline with a neat block diagram. For the following instruction sequence, identify all data dependencies and state whether forwarding can resolve them:**

```
I1: ADD  R1, R2, R3
I2: SUB  R4, R1, R5
I3: AND  R6, R1, R7
I4: OR   R8, R1, R9
I5: LW   R10, 0(R11)
I6: ADD  R12, R10, R13
```

**Model Solution:**

**5-Stage MIPS Pipeline Stages:**
1. **IF (Instruction Fetch):** Fetches instruction from memory using PC.
2. **ID (Instruction Decode & Register Fetch):** Decodes opcode, reads source registers.
3. **EX (Execute):** ALU operation, address calculation, branch resolution.
4. **MEM (Memory Access):** Read/write data memory.
5. **WB (Write Back):** Write result to register file.

**Data Dependency Analysis:**

| Instruction Pair | Dependency Type | Register | Resolvable by Forwarding? |
|---|---|---|---|
| I1 → I2 | RAW (True) | R1 | **Yes** (EX→EX forwarding) |
| I1 → I3 | RAW (True) | R1 | **Yes** (MEM→EX forwarding) |
| I1 → I4 | RAW (True) | R1 | **Yes** (WB→EX forwarding) |
| I5 → I6 | RAW + Load-Use | R10 | **No** — requires **1 stall cycle** |

**Forwarding Path for I1→I2:**
- I1 produces R1 at the end of its **EX stage** (cycle 3).
- I2 needs R1 at the start of its **EX stage** (cycle 3).
- **Bypass from EX/MEM pipeline register of I1 → ALU input of I2** at cycle 3.
- ✅ Zero stalls.

**Load-Use Hazard for I5→I6:**
- I5 produces R10 at the end of its **MEM stage** (cycle 4).
- I6 needs R10 at the start of its **EX stage** (cycle 4).
- ⏳ Data is not yet available → **1-cycle NOP stall required**, then MEM→EX forwarding.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]

**A 6-stage pipelined processor has a clock cycle time of 0.5 ns. If 30% of instructions are branches, each incurring a 2-cycle penalty, compute the throughput, speedup, and efficiency for executing 1000 instructions. Compare with a non-pipelined processor having a CPI of 6.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $k = 6$ stages
- $T_{clk} = 0.5$ ns
- $n = 1000$ instructions
- Branch fraction $f_b = 0.30$
- Branch penalty $P_b = 2$ cycles
- Non-pipelined CPI = 6

**Step 1 — Stall Cycles**
$$
S_{\text{stall}} = n \cdot f_b \cdot P_b = 1000 \times 0.30 \times 2 = 600 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Clock Cycles (Pipelined)**
$$
C_{\text{pipe}} = k + (n-1) + S_{\text{stall}} = 6 + 999 + 600 = 1605 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Pipelined Execution Time**
$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = 1605 \times 0.5 \text{ ns} = 802.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Non-Pipelined Execution Time**
$$
T_{\text{non-pipe}} = n \times CPI \times T_{clk} = 1000 \times 6 \times 0.5 = 3000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput**
$$
TP = \frac{n}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{1000}{802.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.246 \times 10^{9} \text{ instr/sec} \approx 1.246 \text{ GIPS}
$$

**Step 6 — Speedup**
$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{non-pipe}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{3000}{802.5} \approx 3.74
$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency**
$$
\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{3.74}{6} \approx 0.6233 = 62.33\%
$$

**Inference:** The pipeline achieves ~3.74× speedup over the non-pipelined design, but the efficiency drops from a theoretical 100% to 62.33% due to branch penalties. Without stalls, $S = 6$ and $\eta = 100\%$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) [7 Marks]

**With a timing diagram, show how 4 instructions are processed in a 5-stage pipeline. What is the pipeline throughput and speedup over a non-pipelined processor?**

**Model Solution:**

**Timing Diagram (5 stages × 8 cycles):**

| Instruction | CC1 | CC2 | CC3 | CC4 | CC5 | CC6 | CC7 | CC8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I1** | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — | — | — |
| **I2** | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — | — |
| **I3** | — | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — |
| **I4** | — | — | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |

**Observations:**
- Pipeline **fill time** = 5 cycles.
- After fill, **1 instruction completes per cycle**.
- **Total cycles for 4 instructions = 5 + (4-1) = 8 cycles.**

**Non-Pipelined Comparison:**
- Each instruction takes 5 cycles sequentially.
- Total cycles = 4 × 5 = 20 cycles.

**Speedup:**
$$
S = \frac{20}{8} = 2.5
$$

**Throughput:**
$$
TP_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{4 \text{ instr}}{8 \text{ cycles}} = 0.5 \text{ instr/cycle}
$$

$$
TP_{\text{non-pipe}} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.2 \text{ instr/cycle}
$$

**Throughput Improvement:** 2.5×

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common student error is to compute speedup as $\frac{k}{1} = k = 5$ directly, forgetting that the speedup formula $\frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ requires both *n* and *k*. Always use the full formula unless $n \to \infty$ is explicitly stated. Marks lost: 2/7.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]

**Explain Amdahl's Law. A pipelined processor spends 30% of its time on a part that can be sped up by a factor of 10. What is the overall speedup?**

**Model Solution:**

**Amdahl's Law** states that the overall speedup obtained by enhancing a portion of a system is limited by the fraction of execution time that portion represents:

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{k}}
$$

Where:
- $f$ = fraction of execution time affected by the enhancement.
- $k$ = speedup factor of the enhanced portion.

**Given:** $f = 0.30$, $k = 10$

**Substitution:**
$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \frac{0.30}{10}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.03} = \frac{1}{0.73}
$$

$$
S_{\text{overall}} \approx 1.37
$$

**Inference:** Even though the enhanced portion is sped up 10×, the overall speedup is only **1.37×** because the un-enhanced 70% of execution time becomes the dominant bottleneck. This illustrates why diminishing returns are inherent to localized enhancements in pipelined architectures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $(1-f) \cdot k$ or $\frac{1}{1 - f \cdot k}$. The denominator must use the **harmonic form** $(1-f) + f/k$. Marks lost: 1–2 per KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Pipelining increases throughput, not the latency of a single instruction.** Each instruction still takes *k* cycles; instructions just *overlap*.
- ✅ **Ideal speedup = k** (number of stages), achieved only when $n \to \infty$ and there are **zero hazards**.
- ✅ **MIPS 5 stages:** IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB. Memorize the order and the data flow direction.
- ✅ **Three hazards:** Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control — and each has a specific mitigation.
- ✅ **Forwarding (bypassing)** is the most common data hazard fix — but **cannot** resolve a load-use hazard; it requires **1 stall**.
- ✅ **Branch penalty** = number of cycles lost due to wrong path execution; mitigated by **delayed branch, prediction, BTB**.
- ✅ **CPI for ideal pipeline = 1**. Real CPI = $1 + \text{average stall cycles per instruction}$.
- ✅ **Efficiency** drops with more stalls and decreases for shorter instruction streams (fill-up overhead dominates).
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law** is the fundamental bound on pipeline improvement due to non-pipelinable portions.
- ✅ **Pipeline registers** (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB) isolate the stages — these are critical for correct timing.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 ESE, expect at least **one 14-mark full derivation** (speedup/throughput calculation) and **one 3-mark hazard identification** problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branch Prediction Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Branch Prediction Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Branch Prediction** is a speculative execution mechanism in pipelined microprocessors wherein the hardware attempts to *guess* the outcome of a conditional branch instruction *before* the actual condition flags are evaluated by the Execution Unit (EX stage). The objective is to keep the Instruction Fetch Unit (IF) fed with a non-stall stream of instructions, thereby mitigating the performance penalty imposed by **control hazards**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> A *control hazard* arises when the Program Counter (PC) update depends on a runtime condition that has not yet been resolved. Without prediction, the pipeline must either **stall (freeze)** or **flush**, both of which introduce empty pipeline stages (bubbles) and degrade throughput. Branch prediction converts a *mandatory stall* into a *probabilistic speculation* whose cost is paid only on a misprediction.

Mathematically, the prediction problem reduces to a **binary classification task**:

$$
\hat{y} = f(PC,\ \text{history bits}) \in \{T,\ NT\}
$$

where $T$ = Taken, $NT$ = Not-Taken, and the predictor $f(\cdot)$ is a Boolean function of the Branch Instruction Address (BIA) and a small history register.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Railway Signal Predictor**
> Imagine a high-speed train approaching a railway junction. The actual direction of the track (Taken vs Not-Taken) is decided *behind* the train, deep inside a control cabin. The train cannot stop and wait. So the station master at the front of the train (the **Branch Predictor**) examines:
> 1. The train's **route number** (analogous to the **PC / Branch Address**).
> 2. The recent **switching history** of that junction (analogous to the **Branch History Table / PHT**).
> 3. The **weather and time** (analogous to **global correlation**).
>
> Based on these, he flips the track pre-emptively. If he guesses right → the train sails through with zero delay. If he guesses wrong → the train must reverse, re-route, and re-accelerate, paying a heavy **misprediction penalty** (typically 10–20 cycles in modern out-of-order cores).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Branch Prediction Techniques

| Family | Description | Decision Basis | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static** | Decision fixed at compile time or hard-wired | Opcode / branch direction | Lowest |
| **Dynamic** | Decision updated at run-time using history | PC + history bits | Moderate–High |
| **Hybrid** | Combines multiple predictors via a selector meta-predictor | Output of sub-predictors | Highest |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** "**S**tatic is **S**imple, **D**ynamic is **D**ata-driven, **H**ybrid is **H**euristic" — used to recall the three families in two-mark definitions.

---

## 1.4 Why Branch Prediction Is Indispensable in Modern Pipelines

For a processor with:
- Pipeline depth $\;=\; n$ stages
- Conditional branch frequency $\;=\; f_b$
- Misprediction penalty $\;=\; p$ cycles
- Prediction accuracy $\;=\; a$

The effective CPI degradation is non-trivial:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{stall}} = 1 + f_b \cdot (1 - a) \cdot p
$$

For Deep Learning workloads, $f_b \approx 5\text{–}10\%$; for general-purpose code, $f_b$ can exceed **15–20%**. Hence, even a 95% accurate predictor that is *wrong* 5% of the time on a 20-cycle-deep pipeline can cost **1 full IPC** — making prediction accuracy one of the most critical micro-architectural parameters.

---

## 1.5 Types of Branches Encountered

| Branch Type | Static Predictability | Typical Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| **Unconditional jump** | Always taken | No prediction needed — target fetched directly |
| **Conditional branch (forward)** | Mostly not-taken | BTNTN rule |
| **Conditional branch (backward, loop)** | Mostly taken | BTFNT rule |
| **Function call (`CALL`)** | Always taken | Target predicted via **BTB** |
| **Function return (`RET`)** | Matches most-recent `CALL` | Predicted via **Return Address Stack (RAS)** |
| **Indirect jump (`JMP reg`)** | Hardest | Predicted via **BTB + target history** |

> [!WARNING]
> Many students lose marks by claiming *all* conditional branches are predicted. Unconditional jumps, calls, and returns have a **known target** and require only a **Branch Target Buffer (BTB)** lookup — not a true Taken/Not-Taken predictor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Control Hazard in a 5-Stage Pipeline

Consider the classic 5-stage pipeline: **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB**.

A conditional branch `BEQ R1, R2, target` is resolved in the **EX stage** because the comparator requires the values produced by the ALU. By the time the branch is resolved, **2 instructions have already been incorrectly fetched** (one in IF, one in ID). If we naïvely flush these, we lose 2 cycles per branch.

$$
\text{Naïve branch penalty}_{5\text{-stage}} = n_{\text{resolve}} - 1
$$

where $n_{\text{resolve}}$ is the pipeline stage at which the branch outcome is known.

---

## 2.2 Static Branch Prediction Strategies

### 2.2.1 Always Not-Taken (ANT)
- **Mechanism:** Assume the branch falls through.
- **Pros:** Zero hardware; perfect for forward branches (e.g., `if` conditions).
- **Cons:** Catastrophic for loop-back branches — every loop iteration is mispredicted.

### 2.2.2 Always Taken (AT)
- **Mechanism:** Assume the branch is taken.
- **Pros:** Good for loops; targets are pre-fetched.
- **Cons:** Bad for forward `if` statements.

### 2.2.3 Backward Taken, Forward Not-Taken (BTFNT)
- **Mechanism:** Use the branch displacement sign.
  - If displacement $< 0$ (backward, e.g., loop end) → predict **Taken**.
  - If displacement $\geq 0$ (forward, e.g., `if` skip) → predict **Not-Taken**.
- **Pros:** Achieves $\approx$ 65–70% accuracy on typical integer code with essentially no hardware.
- **Cons:** Cannot learn program behaviour; static forever.

### 2.2.4 Profile-Based / Static Heuristic
- Compiler uses profiling runs to attach *hints* (`likely` / `unlikely` in GCC) which the branch opcode encodes. Hardware respects the hint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does BTFNT work so well?**
> Because most backward branches are **loop-back edges** and loops execute their body many times. Forward branches are mostly **error-handling or "else" paths** that are usually not taken.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Branch Prediction Strategies

### 2.3.1 1-Bit Predictor
- One bit of state per branch, indexed by PC.
- Bit = 1 → predict Taken; Bit = 0 → predict Not-Taken.
- **Update rule:** After resolution, set the bit to the actual outcome.
- **Major flaw:** On a loop of $N$ iterations, mispredicts **twice** (once on entry, once on exit) — yields accuracy $1 - 2/N$, which is poor for tight loops.

### 2.3.2 2-Bit Saturating Counter (Bimodal Predictor)
- Each entry is a 2-bit saturating up/down counter, range $\{0,1,2,3\}$.
- States:
  - **Strongly Not-Taken (00)**
  - **Weakly Not-Taken (01)**
  - **Weakly Taken (10)**
  - **Strongly Taken (11)**
- **Update rule:**
  - On Taken outcome → counter $\leftarrow \min(\text{counter} + 1,\ 3)$
  - On Not-Taken outcome → counter $\leftarrow \max(\text{counter} - 1,\ 0)$
- **Decision rule:** Predict Taken if counter $\geq 2$, else Not-Taken.
- **Why "saturating"?** The counter stops incrementing at 3 and stops decrementing at 0 — providing **hysteresis** that makes the predictor immune to a single anomalous outcome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
