> The 2-bit saturating counter is the **workhorse** of the industry — it forms the base of every modern PHT (Pattern History Table). The Intel Pentium (1993) used a 2-bit global predictor; the Pentium Pro (1995) used a 2-bit local predictor with 512 entries.

### 2.3.3 Correlating (Two-Level) Predictors — McFarling (1993)

The key insight: **a branch's behaviour is often correlated with the outcome of *recent* branches**, even unrelated ones in the same program region.

A two-level predictor has two tables:

$$
\text{Level-1: } \text{GHR} = \langle b_{n-1},\ b_{n-2},\ \ldots,\ b_{n-k} \rangle
$$
$$
\text{Level-2: } \text{PHT}[\text{PC}][\text{GHR}] \rightarrow \text{2-bit counter}
$$

- **GHR (Global History Register):** A $k$-bit shift register that records the outcome of the last $k$ branches (1 = T, 0 = NT). Shifted on every branch.
- **GAg (Global history, Global PHT):** Shared PHT indexed by $\text{GHR} \oplus \text{PC}$.
- **GAp (Global history, Per-branch PHTs):** PHTs indexed by $\text{PC}$, selected by $\text{GHR}$.
- **PAp (Per-branch history, Per-branch PHTs):** Each branch has its own BHT of recent outcomes — *local* history.

The McFarling paper (1993) showed a **$GAg$ predictor with a 4-bit GHR and 8K PHT entries** beats a *local* 2-bit predictor of the same size on the SPECint92 benchmarks by $\approx$ **1.5× accuracy** on correlated branches.

### 2.3.4 Tournament Predictor (Alpha 21264 Style)
- Two sub-predictors:
  - **Local predictor:** 2-bit per-branch counter indexed by PC.
  - **Global predictor:** GHR-indexed 2-bit counter.
- A third **chooser** PHT (also 2-bit) selects which sub-predictor's output to trust for each PC.
- Achieves the **highest accuracy** of single-core designs ($\approx 95\text{–}98\%$).

### 2.3.5 Branch Target Buffer (BTB)
- A small, fully-associative cache of $\{\text{PC} \rightarrow \text{Target Address}\}$ entries.
- Looked up in **IF stage** (cycle 1) using PC $\rightarrow$ supplies the predicted PC and the predicted instruction simultaneously.
- BTB does *not* predict Taken/Not-Taken; that is the PHT's job. A branch is in the BTB **iff it is predicted Taken**.
- Typical size: 4K–16K entries, 4-way set-associative.

### 2.3.6 Return Address Stack (RAS)
- For `CALL`/`RET` pairs, the return address is highly predictable (it's the address *after* the call).
- A small LIFO hardware stack (8–32 entries deep) pushed on `CALL`, popped on `RET`.
- Eliminates virtually all mispredictions on `RET` ($\approx$ 99% accuracy).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_b$ | Branch frequency | Branches / Total instructions | 15–25 | % |
| $a$ | Prediction accuracy | Correct predictions / Total predictions | 90–98 | % |
| $p$ | Misprediction penalty | Cycles wasted on a flush | 10–20 | cycles |
| $\text{BTAC}$ | BTB Access cycles | Lookup latency | 1 | cycle |
| $\text{MHR}$ | Misprediction Rate | $\vert 1 - a \vert$ | 2–10 | % |
| $\text{MPKI}$ | Mispredictions Per Kilo-Instructions | $\text{MHR} \times f_b \times 1000$ | 3–15 | — |
| $\text{CPI}_{\text{stall}}$ | Effective CPI with branches | $1 + f_b \cdot (1 - a) \cdot p$ | 1.0–1.5 | cycles/instr |
| $\text{IPC}$ | Instructions per cycle | $1 / \text{CPI}_{\text{eff}}$ | 0.7–4 | instr/cycle |
| $n$ | History length | Bits in GHR | 4–16 | bits |
| $N_{\text{BHT}}$ | PHT entries | $2^{n_{\text{index}}}$ | 4K–64K | entries |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** When asked to compute pipeline throughput, **always** include the branch penalty term. The common omission is forgetting the factor $(1 - a)$, i.e., assuming the predictor is *always* correct.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **General-purpose CPUs** (Intel Core, AMD Zen) | 2-bit tournament predictor + BTB + RAS; ~96% accuracy |
| **Mobile SoCs** (ARM Cortex-A78) | 2-bit correlating predictor; BTB of 8K entries |
| **GPU Shader Cores** (NVIDIA Ampere) | Predominantly static BTFNT; lightweight BTB |
| **Embedded Microcontrollers** (ARM Cortex-M) | Mostly no prediction — branches resolved in single cycle |
| **ML Accelerators** (TPU) | Branch-free dataflow → prediction largely irrelevant |
| **DSPs** (TI C6x) | Static delayed-branch slots filled by compiler |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Branch Penalty in an $n$-Stage Pipeline

**Setup.** Consider an $n$-stage pipeline. The branch instruction is fetched in stage 1 (IF) and resolved in stage $k$, where $k \leq n$. Without prediction, every branch stalls the pipeline for $k - 1$ cycles.

**Step 1 — Define stall cycles per branch:**

$$
S_{\text{stall}} = k - 1
$$

*Explanation:* In the first $k - 1$ cycles after IF, the branch outcome is unknown, so the next $k - 1$ fetched instructions are *speculative* and must be discarded.

**Step 2 — Introduce prediction accuracy:**

With a predictor of accuracy $a$, only a fraction $(1 - a)$ of branches are mispredicted. Each misprediction costs $k - 1$ wasted fetch slots plus 1 cycle to refill the pipeline (total $p = k - 1 + 1 = k$, or model-specifically $p$).

$$
S_{\text{avg}} = (1 - a) \cdot p
$$

*Explanation:* The expected stall is the probability of a misprediction multiplied by the penalty.

**Step 3 — Compute effective CPI:**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + f_b \cdot S_{\text{avg}}
$$

Substituting $\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = 1$ for an ideal pipeline:

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + f_b \cdot (1 - a) \cdot p}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve a representative numerical problem:**

> **Given:** $f_b = 0.20$, $a = 0.90$, $p = 12$ cycles. Find $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}}$ and IPC.

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + (0.20) \cdot (1 - 0.90) \cdot 12
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + 0.20 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 12
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + 0.24 = 1.24
$$

$$
\text{IPC} = \frac{1}{1.24} = 0.806
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to a 99% predictor:**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}}^{99\%} = 1 + 0.20 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 12 = 1 + 0.024 = 1.024
$$

$$
\text{IPC}^{99\%} = \frac{1}{1.024} = 0.977
$$

**Conclusion:** Improving accuracy from 90% to 99% reduces CPI from **1.24 to 1.024** (a 17% throughput gain) — illustrating the *non-linear* value of high accuracy.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Predictor Performance

We assume a SPECint-like workload with $f_b = 0.18$, $p = 15$ cycles, and standard benchmark traces.

| Predictor | $a$ (Accuracy) | $\text{MHR} = (1-a)$ | $\text{MPKI}$ | $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}}$ | Hardware Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Always Not-Taken | 0.40 | 0.60 | 108 | 2.62 | 0 gates |
| Always Taken | 0.60 | 0.40 | 72 | 2.08 | 0 gates |
| BTFNT (static) | 0.70 | 0.30 | 54 | 1.81 | 0 gates |
| 1-bit (1K entries) | 0.80 | 0.20 | 36 | 1.54 | 1 Kbits |
| 2-bit bimodal (1K) | 0.88 | 0.12 | 21.6 | 1.32 | 2 Kbits |
| 2-bit global (4K) | 0.93 | 0.07 | 12.6 | 1.19 | 8 Kbits |
| Local 2-bit (1K) | 0.94 | 0.06 | 10.8 | 1.16 | 10 Kbits |
| GAg (12-bit hist, 4K) | 0.95 | 0.05 | 9.0 | 1.13 | 16 Kbits |
| Tournament (Alpha 21264) | 0.97 | 0.03 | 5.4 | 1.08 | 32 Kbits |

**Mathematical sanity check (Alpha 21264 row):**

$$
\text{CPI} = 1 + 0.18 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 15 = 1 + 0.081 = 1.081
$$

$$
\text{IPC} = 1 / 1.081 = 0.925
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — 2-Bit Saturating Counter State Transitions

**Problem Trace:** A branch is executed 6 times with the following actual outcomes: T, T, T, NT, T, T. Initial state = Weakly Not-Taken (01). For each execution, state the prediction and the new state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter Convention:** $\text{00} = \text{SNT},\ \text{01} = \text{WNT},\ \text{10} = \text{WT},\ \text{11} = \text{ST}$. Decision boundary: predict T if value $\geq 2$, else NT.

| Step | Initial State | Decimal | Prediction | Actual | New State | Decimal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | WNT | 1 | NT | T | WT (incr) | 2 |
| 2 | WT | 2 | T | T | ST (incr) | 3 |
| 3 | ST | 3 | T | T | ST (saturated) | 3 |
| 4 | ST | 3 | T | NT | WT (decr) | 2 |
| 5 | WT | 2 | T | T | ST (incr) | 3 |
| 6 | ST | 3 | T | T | ST (saturated) | 3 |

**Observations:**
- **Mispredictions:** Step 1 (predicted NT, actual T).
- **Anomaly tolerance:** Step 4's NT *decremented* the counter but did not flip the prediction (still WT → predict T). This is the **hysteresis advantage** over a 1-bit predictor.
- A 1-bit predictor would have mispredicted **twice** in this trace (Step 1 and Step 4), while the 2-bit predictor mispredicted **once** — a 50% reduction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Two-Level Correlating Predictor

**Problem Setup:** GHR is 2 bits wide. There are 4 PHT entries, each a 2-bit counter, all initialized to WT (10). The first four branches in the program are at PCs $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with the following pattern of outcomes:
- Branch A → T
- Branch B → T
- Branch C → NT
- Branch D → T

Predict the outcome of branch E at PC = $A$ if the next GHR is `10`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Indexing rule (GAg):** PHT index = $\text{PC}_{n} \oplus \text{GHR}$. The XOR mixes the global history with the local PC to capture cross-branch correlation.

| Branch | PC | GHR before | PHT index | State | Prediction | Actual | New State | GHR after |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 00 | 00 | 00 | 10 (WT) | T | T | 11 (ST) | 01 |
| B | 01 | 01 | 00 | 11 (ST) | T | T | 11 (ST) | 11 |
| C | 10 | 11 | 01 | 10 (WT) | T | NT | 01 (WNT) | 10 |
| D | 11 | 10 | 01 | 01 (WNT) | NT | T | 10 (WT) | 01 |
| E | 00 | 01 | 01 | 10 (WT) | T | ? | ? | ? |

**For branch E (PC = 00, GHR = 01):** PHT index = $00 \oplus 01 = 01$. The state at PHT[01] is currently **WT (10)** → Predict **T**.

**Interpretation:** Branch E's prediction *now* depends not on its own past but on the **recent history of the global flow** — capturing the correlation "if B and C went T and NT, then A is likely T."

---

## 3.5 Long-Form Derivation of Misprediction Penalty Cost

Let us model a $k$-stage pipeline where, on a misprediction, the **front-end must drain** $k$ cycles of wrong-path instructions and **back-end must drain** the in-flight wrong-path uops. Total penalty is:

$$
p = k_{\text{front}} + k_{\text{back}} - \text{overlap}
$$

For an in-order front-end and out-of-order back-end (modern Intel/AMD):

$$
k_{\text{front}} = n \quad \text{and} \quad k_{\text{back}} \approx 4\ \text{to}\ 12\ \text{cycles}
$$

The **branch resolution-to-steering** latency hides part of $k_{\text{back}}$ behind $k_{\text{front}}$, giving an effective penalty of approximately:

$$
\boxed{p_{\text{eff}} \approx 12\ \text{to}\ 20\ \text{cycles}}
$$

This explains why modern CPUs (Zen 4, Lion Cove) spend enormous die area on **speculative execution recovery** (the Reorder Buffer) to *amortise* this penalty.

---

## 3.6 Misprediction Penalty Energy Model

Dynamic energy per misprediction:

$$
E_{\text{miss}} = E_{\text{fetch}} \cdot k + E_{\text{decode}} \cdot k + E_{\text{flush}} + E_{\text{RAS\ restore}}
$$

Total branch-related energy per kilo-instructions:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 1000 \cdot f_b \cdot [ a \cdot E_{\text{correct}} + (1 - a) \cdot E_{\text{miss}} ]
$$

This is why **aggressive prediction** can paradoxically **increase power** if the predictor is wrong often — a key design trade-off in mobile/embedded cores.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Branch Prediction Decision Flow in IF Stage

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **per-cycle decision flow** inside the IF stage of a modern pipelined processor. Every cycle, the PC is used to look up three structures in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cycle Start: PC available] --> B[BTB Lookup using PC]
    A --> C[Branch History Register provides GHR]
    A --> D[RAS Lookup if opcode is RET]
    B --> E{Is PC tagged as branch in BTB?}
    E -- No --> F[Fetch from PC + 4 sequentially]
    E -- Yes --> G[Use BTB target as predicted PC]
    C --> H[Index PHT with PC XOR GHR]
    H --> I{PHT counter greater than or equal 2?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Prediction is TAKEN]
    I -- No --> K[Prediction is NOT TAKEN]
    J --> G
    K --> F
    D --> L{RAS hit?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Override PC with RAS top]
    L -- No --> F
    G --> N[Mux selects predicted next PC]
    F --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Fetch next instruction]
    O --> P[IF Stage Output]
    P --> Q[Later in EX: branch actually resolved]
    Q --> R{Outcome matches prediction?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Continue speculatively]
    R -- No --> T[Flush wrong path, restore RAS, redirect PC]
```

---

## 4.2 State Diagram — 2-Bit Saturating Counter

This is the canonical *Smith's predictor* state machine, introduced by J. E. Smith (1981). Every branch execution causes a transition; the predictor is read at the start of the cycle to decide IF-stage redirect.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SNT
    SNT --> WNT : Actual NT
    WNT --> SNT : Actual NT
    WNT --> WT : Actual T
    WT --> ST : Actual T
    ST --> WT : Actual NT
    WT --> WNT : Actual NT
    ST --> ST : Actual T
    SNT --> WT : Actual T
    note right of SNT : 00 : Predict NT
    note right of WNT : 01 : Predict NT
    note right of WT : 10 : Predict T
    note right of ST : 11 : Predict T
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each node is a counter value. Transitions occur on a *branch resolution* event (the label is the **actual** outcome, not the prediction). Reading a node's value: $\geq 2$ ⇒ predict Taken.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Two-Level Adaptive Branch Predictor (GAg Variant)

This is the McFarling-style architecture used in the **MIPS R10000** and the **Intel Pentium Pro**. It is the conceptual ancestor of every modern predictor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1 [Level 1 History]
        PCin[PC of branch]
        GHR[Global History Register n bits shift]
    end
    subgraph L2 [Level 2 PHT]
        IDX[XOR combiner]
        PHT[Pattern History Table 2 to the n entries x 2 bits]
        SEL[Selector Multiplexer]
    end
    PCin --> IDX
    GHR --> IDX
    IDX --> SEL
    PHT --> SEL
    SEL --> OUT[Final 2 bit saturating counter]
    OUT --> DEC{Predict T or NT}
    PCin --> RES[In EX stage, compute actual outcome]
    RES --> GHR
    RES --> PHT
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Tournament (Meta) Predictor

This is the architecture used in the **Alpha 21264** (2000) — historically the first commercial tournament predictor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PC[Branch PC] --> LP[Local Predictor 2 bit per branch PHT 1K entries]
    PC --> GP[Global Predictor GHR 12 bits plus GAg PHT 4K entries]
    PC --> CH[Chooser PHT 2 bit selector 4K entries]
    LP --> P1[Local prediction]
    GP --> P2[Global prediction]
    P1 --> CH
    P2 --> CH
    CH --> FINAL[Meta prediction chosen by chooser]
    EX[EX stage resolves branch] --> LP
    EX --> GP
    EX --> CH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Chooser update rule (in EX stage):** If local and global agree → no chooser update. If they disagree, increment the chooser toward the *correct* predictor. This lets the hardware learn, per-branch, which sub-predictor is more reliable.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Branch Target Buffer (BTB) Organisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCq[Query PC] --> TAG[Tag compare associative]
    TAG -->|Hit| TGT[Predicted target address]
    TAG -->|Miss| SEQ[Sequential PC plus 4]
    TGT --> REDIR[Redirect fetch to predicted target]
    SEQ --> REDIR
    EX[EX stage actual target] --> UPD[Update BTB on mispredict]
    UPD --> TAG
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE):**
> - Module 1 carries $\approx 20\text{–}25\%$ weightage in the End Semester Exam.
> - Typical 14-mark question on this topic may be split: 7 marks (a) for *description/diagram* + 7 marks (b) for *numerical/trade-off analysis*.
> - Always draw a **labelled block diagram** for full marks in descriptive parts.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define branch prediction. Why is it essential in deeply pipelined processors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark** — *Definition:* Branch prediction is a hardware speculation mechanism that guesses the outcome (Taken / Not-Taken) and target of a conditional branch before it is resolved, so that the Instruction Fetch unit can continue to feed the pipeline without stalling.
- **1 mark** — *Control hazard reduction:* In an $n$-stage pipeline, every conditional branch would otherwise introduce $n-1$ wasted fetch slots. Prediction converts this mandatory stall into a probabilistic speculation whose cost is paid *only* on a misprediction.
- **1 mark** — *Quantitative justification:* With branch frequency $f_b \approx 20\%$ and pipeline depth $n = 20$, the CPI without prediction is $\text{CPI} = 1 + 0.20 \times 19 = 4.8$. A 95%-accurate predictor reduces this to $\text{CPI} = 1 + 0.20 \times 0.05 \times 19 = 1.19$, restoring near-ideal throughput.

---

### Question A2  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between static and dynamic branch prediction with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark** — *Static:* Decision is fixed at *compile time* or hard-wired in hardware; does not depend on runtime history. Examples: (i) Always Not-Taken, (ii) Backward-Taken-Forward-Not-Taken (BTFNT).
- **1 mark** — *Dynamic:* Decision is updated at runtime based on recent outcomes of the branch itself (local) or other recent branches (global). Examples: (i) 2-bit saturating counter, (ii) Correlating (two-level) predictor with GHR + PHT.
- **1 mark** — *Trade-off:* Static predictors are cheap but achieve $\approx 65\text{–}70\%$ accuracy; dynamic predictors achieve $90\text{–}98\%$ accuracy at the cost of a small hardware table (kB range).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Internal Choice rules: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Solutions must include diagrams for full credit.

---

### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a **2-bit saturating counter-based dynamic branch predictor** with a neat state diagram. Discuss why a 2-bit scheme is preferred over a 1-bit scheme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-stage pipelined processor has the following characteristics: conditional branches occur in 22% of instructions, the branch resolution stage is EX (3rd stage), and a 2-bit dynamic predictor is used with 90% accuracy. Calculate the effective CPI. If the predictor accuracy is improved to 96%, what is the percentage improvement in throughput (IPC)? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) — 2-Bit Saturating Counter (7 marks):**

**1 mark — Definition:**
A 2-bit saturating counter (Smith, 1981) is a dynamic branch predictor that stores a 2-bit unsigned counter in a Per-branch History Table (PHT) indexed by the branch PC. The counter value determines the prediction; the actual outcome *saturatingly* updates it.

**2 marks — Four States:**
The counter can take values $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ representing:
- **00 — Strongly Not-Taken (SNT):** predict NT, very confident.
- **01 — Weakly Not-Taken (WNT):** predict NT, mildly confident.
- **10 — Weakly Taken (WT):** predict T, mildly confident.
- **11 — Strongly Taken (ST):** predict T, very confident.

**Decision rule (½ mark):** Predict Taken if counter $\geq 2$, else Not-Taken.

**2 marks — State Diagram (textual description; the Mermaid state diagram in §4.2 is acceptable in the exam sheet):**
- On a Taken actual outcome: counter $\leftarrow \min(\text{ctr}+1,\ 3)$.
- On a Not-Taken actual outcome: counter $\leftarrow \max(\text{ctr}-1,\ 0)$.
- The "saturating" behaviour ensures the counter does not wrap around 0 or 3.

**1½ marks — Why 2-bit Beats 1-bit:**
- A 1-bit predictor inverts its prediction on every misprediction. In a tight loop of $N$ iterations, it mispredicts **twice** (once on entry, once on exit) → accuracy $\approx 1 - 2/N$.
- A 2-bit predictor must observe **two consecutive opposite outcomes** before flipping its prediction, providing **hysteresis**. In the same loop, it mispredicts only **once** (on entry) → accuracy $\approx 1 - 1/N$.
- Example: a 5-iteration loop → 1-bit accuracy = 60%, 2-bit accuracy = 80%.

---

**(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**1 mark — Identify parameters:**
$$
f_b = 0.22,\quad k = 3\ (\text{EX stage}),\quad p = k - 1 = 2\ \text{cycles},\quad a_1 = 0.90
$$

**3 marks — Compute effective CPI at 90% accuracy:**
$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + f_b \cdot (1 - a_1) \cdot p
$$
$$
\text{CPI}_1 = 1 + 0.22 \cdot (1 - 0.90) \cdot 2 = 1 + 0.22 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 2 = 1 + 0.044 = 1.044
$$

**1 mark — Compute CPI at 96% accuracy:**
$$
\text{CPI}_2 = 1 + 0.22 \cdot (1 - 0.96) \cdot 2 = 1 + 0.22 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 2 = 1 + 0.0176 = 1.0176
$$

**2 marks — Compute IPCs and percentage improvement:**
$$
\text{IPC}_1 = \frac{1}{1.044} = 0.9579
$$
$$
\text{IPC}_2 = \frac{1}{1.0176} = 0.9827
$$
$$
\Delta\text{IPC}\ \% = \frac{0.9827 - 0.9579}{0.9579} \times 100\% \approx 2.59\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Effective CPI = **1.044 cycles/instruction**; percentage IPC improvement from 90% → 96% accuracy = **2.59%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.A-b):** Students often compute CPI directly as $1 + f_b \cdot p$ *without* the $(1 - a)$ factor, i.e., they assume the predictor is *always* wrong on the first try. **This loses 2 full marks.** The factor $(1 - a)$ is the *probability* of a misprediction, and it MUST multiply the penalty.

---

### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **two-level adaptive branch predictor** (McFarling) with a neat block diagram. Explain the roles of the GHR and the PHT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor uses a 1-bit dynamic branch predictor with 1024 PHT entries (indexed by PC). For a benchmark, the branch at PC `0x4000` executes in the sequence T, T, T, NT, NT, T, T. Initial state of the entry = NT (0). For each execution, list the *prediction*, the *actual outcome*, and the *new state*. Count the total mispredictions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) — Two-Level Adaptive Predictor (7 marks):**

**½ mark — Origin & Motivation:**
The two-level adaptive predictor was proposed by McFarling (1993). The key observation is that *a branch's behaviour is often correlated with the outcomes of recent, sometimes unrelated, branches* — pure per-branch local history is insufficient.

**2 marks — Level 1 (GHR — Global History Register):**
- A shift register of $k$ bits, often $k = 8$ to $16$.
- Stores the outcomes of the last $k$ conditional branches in program order: shift in `1` for Taken, `0` for Not-Taken.
- After every branch resolution, GHR is shifted left by 1 and the new outcome is shifted in.
- The GHR provides a *fingerprint* of the recent control flow that can disambiguate context-dependent branches.

**2 marks — Level 2 (PHT — Pattern History Table):**
- A table of 2-bit saturating counters indexed by a function of $(\text{PC}, \text{GHR})$.
- The most common indexing scheme: $\text{index} = \text{PC}_{[\text{low bits}]} \oplus \text{GHR}_{[\text{low bits}]}$ (XOR-folding) for $GAg$ variants.
- For each $(\text{PC}, \text{GHR})$ pair, the PHT stores a counter that records whether the *last few times* the global flow was in this state, this branch was Taken or Not-Taken.
- $GAg$: one PHT shared by all PCs.
- $GAp$: PHT-per-PC, GHR selects which entry.
- $PAp$: per-PC local history register + per-PC PHT.

**2 marks — Block Diagram (described; Mermaid §4.3 acceptable):**
- IF stage: GHR is shifted, PC indexes PHT, PHT outputs counter → compare against 2 to predict.
- EX stage: actual outcome known → update the GHR and the PHT entry for the current $(\text{PC}, \text{GHR})$ pair.
- On misprediction: pipeline flush + redirect fetch.

**½ mark — Why "Adaptive":**
The predictor's tables are *learned* during execution; no compiler hints or profile runs are required. On phase changes, the predictor relearns within $\approx 2k$ branches.

---

**(b) — 1-Bit Predictor Trace (7 marks):**

**1 mark — Setup:**
- 1-bit PHT entry for PC `0x4000`, initial state = **0 (NT)**.
- Trace: T, T, T, NT, NT, T, T.

**5 marks — Step-by-Step Table:**

| Step | State Before | Prediction | Actual | Mispredict? | State After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 (NT) | NT | T | YES (✗) | 1 (T) |
| 2 | 1 (T) | T | T | No (✓) | 1 (T) |
| 3 | 1 (T) | T | T | No (✓) | 1 (T) |
| 4 | 1 (T) | T | NT | YES (✗) | 0 (NT) |
| 5 | 0 (NT) | NT | NT | No (✓) | 0 (NT) |
| 6 | 0 (NT) | NT | T | YES (✗) | 1 (T) |
| 7 | 1 (T) | T | T | No (✓) | 1 (T) |

**1 mark — Misprediction count:** **3 mispredictions** out of 7 executions → accuracy = $\frac{4}{7} \approx 57.14\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q.B-b):** A common error is to write the *prediction* column equal to the *actual outcome* of the *previous* step. **No.** The prediction is decided by the *current* state of the bit *before* it is updated. For example, at Step 2, the state entering the step is `1` (set by Step 1) — the prediction is T, and since actual is T, the state stays at `1`. Mark deduction: 1 mark per such error, up to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Branch Prediction Questions**
> 1. **Confusing BTB with PHT.** BTB predicts the *target address*; PHT predicts *Taken/Not-Taken*. They are *separate* structures, often co-existing in the IF stage.
> 2. **Omitting the $(1 - a)$ factor in CPI calculations.** A predictor with 100% accuracy adds *zero* branch penalty — this must be reflected in the formula.
> 3. **Drawing a single-line block diagram** instead of showing the **GHR → PHT → MUX → PC** pipeline. Partial diagrams lose up to 2 marks in 7-mark descriptive questions.
> 4. **Claiming 1-bit and 2-bit predictors have the same accuracy.** The 2-bit's hysteresis is a *key* concept worth 1½–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to update the GHR** in two-level predictor traces. The GHR must be shifted **before** the PHT is read on the next branch (or after, depending on the indexing convention — be consistent).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute glance before exam):**
>
> - [x] **Branch prediction = IF-stage speculation** on outcome and target of a conditional branch.
> - [x] **Static predictors:** ANT, AT, BTFNT, profile-hints — all 0 hardware, ≤ 70% accuracy.
> - [x] **1-bit predictor:** 1 BHT per branch; mispredicts **twice** per loop.
> - [x] **2-bit saturating counter (Smith 1981):** states 00, 01, 10, 11; predict T if $\geq 2$; **hysteresis** is the key benefit.
> - [x] **Two-level predictor (McFarling 1993):** GHR (k bits) + PHT (2-bit counters); GAg / GAp / PAp variants.
> - [x] **Tournament predictor:** local + global + chooser; used in Alpha 21264, $\approx$ 97% accurate.
> - [x] **BTB:** $\{\text{PC} \rightarrow \text{target}\}$ cache, looked up every cycle; supplies redirect target.
> - [x] **RAS:** LIFO stack for `CALL`/`RET` matching; $\geq$ 99% accuracy on returns.
> - [x] **Master CPI formula:** $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + f_b \cdot (1 - a) \cdot p$.
> - [x] **MPKI metric:** $\text{MPKI} = (1 - a) \cdot f_b \cdot 1000$.
> - [x] **Misprediction penalty** in modern CPUs: $\approx 12\text{–}20$ cycles.
> - [x] **Hysteresis** of 2-bit counter = the "saturating" behaviour at boundaries 0 and 3.
> - [x] **Always mention hysteresis, history length, and indexing scheme** in any descriptive answer.
> - [x] **Numerical answers must show the $(1-a)$ factor** explicitly.
> - [x] **Diagrams:** always labelled with arrows showing data flow direction (PC in, target out, GHR shift-in, PHT update).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparative Study of Features of Pentium-II, Pentium-III, and Pentium-IV Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Comparative Study of Features of Pentium-II, Pentium-III, and Pentium-IV Processors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Intel Pentium family** refers to a lineage of superscalar, IA-32 (Intel Architecture 32-bit) compatible microprocessors that evolved through the late 1990s and early 2000s, each generation introducing architectural refinements such as deeper pipelines, wider SIMD instruction sets, larger on-die caches, and improved front-side bus (FSB) bandwidth. - **Pentium-II (P6 Microarchitecture variant, code-named Klamath/Deschutes):** The successor to the Pentium Pro, packaged in a Single Edge Contact Cartridge (SECC) using Slot-1. Introduced **MMX technology** at consumer level and used a **dual-independent-bus (DIB)** architecture to decouple the L2 cache bus from the system memory bus. - **Pentium-III (P6 microarchitecture derivative, code-named Katmai/Coppermine/Tualatin):** Added the **SSE (Streaming SIMD Extensions)** instruction set with **70 new instructions** and integrated the L2 cache onto the processor die, drastically reducing memory access latency. - **Pentium-4 (NetBurst microarchitecture, code-named Willamette/Northwood/Prescott):** Marked a radical architectural shift using a **hyper-pipelined 20-stage pipeline** (later extended to 31 stages in Prescott), achieving very high clock frequencies through deep pipelining and rapid-execution ALU units. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under **Module 1 – Evolution of Advanced Microprocessors**, with Course Outcomes mapping typically to **CO1: Understand the architecture and features of advanced microprocessors (Bloom Level: Remember/Understand).** Expect short notes, comparison tables, and architectural block diagram questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Car Engine Evolution" Imagine the Pentium family as three generations of high-performance car engines designed for the same fuel (x86 instructions): - **Pentium-II** is the **classic V6 engine with turbocharger mounted externally** (L2 cache off-die in a cartridge): great power, but the turbo suffers from a long air path, causing *latency*. - **Pentium-III** is the **same V6 engine but with the turbocharger integrated directly onto the engine block** (on-die L2 cache): same combustion cycle, but response is dramatically snappier because the air travels a shorter path. - **Pentium-4** is a **completely new inline-4 engine designed purely for high RPM** (NetBurst microarchitecture with a 20-stage pipeline): each piston stroke is split into many tiny micro-movements, so although the engine revs to 8000 RPM (3.8 GHz), individual "pushes" per cycle are weaker — but it processes *many more instructions per second overall* through sheer throughput. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** A deeper pipeline (Pentium-4) does **not** mean a faster instruction — it means **more instructions in flight at once**, similar to having more checkout counters at a supermarket even if each counter is not the fastest. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Transistor Count:** Pentium-II ≈ **7.5 Million**, Pentium-III ≈ **9.5 Million**, Pentium-4 (Willamette) ≈ **42 Million**. - **Process Technology (Lithography):** 0.35 µm (Klamath) → 0.25 µm (Deschutes/Katmai) → 0.18 µm (Coppermine) → 0.18 µm (Willamette) → 0.13 µm (Northwood). - **Clock Frequency Range:** 233 MHz – 450 MHz (P-II) → 450 MHz – 1.4 GHz (P-III) → 1.3 GHz – 3.8 GHz (P-4). - **Front Side Bus (FSB):** 66/100 MHz (P-II) → 100/133 MHz (P-III) → 400/533/800 MHz (P-4). - **Standard L2 Cache:** 256 KB – 512 KB external (P-II) → 128 KB – 256 KB on-die (P-III) → 256 KB – 1 MB on-die (P-4). - **Supply Voltage:** 2.8 V (P-II) → 2.0 V/1.65 V/1.5 V (P-III) → 1.5 V / 1.3 V (P-4). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architectural Evolution – The "Why" and "How" The progression of the Pentium family was driven by three primary engineering objectives: 1. **Increase Instructions Per Cycle (IPC):** Achieved via wider SIMD units and additional execution units. 2. **Increase Operating Frequency:** Achieved by shortening the critical path (deeper pipelines, faster transistors). 3. **Reduce Memory Bottleneck:** Achieved by integrating caches, increasing cache associativity, and widening the FSB. #### 2.1.1 Pentium-II – The Cartridge Era - **How it evolved from Pentium Pro:** Retained the P6 core's out-of-order execution engine (12-stage pipeline) but added **MMX** instructions and switched to a **Single Edge Connector (SEC) cartridge** so the L2 cache could sit on a high-speed dedicated bus. - **Why dual-independent bus:** The L2 cache (operating at half CPU clock) was decoupled from the memory bus, allowing **simultaneous** L2 and memory transactions. - **Limitation:** L2 cache was *off-die*, sitting on a substrate beside the CPU die inside the cartridge. This imposed latency penalties despite the DIB design. #### 2.1.2 Pentium-III – The On-Die Cache Revolution - **How it improved over P-II:** Integrated the L2 cache directly onto the processor die, allowing the cache to run at *full CPU clock speed* and eliminating the SEC cartridge in later versions (Socket-370 FC-PGA). - **Why SSE matters:** Added 70 new SIMD instructions for floating-point-intensive workloads (3D graphics, multimedia, scientific computation). Each instruction could operate on **4 single-precision floats** packed into a 128-bit XMM register. - **Architectural details:** Continued with the 10-stage P6 pipeline (P-III Coppermine), preserving strong IPC while boosting clock speed through lithography shrinks (0.18 µm). #### 2.1.3 Pentium-4 – The NetBurst Paradigm Shift - **How it departed from P6:** Abandoned the P6 microarchitecture entirely. Introduced **NetBurst**, featuring: - A **hyper-pipelined 20-stage pipeline** (Willamette), extended to **31 stages** in Prescott. - A **Rapid Execution Engine (REE)** where the ALU operates at 2× the core clock. - **400/533/800 MHz Quad-Pumped FSB** delivering up to **6.4 GB/s** of bandwidth. - **SSE2** (144 new instructions) and later **SSE3** (13 new instructions). - **Why deeper pipelining?** Each pipeline stage performs less work, allowing the clock period to be shortened. This trades *single-thread latency* for *clock frequency scalability*. - **Why it underperformed per-cycle:** Deep pipelines incur huge branch misprediction penalties (15+ cycles wasted), so the high MHz did not linearly translate to proportional performance gains. This is the famous *"megahertz myth"* Intel later publicly dismantled. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metrics Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are not CPU-physics equations but **standard performance metrics** examiners expect students to compute or explain. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CPU Time} &= \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}} \\ &= \frac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clock}}} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $IC$ = Instruction Count (workload dependent). - $CPI$ = Cycles Per Instruction (architecture dependent). - $T_{\text{clock}}$ = Clock period, $T_{\text{clock}} = 1 / f_{\text{clock}}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MIPS} &= \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FSB Bandwidth} &= \text{FSB Width} \times \text{FSB Frequency} \times \frac{\text{Transfers}}{\text{cycle}} \end{aligned} $$ #### Tabular Cheat-Sheet (Comparison Anchors) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Markdown Rule Enforced:** No vertical pipes inside cells. All delimiters replaced with `\vert` for math symbols, standard `\|` for table structure. | Feature | Pentium-II | Pentium-III | Pentium-4 | |---|---|---|---| | Microarchitecture | P6 (DIB) | P6 (Tualatin optimized) | NetBurst | | Pipeline Stages | 12 | 10 – 12 | 20 (Willamette) $\vert$ 31 (Prescott) | | Process (µm) | 0.35 / 0.25 | 0.25 / 0.18 / 0.13 | 0.18 / 0.13 / 0.09 | | Transistors | 7.5 M | 9.5 M – 28 M | 42 M – 178 M | | Clock Range | 233 – 450 MHz | 450 MHz – 1.4 GHz | 1.3 – 3.8 GHz | | FSB (MHz) | 66 / 100 | 100 / 133 | 400 / 533 / 800 (quad-pumped) | | L1 Cache | 16 KB I + 16 KB D | 16 KB I + 16 KB D | 8 KB I + 8 KB D (Trace Cache 12k µops) | | L2 Cache | 256 – 512 KB (off-die) | 128 – 256 KB (on-die) | 256 KB – 2 MB (on-die) | | SIMD Extensions | MMX | MMX + SSE (70) | MMX + SSE + SSE2 + SSE3 | | Package | Slot-1 SECC / SEPP | Slot-1 / Socket-370 | Socket-423 / 478 / LGA-775 | | Voltage (V) | 2.8 / 2.0 | 2.0 / 1.65 / 1.5 | 1.5 / 1.3 / 1.2 | | Year Released | 1997 | 1999 | 2000 | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pentium-II** dominated **entry-level servers and consumer desktops** of the late 1990s. The Slot-1 cartridge, while bulky, allowed easy upgrade of L2 cache modules. - **Pentium-III** became the **workhorse for early internet, e-commerce, and engineering workstations** running CAD/CAE. Its strong IPC made it excellent for branch-heavy integer code. - **Pentium-4** powered the **early 2000s multimedia and gaming boom**, with high FSB bandwidth enabling faster streaming, video encoding, and the first era of broadband internet. It also formed the basis of the **first hyper-threaded CPUs (HT Technology, 2002)** and embedded the **x86-64 (EM64T) extensions** in Prescott revisions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How Pipeline Depth Affects Clock Frequency > [!NOTE] > This derivation shows **why** Intel made the architectural decision to deepen the pipeline from P6 (Pentium-III) to NetBurst (Pentium-4), justifying the underlying engineering intuition. #### Assumptions - Each pipeline stage performs a small logic block whose propagation delay is roughly equal across stages. - Let $T_{\text{stage}}$ be the propagation delay of one pipeline stage. - Total combinational logic delay of the *original* (P6) datapath is $T_{\text{P6}}$. - Pipeline depth in Pentium-3 is $D_{\text{P3}} = 10$ stages. - Pipeline depth in Pentium-4 (Willamette) is $D_{\text{P4}} = 20$ stages. #### Step 1: Express stage delay The clock period must be at least the slowest stage delay. Assuming balanced stages: $$ T_{\text{stage}} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{D} $$ #### Step 2: Apply to Pentium-III (P6, 10-stage) $$ T_{\text{clock, P3}} = T_{\text{stage, P3}} = \frac{T_{\text{P6}}}{10} $$ $$ f_{\text{clock, P3}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{clock, P3}}} = \frac{10}{T_{\text{P6}}} $$ #### Step 3: Apply to Pentium-4 (NetBurst, 20-stage) Assuming the same total logic $T_{\text{P6}}$ is partitioned into 20 stages (a simplification, since real logic was rewritten): $$ T_{\text{clock, P4}} = \frac{T_{\text{P6}}}{20} $$ $$ f_{\text{clock, P4}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{clock, P4}}} = \frac{20}{T_{\text{P6}}} $$ #### Step 4: Compute the ratio of achievable clock frequencies $$ \frac{f_{\text{clock, P4}}}{f_{\text{clock, P3}}} = \frac{20 / T_{\text{P6}}}{10 / T_{\text{P6}}} = 2 $$ #### Step 5: Interpretation In theory, **doubling the pipeline depth allows the clock frequency to double** (assuming perfect logic partitioning and no overhead stages). This is the fundamental motivation behind NetBurst's hyper-pipelined design. #### Step 6: Account for the CPI penalty (the catch) With a deeper pipeline, a **branch misprediction** flushes more in-flight instructions, increasing the *effective* CPI: $$ \text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + P_{\text{branch}} \times P_{\text{mispredict}} \times D_{\text{flush}} $$ Where: - $P_{\text{branch}}$ = probability that an instruction is a branch. - $P_{\text{mispredict}}$ = probability of misprediction. - $D_{\text{flush}}$ = pipeline depth flushed (≈ full pipeline). For Pentium-4, $D_{\text{flush}} \approx 20$, so each misprediction wastes ~20 cycles, whereas Pentium-III wastes only ~12. #### Step 7: Final performance comparison Plugging both into the CPU Time equation: $$ \text{CPU Time}_{\text{P4}} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \left(\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + P_{\text{branch}} \times P_{\text{mispredict}} \times 20\right)}{2 \times f_{\text{clock, P3}}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Pentium-4's 2× clock advantage is **eroded** by the 1.67× deeper misprediction penalty and the smaller L1 cache (8 KB each in Prescott vs. 16 KB each in P-III). This is the engineering reality behind the *"NetBurst failure"* that eventually led Intel to abandon the architecture in 2006 in favor of the Core microarchitecture (which returned to short, wide P6-style pipelines). ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Metric | Pentium-II | Pentium-III | Pentium-4 (NetBurst) | Verdict | |---|---|---|---|---| | Peak Clock (Shipping) | 450 MHz | 1.4 GHz | 3.8 GHz | P-4 wins on raw MHz. | | IPC (integer, branch-heavy) | ~1.0 | ~1.1 | ~0.7 – 0.9 | P-III wins per cycle. | | Single-Thread Throughput | Moderate | High | Moderate (clock dependent) | P-III is generally more efficient. | | FSB Bandwidth | 800 MB/s | 1.06 GB/s | 6.4 GB/s | P-4 wins on memory throughput. | | Multimedia SIMD Width | 64-bit MMX | 128-bit SSE | 128-bit SSE2/SSE3 | P-4 wins on SIMD throughput. | | Branch Misprediction Penalty | ~12 cycles | ~12 cycles | ~20 – 31 cycles | P-4 pays heaviest penalty. | | Cache Coherence Latency | High (off-die) | Low (on-die) | Lowest (on-die + larger) | P-4 wins, P-III strong. | | Power Efficiency (perf/W) | Low | Better | Worst | P-III is the sweet spot. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This matrix is a high-yield revision tool. KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask *"Compare Pentium III and Pentium 4 architectures"* (commonly for 7-10 marks). Memorizing the **bolded** points above guarantees complete coverage. ## 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Evolution of the Pentium Family > [!NOTE] > The diagram below renders a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing the major building blocks and how they evolved from Pentium-II to Pentium-4. Mermaid syntax rules strictly applied: alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords as standalone IDs. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef common fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef p2 fill:#059669,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef p3 fill:#d97706,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef p4 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef ext fill:#6b7280,stroke:#111827,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff INSTR[Instruction Fetch Unit]:::common DECODE[Out of Order Decoder]:::common RENAME[Register Renaming / ROB]:::common EXEC[Reservation Station / Dispatch]:::common ALU[ALU Integer Execution]:::common FPU[FPU / SIMD Engine]:::common RETIRE[Retire / Commit Unit]:::common L1D[L1 Data Cache 16 KB]:::common P2BUS[Dual Independent Bus 100 MHz]:::p2 P2L2[L2 Cache 256 to 512 KB OFF DIE on substrate]:::p2 P2MMX[MMX 64 bit SIMD]:::p2 P2SLOT[Slot 1 SECC Cartridge]:::p2 P3L2[L2 Cache 128 to 256 KB ON DIE full speed]:::p3 P3SSE[SSE 70 new instructions 128 bit XMM]:::p3 P3SOC[Socket 370 FC PGA]:::p3 P4PIPE[Hyper Pipelined 20 to 31 stage pipeline]:::p4 P4REE[Rapid Execution Engine ALU at 2x core clock]:::p4 P4TC[Trace Cache 12k uops replacing L1 I cache]:::p4 P4FSB[Quad Pumped FSB 400 to 800 MHz delivering 6.4 GB per s]:::p4 P4SSE2[SSE2 SSE3 144 plus 13 new instructions]:::p4 P4LGA[LGA 775 Socket]:::p4 INSTR --> DECODE --> RENAME --> EXEC EXEC --> ALU EXEC --> FPU ALU --> L1D FPU --> L1D L1D --> RETIRE %% Pentium-II path L1D -.-> P2BUS P2BUS -.-> P2L2 FPU -.-> P2MMX P2L2 -.-> P2SLOT %% Pentium-III path L1D -.-> P3L2 FPU -.-> P3SSE P3L2 -.-> P3SOC %% Pentium-4 path EXEC -.-> P4PIPE ALU -.-> P4REE INSTR -.-> P4TC P4TC -.-> P4FSB FPU -.-> P4SSE2 P4FSB -.-> P4LGA ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: NetBurst Microarchitecture (Pentium-4) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef stage fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff classDef unit fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,color:#ffffff classDef cache fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff classDef io fill:#059669,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff TC[Trace Cache
12k Micro Ops]:::cache MC[Mop Queue and Branch Predictor]:::unit RENAME[Register Alias Table and Allocator]:::unit SCHED[Unified Scheduler]:::unit ALU1[Fast ALU x2 at Double Clock]:::unit ALU2[Slow Integer ALU]:::unit FP[FP Move and SSE2 Engine]:::unit LDSTORE[Load and Store Unit]:::unit L1D[L1 D Cache 16 KB]:::cache L2[L2 Unified Cache 256 KB to 1 MB]:::cache FSB[System Bus 64 bit 800 MHz Quad Pumped]:::io TC --> MC --> RENAME --> SCHED SCHED --> ALU1 SCHED --> ALU2 SCHED --> FP SCHED --> LDSTORE LDSTORE --> L1D --> L2 --> FSB ALU1 --> L1D ALU2 --> L1D FP --> L1D ``` ### 4.3 Description of Key Architectural Blocks - **Trace Cache (Pentium-4 unique):** Replaces the traditional L1 instruction cache. It stores *decoded* micro-operations (µops) in the order they were executed (trace), avoiding repeated decoding of loop bodies. This is critical because the 20-stage pipeline would otherwise spend many cycles decoding. - **Rapid Execution Engine (REE):** The two simple ALUs are clocked at **2× the core frequency**, so arithmetic/logic operations complete in *half a core clock cycle*. This is why Pentium-4 can issue two integer instructions per cycle effectively. - **Quad-Pumped FSB:** The FSB transfers data **4 times per clock cycle** on both the rising and falling edges of two phases, effectively quadrupling the peak transfer rate. For an 800 MHz quad-pumped bus with a 64-bit (8-byte) width: $$ \text{Bandwidth} = 800 \times 10^{6} \times 4 \times 8 = 25.6 \text{ Gbps} \approx 3.2 \text{ GB/s} $$ (For the 64-bit *double-clocked* 400/533/800 variants, Intel specifies 3.2 / 4.2 / 6.4 GB/s respectively, achieved by wider internal paths and full-clock double-pumping.) ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Modeled after KTU 2024 ESE/ISE Part A. Each question requires a crisp 80–120 word answer with bullet-style structure. #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any three major architectural features that distinguish the Intel Pentium-4 (NetBurst) processor from the Pentium-III.** **Model Answer:** - **Hyper-Pipelined Technology:** Pentium-4 uses a 20-stage (later 31-stage in Prescott) pipeline, much deeper than the P6-derived 10-12 stages of Pentium-III. This enables much higher clock frequencies at the cost of higher branch misprediction penalty. - **Rapid Execution Engine (REE):** The two simple integer ALUs operate at **twice the core clock frequency**, completing simple integer ops in half a clock cycle. - **Trace Cache:** Replaces the L1 instruction cache. Stores pre-decoded micro-operations (µops) in execution order, removing the need for repeated decoding. - **Quad-Pumped FSB:** System bus transfers 4× per cycle, providing 400/533/800 MHz effective rates (3.2–6.4 GB/s bandwidth). *(3 marks: 1 mark per correct point with one-line justification.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Explain the concept of Dual Independent Bus (DIB) architecture used in Pentium-II processors.** **Model Answer:** - The Dual Independent Bus (DIB) architecture allows the **L2 cache** to communicate with the CPU over a **dedicated high-speed bus**, *separate* from the system memory bus. - This means **simultaneous** transactions are possible: the CPU can read L2 cache *and* system memory in the same cycle, eliminating the contention of a single shared bus. - In Pentium-II, the L2 cache runs at **half the core clock speed** and resides on a substrate inside the SECC cartridge (off-die). - DIB effectively **doubles** the bandwidth available to the CPU and was a key reason Pentium-II outperformed Pentium Pro in memory-intensive workloads. - *(3 marks: 1 mark per major point.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE Part B follows a strict **Module Internal Choice** pattern: students are offered two full 14-mark alternatives per module. Below, both choices are fully solved. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **"Compare the architectural features of Pentium-III and Pentium-IV processors. Discuss how the deeper pipeline of Pentium-4 affects its performance and power characteristics."** ##### **Part (a) – Architectural Comparison (7 Marks)** | Feature | Pentium-III | Pentium-4 (NetBurst) | |---|---|---| | Microarchitecture | Enhanced P6 (Katmai/Coppermine/Tualatin) | NetBurst (Willamette/Northwood/Prescott) | | Pipeline Depth | 10 – 12 stages | 20 stages (Willamette), 31 stages (Prescott) | | L1 Cache | 16 KB I + 16 KB D (Harvard) | 12k µop Trace Cache + 8/16 KB D-cache | | L2 Cache | 128 – 256 KB on-die, full clock | 256 KB – 2 MB on-die | | FSB | 100 / 133 MHz | 400 / 533 / 800 MHz (quad-pumped) | | SIMD Support | MMX + SSE (70 instructions) | MMX + SSE + SSE2 + SSE3 | | Socket | Slot-1 / Socket-370 | Socket-423 / 478 / LGA-775 | | Process | 0.25 → 0.13 µm | 0.18 → 0.09 µm | | Clock Range | 450 MHz – 1.4 GHz | 1.3 – 3.8 GHz | | Transistors | 9.5 M – 28 M | 42 M – 178 M | **Step-by-step model solution:** 1. **Pipeline & Clock:** The Pentium-3 retains the proven P6 10-12 stage pipeline, providing a strong IPC of ~1.1 integer instructions per cycle. The Pentium-4's 20-stage (or 31-stage) pipeline divides the logic into smaller pieces, enabling a much shorter clock period and clock rates up to 3.8 GHz. 2. **Cache Hierarchy:** Both have on-die L2 cache, but the P-4 introduces the **Trace Cache** (a µop-level L1 I-cache) which stores pre-decoded instructions, avoiding repeated decoding in the long pipeline. 3. **Bus & Bandwidth:** The P-4's quad-pumped FSB provides up to **6.4 GB/s** versus P-3's ~1 GB/s, crucial for high-bandwidth workloads like video encoding. 4. **SIMD Evolution:** P-3 added SSE (128-bit XMM); P-4 added SSE2 (integer SIMD on 128 bits) and SSE3 (13 new instructions for complex arithmetic, video). 5. **Packaging:** P-3 migrated from Slot-1 to Socket-370; P-4 evolved from Socket-478 to **LGA-775** (Land Grid Array), enabling higher pin counts and better signaling. 6. **Power and Die Size:** P-4 (especially Prescott, 125 W TDP) had significantly higher power draw, while P-3 typically operated at 30-60 W. *(7 marks: 1 mark per significant feature pair explained clearly.)* ##### **Part (b) – Impact of Deeper Pipeline on Performance & Power (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Higher Clock Frequency — The "How":** Deeper pipelines reduce stage delay $T_{\text{stage}} = T_{\text{total}} / D$, allowing shorter clock periods. For P-4 vs P-3: $$ \frac{f_{\text{P4}}}{f_{\text{P3}}} \approx \frac{20}{12} \approx 1.67 \times $$ This theoretical factor explains how P-4 reached 3.8 GHz vs P-3's 1.4 GHz maximum. 2. **Branch Misprediction Penalty:** Each misprediction flushes the pipeline. The penalty grows with depth $D$: $$ \text{Penalty} \propto D \times t_{\text{clock}} $$ So P-4 loses 20+ cycles per misprediction vs P-3's 12 — a significant performance drain in branch-heavy code. 3. **Effective CPI Degradation:** The effective CPI rises due to mispredictions. For a workload with 20% branches and 50% misprediction rate: $$ \Delta \text{CPI}_{\text{P4}} = 0.20 \times 0.50 \times 20 = 2.0 $$ vs P-3: $\Delta \text{CPI}_{\text{P3}} = 0.20 \times 0.50 \times 12 = 1.2$ 4. **Power Characteristics:** Dynamic power depends on switching capacitance $C$ and clock frequency $f$: $$ P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V^{2} f $$ Higher $f$ and longer pipelines (more registers, latches) raise $C$ and switching activity $\alpha$, leading to the P-4's notorious 100+ W TDP. This forced Intel to scale back the NetBurst roadmap. 5. **Net Performance Verdict:** The P-4 achieved higher peak throughput in streaming/multimedia code (where SIMD and bandwidth matter) but lost in branch-heavy integer code. This is the "NetBurst trade-off." 6. **Engineering Lesson:** The deeper-pipeline design ultimately failed because the **memory wall** and **branch misprediction penalty** outweighed frequency gains. Intel returned to the P6-style short-pipeline philosophy with the **Core microarchitecture (2006)**. *(7 marks: 1.5 marks per major point + 1 mark for the concluding engineering lesson.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2022]** **"Explain the evolution of cache memory and bus architecture across the Pentium-II, Pentium-III, and Pentium-IV processors. How did integrating the L2 cache on-die change system performance?"** ##### **Part (a) – Cache and Bus Evolution (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Pentium-II Cache Architecture:** - L1 cache: **16 KB I + 16 KB D** (Harvard, on-die). - L2 cache: **256 KB – 512 KB**, located *off-die* on the SECC cartridge substrate, operating at **half the core clock speed**. - Bus architecture: **Dual Independent Bus (DIB)** — L2 cache had its own dedicated high-speed bus, independent of the 64-bit system memory bus (66/100 MHz). 2. **Pentium-III Cache Architecture:** - L1 cache: **16 KB I + 16 KB D** (unchanged). - L2 cache: **128 KB – 512 KB**, *integrated on the processor die* (Coppermine onwards), running at **full core clock speed**. - Bus architecture: Maintained the DIB concept, but the L2 bus became a *die-internal* connection, eliminating external signaling delays. 3. **Pentium-4 Cache Architecture:** - L1 *instruction* cache: Replaced by **Trace Cache** (12k µops, decoded instructions in execution order). - L1 *data* cache: **8 KB** (Willamette) or **16 KB** (Prescott). - L2 cache: **256 KB – 2 MB** on-die, unified 8-way set associative, full clock speed. - Bus architecture: **Quad-Pumped FSB** at 400/533/800 MHz delivering 3.2–6.4 GB/s; later **Dual Independent Bus** at memory controller level. 4. **Tabular Summary:** | Generation | L1 (I/D) | L2 | L2 Location | FSB | Peak Bandwidth | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Pentium-II | 16 KB / 16 KB | 256–512 KB | Off-die (cartridge) | 66/100 MHz | 800 MB/s | | Pentium-III | 16 KB / 16 KB | 128–512 KB | On-die | 100/133 MHz | 1.06 GB/s | | Pentium-4 | Trace 12k µops / 8–16 KB | 256 KB – 2 MB | On-die | 400/533/800 MHz | 3.2–6.4 GB/s | *(7 marks: 1.5 marks per generation + 1 mark for tabulation.)* ##### **Part (b) – Impact of On-Die L2 Cache (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Latency Reduction:** Off-die L2 in P-II had to traverse PCB traces and pins, incurring 10-15 ns latency. On-die L2 in P-III/4 reduced this to 1-3 ns — a **5–10× improvement** in cache access time. 2. **Clock Speed Coupling:** P-II's L2 ran at half CPU clock; P-III's L2 ran at *full* clock, doubling effective bandwidth to the cache. 3. **Bandwidth Calculation:** $$ \text{Bandwidth}_{\text{internal}} = \frac{\text{Cache Size} \times \text{Clock Rate} \times \text{Bytes per transfer}}{\text{Access Time}} $$ - For P-III 1 GHz with 256 KB L2: ~16 GB/s internal cache bandwidth. - For P-II 450 MHz with 512 KB L2: ~7.2 GB/s (limited by half-clock L2). 4. **Power Efficiency:** Internal on-die signaling uses lower voltage swing (1.2 V – 1.5 V) versus off-die signalling (2.5 V – 3.3 V on the cartridge bus), reducing power per access. 5. **System Performance:** SPECint and SPECfp benchmarks showed **15–30% performance gains** when moving from P-II to P-III at the same clock, attributable to L2 integration. 6. **Manufacturing Trade-off:** On-die L2 takes up significant die area (often 30–50% of total die), reducing yield and increasing cost. This is why P-II used off-die L2 in the first place. 7. **Conclusion:** On-die L2 cache was the **single most impactful architectural change** between P-II and P-III, enabling both higher clock rates and better IPC simultaneously. *(7 marks: 1 mark per major impact, 1 mark for the conclusion.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Topic:** > 1. **Confusing "NetBurst" with "P6" architecture.** Pentium-3 uses the **enhanced P6**, NOT NetBurst. Many students incorrectly attribute Hyper-Pipelining to P-3. Marks lost: **1–2 marks per occurrence.** > 2. **Stating "Pentium-4 has 16 KB L1 I-cache" — INCORRECT.** The L1 *instruction* path in P-4 is the **Trace Cache (12k µops)**. The L1 *data* cache is 8 KB (Willamette). This distinction is worth **1–2 marks.** > 3. **Forgetting to mention on-die vs off-die L2.** Saying *"Pentium-III has 256 KB L2"* without specifying **on-die at full clock** loses a critical point. > 4. **Confusing quad-pumped FSB with quad-core.** Quad-pumped means **4 data transfers per clock cycle on a single bus**, not 4 cores. (Pentium-4 is single-core; dual-core came later with Pentium-D.) > 5. **Skipping the engineering "why" questions.** KTU 2024 examiners reward *reasoning* (Bloom Level 4: Analyze). Always justify *why* P-4 abandoned the P6 pipeline. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Use this the night before the exam.** - [ ] **Pentium-II (1997):** 0.35 → 0.25 µm, 7.5 M transistors, **Slot-1 SECC cartridge**, **DIB architecture**, **off-die L2** (256–512 KB at half clock), MMX, 12-stage P6 pipeline, 233–450 MHz. - [ ] **Pentium-III (1999):** 0.25 → 0.13 µm, 9.5 M – 28 M transistors, **on-die L2** at full clock, **SSE (70 instructions, 128-bit XMM registers)**, Slot-1 → Socket-370, 450 MHz – 1.4 GHz. - [ ] **Pentium-4 (2000):** **NetBurst microarchitecture**, 42 M – 178 M transistors, **hyper-pipelined (20 → 31 stages)**, **Trace Cache (12k µops)**, **Rapid Execution Engine (ALU at 2× core clock)**, **quad-pumped FSB up to 800 MHz (6.4 GB/s)**, SSE2/SSE3, 1.3 – 3.8 GHz. - [ ] **Pipeline Formula:** $T_{\text{clock}} = T_{\text{total}} / D$, $f \propto D$. Deeper $D$ → higher $f$ but higher misprediction penalty. - [ ] **Misprediction Penalty:** P-II/P-III ≈ 12 cycles; P-4 (Willamette) ≈ 20 cycles; P-4 (Prescott) ≈ 31 cycles. - [ ] **CPI Equation:** $\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}$. - [ ] **Power Equation:** $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V^2 f$ — P-4's high $f$ led to 100+ W TDP. - [ ] **L1 Cache:** P-II/P-III = 16 KB I + 16 KB D; P-4 = Trace Cache + 8 KB (Willamette) or 16 KB D. - [ ] **L2 Cache Evolution:** Off-die (P-II) → On-die at full clock (P-III) → Larger on-die 8-way associative (P-4). - [ ] **SIMD Evolution:** MMX (64-bit, integer) → SSE (128-bit XMM, FP) → SSE2 (128-bit integer+FP) → SSE3 (specialized). - [ ] **Engineering Lesson:** NetBurst's deep-pipeline, high-frequency approach ultimately lost to the **memory wall** and **branch misprediction penalty**, leading Intel to revert to P6-style (short pipeline, wide issue) in the Core architecture. - [ ] **KTU 2024 Hot Question Pattern:** "Compare Pentium-III and Pentium-4" appears in nearly every exam cycle — **memorize the comparison table and the performance-power trade-off derivation.**

topics.title: High Performance CISC Architecture: 80386 - Salient Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Performance CISC Architecture: 80386 - Salient Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Intel 80386** (also written as **i386** or **80386DX**) is a **32-bit high-performance CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) microprocessor** introduced by Intel Corporation in **October 1985**. It is the first commercially successful fully 32-bit microprocessor in the x86 family and serves as the architectural foundation for all subsequent IA-32 processors (80486, Pentium, Core series).

The 80386 is fabricated using **CHMOS (Complementary High-performance Metal-Oxide Semiconductor) III** technology, contains approximately **2,75,000 transistors**, is housed in a **132-pin PGA (Pin Grid Array)** package, and operates on a **single +5V power supply**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, "Salient Features" questions typically expect students to enumerate architectural specifications, register set enhancements, addressing modes, memory management capabilities, and operating modes — not to derive low-level transistor-level circuitry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — "The 32-Story Smart Office Building":**

Imagine upgrading from a small **2-storey building with 8 rooms (8086)** to a massive **32-storey skyscraper with thousands of rooms (80386)**:

| Old Building (8086 / 16-bit) | New Skyscraper (80386 / 32-bit) |
|---|---|
| 16 corridors wide | **32 corridors wide** — can move twice as much data per trip |
| 20 floors of addressable rooms (1 MB) | **4,29,49,67,296 floors (4 GB)** of directly accessible rooms |
| A small PA system to announce messages | **Three intercom systems** (Real, Protected, Virtual 8086 modes) |
| Manual guest list & visitor pass | **Four security rings + dedicated paging system** (Memory Management Unit) |
| Single elevator shaft | **Six-stage pipeline** — multiple construction tasks happen simultaneously |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The 80386 is essentially a "32-bit upgrade" of the 8086 with an on-chip **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**, **paging hardware**, **hardware-supported multitasking**, and **pipelined execution** — making it a true "high-performance CISC architecture."

## 1.3 Key Salient Features — At a Glance

The most important architectural highlights of the 80386 are summarized below:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Salient Features of Intel 80386 (KTU-Mandated List)**
> 1. **32-bit data bus** and **32-bit address bus**
> 2. **32-bit internal registers** (General Purpose, Segment, Control, Debug, Test)
> 3. **Physical memory address space = 4 GB** ($2^{32}$ bytes)
> 4. **Virtual memory address space = 64 TB** ($2^{46}$ bytes)
> 5. **Pipelined architecture** with a **6-stage instruction pipeline**
> 6. **Integrated Memory Management Unit (MMU)** — Segmentation + Paging
> 7. **Three operating modes**: Real, Protected, Virtual 8086
> 8. **Four privilege levels (Rings 0–3)** for system protection
> 9. **16-byte (4-byte) instruction prefetch queue**
> 10. **Built-in hardware for multitasking** (task state segment support)
> 11. **Operating frequencies: 12, 16, 20, 25, 33 MHz**
> 12. **132-pin PGA package**, single **+5V** supply, **CHMOS III** process

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Enhancements over 8086

The 80386 extends every subsystem of the 8086 while maintaining **full backward binary compatibility**. Let us analyze each enhancement in detail.

### 2.1.1 Register Set Enhancements

The 80386 expands all 16-bit 8086 registers to **32 bits**, prefixing the letter **"E"** (Extended):

| 8086 Register (16-bit) | 80386 Register (32-bit) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| AX, BX, CX, DX | **EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX** | General Purpose Registers |
| SI, DI | **ESI, EDI** | Source/Destination Index |
| BP | **EBP** | Base Pointer |
| SP | **ESP** | Stack Pointer |
| IP | **EIP** | Instruction Pointer |
| FLAGS | **EFLAGS** | Flag Register |
| CS, DS, SS, ES | **CS, DS, SS, ES, FS, GS** | Segment Registers (two added) |
| — | **CR0, CR1, CR2, CR3** | Control Registers (New) |
| — | **GDTR, IDTR, LDTR, TR** | System Address Registers (New) |
| — | **DR0–DR7** | Debug Registers (New) |
| — | **TR0–TR7** | Test Registers (New) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Backward Compatibility Note:** The lower 16 bits of every E-register retain the original 8086 names (e.g., `AX = EAX[15:0]`, `AL = EAX[7:0]`). This allows 16-bit 8086 code to run unmodified.

### 2.1.2 Data Types Supported

The 80386 natively supports the following operand sizes:

| Operand Size | Range | Bits |
|---|---|---|
| Bit | 0 or 1 | 1 |
| Byte | $0$ to $2^8 - 1$ | 8 |
| Word (Word) | $0$ to $2^{16} - 1$ | 16 |
| **Doubleword** | $0$ to $2^{32} - 1$ | **32** (New in 80386) |
| **Quadword** | (used as 64-bit in FPU) | 64 (New) |

### 2.1.3 Addressing Modes

The 80386 supports **11 addressing modes** (an extension over the 8086's 8 modes):

1. Register operands
2. Immediate operands
3. Direct memory
4. Register indirect
5. Based
6. Indexed
7. Based + Indexed
8. Based + Indexed + Displacement
9. **Scaled indexed** (New in 80386 — `index × scale`)
10. **Based scaled indexed** (New in 80386)
11. **Based scaled indexed + displacement** (New in 80386)

The **scaled index mode** is the key new addition, allowing array indexing like `[ESI × 4 + EDX]`.

## 2.2 Physical & Virtual Memory Architecture

### 2.2.1 Address Space Calculations

The address bus width determines directly addressable memory:

$$
\text{Physical Address Space} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}
$$

where $n$ = number of address bus lines. For 80386, $n = 32$:

$$
\text{Physical Address Space} = 2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}
$$

The **virtual address space** for each task is $2^{46}$ bytes = **64 TB**, achieved via **segmentation + paging**:

$$
\text{Virtual Address} = \underbrace{16\text{-bit selector}}_{\text{segment}} : \underbrace{32\text{-bit offset}}_{\text{effective}} = 48 \text{ bits}
$$

### 2.2.2 Linear vs Physical Address

The 80386 introduces the concept of a **Linear Address** which may or may not equal the Physical Address:

$$
\text{Linear Address} = \underbrace{\text{Segment Base}}_{\text{from descriptor}} + \text{Effective Offset}
$$

$$
\text{Physical Address} = \text{Linear Address} \quad \text{(if paging is DISABLED)}
$$

$$
\text{Physical Address} = \text{Paging Translation}(\text{Linear Address}) \quad \text{(if paging is ENABLED)}
$$

## 2.3 Segmentation & Paging Unit

The 80386's **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** is composed of two cooperating units:

### Segmentation Unit
- Translates a **logical address** (selector : offset) into a **linear address**.
- Segment descriptors are stored in **GDT (Global Descriptor Table)** or **LDT (Local Descriptor Table)**.
- Each descriptor is **8 bytes** holding base, limit, and access rights.

### Paging Unit
- Translates **linear addresses** into **physical addresses**.
- Uses a **two-level page table** structure: **Page Directory → Page Table → 4 KB Page**.
- The page directory itself occupies **one 4 KB page**.
- Page Directory Entry (PDE) and Page Table Entry (PTE) are each **4 bytes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB):** The 80386 includes a small on-chip cache (typically 32 entries) called the **TLB** that stores recently used page table translations. On a TLB miss, the processor walks the two-level page table in external memory.

## 2.4 Three Operating Modes

The 80386 operates in one of three modes selected via `CRO.PE` and `CRO.PG` bits:

| Mode | CR0.PE | CR0.PG | Address Space | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Real Mode** | 0 | 0 | 1 MB (20-bit) | Boot, legacy 8086 |
| **Protected Mode** | 1 | 0 | 4 GB (32-bit linear) | Native 32-bit OS |
| **Protected Mode + Paging** | 1 | 1 | 4 GB (physical) / 64 TB (virtual) | Modern OS with virtual memory |
| **Virtual 8086 Mode** | 1 | 1 | 1 MB (per V86 task) | Running DOS inside protected mode |

## 2.5 Four Privilege Levels (Rings)

The 80386 implements **four hierarchical privilege levels (rings 0–3)** for system protection:

- **Ring 0** — Highest privilege (OS Kernel)
- **Ring 1** — OS Services
- **Ring 2** — Device Drivers / Custom OS extensions
- **Ring 3** — Lowest privilege (User Applications)

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

The 80386's salient features enabled:

- **Desktop Operating Systems:** Windows 3.x, Windows 95, Linux (early), OS/2.
- **Embedded Systems Foundation:** Modern x86 industrial PCs trace lineage to 80386.
- **Virtualization Concept:** First hardware-assisted virtual memory + privilege rings in x86.
- **Software Industry Boom:** Addressed >1 MB memory barrier of 8086.
- **Backward Compatibility:** 16-bit code runs unmodified under V86 mode.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Physical Memory Address Space

**Step 1 — Identify the address bus width of the 80386.**
The 80386 has a **32-bit address bus**, denoted $A_{31}$ to $A_0$.

**Step 2 — State the relationship between address lines and memory capacity.**
Each address line can independently carry two logic states (0 or 1). Therefore $n$ address lines can uniquely identify $2^n$ memory locations.

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\text{Maximum Addressable Memory} = 2^{n} \text{ locations}
$$

Substituting $n = 32$:

$$
\text{Memory} = 2^{32} \text{ locations}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to convenient units.**
Since each location holds 1 byte:

$$
2^{32} = 2^{2} \times 2^{30} = 4 \times 1{,}07{,}37{,}41{,}824 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\boxed{2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB (Giga Bytes)}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Virtual Memory Address Space

**Step 1 — Identify the structure of a logical address in 80386.**
A logical address consists of a **segment selector** (16 bits) and an **effective offset** (32 bits).

**Step 2 — Compute the total logical address bits.**

$$
\text{Logical Address Width} = 16 \text{ (selector)} + 32 \text{ (offset)} = 48 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the memory capacity formula.**

$$
\text{Virtual Address Space} = 2^{48} \text{ bytes}
$$

However, Intel limits the segment base field in descriptors to **24 bits** (base) + page table mapping = **46-bit effective** virtual space:

$$
\text{Virtual Address Space} = 2^{46} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to TB.**

$$
2^{46} = 2^{16} \times 2^{30} = 65{,}536 \times 1 \text{ GB} = 64{,}512 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
2^{46} = 2^{6} \times 2^{40} = 64 \text{ TB (Tera Bytes)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Virtual Memory} = 2^{46} = 64 \text{ TB}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Page Table Structure

**Step 1 — Page Size.**
The 80386 page size is fixed at **4 KB**:

$$
\text{Page Size} = 2^{12} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Pages in Physical Memory.**

$$
\text{Number of Pages} = \frac{\text{Physical Memory}}{\text{Page Size}} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Number of Pages} = 2^{20} = 10{,}48{,}576 \text{ pages}}
$$

**Step 3 — Size of Each Page Table Entry.**
Each PTE/PDE contains **physical page frame address (20 bits) + control bits (12 bits) = 32 bits = 4 bytes**.

**Step 4 — Number of Entries per Page Table.**
A page table occupies **one 4 KB page**. Each entry is 4 bytes:

$$
\text{Entries per Page Table} = \frac{4096 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes}} = 1024 = 2^{10}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Page Tables Needed.**
Since one page table maps $2^{10}$ pages, the number of page tables required is:

$$
\text{Number of Page Tables} = \frac{\text{Total Pages}}{\text{Entries per Table}} = \frac{2^{20}}{2^{10}} = 2^{10} = 1024
$$

**Step 6 — Page Directory Requirement.**
A second-level structure — the **Page Directory** — points to all 1024 page tables. Since the page directory itself can fit into one page:

$$
\text{Page Directory Size} = 1024 \text{ PDEs} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 7 — Linear Address Breakdown.**
The 80386 splits the 32-bit linear address into three fields:

$$
\text{Linear Address} = \underbrace{[31:22]}_{\text{Page Directory Index (10 bits)}} : \underbrace{[21:12]}_{\text{Page Table Index (10 bits)}} : \underbrace{[11:0]}_{\text{Offset within Page (12 bits)}}
$$

$$
10 + 10 + 12 = 32 \text{ bits} \checkmark
$$

**Step 8 — Translation Walk (on TLB miss).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{PDE Address} = \text{CR3} \, + \, (\text{Linear Address}[31:22] \times 4) \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \text{Read PDE to find Page Table Base} \\
\text{Step C:} \quad & \text{PTE Address} = \text{Page Table Base} + (\text{Linear Address}[21:12] \times 4) \\
\text{Step D:} \quad & \text{Read PTE to find Physical Page Frame} \\
\text{Step E:} \quad & \text{Physical Address} = \text{Page Frame} \, + \, \text{Linear Address}[11:0]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Predecessors

This matrix is a **favourite KTU question pattern** ("Compare 8086, 80286, and 80386"):

| Feature | 8086 (1978) | 80286 (1982) | **80386 (1985)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Bus | 16-bit | 16-bit | **32-bit** |
| Address Bus | 20-bit | 24-bit | **32-bit** |
| Physical Memory | 1 MB | 16 MB | **4 GB** |
| Virtual Memory | None | 1 GB | **64 TB** |
| Pipeline Stages | 2 | 4 | **6** |
| Operating Modes | Real | Real, Protected | **Real, Protected, V86** |
| Paging | No | No | **Yes (4 KB pages)** |
| MMU | No | Yes (segment only) | **Yes (segment + page)** |
| Transistors | 29,000 | 1,34,000 | **2,75,000** |
| Clock | 5–10 MHz | 6–25 MHz | **12–33 MHz** |
| Registers | 16-bit | 16-bit | **32-bit (E-registers)** |

## 3.5 Derivations of Performance Metrics

**Step 1 — Clock Period at 33 MHz.**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{33 \times 10^6} \text{ seconds} \approx 30.3 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Instruction Execution Time (assuming 2 clocks/instruction average).**

$$
T_{inst} = 2 \times 30.3 \text{ ns} = 60.6 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second).**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{1}{T_{inst} \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^6}{60.6 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 16.5 \text{ MIPS}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{2} \approx 16.5 \text{ MIPS at 33 MHz}}
$$

**Step 4 — Data Throughput on 32-bit Bus at 33 MHz.**

$$
\text{Throughput} = 32 \text{ bits} \times 33 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1056 \text{ Mbits/s}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{1056}{8} = 132 \text{ MB/s}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of the 80386

The 80386 microprocessor consists of **six major functional units** that operate concurrently. The following Mermaid block diagram maps these interactions:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BUS["32-BIT EXTERNAL BUS INTERFACE UNIT"]
    IU["INSTRUCTION UNIT\n(6-stage pipeline + 16-byte prefetch queue)"]
    DU["DECODE UNIT"]
    EU["EXECUTION UNIT\n(ALU + Barrel Shifter + Multiplier/Divider)"]
    SU["SEGMENTATION UNIT\n(Segment Descriptor Cache)"]
    PU["PAGING UNIT\n(TLB + Page Walk Logic)"]
    MMU["MEMORY MANAGEMENT UNIT"]
    REG["REGISTER FILE\n(8 x 32-bit GPRs + EIP + EFLAGS)"]
    CR["CONTROL REGISTERS\nCR0-CR3 + GDTR/IDTR/LDTR/TR"]
    EXT["EXTERNAL MEMORY\n(4 GB Physical)"]

    BUS <--> EXT
    BUS --> IU
    IU --> DU
    DU --> EU
    DU --> REG
    EU --> REG
    EU --> SU
    EU --> PU
    SU --> PU
    SU --> MMU
    PU --> MMU
    MMU --> BUS
    CR --> SU
    CR --> PU
    REG --> EU
```

## 4.2 Two-Level Paging Architecture Diagram

The paging unit performs address translation via a hierarchical page table walk:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LA["32-BIT LINEAR ADDRESS\nBits 31 to 22 : Bits 21 to 12 : Bits 11 to 0"]
    CR3R["CR3 REGISTER\n(Page Directory Base Address)"]
    PDE["PAGE DIRECTORY ENTRY\n4 bytes (Bits 31 to 12 = PT base)"]
    PT["PAGE TABLE\n(1024 entries, 4 KB)"]
    PTE["PAGE TABLE ENTRY\n4 bytes (Bits 31 to 12 = PFN)"]
    PF["PHYSICAL PAGE FRAME\n4 KB in memory"]
    PA["PHYSICAL ADDRESS = PFN + Offset"]

    LA -->|bits 31 to 22| CR3R
    CR3R --> PDE
    PDE -->|bits 21 to 12| PT
    PT --> PTE
    PTE --> PF
    PF --> PA
```

## 4.3 Three Operating Mode State Diagram

The processor transitions between operating modes by manipulating **CR0.PE** and **CR0.PG** bits:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> REAL_MODE
    REAL_MODE --> PROTECTED_NO_PAGE : CR0_PE set to 1
    PROTECTED_NO_PAGE --> PROTECTED_PAGED : CR0_PG set to 1
    PROTECTED_NO_PAGE --> REAL_MODE : Processor Reset
    PROTECTED_PAGED --> VIRTUAL_8086 : EFLAGS_VM set to 1
    VIRTUAL_8086 --> PROTECTED_PAGED : Interrupt or IRET
    PROTECTED_PAGED --> PROTECTED_NO_PAGE : CR0_PG set to 0
```

## 4.4 80386 Six-Stage Instruction Pipeline

The 80386 implements a **six-stage pipeline** that overlaps fetch, decode, and execute operations:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STAGE 1\nBUS FETCH 1\n(Reads 4 bytes)"]
    S2["STAGE 2\nBUS FETCH 2\n(Reads 4 bytes)"]
    S3["STAGE 3\nINSTRUCTION DECODE"]
    S4["STAGE 4\nOPERAND FETCH\n(Address generation)"]
    S5["STAGE 5\nEXECUTE\n(ALU / Shift)"]
    S6["STAGE 6\nWRITE BACK\n(Store result)"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "draw the block diagram" of 80386, students should at minimum draw: Bus Interface Unit, Prefetch Queue, Instruction Decoder, Execution Unit (with ALU and Barrel Shifter), Segmentation Unit, Paging Unit, and Register File.

## 4.5 Four Privilege Rings Hierarchy

The four privilege levels of the 80386 form a concentric protection hierarchy:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RING0["RING 0\nOS KERNEL\nHighest Privilege"]
    RING1["RING 1\nOS SERVICES"]
    RING2["RING 2\nDEVICE DRIVERS"]
    RING3["RING 3\nUSER APPLICATIONS\nLowest Privilege"]

    RING0 --- RING1
    RING1 --- RING2
    RING2 --- RING3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define the salient features of the Intel 80386 microprocessor. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Answer:**

The Intel 80386 is a **32-bit CISC microprocessor** with the following salient features:

1. It has a **32-bit data bus** and a **32-bit address bus**, allowing direct access to **4 GB of physical memory** ($2^{32}$ bytes).
2. All internal registers are extended to **32 bits** (EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP, ESP, EIP, EFLAGS).
3. It supports **virtual memory of 64 TB** ($2^{46}$ bytes) per task via an on-chip MMU.
4. It operates in **three modes**: Real Address Mode, Protected Mode, and Virtual 8086 Mode.
5. It has a **six-stage instruction pipeline** with a **16-byte prefetch queue**.
6. It supports **four privilege levels (rings 0–3)** for memory and I/O protection.
7. It contains approximately **2,75,000 transistors**, operates on **+5V**, and is housed in a **132-pin PGA** package.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Real Mode and Protected Mode of the 80386. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Real Mode | Protected Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Address Bus Width | 20-bit (A0–A19) | 32-bit (A0–A31) |
| Addressable Memory | 1 MB ($2^{20}$) | 4 GB ($2^{32}$) |
| Memory Protection | Not available | Full protection via rings |
| Multitasking | Not supported | Supported via TSS |
| Segment Limit | Fixed (64 KB max) | Configurable per segment (4 GB) |
| Paging | Disabled | Can be enabled |
| Use Case | Booting, legacy DOS | Native 32-bit OS |

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B Module-2 questions must include an internal choice. Either Question A or Question B is to be answered. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.
