- **Cache Coherence:** Required for shared-memory multicore; uses MESI/MOESI protocols — a probable 7-mark sub-question.
- **Mermaid/Block Diagram Skill:** Practice drawing a 4-core CMP block with private L1 and shared L3 — this is a recurring diagram question in KTU board papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Hyper-Threading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Concept of Hyper-Threading # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Hyper-Threading Technology (HTT)**, also known as **Simultaneous Multi-Threading (SMT)**, is a proprietary Intel microarchitecture innovation that enables a **single physical processor core** to behave as **two (or more) logical processors** to the operating system and application software. Each logical processor maintains its own **architectural state** (general-purpose registers, control registers, advanced programmable interrupt controller/APIC registers, and machine state) while sharing the core's **physical execution resources** (execution units, caches, bus interface, branch prediction hardware, etc.). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** > *"Hyper-Threading is an implementation of Simultaneous Multi-Threading (SMT) where a single physical CPU core exposes two logical processors, allowing the OS to schedule and execute two independent threads in parallel within the same cycle, by dynamically partitioning and sharing the underlying physical execution resources."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Baristas, One Coffee Machine" Model Imagine a small café with **one physical coffee machine** but **two baristas** (workers). Instead of having one barista idle whenever the machine is grinding, both baristas are assigned to the same machine: - **Barista A** prepares espresso shots while the machine is heating milk. - **Barista B** grinds beans while Barista A is steaming. Both baristas share the **same physical resources** (the machine, grinder, cups, water supply), but to the customer, **two orders can be served nearly in parallel**. This is precisely how Hyper-Threading works: **one physical core is split into two "logical workers"** that interleave their instructions through the shared execution units of the core. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Hyper-Threading is **NOT** a true dual-core system. Both logical processors run on the *same* silicon die, share caches, share execution units, and share the branch predictor. They only duplicate the **architectural state**. ## 1.3 Why Was Hyper-Threading Invented? Modern superscalar processors contain **far more execution resources** than a single thread can typically utilize. Studies of typical workloads show that on average, only about **25%–35%** of available execution resources are kept busy by a single thread at any given instant, due to: - **Long-latency memory stalls** (cache misses) - **Branch mispredictions** - **Data dependencies** between instructions - **Pipeline bubbles** during instruction fetch/decode HTT recovers this lost throughput by giving the core a **second thread to issue from** whenever the first thread stalls. > [!TIP] > **Memory Stickiness:** HTT is most effective when one thread is **memory-bound** (waiting for cache/D RAM) while the other is **compute-bound** (ready to execute). This complementary resource usage is called **"co-execution synergy."** ## 1.4 Hyper-Threading vs. Multi-Core — The Critical Difference | Feature | Hyper-Threading (SMT) | Multi-Core | |---|---|---| | **Physical Cores** | **1** | **2 or more** | | **Logical Processors** | **2** | **1 per core** (without HT) | | **Architectural State** | Duplicated | Each core has its own | | **Execution Units** | **Shared** | **Independent** | | **L1/L2 Cache** | **Shared** | Per-core private | | **Silicon Cost Increase** | **~5%** | **~100% per extra core** | | **Performance Gain (typical)** | **15%–30%** | **70%–90%** (near-linear) | | **OS Visibility** | Two logical CPUs | Multiple physical CPUs | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Internal Architecture of a Hyper-Threaded Core A standard Intel Pentium 4 / Core i-series physical core with HTT enabled is partitioned into three logical zones: ### Zone A — Duplicated (Replicated) Resources - **Architectural Registers:** EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP, ESP, EIP - **Control Registers:** CR0, CR2, CR3, CR4, CR8 (x86 control) - **Debug Registers:** DR0–DR7 - **APIC Registers** (Local APIC, task priority, etc.) - **Machine State:** Segment registers (CS, DS, ES, FS, GS, SS) - **Branch Target Buffer (BTB) entries tagged per logical processor** > These resources are **replicated twice** (one set per logical processor) because they describe the *state* of the thread, not the *work* being done. ### Zone B — Shared (Partitioned) Resources - **Execution Units (EU):** ALU, FPU, SSE/AVX units, load/store ports - **L1 Instruction Cache, L1 Data Cache** - **L2 Unified Cache** - **Microcode ROM and Instruction Decoders** - **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** - **Register Alias Table (RAT)** - **Reservation Stations (RS)** > These are **shared** but with a dynamic **"tag-and-replay"** mechanism — instructions from both threads carry a thread-ID, and resource allocation is decided per-cycle. ### Zone C — Competitively Shared Resources - **Cache Bandwidth** - **Bus Bandwidth (Front-Side Bus / Ring / Mesh)** - **Branch Predictor Tables** (entries can be claimed by either thread) ## 2.2 The Pipeline of a Hyper-Threaded Core (Step-by-Step) 1. **Front-End Fetch:** The fetch unit pulls instructions from **either** logical processor based on a fairness/priority algorithm. The selection logic alternates threads or picks whichever is "ready" (no stall). 2. **Decode:** Both threads' instructions are decoded and placed in a shared micro-op queue. A **thread ID tag** is appended to each micro-op. 3. **Rename / Allocate:** Register renaming allocates physical registers from the shared pool, with thread tags preserved. 4. **Issue / Execute:** When a thread stalls (e.g., L2 cache miss), the scheduler immediately issues micro-ops from the **other thread** into the execution units. 5. **Retire:** Both threads' completed instructions are written back to their **own** architectural state in program order. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** HTT does NOT double execution resources — it only doubles the **sources of work** that can be fed to those resources. If both threads are compute-heavy and saturate the same ALU, **no speedup is achieved** (this is a phenomenon called "**thread starvation**" or "**resource contention**"). ## 2.3 Performance Metrics for HTT ### 2.3.1 Speedup (S) $$S = \frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}$$ Where: - $T_{1}$ = Execution time of one thread running alone - $T_{2}$ = Execution time of the same workload split across 2 logical processors ### 2.3.2 Efficiency (E) $$E = \frac{S}{N} \times 100\%$$ Where $N$ = number of logical processors used. ### 2.3.3 Theoretical Maximum Speedup — Amdahl's Law $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$ Where: - $P$ = **Parallelizable fraction** of the workload - $(1 - P)$ = Serial fraction - $N$ = Number of logical processors > **Typical HTT Case:** $N = 2$. Even if 100% parallel ($P = 1$), ideal speedup is capped at **2×**, but in practice HTT achieves only **1.15×–1.30×** because of resource contention. ### 2.3.4 Resource Utilization Metric $$U = \frac{\text{Active Execution Unit Cycles}}{\text{Total Available Cycles}} \times 100\%$$ - Without HTT: Typical $U \approx 25\% - 35\%$ - With HTT: $U$ can approach **60%–70%** in well-tuned workloads ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units / Range** | |---|---|---| | $S = T_{1}/T_{2}$ | Speedup factor | Dimensionless (1.0 to 2.0) | | $E = S/N$ | Parallel efficiency | 0% to 100% | | $S_{max} = 1/\left[(1-P)+P/N\right]$ | Amdahl's limit | Dimensionless | | $U = \text{Active cycles} / \text{Total cycles}$ | Pipeline utilization | 0% to 100% | | $T_{parallel} = T_{serial} + T_{parallel\_part}/N$ | Execution time decomposition | Seconds | | $\text{Thread Affinity Mask}$ | OS-level binding to logical CPU | Hex bitmask | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *logical processors* with *physical cores*. In `lscpu` or Device Manager, a 4-core / 8-thread CPU must be reported as **4 physical cores, 8 logical processors**, not as "8 cores." ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Server workloads** (databases, web servers): Up to **30% throughput gain** because I/O wait of one thread overlaps with compute of another. - **Desktop / Gaming**: Marginal benefit (~5%–10%) because games typically are not multi-threaded at fine granularity. - **Scientific computing** (HPC): Limited benefit since the workload fully saturates FP units, leaving no idle resources for the second thread to exploit. - **Embedded/Real-Time**: HTT is often **disabled** in real-time OS deployments to guarantee deterministic scheduling. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Speedup of Hyper-Threading (Amdahl's Law Applied to N=2) We begin with the general Amdahl's law expression and apply the constraints of an HTT-enabled core (two logical processors sharing one physical core's resources). **Step 1 — General Amdahl's Law** $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute N = 2** (the number of logical processors in a single HTT core) $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the denominator** $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{\frac{2(1 - P) + P}{2}}$$ $$S_{max} = \frac{2}{2 - 2P + P}$$ $$S_{max} = \frac{2}{2 - P}$$ **Step 4 — Final HTT-Specific Speedup Formula** $$\boxed{S_{max}^{HTT} = \frac{2}{2 - P}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical Examples for KTU Practice** | **Parallel Fraction (P)** | **Speedup $S_{max}$** | **Efficiency $E = S/2$** | |---|---|---| | $P = 0.25$ (25%) | $S = 2 / 1.75 = 1.143\times$ | $E = 57.1\%$ | | $P = 0.50$ (50%) | $S = 2 / 1.50 = 1.333\times$ | $E = 66.7\%$ | | $P = 0.75$ (75%) | $S = 2 / 1.25 = 1.600\times$ | $E = 80.0\%$ | | $P = 0.90$ (90%) | $S = 2 / 1.10 = 1.818\times$ | $E = 90.9\%$ | | $P = 1.00$ (100%) | $S = 2 / 1.00 = 2.000\times$ | $E = 100.0\%$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reality Check:** The above assumes **zero resource contention**. In real silicon, contention reduces $S$ by 20%–40%, so practical HTT speedup is typically capped around **1.25×–1.30×**. ## 3.2 Derivation: How Resource Sharing Recovers "Lost" Execution Cycles **Step 1 — Single-threaded utilization model** Let $U_1$ be the fraction of cycles a single thread keeps the execution units busy. $$U_1 = \frac{\text{Busy cycles}}{\text{Total cycles}} = 0.30 \text{ (typical 30\%)}$$ **Step 2 — Idle fraction** $$I = 1 - U_1 = 0.70 \text{ (70\% idle, due to stalls/mispredicts)}$$ **Step 3 — Assume the second thread can fill the idle cycles** Let $\alpha$ be the **co-execution efficiency factor** (fraction of the other thread's stalls that the second thread can overlap). For a memory-bound + compute-bound pairing, $\alpha \approx 0.40$. **Step 4 — Effective utilization with HTT** $$U_{2}^{HTT} = U_1 + \alpha \cdot I = 0.30 + 0.40 \times 0.70 = 0.30 + 0.28 = 0.58$$ **Step 5 — Speedup achieved** $$S = \frac{U_{2}^{HTT}}{U_1} = \frac{0.58}{0.30} = 1.93\times \text{(theoretical, no contention)}$$ **Step 6 — Apply contention factor $\beta = 0.65$** (empirically observed for HTT) $$S_{real} = S \times \beta = 1.93 \times 0.65 = 1.26\times$$ > **Conclusion:** This matches the well-known empirical 25%–30% speedup from HTT in well-tuned multi-threaded workloads. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Workload Behavior Under HTT | **Workload Class** | **Thread 1 Behavior** | **Thread 2 Behavior** | **HTT Benefit** | |---|---|---|---| | Memory-bound | Long cache miss stalls | CPU-bound execution | **High (25%–30%)** | | Compute-bound (FP-heavy) | Saturates ALU/FPU | Saturates ALU/FPU | **Negligible (~0%)** | | Branch-heavy | Frequent mispredicts | Linear code | **Moderate (10%–15%)** | | I/O bound | Waits on disk/network | CPU-bound execution | **High (20%–30%)** | | Identical CPU loops | Same instruction pattern | Same instruction pattern | **Negative (thrashing)** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Single-Threaded vs Hyper-Threaded Core ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TRAD["TRADITIONAL SINGLE-THREADED CORE"] OS1["Operating System
Sees 1 CPU"] T1["Thread 1"] EXU1["Execution Units
ALU FPU LSU"] DEC1["Decoder / ROB"] L1A["L1 Cache"] L1A -.- EXU1 OS1 --> T1 --> DEC1 --> EXU1 EXU1 --> L1A end subgraph HT["HYPER-THREADED CORE (Same Silicon)"] OS2["Operating System
Sees 2 CPUs"] LA["Logical CPU 0
Thread 1 Registers
APIC State"] LB["Logical CPU 1
Thread 2 Registers
APIC State"] FRONT["Shared Front-End
Fetch + Decoder
Thread-Tagged uops"] EXU2["Shared Execution Units
ALU FPU SSE/AVX LSU
(Partitioned per cycle)"] L2["Shared L1 + L2 Cache
(Data shared between threads)"] OS2 --> LA OS2 --> LB LA --> FRONT LB --> FRONT FRONT --> EXU2 EXU2 --> L2 end ``` ## 4.2 Pipeline Flow Diagram — How Two Threads Share a Single Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR C0["Cycle 0
Thread A executes
ALU op"] C1["Cycle 1
Thread A stalls on
L2 miss"] C2["Cycle 2
Thread B executes
ALU op
(fills bubble)"] C3["Cycle 3
Thread B executes
FPU op"] C4["Cycle 4
Thread A still stalled
Thread B continues"] C5["Cycle 5
Thread A returns
Cache line ready"] C6["Cycle 6
Thread A resumes
Thread B stalls on branch"] C7["Cycle 7
Thread A executes
(fills B's bubble)"] C0 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 --> C7 ``` ## 4.3 Resource Partitioning Map (3-Zone Model) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ZONEA["ZONE A - DUPLICATED (Per Logical CPU)"] R1["GPRs EAX..EDI
Control Registers CR0..CR8
Debug Registers DR0..DR7
APIC Registers
Segment Registers"] end subgraph ZONEB["ZONE B - SHARED PARTITIONED (Dynamic Allocation)"] R2["Execution Units ALU FPU
Load Store Unit
L1 Instruction Cache
L1 Data Cache
L2 Unified Cache
Microcode ROM
Reorder Buffer ROB
Register Alias Table RAT"] end subgraph ZONEC["ZONE C - COMPETITIVELY SHARED"] R3["Branch Predictor Tables
Cache Bandwidth
Bus Bandwidth
DRAM Controller Access"] end ZONEA --> ZONEB --> ZONEC ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flow: When Does HTT Help? ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Workload Arrives"] --> B{"Thread
Memory-Bound?"} B -- "Yes" --> C{"Pair with
Compute-Bound Thread?"} B -- "No (pure compute)" --> D["HTT Benefit
= Near 0%"] C -- "Yes" --> E["HTT Benefit
20% to 30%"] C -- "No (both memory-bound)" --> F["HTT Benefit
5% to 10%"] A --> G{"More than 2
Threads Available?"} G -- "Yes" --> H["Disable HTT
Use Physical Cores Instead"] G -- "No" --> E ``` > [!TIP] > **Engineering Heuristic:** For best HTT performance, pair a **stall-prone thread** (DB query, file read, network call) with a **compute-heavy thread** (rendering, compression, scientific loop). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define Hyper-Threading Technology. How does it differ from a true dual-core processor?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Hyper-Threading Technology (HTT) is a hardware implementation of Simultaneous Multi-Threading (SMT) in which a **single physical processor core** is exposed to the operating system as **two (or more) logical processors**. Each logical processor has its own **architectural state** (general-purpose registers, control registers, APIC, machine state) but shares the physical execution resources — execution units, caches, decoders, branch predictor — with the other logical processor. **Key differences from dual-core (3 marks breakdown):** 1. **Silicon area:** HTT needs only ~5% extra transistors (for duplicating architectural state), whereas a true dual-core needs nearly double the die area. *(1 mark)* 2. **Resource sharing vs duplication:** In HTT, the execution units and caches are *shared*; in dual-core, each core has *independent* execution units and L1/L2 caches. *(1 mark)* 3. **Performance gain:** HTT yields only 15%–30% throughput improvement; true dual-core gives near-linear 70%–90% improvement for parallel workloads. *(1 mark)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **List any three advantages and two limitations of Hyper-Threading Technology.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Advantages (any 3):** 1. **Better CPU resource utilization** — recovers idle cycles caused by stalls and mispredictions; utilization rises from ~30% to ~60% in well-tuned workloads. *(1 mark)* 2. **Low silicon cost** — only ~5% extra die area compared to ~100% for an additional physical core, making it cost-effective. *(1 mark)* 3. **Improved throughput for server/multi-tasking workloads** — I/O bound threads overlap with compute-bound threads. *(1 mark)* **Limitations (any 2):** 1. **Resource contention** — when both threads are compute-heavy, they compete for the same execution units, yielding negligible or even *negative* speedup. *(1 mark)* 2. **Security vulnerabilities** — HTT has been the root cause of side-channel attacks (e.g., Spectre, Foreshadow, L1 Terminal Fault), leading to performance-heavy mitigations. *(1 mark)* 3. **Non-deterministic timing** — real-time systems often disable HTT because thread interleaving makes execution time unpredictable. *(Bonus)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the internal architecture of a Hyper-Threaded processor core. With the help of a block diagram, describe the **duplicated, shared, and competitively shared** resources. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the working of Hyper-Threading in the pipeline stages. Show how two threads are interleaved in the fetch, decode, issue, and retire stages, with an example cycle trace. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks** The internal architecture of a Hyper-Threaded core is logically divided into **three resource zones**: **1. Duplicated Resources (Architectural State)** — *(2 marks)* These are replicated for each logical processor: - General-purpose registers: EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP, ESP - Instruction pointer EIP, flags register EFLAGS - Control registers CR0, CR2, CR3, CR4, CR8 - Debug registers DR0–DR7 - Local APIC register set - Segment registers CS, DS, ES, FS, GS, SS These resources describe the **state** of each thread independently; therefore they are duplicated. **2. Shared Partitioned Resources (Execution Back-end)** — *(2 marks)* These are **physically one set**, but dynamically allocated to whichever logical processor is ready: - Execution units: integer ALU, FPU, SSE/AVX units, load-store unit (LSU) - L1 instruction cache, L1 data cache - L2 unified cache - Microcode ROM, instruction decoders - Reorder Buffer (ROB) - Register Alias Table (RAT) and Reservation Stations **3. Competitively Shared Resources** — *(1 mark)* - Branch prediction tables - Cache and bus bandwidth - DRAM memory controller **Block Diagram Representation:** *(2 marks)* ``` +--------------------------------------------+ | Operating System View | | [Logical CPU 0] [Logical CPU 1] | | | | | | [Arch State 0] [Arch State 1] | <- ZONE A | \____ ______/ | | \ / | | [Shared Front-End: Fetch/Decode] | <- ZONE B | [Shared Execution Units] | | [Shared L1 + L2 Cache] | | [Shared Branch Predictor] | <- ZONE C +--------------------------------------------+ ``` **Conclusion (synthesis):** The architecture is **state-duplicated, execution-shared** — this is the defining characteristic of HTT vs dual-core. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks** The HTT pipeline is the standard superscalar pipeline enhanced with **logical-CPU selection logic** and **thread tagging**. **Step 1 — Front-End Selection (1 mark):** At each cycle, the **Next Thread Selector** picks between Logical CPU 0 and Logical CPU 1 using a fairness algorithm (often round-robin with priority boost for starving threads). The selected thread's EIP feeds the fetch unit. **Step 2 — Tagged Micro-op Queue (1 mark):** Decoded micro-ops are placed in a shared queue with a **thread-ID tag** (1 bit for two-thread HTT). This tag travels with each micro-op through the rest of the pipeline. **Step 3 — Issue Logic with Thread-Aware Stall Detection (2 marks):** The reservation station tracks each thread's **readiness** separately. If Thread A's micro-op is waiting on a cache miss and Thread B's micro-op is ready, the scheduler issues Thread B's micro-op, filling the bubble. This is the **core HTT advantage**. **Step 4 — Execution (1 mark):** Both threads' micro-ops execute in the same execution units. Each cycle, the dispatcher decides which thread's micro-op occupies which port. **Step 5 — Retirement (1 mark):** The ROB retires micro-ops in program order **within each thread**, writing results back to the correct architectural register file (Thread 0's or Thread 1's, based on the thread ID). **Cycle Trace Example (1 mark):** | **Cycle** | **Thread A Action** | **Thread B Action** | **Resource Used** | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | ALU add | — | ALU0 | | 1 | Load from L1 (stall) | — | (idle) | | 2 | (still stalled) | FPU multiply | FPU0 | | 3 | (still stalled) | ALU add | ALU0 | | 4 | (still stalled) | Branch predict | BPU | | 5 | Cache returns | — | LSU | | 6 | Continue ALU | Load from L1 (stall) | ALU0 | Without HTT, cycles 2–4 would be **idle bubbles**; with HTT, Thread B fills them, achieving the throughput gain. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **single-threaded, multi-threaded (Hyper-Threaded), and multi-core** processor architectures using a suitable comparison matrix. Discuss the performance implications with Amdahl's law. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A server workload has a **parallelizable fraction P = 0.80** of execution time. Compute the maximum speedup achievable on: (i) a single-core CPU, (ii) a single-core with Hyper-Threading (N=2), and (iii) a dual-core CPU without HTT. Apply a **realistic resource-contention factor of 0.70** for the HTT case and discuss the result. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks** **Comparison Matrix (4 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Single-Threaded** | **Hyper-Threaded (SMT)** | **Multi-Core** | |---|---|---|---| | Physical cores | 1 | 1 | 2+ | | Logical CPUs visible to OS | 1 | 2 | N (= physical cores) | | Architectural state sets | 1 | 2 (duplicated) | 1 per core | | Execution units | 1 set, often idle | 1 set, shared between 2 threads | N independent sets | | L1/L2 cache | 1 private set | Shared between 2 threads | N private sets | | Branch predictor | 1 set | Shared | N sets | | Typical speedup vs single | 1.00× | 1.15× – 1.30× | 1.70× – 1.90× | | Silicon cost | Baseline | +~5% | +~100% per extra core | | Thread management | None | OS-level | OS-level | | Power consumption scaling | Linear | Sub-linear | Near-linear with cores | | Best use case | Single-thread legacy | Mixed I/O + compute | Parallel HPC/Server | **Amdahl's Law Implications (3 marks):** Using $S_{max} = 1/[(1-P) + P/N]$: - **Single-threaded:** $N=1$, so $S_{max} = 1$, **no speedup possible** by definition. - **Hyper-Threaded:** $N=2$, $S_{max}^{ideal} = 2/(2-P)$. For $P=0.90$, $S_{max}^{ideal} = 1.82\times$, but realistic $S = 1.82 \times 0.70 = 1.27\times$. - **Dual-core:** $N=2$, $S_{max}^{ideal} = 1.82\times$ (same algebraically), but **realistically near-ideal** since resources are independent (no contention). **Key insight:** Hyper-Threading and Dual-Core have the **same theoretical $N$**, but HTT is **farther from ideal** due to shared resources. Therefore, a CPU with **2 physical cores + HTT = 4 logical processors** is preferred over **1 core + HTT = 2 logical processors** for highly parallel workloads. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - Parallel fraction: $P = 0.80$ - Serial fraction: $(1 - P) = 0.20$ **Formula:** $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + P/N} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.80/N}$$ **Case (i): Single-core CPU** *(2 marks)* $$S_{max}^{(i)} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.80/1} = \frac{1}{1.00} = 1.00\times$$ No speedup, as expected. **Case (ii): Single-core with HTT (N = 2, with contention factor 0.70)** *(3 marks)* Theoretical speedup: $$S_{ideal}^{(ii)} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.80/2} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.40} = \frac{1}{0.60} = 1.667\times$$ Realistic speedup with contention factor $\beta = 0.70$: $$S_{real}^{(ii)} = S_{ideal}^{(ii)} \times \beta = 1.667 \times 0.70 = 1.167\times$$ **Case (iii): Dual-core CPU without HTT (N = 2, near-ideal)** *(2 marks)* $$S_{max}^{(iii)} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.80/2} = 1.667\times$$ Since resources are independent, no contention factor is applied, so $S_{real}^{(iii)} \approx 1.667\times$. **Discussion (synthesis):** *(Bonus understanding)* - The HTT case delivers only **1.167×** speedup vs dual-core's **1.667×**. - The "**contention tax**" of HTT is approximately $1.667 - 1.167 = 0.50\times$ lost speedup. - For this workload (P = 0.80), a dual-core CPU is **~43% faster** than a single-core + HTT solution, despite both exposing N=2 logical processors. - Conclusion: HTT is a **complementary technology**, not a **substitute** for additional physical cores. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Hyper-Threading Questions:** > 1. **Confusing HTT with multi-core** — Examiners will instantly deduct 2–3 marks if you claim HTT gives "two physical cores" or "double the execution units." Always emphasize *state duplication* vs *resource sharing*. > 2. **Forgetting the silicon cost justification** — Many students miss the *why* of HTT. Always state the ~5% transistor cost vs ~100% for a new core. > 3. **Skipping Amdahl's law in numerical problems** — A 7-mark numerical sub-part that doesn't reference Amdahl's formula or the contention factor $\beta$ will lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **Missing thread-tagging concept** — In pipeline diagrams, students often forget to label the **1-bit thread ID** that travels with each micro-op. > 5. **Reporting CPU count wrongly** — When the question says "Core i7 with 4 cores / 8 threads," write **"4 physical cores, 8 logical processors"** — *never* "8 cores." > 6. **Ignoring security/power downsides** — Bonus marks are awarded for mentioning Spectre-class vulnerabilities and real-time OS limitations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Hyper-Threading (HTT) = Simultaneous Multi-Threading (SMT)** implemented by Intel — one physical core appears as two logical CPUs. - [x] **HTT duplicates only architectural state** (registers, APIC, control regs); **execution resources are shared**. - [x] Typical performance gain: **15%–30%** with well-paired threads; near **0%** with compute-bound identical threads. - [x] Silicon cost is only **~5%** over a non-HTT core, vs **~100%** for a second physical core. - [x] HTT works best when one thread is **memory-bound** and the other is **compute-bound** (co-execution synergy). - [x] Amdahl's Law for HTT: $S_{max} = 2/(2-P)$ (theoretical); with contention: $S_{real} = S_{max} \times \beta$. - [x] Resource utilization rises from ~30% (single-threaded) to ~60% (with HTT) in mixed workloads. - [x] Pipeline tagging: every micro-op carries a **thread ID bit** through the back-end. - [x] **HTT ≠ multi-core** — multi-core has independent execution units, caches, and predictors; HTT shares them all. - [x] Real-world scenarios: HTT enabled in **servers and desktops**, **disabled in real-time and high-security systems**. - [x] Security implication: HTT is the root cause of several **side-channel attacks** (Spectre, Foreshadow, L1TF). - [x] When a CPU is described as "**X cores, Y threads**", then logical processors $Y = X \times 2$ (if HTT is enabled). - [x] Amdahl's Law cap for HTT ($N=2$): even at $P=1.0$, $S_{max} = 2.0\times$, but realistic is **~1.25×**.

topics.title: Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Multicore Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Multicore Processors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multicore Processor:** A single integrated circuit (chip) that contains **two or more independent processing units (cores)**, where each core can independently read, decode, and execute program instructions using shared or distributed on-chip resources. A **Multicore Processor** integrates multiple Central Processing Unit (CPU) cores onto a single silicon die, allowing **parallel instruction execution** and **Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)**. Based on the architectural identity of the cores, multicore processors are classified into two principal categories: | Classification | Definition | |---|---| | **Homogeneous Multicore Processor** | A multicore chip where **all cores are architecturally identical** (same ISA, same pipeline depth, same cache, same clock domain). | | **Heterogeneous Multicore Processor** | A multicore chip where **cores have different microarchitectures, performance, or power profiles**, typically combining high-performance and power-efficient cores. | > [!NOTE] > The terms **Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP)** and **Asymmetric Multiprocessing (AMP)** are the operating-system-level realizations of homogeneous and heterogeneous multicore designs, respectively. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition ### The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy 🍳 Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** preparing 100 orders simultaneously: - **Homogeneous Multicore (SMP) Kitchen** = **A team of 5 identical head chefs**, each with the *same training, same tools, same speed, and same specialty*. You can give any order to any chef — the system is balanced and fair. This is like a typical Intel/AMD desktop CPU with 8 identical cores. - **Heterogeneous Multicore (AMP) Kitchen** = **A team with 2 master chefs (experts, slow, energy-hungry) and 4 junior chefs (fast, energy-efficient, simple tasks)**. The *master chefs* handle complex gourmet orders, while the *junior chefs* quickly handle salads and simple dishes. The restaurant manager (the OS scheduler) must be *smart* about which chef gets which order. This is exactly how ARM's **big.LITTLE** works. > [!TIP] > **Memory hook:** *Homo* = "same," *Hetero* = "different." If cores look the same under the microscope → **Homogeneous**. If they look different → **Heterogeneous**. --- ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Board-Exam Highlights) - **TDP (Thermal Design Power):** The maximum **watts** of heat a chip is rated to dissipate under sustained load. Heterogeneous designs achieve lower aggregate **TDP** at equivalent performance. - **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture):** The contract between hardware and software (e.g., **x86-64**, **ARMv8-A**, **RISC-V**). - **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling):** Real-time adjustment of **V** (voltage) and **f** (frequency) per core. - **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle):** The architectural efficiency metric — heterogeneous systems typically have cores with **different IPC**. --- ## 1.4 Real-World Industry Examples | Processor | Type | Cores | |---|---|---| | **AMD Ryzen 9 7950X** | Homogeneous | 16 identical Zen 4 cores | | **Intel Core i9-13900K** | Heterogeneous | 8 P-cores + 16 E-cores (Raptor Lake) | | **ARM Cortex-A76** | Homogeneous block | 4 identical big cores (in some SoCs) | | **Samsung Exynos 2400** | Heterogeneous | 1 Xclipse + 10 ARM cores (mixed) | | **Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3** | Heterogeneous | 1 Prime + 5 Performance + 2 Efficiency cores | | **NVIDIA Grace CPU** | Homogeneous | 72 identical Neoverse V2 cores | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Homogeneous Multicore Processors — Operational Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Architecture - All **N cores** share the **same ISA** (e.g., all x86-64). - All cores have **identical microarchitecture**: same pipeline stages, same execution units, same L1/L2 cache sizes. - Typically share an **LLC (Last Level Cache)** via a coherent bus (e.g., ring, mesh, or NoC). - Operate under a **single clock domain** (or synchronized domains) and a **single OS image**. ### 2.1.2 Key Properties 1. **Symmetric performance** — any core can run any thread with equal speed. 2. **Uniform load balancing** — the OS scheduler (e.g., Linux CFS) can place any thread on any core without penalty. 3. **Cache coherency** is easier to maintain because protocols (MESI/MOESI) assume identical cache structures. 4. **Programming model is simple** — same binary runs on any core; the OS abstracts the hardware. ### 2.1.3 Advantages - Simple OS scheduler design. - Identical performance for any thread assignment → **trivial load balancing**. - Same binary code compatibility across all cores. - Easier debugging and performance modeling. ### 2.1.4 Disadvantages - **Power wall problem** — every core consumes the same power, even for lightweight threads. - **No specialization** — a single core design cannot be simultaneously optimized for integer arithmetic, SIMD, and low-power background tasks. - Poor energy efficiency under **mixed workloads**. --- ## 2.2 Heterogeneous Multicore Processors — Operational Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Architecture - Cores differ in **microarchitecture, performance, and power**. - **Common configurations:** - **big.LITTLE (ARM):** high-performance "big" cores (e.g., Cortex-A78) + energy-efficient "LITTLE" cores (e.g., Cortex-A55). - **Intel Hybrid (Alder Lake+):** P-cores (Performance) + E-cores (Efficiency). - **CPU + GPU + NPU (SoCs):** general-purpose CPU cores + specialized accelerators. - May share a unified **LLC** or have private caches connected by a **NoC (Network-on-Chip)**. ### 2.2.2 Key Properties 1. **Asymmetric performance** — a "big" core may be 2–3× faster than a "LITTLE" core for compute-bound work. 2. **Power asymmetry** — "LITTLE" cores can run at <1 W; "big" cores may exceed 5 W. 3. **Sophisticated scheduling required** — the OS/hardware must use **task-migration policies** (e.g., Global Task Scheduling in ARM, Intel Thread Director). 4. **ISA compatibility is mandatory** — all cores must execute the same instruction set (e.g., all ARMv8-A) so a single binary runs on any core. ### 2.2.3 Advantages - **Superior energy efficiency** — small threads run on small cores, saving power. - **Better performance-per-watt** for mixed workloads (mobile, edge). - **Workload specialization** — match thread to the right core. - Extends the **power wall** by trading off per-core performance. ### 2.2.4 Disadvantages - **Complex scheduler** — must know thread characteristics. - **Cache coherency** is harder (asymmetric cache hierarchies). - **Performance is non-deterministic** — same code may run at different speeds. - **Migration overhead** — moving a thread between core types costs cycles and cache lines. --- ## 2.3 Comparison Matrix — Board Exam Favorite ⭐ | Parameter | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | |---|---|---| | **Core identity** | Identical | Different | | **ISA** | Same | Same (mandatory for binary compat.) | | **Performance per core** | Uniform | Non-uniform | | **Power per core** | Uniform | Non-uniform (big vs LITTLE) | | **OS model** | SMP (Symmetric MP) | AMP (Asymmetric MP) / HMP | | **Scheduler complexity** | Low | High | | **Load balancing** | Trivial | Requires task-migration logic | | **Energy efficiency** | Moderate | High | | **Programming difficulty** | Low | Moderate–High | | **Examples** | AMD Ryzen, older Intel | ARM big.LITTLE, Intel 12th gen+, Qualcomm | | **Cache coherency** | Easier | More complex | | **Use case** | Servers, HPC, desktops | Mobile, edge, battery-powered devices | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following relationships are the standard metrics used to compare homogeneous vs heterogeneous designs in university problems. $$ \boxed{P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{big}}} P_{\text{big}, i} + \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{LITTLE}}} P_{\text{LITTLE}, j}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Performance} = \frac{1}{\text{Execution Time}} = \frac{\text{Instructions}}{(\text{CPI} \times T_{\text{cycle}})}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Energy} = P_{\text{avg}} \times t_{\text{exec}}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Performance per Watt} = \frac{\text{Performance}}{P_{\text{total}}}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Speedup}_{N} = \frac{T_{1}}{T_{N}} \quad \text{(Amdahl's Law bounded)}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Amdahl: } S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}} $$ Where: - $P_{\text{big}}$, $P_{\text{LITTLE}}$ = power of high-perf and efficiency cores - $T_1$ = single-core execution time, $T_N$ = N-core time - $f$ = parallel fraction of the workload, $N$ = number of cores - $CPI$ = cycles per instruction, $T_{\text{cycle}}$ = clock period --- ## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering - **Mobile SoCs (phones, tablets):** Heterogeneous designs extend battery life by routing background tasks (notifications, OS housekeeping) to LITTLE cores while reserving big cores for foreground apps. - **Data centers:** Homogeneous servers simplify hypervisor scheduling and maximize raw throughput per rack unit. - **Edge AI / IoT:** Heterogeneous SoCs pair CPU cores with **NPUs (Neural Processing Units)** for ML inference under strict power budgets. - **HPC (Supercomputers):** Homogeneous designs (e.g., AMD EPYC, Intel Xeon) dominate because scientific workloads are uniformly compute-intensive and parallel. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup Comparison Using Amdahl's Law ### Problem Setup A workload has **parallel fraction $f = 0.9$** and sequential fraction $(1-f) = 0.1$. We have two 4-core designs: - **Design A (Homogeneous):** 4 identical cores, each $1\times$ performance. - **Design B (Heterogeneous):** 2 big cores ($2\times$ performance) + 2 LITTLE cores ($0.5\times$ performance). Compute the speedup $S_A$ and $S_B$ over a single core of the same baseline. --- ### Step 1 — Establish the Amdahl Form For $N$ cores each with relative performance $\alpha_i$ (where $\alpha = 1$ is the baseline), the parallel execution time is: $$ T_{\text{parallel}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \frac{f \cdot T_1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i} $$ The **speedup** over single-core baseline is: $$ S = \frac{T_1}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{\sum \alpha_i}} $$ *This is the generalized Amdahl's Law for asymmetric multicore.* --- ### Step 2 — Compute for Design A (Homogeneous) For Design A: $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 = \alpha_4 = 1$. $$ \sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_i = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 $$ Plug into the speedup formula with $f = 0.9$: $$ S_A = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \dfrac{0.9}{4}} $$ $$ S_A = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.225} $$ $$ S_A = \frac{1}{0.325} = 3.077 $$ *Convert to two-decimal precision for KTU answers.* --- ### Step 3 — Compute for Design B (Heterogeneous) For Design B: 2 big cores at $\alpha = 2$, 2 LITTLE cores at $\alpha = 0.5$. $$ \sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_i = 2 + 2 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 5 $$ Plug into the speedup formula: $$ S_B = \frac{1}{0.1 + \dfrac{0.9}{5}} $$ $$ S_B = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.18} $$ $$ S_B = \frac{1}{0.28} = 3.571 $$ --- ### Step 4 — Analytical Comparison $$ \Delta S = S_B - S_A = 3.571 - 3.077 = 0.494 $$ $$ \% \text{ improvement} = \frac{0.494}{3.077} \times 100 \approx 16.06\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The heterogeneous design gives **~16% more speedup** than the homogeneous one for this $f=0.9$ workload, even though it uses *the same number of cores*. This is why heterogeneous multicore is the standard for mobile/edge. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Efficiency (Performance per Watt) ### Problem Setup Same two designs, with the following power characteristics: - **Design A (Homogeneous):** Each core uses **$P_A = 5$ W** when active. - **Design B (Heterogeneous):** Big core uses **$P_{\text{big}} = 8$ W**, LITTLE core uses **$P_{\text{LITTLE}} = 1$ W**. The same workload of $W = 10^{10}$ instructions runs on each, and we measure execution time. Compare **energy consumed**. --- ### Step 1 — Compute Total Power **Design A:** $$ P_{\text{total}, A} = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ W} $$ **Design B:** $$ P_{\text{total}, B} = 2 \times 8 + 2 \times 1 = 16 + 2 = 18 \text{ W} $$ --- ### Step 2 — Relate Performance to Time Assume each core runs at $1$ GIPS (Giga Instructions Per Second) per unit of $\alpha$. So the *aggregate throughput* is $\sum \alpha_i \times 1$ GIPS. **Design A throughput:** $4 \times 1 = 4$ GIPS → $T_A = \dfrac{10^{10}}{4 \times 10^9} = 2.5$ s **Design B throughput:** $5$ GIPS → $T_B = \dfrac{10^{10}}{5 \times 10^9} = 2.0$ s --- ### Step 3 — Compute Energy Energy is power times time: $$ E_A = P_{\text{total}, A} \times T_A = 20 \times 2.5 = 50 \text{ J} $$ $$ E_B = P_{\text{total}, B} \times T_B = 18 \times 2.0 = 36 \text{ J} $$ --- ### Step 4 — Energy Savings $$ \Delta E = E_A - E_B = 50 - 36 = 14 \text{ J} $$ $$ \% \text{ energy saved} = \frac{14}{50} \times 100 = 28\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The heterogeneous design is simultaneously **faster (3.571 vs 3.077 speedup)** and **more energy-efficient (28% less energy)**. This is the *dual-win* property that drives industry adoption of heterogeneous multicore. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Engineers | Workload Type | Recommended Architecture | Justification | |---|---|---| | HPC, scientific simulation | **Homogeneous** | Uniform parallel work, no power constraint | | Mobile / battery-powered | **Heterogeneous** | Power budget, mixed foreground/background tasks | | Cloud servers (latency-critical) | **Homogeneous** | Predictable scheduling, simple hypervisor | | Real-time embedded / automotive | **Heterogeneous** | Hard real-time tasks on big cores; sensor I/O on LITTLE | | AI/ML inference at edge | **Heterogeneous** (CPU + NPU) | NPU accelerates matrix ops; CPU handles control flow | | General desktop / laptop | **Hybrid (Hetero)** | Single-threaded bursts → P-cores; idle → E-cores | | Database transaction processing | **Homogeneous** | Highly threaded, uniform queries | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Homogeneous Multicore Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HOMO["HOMOGENEOUS MULTICORE - SMP"] direction TB C0["CORE 0
