
> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference:** ARM7 has a **single shared bus** → an instruction fetch cannot happen simultaneously with a data load/store (bus contention). ARM9's **separate I-bus and D-bus** (Harvard) → simultaneous instruction fetch + data access → higher throughput at the cost of an extra pin count.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define the term "Classic ARM processor" and list any four processors belonging to this category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Classic ARM processor** is a 32-bit RISC processor from ARM Holdings that implements the original ARM architecture (ARMv1 to ARMv6) before the introduction of the Cortex profile system. It is characterized by a **load-store architecture**, a **fixed 32-bit instruction width** (with optional 16-bit Thumb extension), a **16 × 32-bit register file**, and **banked registers for fast exception handling**.

Four Classic ARM processors are:
1. **ARM7TDMI** (1994, ARMv4T, 3-stage pipeline)
2. **ARM9TDMI** (1997, ARMv4T, 5-stage pipeline)
3. **ARM10E** (2000, ARMv5TE, 6-stage pipeline)
4. **ARM11** (2002, ARMv6, 8-stage pipeline)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Explain the term "Register Banking" in Classic ARM processors. Why is the FIQ mode given 5 extra banked registers compared to other privileged modes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Register Banking** in Classic ARM refers to the physical duplication of certain registers in the register file so that each privileged operating mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND) has its **own private copy** of R13 (Stack Pointer) and R14 (Link Register). This avoids the need to **push registers onto the stack** on exception entry, thereby reducing **interrupt latency**.

The **FIQ mode is given 5 extra banked registers (R8_fiq to R12_fiq)** so that a typical FIQ handler — which often performs **bulk data transfer** (e.g., zero-copy DMA-style data movement) — can run **without saving any registers at all** to the stack. This makes the FIQ the **fastest** ARM exception, with the lowest possible entry/exit overhead — an essential feature for **high-bandwidth, latency-critical** interrupt sources.

---

## 5.2 PART B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a) Describe the 3-stage pipeline of ARM7TDMI with a neat timing diagram. How is the 3-stage pipeline different from a non-pipelined processor? [7 marks]**

**(b) Compute the speedup of an ARM7TDMI-style 3-stage pipeline for executing 500 instructions versus a non-pipelined equivalent. Assume clock period = 25 ns. Also calculate the pipeline efficiency. [7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**The ARM7TDMI 3-Stage Pipeline:**

ARM7TDMI uses a **3-stage pipeline** to overlap instruction execution:

1. **Stage 1 – Fetch (F):** The instruction pointed to by the PC is fetched from memory. The PC is then incremented by 4 (ARM) or 2 (Thumb) to point to the next instruction.

2. **Stage 2 – Decode (D):** The fetched instruction is decoded. The **register operands** are read from the register bank. The **barrel shifter** is set up to compute the shifted operand (e.g., `R1 << 2`) for free in this cycle. Decoding and operand read happen in parallel.

3. **Stage 3 – Execute (E):** The ALU operates on the operands. For a data-processing instruction, the result is written back to the destination register. For a load/store, the memory address is computed and the bus cycle begins. For a branch, the new PC value is written to the PC (causing the pipeline to flush the next instruction if it was wrongly fetched).

**Timing Diagram:**

| Cycle → | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I1: ADD R1, R2, R3** | F | D | E |   |   |   |   |
| **I2: SUB R4, R5, R6** |   | F | D | E |   |   |   |
| **I3: LDR R7, [R8]**   |   |   | F | D | E+Mem | WB |   |
| **I4: STR R9, [R10]**  |   |   |   | F | D | E+Mem |   |

**Difference from Non-Pipelined Processor:**

| Aspect | Non-Pipelined | ARM7TDMI Pipelined |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Cycle time | Equal to longest stage | Equal to longest stage (same) |
| Instructions per cycle | $1/k$ (one every 3 cycles) | **1** (after pipeline fill) |
| Throughput | Low | High (3× more) |
| Hardware units | Single shared | **Three independent stages** running concurrently |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $N = 500$ instructions, $k = 3$ stages, $T_{\text{clk}} = 25 \, \text{ns}$.

**Step 1 — Non-pipelined execution time**

$$
T_{\text{no pipe}} = N \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{clk}} = 500 \cdot 3 \cdot 25 \, \text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{no pipe}} = 37{,}500 \, \text{ns} = 37.5 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined execution time**

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = \left[ k + (N - 1) \right] \cdot T_{\text{clk}} = \left[ 3 + 499 \right] \cdot 25 \, \text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = 502 \cdot 25 \, \text{ns} = 12{,}550 \, \text{ns} = 12.55 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{\text{no pipe}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{37{,}500}{12{,}550} = 2.988 \approx 2.99
$$

**Step 4 — Pipeline efficiency**

$$
E = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{2.988}{3} = 0.996 = 99.6\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

For $N = 500$, the pipeline delivers a speedup of **≈ 2.99×** with an efficiency of **99.6%**. This is very close to the theoretical maximum speedup of $k = 3$, because the fill/drain overhead of 3 cycles is amortized over 500 instructions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of the ARM7TDMI processor. Identify and explain the function of the Barrel Shifter. [7 marks]**

**(b) Compare the Classic ARM processor family (ARM7 vs ARM9 vs ARM11) based on pipeline depth, bus architecture, cache, and typical applications. [7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Internal Architecture of ARM7TDMI:**

The ARM7TDMI consists of the following interconnected units:

1. **Register Bank** – 16 × 32-bit registers, physically implemented as 2 read ports + 1 write port for two source operands + one destination operand per cycle.

2. **Barrel Shifter** – A combinational logic unit that performs **shifts and rotations** on the second ALU operand in a *single clock cycle*, without consuming an extra cycle. It supports LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, and RRX.

3. **ALU** – Performs 32-bit arithmetic (ADD, SUB, ADC, SBC, RSB, RSC, CMP, CMN, TST, TEQ, MUL), logic (AND, ORR, EOR, BIC), and bit manipulation (CLZ).

4. **Address Incrementer** – Computes memory addresses for LDR/STR; performs the auto-increment/decrement in load/store with indexing.

5. **Multiplier** – Implements the $32 \times 8 \to 40$-bit MAC operations in 1–4 cycles.

6. **Coprocessor Interface** – Provides a standard interface for external coprocessors (CP14 = Debug, CP15 = System Control / MMU).

7. **Instruction Decoder & Control** – Generates all datapath control signals.

**The Barrel Shifter in Detail:**

The **barrel shifter** is one of the most distinctive features of Classic ARM. Unlike most RISC processors (e.g., MIPS) where a shift must be performed in a separate instruction, the ARM's barrel shifter allows a shift to be specified *as part of any data-processing instruction* without cost. For example:

```asm
ADD R3, R2, R1, LSL #4     ; R3 = R2 + (R1 << 4)   -- in 1 cycle!
```

The barrel shifter operates in parallel with the register read in the **Decode stage**, so the shifted operand is available at the ALU input by the time the Execute stage begins. This is the key reason ARM code is so **dense and efficient** — many operations that require multiple instructions on other RISC ISAs are a single instruction on ARM.

**Applications of the barrel shifter**:
- Bit-field extraction (e.g., `(R1 >> 8) & 0xFF`).
- Address computation (e.g., `STR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` for array indexing).
- Multiplication by powers of 2 (LSL is essentially a free multiply).
- CRC computation (XOR with rotated constant).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Comparison Table – Classic ARM Family:**

| Feature | **ARM7TDMI** | **ARM9TDMI** | **ARM11** |
|---------|--------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Architecture Version** | ARMv4T | ARMv4T | ARMv6 |
| **Pipeline Depth (k)** | 3 stages | 5 stages | 8 stages |
| **Bus Architecture** | Von Neumann (single I/D bus) | **Harvard** (separate I and D buses) | Harvard + AMBA 3 AXI bus |
| **Cache** | None on-chip (external memory only) | Separate **I-cache and D-cache** (typ. 16 KB each) | L1 I-cache + L1 D-cache, optionally L2 |
| **Branch Prediction** | None (1-cycle penalty on taken) | None (2-cycle penalty) | Static + Dynamic branch prediction |
| **Typical Clock Frequency** | 50–80 MHz | 150–220 MHz | 300–500 MHz |
| **Thumb Support** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **SIMD / Media Instructions** | No | No (ARM9E adds DSP) | Yes (ARMv6 SIMD) |
| **Typical Application** | Mobile phones (Nokia 3310), HDD controllers, toys | Smartphones (early PDAs), MP3 players | Early smartphones (iPhone 2G/3G), set-top boxes |
| **Power** | 0.6 mW/MHz | 0.9 mW/MHz | 1.2 mW/MHz |
| **Year** | 1994 | 1997 | 2002 |

**Key Takeaways:**

1. **Pipeline depth trade-off:** Deeper pipelines (ARM11 = 8) achieve higher clock frequencies but suffer larger branch penalties. ARM7's 3-stage pipeline has the lowest branch penalty (1 cycle) but the lowest clock rate.

2. **Bus architecture evolution:** ARM7's Von Neumann bus causes contention (an instruction fetch and a data load cannot happen simultaneously). ARM9's Harvard bus with separate caches eliminates this bottleneck.

3. **Cache integration:** ARM7 relies on external memory (slow off-chip access). ARM9 integrates caches for **first-cycle memory access** and **higher hit rates**. ARM11 adds branch prediction to reduce the cost of deeper pipelining.

4. **Application evolution:** As tasks grew from simple control (ARM7) to multimedia-rich smartphone UI (ARM11), the architecture added media extensions, deeper pipelines, and branch prediction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Classic ARM Questions – KTU Examiner's Pitfall List:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ARM7 pipeline depth:** Writing "2-stage" or "5-stage" for ARM7TDMI. **Correct = 3-stage**. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 2. **Wrong branch penalty for ARM7:** Writing "2-cycle branch penalty" for ARM7TDMI. **Correct = 1 cycle** (since branch resolves in Execute, and the next instruction is in Fetch → 1 instruction must be flushed).
>
> 3. **Ignoring fill/drain in speedup calculation:** Writing $S = k$ directly without computing $T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + N - 1) \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$. **Always** show the formula. Marks lost: 2.
>
> 4. **Counting all ARM instructions as 1-cycle:** `LDR` takes **3 cycles** (1 internal + 1 external I + 1 external N), `STR` takes **2 cycles** (1 internal + 1 external I). Treating these as 1-cycle loses 2–3 marks in code-cycle-counting questions.
>
> 5. **Saying "ARM = 8 modes":** Classic ARM has **7 modes** (not 8). The 8th mode (Monitor) is **Cortex-A only**. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 6. **Confusing CPSR with SPSR:** SPSR exists **only in privileged modes** (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND), one per mode. User/System mode do **not** have an SPSR. Students often write "SPSR is in all modes" — wrong.
>
> 7. **Forgetting that FIQ has 5 extra banked registers:** R8_fiq, R9_fiq, R10_fiq, R11_fiq, R12_fiq — in addition to the standard R13_fiq and R14_fiq. Only listing R13_fiq and R14_fiq loses 1 mark.
>
> 8. **Treating "Banked" as "Saved on stack":** Banked = **physical hardware duplication** in the register file. It is **not** a software save. Marks lost: 1.

## 5.4 Topic Recap – Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Top 25 Must-Remember Points for KTU 2024 ESE on Classic ARM:**

- [ ] **Classic ARM** = ARMv1 to ARMv6; **Cortex** = ARMv7 onwards.
- [ ] Classic ARM is a **32-bit RISC, Load-Store architecture** with **16 × 32-bit visible registers**.
- [ ] The **most important** Classic ARM is **ARM7TDMI** (ARMv4T, 1994, 3-stage pipeline).
- [ ] ARM7TDMI uses a **3-stage pipeline**: **F (Fetch) → D (Decode) → E (Execute)**.
- [ ] **ARM9TDMI** = 5-stage pipeline; **ARM10E** = 6-stage; **ARM11** = 8-stage.
- [ ] ARM7 uses a **Von Neumann** bus (single I/D); ARM9 uses **Harvard** (separate I/D).
- [ ] **R15 = PC** (Program Counter); **R14 = LR** (Link Register); **R13 = SP** (Stack Pointer).
- [ ] Classic ARM has **7 operating modes**: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System.
- [ ] **FIQ** has 7 banked registers (R8_fiq to R14_fiq); other privileged modes have 2 (R13, R14).
- [ ] **CPSR** = Current Program Status Register; **SPSR** = Saved PSR (one per privileged mode, not in User/System).
- [ ] CPSR bit fields: **N** (Negative), **Z** (Zero), **C** (Carry), **V** (Overflow), **I** (IRQ disable), **F** (FIQ disable), **T** (Thumb state), **M[4:0]** (Mode).
- [ ] **T-bit = 0** → ARM state (32-bit instructions); **T-bit = 1** → Thumb state (16-bit instructions).
- [ ] **Thumb** was introduced in **ARM7TDMI** (the 'T' in TDMI); it reduces code size by ~30%.
- [ ] **TDMI** suffix: **T** = Thumb, **D** = Debug, **M** = fast Multiplier, **I** = EmbeddedICE debug.
- [ ] **Pipeline speedup formula:** $S = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + (N - 1)}$
- [ ] **Pipeline efficiency:** $E = \dfrac{S}{k}$
- [ ] As $N \to \infty$, $S \to k$ (theoretical maximum).
- [ ] **Pipelined execution time:** $T_{\text{pipe}} = \left[ k + (N - 1) \right] \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$
- [ ] **Non-pipelined execution time:** $T_{\text{no pipe}} = N \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$
- [ ] **Branch penalty** for ARM7 = 1 cycle (resolved in E stage).
- [ ] **LDR** = 3 cycles (1 internal + 1 I-cycle + 1 N-cycle); **STR** = 2 cycles (1 internal + 1 I-cycle).
- [ ] **Barrel Shifter** performs shifts/rotations for **free** in the Decode stage; supports LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX.
- [ ] ARM is **bi-endian** (configurable via bit in CP15) and supports **Big-Endian / Little-Endian** for both data and instructions.
- [ ] ARM7 = **0.6 mW/MHz** (very low power) — key reason for its dominance in **mobile and embedded** systems.
- [ ] Modern Cortex cores **inherited** the **basic programmer's model** (R0–R15, CPSR/SPSR, 7 modes) directly from Classic ARM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Cortex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Cortex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **ARM Cortex** is a family of **32-bit / 64-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** processor cores designed by **ARM Holdings (now Arm Ltd.)** and licensed to a wide ecosystem of semiconductor manufacturers. Within the KTU 2024 scheme curriculum for *Advanced Microprocessors and Microcontrollers*, the term **ARM Cortex** specifically refers to the **ARM Cortex-M profile**, a microcontroller-optimized processor core that uses the **ARMv6-M (Cortex-M0/M0+), ARMv7-M (Cortex-M3/M4/M7), or ARMv8-M (Cortex-M23/M33)** architecture variants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition:** The ARM Cortex-M is a *load-store*, *von Neumann* (Cortex-M0/M0+) or *Harvard* (Cortex-M3/M4/M7) architecture RISC processor that executes **Thumb / Thumb-2** instruction set and integrates a configurable **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** for deterministic real-time embedded response.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Office Manager"

Imagine a busy office:
- The **ARM Cortex core** is the **office manager** who follows a strict, simple rule-book (the instruction set).
- The **registers** are the manager's **personal notepad** — extremely fast, but only a few pages.
- The **ALU** is the **calculator on the desk** — does math.
- The **bus (AHB/APB)** is the **corridor** connecting the manager to the filing cabinets (memory) and printers (peripherals).
- The **NVIC** is the **intercom system** that interrupts the manager immediately when an urgent call comes in (e.g., a fire alarm) — and the manager knows exactly which fire (interrupt vector) to deal with.

The genius of ARM is that the rule-book is **small, uniform, and predictable**, so the manager processes every instruction in exactly the same number of clock ticks — making it perfect for low-power, real-time embedded systems.

### 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics for KTU Reference

| Metric | Standard Value (KTU Exam-Ready) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Native Data Width** | **32 bits** |
| **Address Space** | **4 GB (2³² bytes)** for Cortex-M3/M4 |
| **General Purpose Registers** | **R0 – R12** (13 registers) |
| **Special Registers** | **R13 (SP), R14 (LR), R15 (PC)** |
| **Instruction Set** | **Thumb (16-bit) and Thumb-2 (mixed 16/32-bit)** |
| **Operating Modes** | **Thread mode + Handler mode** |
| **Privilege Levels** | **Privileged and Unprivileged (User)** |
| **Endianness** | **Little-endian (default), Byte-invariant big-endian selectable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike classic ARM7/ARM9 which have **7 operating modes** (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System), the **Cortex-M family simplifies this to only TWO modes** — *Thread mode* (normal code) and *Handler mode* (exception/interrupt service). This is a recurring **2-mark question**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Philosophy of ARM Cortex-M

ARM Cortex-M is engineered around **three pillars** that distinguish it from classical ARM cores and from complex CISC processors (e.g., Intel x86):

### Pillar 1 — Load/Store Architecture
- **Definition:** Data-processing instructions operate **only on registers**. They can *never* read/write memory directly.
- To load data from memory, a separate **LDR** (Load Register) instruction is used.
- To store data, a separate **STR** (Store Register) instruction is used.
- **Why?** Decouples computation from memory access, enabling the pipeline to schedule memory operations in parallel with arithmetic.

### Pillar 2 — Fixed-Length / Thumb-2 Encoding
- **Thumb-1** (used in ARM7TDMI): 16-bit instructions only — dense but limited.
- **Thumb-2** (used in Cortex-M3/M4/M7): a **mix of 16-bit and 32-bit instructions**, no extra mode-switch overhead.
- **Result:** Code density comparable to 8-bit MCUs, with performance comparable to 32-bit ARM.

### Pillar 3 — Deterministic Interrupt Latency
- The **NVIC** is tightly coupled to the core.
- Interrupt entry takes a **fixed number of clock cycles** (e.g., 12 cycles on Cortex-M3 from event to first instruction of ISR).
- **Tail-chaining** allows back-to-back interrupts with only **6 cycles** of overhead.

## 2.2 The Register Bank — The Heart of the Cortex Core

The Cortex-M core has the following register organization:

| Register Group | Registers | Width | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low Registers** | R0 – R7 | 32-bit | General-purpose, accessible by all instructions |
| **High Registers** | R8 – R12 | 32-bit | General-purpose, some 16-bit Thumb instructions can only access R0–R7 |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | R13 (two banks) | 32-bit | **MSP** (Main SP) for kernel; **PSP** (Process SP) for user tasks |
| **Link Register (LR)** | R14 | 32-bit | Stores the return address on function call (BL/BLX) |
| **Program Counter (PC)** | R15 | 32-bit | Points to the next instruction (address + 4 in ARM, but Thumb uses +2 step) |
| **Program Status Registers** | xPSR, APSR, IPSR, EPSR | 32-bit | Condition flags, interrupt number, execution state |
| **Control Register** | CONTROL | 32-bit | Selects SP (MSP/PSP) and privilege level |
| **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI** | — | 32-bit | Interrupt masking registers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2-Mark Favourite:** "Differentiate MSP and PSP."  
> - **MSP (Main Stack Pointer):** Default stack pointer; used in **Handler mode** and **Thread mode with privileged access**. Holds kernel/OS stack frames.  
> - **PSP (Process Stack Pointer):** Used in **Thread mode only**; typically assigned to user application tasks under an RTOS for memory protection isolation.

## 2.3 Memory Map of Cortex-M (4 GB Address Space)

The 32-bit address bus divides the **4 GB** addressable memory into the following vendor-defined regions:

| Address Range | Region | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **0x0000 0000 – 0x1FFF FFFF** | **Code (Flash)** | Program memory, alias for boot |
| **0x2000 0000 – 0x3FFF FFFF** | **SRAM** | Data memory, heap, stack |
| **0x4000 0000 – 0x5FFF FFFF** | **Peripherals** | On-chip peripherals (UART, GPIO, Timer, ADC) |
| **0x6000 0000 – 0x9FFF FFFF** | **External RAM** | External memory (if any) |
| **0xA000 0000 – 0xDFFF FFFF** | **External Devices** | External peripherals |
| **0xE000 0000 – 0xE00F FFFF** | **System Control Space** | NVIC, SysTick, MPU, FPU control |
| **0xF000 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF** | **System** | Vendor-specific system registers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Bit-Banded" Regions?** In Cortex-M3/M4, the first 1 MB of SRAM and the first 1 MB of peripheral region are **bit-band aliases**. Writing to a bit-band alias atomically sets/resets a single bit — useful for **lock-free** flag manipulation in RTOS environments.

## 2.4 Operating Modes & Privilege Levels — The Security Model

```
                ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                │      CONTROL Register        │
                │  [nPRIV] [SPSEL]            │
                └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                               │
       ┌───────────────────────┴────────────────────────┐
       │                                                │
  SPSEL=0  → MSP (Main SP)                  SPSEL=1 → PSP (Process SP)
  nPRIV=0  → Privileged (full access)       nPRIV=1 → Unprivileged
       │                                                │
       └────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                    │
        ┌───────────┴────────────┐
        │   Two Modes:           │
        │  • Thread Mode        │
        │  • Handler Mode       │
        └────────────────────────┘
```

- **Thread Mode:** Where normal application code executes. Privilege can be Privileged or Unprivileged. Both MSP and PSP can be active.
- **Handler Mode:** Entered automatically upon exception/interrupt or reset. **Always Privileged**. SP is **always MSP**. Cannot be entered via software.

## 2.5 The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)

The **NVIC** is the cornerstone of real-time behaviour in Cortex-M:

| NVIC Feature | Engineering Benefit |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Nested interrupts** | Higher-priority IRQ can preempt lower-priority IRQ |
| **Vectored entry** | CPU fetches ISR address directly from vector table — no software polling |
| **Dynamic priority** | Priorities can be changed at runtime |
| **Tail-chaining** | Saves up to **6 cycles** vs. legacy ARM7 |
| **Late-arrival** | Higher-priority IRQ arriving during stacking can be serviced first |
| **Interrupt latency** | **12 cycles** worst case on Cortex-M3 (deterministic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Formula — Interrupt Latency:**  
> $$\text{Total Interrupt Latency} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{stack}} + T_{\text{vector fetch}} + T_{\text{ISR entry}}$$  
> On Cortex-M3: $T_{\text{latency}} = 12 \text{ cycles} \times T_{\text{clk}}$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Addressable Memory | $2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}$ | Maximum memory map size |
| Bit-Band Address | $\text{alias} = 0x22000000 + (byte\_addr - 0x20000000) \times 32 + bit \times 4$ | Atomic bit manipulation |
| Interrupt Latency | $L = N \times T_{\text{clk}}, \quad N=12 \text{ (Cortex-M3)}$ | Real-time budget analysis |
| Power (Dynamic) | $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Low-power design trade-off |
| Pipeline Stages (M3) | 3 stages: Fetch, Decode, Execute | CPI = 1 in best case |
| Vector Table Base | $\text{VTOR} \rightarrow 0xE000ED08$ | Relocate vector table to RAM/Flash |
| NVIC Priority Bits | **3 to 8 bits** (configurable per vendor) | Number of priority levels = $2^{N}$ |
| Stack Alignment | **8-byte aligned** at function entry (AAPCS) | Hardware enforces on Cortex-M3+ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stack Behaviour During Exception Entry on Cortex-M3

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Demonstrate the order in which registers are pushed onto the stack when the Cortex-M3 processor takes an exception, and derive the total stack frame size for a given configuration.

### Step 1 — Identify the Trigger
The CPU detects an exception request (e.g., IRQ from a peripheral). It then completes the current instruction, then begins **stacking**.

### Step 2 — Hardware-Automatic Stacking Sequence
The Cortex-M3 hardware automatically pushes the following **8 registers** onto the **active stack pointer's memory region** in the order shown:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Push Order (highest address first):} \quad & \\
& xPSR \\
& PC \\
& LR \\
& R12 \\
& R3 \\
& R2 \\
& R1 \\
& R0
\end{aligned}
$$

This block consumes **8 words × 4 bytes/word = 32 bytes** of stack.

### Step 3 — Vector Fetch
After stacking, the CPU reads the **ISR vector address** from the vector table (offset 16 × IRQ number). It loads this into the **PC** and simultaneously updates **LR** to a special value `EXC_RETURN = 0xFFFF_FFFD` (for MSP-based return).

### Step 4 — Stack Pointer Updates
Because stacking is *descending* (full-descending stack on ARM), the SP is decremented by 32 bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{\text{new}} &= SP_{\text{old}} - 32 \\
SP_{\text{old}} - 32 &\rightarrow \text{contains } R0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Derivation of Total Stack Size During Nested Exceptions
If **N** nested exceptions are active simultaneously, the maximum stack consumed is:

$$
S_{\text{max}} = N \times 32 \text{ bytes} + S_{\text{user\_stack}}
$$

Where $S_{\text{user\_stack}}$ is the working stack of the interrupted program (function calls, local variables).

### Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)
> *"If a Cortex-M3 system services a UART IRQ, which is preempted by a higher-priority ADC IRQ, and the original application had allocated 256 bytes of stack, what is the **total** stack depth required to safely avoid overflow?"*

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{max}} &= 2 \times 32 + 256 \\
&= 64 + 256 \\
&= 320 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** **320 bytes** of stack must be reserved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting that the hardware pushes **xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0** — the order is *not* R0–R12. The vector fetch happens *after* the stack push. Drawing the stack pointer moving *down* (descending) is mandatory in KTU diagrams.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cortex-M Family Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| Feature | **Cortex-M0** | **Cortex-M0+** | **Cortex-M3** | **Cortex-M4** | **Cortex-M7** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture** | ARMv6-M | ARMv6-M | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M |
| **Pipeline** | 3-stage | 2-stage | 3-stage | 3-stage | 6-stage dual-issue |
| **Thumb-2** | Subset | Subset | Full | Full | Full |
| **HW Multiply** | ✓ (1 cycle) | ✓ (1 cycle) | ✓ (32-bit) | ✓ (32-bit) | ✓ (dual 32×32) |
| **HW Divide** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **FPU** | ✗ | ✗ | Optional (single) | **✓ Single Precision** | **✓ Double Precision** |
| **DSP Extensions** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | **✓ MAC, SIMD** | **✓ Advanced SIMD** |
| **MPU** | ✗ | Optional (8) | Optional (8) | Optional (8) | Optional (16) |
| **Interrupt Lines** | 1–32 | 1–32 | 1–240 | 1–240 | 1–240 |
| **Typical Use** | Sensors, IoT | Energy-harvesting | Industrial control | Motor control, audio | High-end DSP, IoT gateways |

## 3.3 Derivation: Bit-Band Alias Address Formula

The **bit-band region** allows atomic single-bit access. For SRAM bit-band:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bitband\_word\_addr} &= 0x22000000 + (\text{byte\_addr} - 0x20000000) \times 32 + \text{bit\_num} \times 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:** Set bit 5 of the byte located at `0x2000_0100` atomically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bitband\_alias} &= 0x22000000 + (0x20000100 - 0x20000000) \times 32 + 5 \times 4 \\
&= 0x22000000 + 0x100 \times 32 + 20 \\
&= 0x22000000 + 0x2000 + 0x14 \\
&= 0x22002014
\end{aligned}
$$

Writing a `1` to address `0x22002014` will atomically set bit 5 of `0x20000100` without a read-modify-write sequence. This is **essential for RTOS flag operations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cortex-M3 Internal Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Code Bus ICode 32-bit"] --> F["INSTRUCTION FETCH UNIT"]
    B["System Bus SCode"] --> F
    F --> D["DECODE STAGE"]
    D --> EX["EXECUTE STAGE WITH ALU"]
    EX --> W["WRITE BACK TO REGISTER BANK"]
    
    EX --> MUX1["AHB-Lite BUS MATRIX"]
    MUX1 --> DM["DATA SRAM 0x20000000"]
    MUX1 --> PER["PERIPHERAL BUS 0x40000000"]
    
    NVIC["NVIC PRIORITY LOGIC"] --> EX
    NVIC --> VT["VECTOR TABLE READ"]
    VT --> F
    
    W --> REGBANK["REGISTER BANK R0-R12, SP, LR, PC, xPSR"]
    
    MPU["MEMORY PROTECTION UNIT OPTIONAL"] --> MUX1
    FPU["FPU SINGLE PRECISION OPTIONAL"] --> EX
    SYST["SYSTICK TIMER"] --> NVIC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The diagram above is the *expected* block-level representation for a 7-mark "Draw and explain the architecture of ARM Cortex-M3" question. Always label the **ICode**, **DCode**, **System bus**, and **NVIC** separately.

## 4.2 Exception Entry and Exit Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Normal Program Execution Thread Mode"] --> DETECT{"Exception Detected NVIC Higher Priority"}
    DETECT -- YES --> STACK["Hardware Stacking 8 Registers onto Active SP 32 bytes"]
    DETECT -- NO --> START
    STACK --> VFETCH["Vector Fetch from Vector Table offset = 4 x IRQ_Number + 16"]
    VFETCH --> PC_UPDATE["Load PC with ISR Address LR = EXC_RETURN 0xFFFFFFFx"]
    PC_UPDATE --> HANDLER["Handler Mode Execute Interrupt Service Routine"]
    HANDLER --> RETURN{"Return Instruction BX LR or POP PC with EXC_RETURN"}
    RETURN -- YES --> UNSTACK["Hardware Unstacking 8 Registers in reverse order"]
    UNSTACK --> RESUME["Resume Interrupted Program Thread Mode"]
    RESUME --> START
```

## 4.3 Memory Map of Cortex-M (4 GB Linear Space)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["0x00000000\n512 MB\nCODE FLASH"] --> A1["0x20000000\n512 MB\nSRAM BIT-BAND ALIAS\n0x22000000"]
    A1 --> A2["0x40000000\n512 MB\nPERIPHERALS\nBIT-BAND 0x42000000"]
    A2 --> A3["0x60000000\n1 GB\nEXTERNAL RAM"]
    A3 --> A4["0xA0000000\n1 GB\nEXTERNAL DEVICES"]
    A4 --> A5["0xE0000000\n0.5 GB\nSYSTEM CONTROL NVIC, MPU, SYSTICK"]
    A5 --> A6["0xF0000000\n0.25 GB\nVENDOR SYSTEM"]
```

## 4.4 Register Bank Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0["R0 32-bit Low Register General Purpose"] 
    R1["R1 32-bit Low Register"]
    R2["R2 32-bit Low Register"]
    R3["R3 32-bit Low Register"]
    R4["R4 32-bit Low Register"]
    R5["R5 32-bit Low Register"]
    R6["R6 32-bit Low Register"]
    R7["R7 32-bit Low Register"]
    R8["R8 32-bit High Register"]
    R9["R9 32-bit High Register"]
    R10["R10 32-bit High Register"]
    R11["R11 32-bit High Register"]
    R12["R12 32-bit High Register"]
    SP["R13 STACK POINTER MSP / PSP"]
    LR["R14 LINK REGISTER"]
    PC["R15 PROGRAM COUNTER"]
    PSR["xPSR APSR IPSR EPSR COMBINED"]
    CTRL["CONTROL REGISTER SPSEL nPRIV"]
    MASK["PRIMASK FAULTMASK BASEPRI"]
    
    R0 --- SP
    R1 --- LR
    R2 --- PC
    R3 --- PSR
    R4 --- CTRL
    R5 --- MASK
    R6 --- R7
    R7 --- R8
    R8 --- R9
    R9 --- R10
    R10 --- R11
    R11 --- R12
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**"List any six features of the ARM Cortex-M3 processor."**

**Model Answer (6 points):**
1. **32-bit RISC architecture** with Thumb-2 instruction set.
2. **Three-stage pipeline** (Fetch, Decode, Execute) for high instruction throughput.
3. **Integrated NVIC** with deterministic 12-cycle interrupt latency and tail-chaining.
4. **Harvard bus architecture** — separate I-Code and D-Code buses for simultaneous fetch and data access.
5. **4 GB linear address space** with bit-band regions for atomic bit manipulation.
6. **Optional MPU** for memory protection in RTOS environments.
7. **Single-cycle 32-bit multiplication** and hardware division.
8. **Wakeup Interrupt Controller (WIC)** support for ultra-low-power sleep modes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse Cortex-M3 with Cortex-M0 features. **Cortex-M0 has NO hardware divide and NO MPU**. Writing "M3 supports hardware divide" is correct — for M0, it is **incorrect**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]  
**"Differentiate between MSP and PSP in ARM Cortex-M3."**

**Model Answer (Tabular Format):**

| Aspect | **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** | **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default** | Default SP on reset | Optional, set via CONTROL register |
| **Used in** | Handler mode + Privileged Thread mode | Thread mode (Unprivileged or Privileged) |
| **Purpose** | OS kernel stack, exception stacking | Application task stacks in RTOS |
| **CONTROL.SPSEL** | When SPSEL = 0 | When SPSEL = 1 |
| **Selection** | Automatic in Handler mode | Programmer-controlled in Thread mode |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & NVIC Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of ARM Cortex-M3 and explain the function of each major block. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**. List at least **four features** of NVIC. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid schematic).**

**Functional Explanation of Blocks:**

1. **Instruction Fetch Unit (IFU):** Fetches 32-bit aligned instructions from the Code region (0x00000000) via the I-Code bus. Pre-fetches instructions into a small buffer to hide flash latency.

2. **Decode Stage:** Decodes Thumb-2 instructions (16-bit or 32-bit). Identifies data-processing, load/store, branch, and control instructions.

3. **Execute Stage + ALU:** Performs arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`), logic (`AND`, `ORR`), shift, and saturation operations in a single cycle (in best case).

4. **Register Bank:** 13 general-purpose 32-bit registers (R0–R12) plus SP, LR, PC, and xPSR. SP has two physical banks (MSP, PSP).

5. **AHB-Lite Bus Matrix:** Routes data accesses to either SRAM (DCode bus) or peripherals (System bus) without contention with instruction fetch.

6. **NVIC:** Receives interrupt requests from peripherals, arbitrates by priority, and signals the core. Integrates with vector table for direct ISR addressing.

7. **MPU (Optional):** Enforces 8 region-based access permissions for RTOS memory protection.

8. **FPU (Optional on M3):** Single-precision IEEE-754 floating-point unit.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**NVIC Working (Refer to flowchart in Section 4.2):**

1. Peripheral asserts an **IRQ line** (e.g., line 5 for TIM2).
2. The NVIC **compares priority** with the current execution priority.
3. If new IRQ is higher, NVIC **preempts** current execution.
4. CPU **completes current instruction**, then begins automatic stacking of 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) — 32 bytes.
5. NVIC **fetches the vector address** from vector table:  
$$\text{VectorAddress} = \text{VTOR} + 4 \times (\text{IRQ Number} + 16)$$
6. The **PC is loaded with ISR address**, and execution begins in **Handler mode** (always Privileged, uses MSP).
7. ISR runs to completion. On the last instruction, **`BX LR`** or `POP {PC}` is executed where LR = `EXC_RETURN` (`0xFFFF_FFFD` or `0xFFFF_FFFD`).
8. CPU performs **hardware unstacking** in reverse order, restoring R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR.
9. **Pre-empted program resumes** in Thread mode from where it was interrupted.

**Four Features of NVIC:**
1. **Configurable priority levels (3 to 8 bits, vendor-dependent)** — up to 256 levels.
2. **Nested interrupt support** — higher-priority IRQ can preempt lower-priority ISR.
3. **Tail-chaining** — reduces back-to-back interrupt latency to ~6 cycles.
4. **Vectored interrupts** — direct ISR fetch, no software vector decoding required.
5. **Dynamic priority reconfiguration** at runtime.
6. **Interrupt masking** via PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI registers.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **operating modes** of ARM Cortex-M3 with the help of a **mode transition diagram**. What is the role of the **CONTROL register**? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compare the architectural features of **Cortex-M3 and Cortex-M4** processors. Justify why M4 is preferred for **DSP applications**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Operating Modes (Only TWO in Cortex-M3):**

1. **Thread Mode:**
   - Normal application execution.
   - Can be **Privileged** or **Unprivileged**.
   - Can use **MSP or PSP** based on `CONTROL.SPSEL`.

2. **Handler Mode:**
   - Entered automatically on **reset, NMI, HardFault, or any IRQ**.
   - **Always Privileged**.
   - **Always uses MSP**.

**Mode Transition Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset_Handler
    Reset_Handler --> Handler_Mode: Reset vector fetched MSP active
    Handler_Mode --> Thread_Mode: Return from main Exception EXC_RETURN
    Thread_Mode --> Handler_Mode: Interrupt or Exception detected
    Thread_Mode --> Thread_Mode: CONTROL register toggle SPSEL or nPRIV
    Handler_Mode --> Handler_Mode: Nested IRQ higher priority
```

**Role of the CONTROL Register (3 bits used, 1 bit reserved):**

| Bit | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bit 0** | **nPRIV** | 0 = Privileged in Thread mode; 1 = Unprivileged in Thread mode |
| **Bit 1** | **SPSEL** | 0 = Use MSP as current SP; 1 = Use PSP as current SP (Thread mode only) |
| **Bit 2** | **FPCA** | (M4F only) 1 = Lazy-stacking enabled for FPU |

> **Key Note:** CONTROL can **only be modified in Privileged mode**. Writing to it from Unprivileged code is silently ignored (or causes a HardFault, depending on configuration).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (excerpt — full table in Section 3.2):**

| Feature | Cortex-M3 | Cortex-M4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DSP Extensions** | ✗ None | **✓ MAC, SIMD, saturating arithmetic** |
| **FPU** | Optional (single-precision) | **Built-in single-precision FPU (M4F)** |
| **Multiply** | 32-bit single-cycle | 32-bit single-cycle, plus **dual 16-bit MAC** |
| **Pipeline** | 3-stage | 3-stage (same) |
| **Interrupt Latency** | 12 cycles | 12 cycles |
| **Power** | Lower (no DSP) | Slightly higher |

**Why Cortex-M4 is Preferred for DSP Applications (Justification):**

1. **Single-Cycle MAC Instruction:** The M4 has `MAC`, `MLS`, `SMMLA`, `SMMLS` instructions that perform **Multiply-Accumulate in one cycle** — the core operation of FIR/IIR digital filters.

2. **SIMD Instructions:** Packs two 16-bit operations into a single 32-bit instruction — e.g., `ADD16`, `SUB16` — doubling throughput for audio/video processing.

3. **Hardware FPU:** Performs single-precision floating-point operations in hardware, eliminating software emulation overhead. Critical for **adaptive filters** and **FFT**.

4. **Saturating Arithmetic:** `SSAT` and `USAT` instructions clamp results to fixed limits, preventing overflow wrap-around — essential in audio codecs.

5. **Example FIR Filter Operation:**  
$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]
$$
A single `SMLAL` (Signed Multiply-Accumulate Long) on M4 performs 2 multiply-accumulates per cycle vs. ~10 cycles emulated in software on M3.

6. **Reduced Code Size & Power:** DSP loops execute faster → CPU sleeps more → **lower average power**, a critical factor in **battery-powered IoT audio devices**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Triggers in ARM Cortex Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Cortex-M with classic ARM (ARM7/ARM9):** Writing "7 operating modes" for Cortex-M3 — **wrong**. Only 2 modes.
> 2. **Forgetting the EXC_RETURN mechanism:** The LR does not store a return address in Handler mode — it stores the special EXC_RETURN value (`0xFFFF_FFFD`). Students writing `LR = return address` lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Mis-stating interrupt latency:** Cortex-M3 = **12 cycles**, not 20 or 30.
> 4. **Confusing MSP and PSP:** PSP is for **user tasks under RTOS**, not for exceptions. Exceptions always use MSP.
> 5. **Drawing Thumb-2 as 32-bit only:** Thumb-2 is **mixed 16/32-bit**; this is the entire point of its existence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ARM Cortex-M is a 32-bit RISC core** using **Thumb-2** instruction set.
- ✅ **Two operating modes only**: **Thread** (privileged/unprivileged, MSP/PSP) and **Handler** (always privileged, always MSP).
- ✅ **Register set:** R0–R12 (GPR), R13 (SP), R14 (LR), R15 (PC), xPSR, CONTROL, PRIMASK/FAULTMASK/BASEPRI.
- ✅ **4 GB address space** divided into Code, SRAM, Peripheral, External, System regions.
- ✅ **NVIC** provides **deterministic 12-cycle** interrupt latency with **tail-chaining** and **late-arrival** features.
- ✅ **Vector table** is at address `0x00000000` initially; can be relocated via **VTOR** (`0xE000ED08`).
- ✅ **Bit-banding** allows atomic single-bit access in 1 MB SRAM + 1 MB peripheral regions.
- ✅ **MSP** = kernel stack, **PSP** = task stack (RTOS).
- ✅ **Stacking order** on exception: xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0 (8 regs = 32 bytes).
- ✅ **Cortex-M4 adds:** FPU (single-precision), DSP extensions (MAC, SIMD), saturating arithmetic — preferred for **DSP, motor control, audio**.
- ✅ **Cortex-M0/M0+** are **ARMv6-M**, simplest and lowest power; **M3/M4/M7** are **ARMv7-M**.
- ✅ **Cortex-M7** has 6-stage dual-issue pipeline and double-precision FPU.
- ✅ **EXC_RETURN value** is `0xFFFF_FFFD` (MSP-based return) — stored in LR on exception entry.
- ✅ **Interrupt priority** in Cortex-M3 is **lower numerical value = higher priority** (counter-intuitive!).
- ✅ **Wakeup Interrupt Controller (WIC)** enables deep-sleep wakeup without full NVIC clocking.
- ✅ **AAPCS** (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard) requires **8-byte stack alignment** at function call.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM for Embedded Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM for Embedded Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** is a family of reduced instruction set computing (RISC) architectures for computer processors, configured and licensed by **Arm Holdings**. In the context of **embedded applications**, an ARM-based embedded system is a tightly coupled, application-specific computing platform that integrates an ARM processor core (typically from the **Cortex-M** or **Cortex-R** families), on-chip memory, dedicated peripherals, and power-management blocks onto a single **System-on-Chip (SoC)** to perform a narrowly defined real-time control or signal-processing function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme – PEBMT741, Module 3):**
> "ARM for Embedded Applications" focuses on the architectural features, exception handling, low-power design philosophy, and peripheral integration that make ARM the dominant 32-bit/64-bit instruction set in modern embedded SoCs (Internet-of-Things, automotive ECUs, industrial controllers, biomedical implants, and consumer electronics).

The defining characteristics of ARM for embedded use are:

1. **Load–Store RISC ISA** – arithmetic operates only on registers; memory is touched only by explicit load/store instructions, simplifying pipelining and deterministic timing.
2. **Thumb-2 / Thumb Instruction Set** – a 16-bit compressed encoding that reduces code density by ~30 %, critical for cost-sensitive embedded systems with on-chip Flash ≤ 256 KB.
3. **Deterministic Interrupt Response** – the built-in **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** provides fixed, low-latency interrupt entry (typically **12 clock cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4).
4. **Bit-Banding** – atomic bit-level access to memory-mapped peripheral registers, eliminating read-modify-write races in ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) code.
5. **Wake-up Interrupt Controller (WIC)** – allows the core to be powered down to deep-sleep states (< 1 µA) while still detecting external events.
6. **Single-Cycle I/O Port** – fast General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) toggle rates suitable for emulating communication protocols in software (bit-banging).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Workshop"

Think of an **ARM-based embedded system** as a **specialist mechanic's workshop** in a small town, as opposed to a **generalist office building** (which is what a desktop x86 PC resembles):

| Workshop Analogue | Embedded ARM Component |
|---|---|
| The workbench | CPU register file (R0–R12) |
| The toolbox drawer (always within reach) | On-chip SRAM (Cache) |
| The slow attic archive | External Flash / EEPROM |
| The doorbell system with priority queue | NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller) |
| The "OFF" sign with doorbell pass-through | WIC (Wake-up Interrupt Controller) |
| The specialized screwdriver set | Thumb-2 instruction set (compact, every tool exactly fits the job) |
| The single-purpose repair forms | Application firmware in Flash |

Unlike the office building (a general-purpose processor) that has a vast reception area (large OS, MMU, virtual memory, branch predictors, out-of-order engine) for *any* kind of work, the workshop is **physically small, fast to respond at the doorbell, and uses very little electricity when idle** — exactly the traits required for an **embedded** system that must run unattended for years on a battery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** A typical Cortex-M4 core consumes only **~100 µA/MHz** in Run mode and drops to **< 1 µA** in Deep-Sleep with WIC, making it ideal for IoT nodes powered by coin-cell batteries.

## 1.3 Why ARM Dominates Embedded — Five Engineering Reasons

1. **Power Efficiency per Instruction** — ~32 % lower energy per instruction than equivalent CISC cores at the same process node.
2. **Scalability** — One ISA scales from **Cortex-M0 (8 KB Flash, 4 KB RAM)** to **Cortex-A78 (smartphone class)** without code rewrite at the assembly level.
3. **Licensing Model** — Hundreds of silicon vendors (STMicro, NXP, Microchip, Nordic, Renesas) license the core, ensuring a vibrant supply chain and tool ecosystem.
4. **Toolchain Maturity** — Free **GCC**, **Keil MDK**, and **IAR EWARM** toolchains; CMSIS (Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard) HAL libraries are vendor-portable.
5. **Real-Time Determinism** — Harvard architecture, fixed interrupt latency, and absence of cache misses in M-class cores give **worst-case execution time (WCET)** that is provably bounded — a hard requirement for **AUTOSAR** automotive and **DO-178C** avionics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of an Embedded ARM SoC

An ARM-based embedded SoC is constructed from several modular IP blocks, all connected by the **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** on-chip interconnect family. The most common buses are:

- **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** — high-speed CPU ↔ RAM ↔ DMA backbone.
- **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** — low-bandwidth peripheral bridge (UART, I²C, Timers, GPIO).
- **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)** — used in Cortex-A applications processors with coherent multi-core traffic.

### 2.1.1 Functional Block Listing

1. **CPU Core** — Cortex-M0/M0+/M3/M4/M7/M33 (32-bit) or Cortex-R4/R5 (32-bit, locked-cache real-time).
2. **NVIC** — up to 240 external interrupts, 8 priority bits (configurable), tail-chaining (4-cycle back-to-back IRQ).
3. **WIC** — wake-up source routing during Deep-Sleep.
4. **MPU** (Memory Protection Unit) — 8/16 regions, optional (not present on M0/M0+).
5. **On-Chip Memory** — Flash (program), SRAM (data), optional EEPROM or One-Time-Programmable (OTP).
6. **Bus Matrix / AHB-Lite Fabric** — multi-master arbitration (CPU + DMA).
7. **Peripherals** — GPIO, USART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, ADC, DAC, Timers, RTC, PWM.
8. **Debug Access Port (DAP)** — SWD (Serial Wire Debug, 2-pin) or JTAG (4-pin).
9. **Power-Management Unit (PMU)** — voltage regulators, clock gates, low-power mode controller.
10. **Reset & Clock Control (RCC)** — PLL, LSE, HSE, HSI oscillators; system clock tree.

## 2.2 ARM Processor Operating Modes (Cortex-M3 / M4)

Cortex-M processors operate in one of two privilege levels and two stacks:

- **Thread Mode** — entered on reset; can run privileged or unprivileged (user) code.
- **Handler Mode** — entered on exception/interrupt; always privileged; uses **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)**.

The **CONTROL register** selects between **MSP** and **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** for Thread mode and toggles the unprivileged bit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The PSP exists primarily to support RTOS context switching. Each task gets its own PSP, allowing the kernel (running on MSP) to remain isolated and protected by the MPU.

## 2.3 Core "Why" and "How" — Bullet Logic

### 2.3.1 Why Load-Store Architecture in Embedded ARM?

- **Reason 1:** Each instruction is fixed-length (16-bit Thumb-2 or 32-bit Thumb-2), so the decoder can fetch a word every cycle without needing complex length-decoding.
- **Reason 2:** Memory access is decoupled from ALU, making pipelining trivial — no need for microcode ROM (which CISC x86 has and which costs area/power).
- **Reason 3:** Deterministic bus utilization → bounded WCET → certifiable for safety-critical code.

### 2.3.2 How does Thumb-2 improve code density?

- Most instructions have a 16-bit encoding; frequently used 32-bit instructions (BL, MOVW, LDR literal) exist for performance.
- **GCC flag:** `-mthumb` selects the Thumb-2 ISA; `-marm` reverts to full 32-bit ARM.
- Compromise between pure ARM (32-bit, 4 GB addressability, full performance) and original Thumb (16-bit only, no conditional execution).

### 2.3.3 How is Interrupt Latency Reduced?

- Vector table is stored in Flash at address `0x0000_0000`.
- NVIC reads the vector in 2 cycles; stacking of R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR is hardware-driven in 12 cycles (no software prologue).
- Tail-chaining: if IRQ2 arrives while ISR1 runs, NVIC skips unstacking IRQs1's frame and immediately vectors to IRQ2 in **6 cycles** total.
- Late-arriving: if higher-priority IRQ arrives in the first 12 cycles of stacking a lower-priority IRQ, NVIC re-vectors directly, saving the stacked context restoration.

### 2.3.4 How does Bit-Banding Work?

- A 1-MB **bit-band region** is aliased to a 32-MB **bit-band alias** region.
- Writing to `0x2200_0000 + (byte_offset × 32) + (bit_number × 4)` atomically sets that bit in the underlying byte.
- Eliminates **read-modify-write** hazards in shared ISR ↔ main-loop code without disabling interrupts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Symbol | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Clock Period** | $$T_{clk} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f_{CPU}}$$ | Seconds; $f_{CPU}$ in Hz. |
| 2 | **Instruction Throughput** | $$\text{MIPS} \;=\; \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$$ | Million Instructions Per Second; $CPI$ = clocks per instruction. |
| 3 | **CPU Utilization** | $$U \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{T}$$ | Ratio of active time $\tau$ to total period $T$. |
| 4 | **AMBA Bus Throughput** | $$\Theta_{bus} \;=\; \dfrac{D_{bytes}}{T_{tx}}$$ | Bytes per second on AHB/APB. |
| 5 | **Interrupt Latency** | $$t_{lat} \;=\; t_{stack} + t_{vec} + t_{ISR\;entry}$$ | 12 cycles stack + 2 vector + ISR prologue. |
| 6 | **Energy per Instruction** | $$E_{inst} \;=\; V_{DD}^{2} \cdot C_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_{CPU}}$$ | Joules; $C_{eff}$ = effective switched capacitance. |
| 7 | **Average Power** | $$P_{avg} \;=\; V_{DD} \cdot I_{active} + V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$ | Watts; includes static + dynamic. |
| 8 | **Duty-Cycle for Battery Life** | $$t_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{mAh} \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{avg} \cdot 1000}$$ | Hours of operation. |
| 9 | **Pipeline Speedup (Amdahl-aware)** | $$S_{n} \;=\; \dfrac{n}{1 + (n-1)\cdot f_{seq}}$$ | $n$ pipeline stages, $f_{seq}$ = sequential fraction. |
| 10 | **Bit-Band Alias Address** | $$A_{alias} \;=\; 0x2200\,0000 + (A_{reg} - 0x2000\,0000)\cdot 32 + b\cdot 4$$ | $A_{reg}$ byte address, $b \in [0,7]$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Formula 5):** On Cortex-M3 at 72 MHz, $t_{lat} = (12+2)\times T_{clk} = 14 \times 13.89\,\text{ns} \approx 194\,\text{ns}$ — well within the 1 µs budget required for 1 MHz PWM and CAN-bus arbitration.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU (e.g., NXP S32K3 with Cortex-M7):** ARM Cortex-M7's deterministic interrupt and DSP extensions run airbag-deployment algorithms within a hard 100 µs deadline.
- **Wearable Health Monitor (Nordic nRF5340, dual Cortex-M33):** Thumb-2 + low-power modes allow 30-day continuous heart-rate monitoring on a 200 mAh Li-Po cell.
- **Industrial PLC (ST STM32H7, Cortex-M7 @ 480 MHz):** Bit-banding and NVIC tail-chaining enable sub-µs deterministic control loops for high-speed assembly lines.
- **Satellite CubeSat (Microchip SAM-V71 Cortex-M7 with rad-hard SRAM):** Locked-cache + MPU protection isolates critical tasks from SEU-induced faults.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CPU Throughput (MIPS) for a Cortex-M4 Running a DSP Filter

> **Problem Context:** A Cortex-M4 runs a 64-tap FIR filter on a 16 kHz audio stream. Each tap requires 1 MAC (multiply-accumulate, 1 cycle on M4). Compute the maximum audio sampling rate the CPU can sustain.

### Step 1 — Identify the instruction count per sample

Each filter sample requires 64 MACs, and each MAC is **1 cycle** (single-cycle MAC on M4's hardware multiplier).

$$
N_{inst} \;=\; 64 \;\text{cycles/sample}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to CPU time per sample

The CPU time required to process one sample is simply the number of cycles divided by the clock frequency.

$$
t_{sample} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{inst}}{f_{CPU}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the maximum sustainable sample rate

The maximum sample rate $f_s^{max}$ is the reciprocal of $t_{sample}$.

$$
f_{s}^{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{t_{sample}} \;=\; \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{N_{inst}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numeric values

Assume $f_{CPU} = 168\,\text{MHz}$ (typical STM32F407).

$$
f_{s}^{max} \;=\; \dfrac{168 \times 10^{6}}{64} \;=\; 2.625 \times 10^{6}\,\text{samples/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to convenient units

$$
f_{s}^{max} \;=\; 2.625\,\text{MHz}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare to required rate

Required audio sample rate is 16 kHz. The available margin is:

$$
\text{Margin} \;=\; \dfrac{2.625\,\text{MHz}}{16\,\text{kHz}} \;\approx\; 164\times
$$

This means the CPU spends only **0.61 %** of its time on the DSP and can service other interrupts/tasks easily.

$$
\text{DSP Load \%} \;=\; \dfrac{16\,\text{kHz} \times 64}{168\,\text{MHz}} \times 100 \;=\; 0.61\,\%
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case Interrupt Latency with NVIC Tail-Chaining

> **Problem:** Compute the total worst-case latency for servicing two back-to-back interrupts on a Cortex-M3 @ 72 MHz.

### Step 1 — Stacking of the first ISR

When IRQ1 fires, the NVIC stacks R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR in 12 cycles.

$$
t_{stack,1} \;=\; 12 \times T_{clk} \;=\; 12 \times \dfrac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 166.67\,\text{ns}
$$

### Step 2 — Vector fetch for IRQ1

$$
t_{vec,1} \;=\; 2 \times T_{clk} \;=\; 27.78\,\text{ns}
$$

### Step 3 — ISR1 entry (assume 4-cycle prologue)

$$
t_{entry,1} \;=\; 4 \times T_{clk} \;=\; 55.56\,\text{ns}
$$

### Step 4 — IRQ2 arrives exactly 1 cycle after IRQ1's vector fetch

NVIC uses **tail-chaining** — it skips unstacking and re-stacking; the second vector fetch is taken immediately after ISR1's `BX LR`.

$$
t_{tail,2} \;=\; 6 \times T_{clk} \;=\; 83.33\,\text{ns}
$$

### Step 5 — Total worst-case latency to first instruction of ISR2

$$
t_{latency,2} \;=\; t_{stack,1} + t_{vec,1} + t_{entry,1} + t_{tail,2}
$$

$$
t_{latency,2} \;=\; (12 + 2 + 4 + 6) \times T_{clk} \;=\; 24 \times \dfrac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
t_{latency,2} \;=\; 333.33\,\text{ns}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare to bare-metal baseline

Without tail-chaining (legacy ARM7TDMI), the equivalent latency would be:

$$
t_{legacy} \;=\; (12 + 2 + 4) + (12 + 2 + 4) + \text{stack+vec} \;\approx\; 36 + 18 + 6 \;=\; 60\,\text{cycles} \;\approx\; 833\,\text{ns}
$$

The Cortex-M3 is therefore **2.5× more responsive** under high interrupt load.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Battery Life for a Wireless Sensor Node

> **Problem:** A wireless sensor node uses an nRF52832 (Cortex-M4F @ 64 MHz, 5.5 mA active at 3 V, 1.8 µA in System OFF). It sleeps 99 % of the time, wakes every 10 s, processes for 50 ms (transmit + ADC + crypto). Battery is a CR2032 (225 mAh, 3 V). Compute battery life.

### Step 1 — Compute charge per wake-up cycle

$$
Q_{cycle} \;=\; I_{active}\cdot t_{active} + I_{sleep}\cdot t_{sleep}
$$

$$
Q_{cycle} \;=\; (5.5\,\text{mA})(50\,\text{ms}) + (1.8\,\mu\text{A})(9.95\,\text{s})
$$

$$
Q_{cycle} \;=\; 275\,\mu\text{C} + 17.91\,\mu\text{C} \;=\; 292.91\,\mu\text{C}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute number of cycles per hour

$$
N_{hr} \;=\; \dfrac{3600\,\text{s}}{10\,\text{s/cycle}} \;=\; 360\,\text{cycles/hr}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute average current draw

$$
I_{avg} \;=\; N_{hr}\cdot Q_{cycle}\cdot \dfrac{1}{3600\,\text{s}}
$$

$$
I_{avg} \;=\; 360 \times 292.91\,\mu\text{C} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3600\,\text{s}} \;\approx\; 29.3\,\mu\text{A}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute battery life in hours

$$
t_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg}} \cdot 1000
$$

$$
t_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{225\,\text{mAh}}{0.0293\,\text{mA}} \;\approx\; 7680\,\text{hours}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to years

$$
t_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{7680}{8760}\,\text{yr} \;\approx\; 0.877\,\text{years} \;\approx\; 10.5\,\text{months}
$$

### Step 6 — Sensitivity analysis

Doubling the active time (100 ms) increases $I_{avg}$ to ~58 µA, halving battery life to ~5.3 months. This shows why **minimizing active time** is the single most effective lever in duty-cycled embedded designs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cortex-M Family Comparison

| Feature | M0 | M0+ | M3 | M4 | M7 | M33 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ISA | Thumb / Thumb-2 subset | Thumb / Thumb-2 subset | Thumb-2 | Thumb-2 + DSP | Thumb-2 + DSP + FPU (DP) | Thumb-2 + DSP + FPU + TrustZone |
| Pipeline Stages | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 6 (dual-issue) | 3 |
| Typical Clock | ≤ 50 MHz | ≤ 50 MHz | ≤ 100 MHz | ≤ 180 MHz | ≤ 480 MHz | ≤ 200 MHz |
| MPU Regions | 0 | 0 | 8 | 8 | 8 / 16 | 8 |
| FPU | No | No | No | Single-precision | Double-precision | Single-precision |
| DSP Extensions | No | No | No | Yes (SIMD MAC) | Yes | Yes |
| Typical Use | Sensor node | Wearable | Industrial | Motor control | Audio / Vision | Secure IoT |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — ARM-Based Embedded SoC Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical architecture of a Cortex-M-class embedded SoC, showing bus interconnects, masters, slaves, and the AMBA fabric. This is the **functional block diagram** that KTU examiners most commonly ask students to reproduce.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[CPU CORE CORTEX M4] --> B1[AHB LITE BUS MATRIX]
    A2[DMA CONTROLLER] --> B1
    A3[DEBUG ACCESS PORT SWD JTAG] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[CODE FLASH 512 KB]
    B1 --> C2[SRAM 128 KB]
    B1 --> C3[ROM BOOTLOADER]
    B1 --> D1[AHB TO APB BRIDGE 1]
    B1 --> D2[AHB TO APB BRIDGE 2]
    D1 --> E1[GPIO PORTS A B C D E]
    D1 --> E2[USART 1 2 3]
    D1 --> E3[SPI 1 2]
    D1 --> E4[I2C 1 2]
    D2 --> F1[ADC 12 BIT 16 CH]
    D2 --> F2[GP TIMERS 1 2 3 4]
    D2 --> F3[RTC]
    D2 --> F4[WATCHDOG IWDG WWDG]
    B1 --> G1[NVIC 60 IRQ VECTORS]
    B1 --> G2[MPU 8 REGIONS]
    B1 --> G3[POWER CONTROL PMU]
    B1 --> G4[RESET AND CLOCK CONTROL RCC PLL]
    G3 --> H1[VOLTAGE REGULATOR LDO]
    G3 --> H2[WAKEUP INTERRUPT CONTROLLER WIC]
    G4 --> I1[HSI RC 16 MHZ]
    G4 --> I2[HSE CRYSTAL 8 MHZ]
    G4 --> I3[LSE 32 KHZ RTC]
    G4 --> I4[PLL VCO 168 MHZ]
```

**How to read this diagram for KTU answers:**

- **Master blocks** (CPU, DMA, DAP) sit on the left and feed into the **AHB-Lite Bus Matrix** (the central crossbar switch).
- **Slaves** (Flash, SRAM, ROM) are memory-mapped and accessible by any master.
- **Peripherals** hang off **APB bridges** — the bridges slow down access (APB is a non-pipelined, simpler bus) to save power.
- The **NVIC, MPU, PMU, RCC** are tightly coupled to the CPU for low-latency response and power gating.
- The **WIC** can wake the system from Deep-Sleep via a pre-defined GPIO/RTC event without powering the NVIC.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — ARM Cortex-M Exception Handling Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[RESET RELEASE] --> A1[INIT STACK POINTER MSP FROM VECTOR 0]
    A1 --> A2[LOAD RESET VECTOR PC FROM ADDRESS 0x00000004]
    A2 --> A3[ENTER THREAD MODE PRIVILEGED]
    A3 --> A4[APPLICATION CODE EXECUTING]
    A4 --> A5{INTERRUPT REQUEST LINE HIGH}
    A5 -- NO --> A4
    A5 -- YES --> A6[NVIC PRIORITY FILTER]
    A6 --> A7{PRIORITY HIGHER THAN CURRENT BASEPRI}
    A7 -- NO --> A4
    A7 -- YES --> A8[PUSH R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC XPSR ONTO STACK]
    A8 --> A9[READ ISR VECTOR FROM VECTOR TABLE]
    A9 --> A10[BRANCH TO ISR BODY HANDLER MODE PRIVILEGED]
    A10 --> A11[ISR EXECUTES AND CLEARS IRQ FLAG]
    A11 --> A12{ANOTHER PENDING IRQ}
    A12 -- NO --> A13[EXCEPTION RETURN BX LR]
    A13 --> A14[NVIC POPS SAVED CONTEXT FROM STACK]
    A14 --> A15[RESUME PRE INTERRUPTED CODE]
    A12 -- YES --> A16[TAIL CHAIN TO NEXT ISR NO UNSTACK]
    A16 --> A10
```

**Key takeaways from the diagram:**

- The **hardware stacking** (yellow node) is invisible to the software; the C ISR function `void EXTI0_IRQHandler(void)` simply begins executing.
- The **`BX LR`** instruction uses the value `0xFFFF_FFFX` loaded into LR on entry, which the NVIC interprets as an exception return opcode.
- **Tail-chaining** keeps the stacked frame intact, so re-entering a new ISR saves the extra 12-cycle stack operation.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Typical ARM-Based Wireless Sensor Node Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[SENSOR E.G. TEMPERATURE I2C] --> MCU1[ARM CORTEX M4 MCU NRF52832]
    S2[SENSOR E.G. ACCELEROMETER SPI] --> MCU1
    BTN1[USER PUSH BUTTON GPIO] --> MCU1
    LED1[STATUS LED GPIO] --> MCU1
    MCU1 --> RF1[RADIO TRANSCEIVER 2.4 GHZ]
    RF1 --> ANT1[PCB ANTENNA OR CHIP ANTENNA]
    MCU1 --> PMU1[POWER MANAGEMENT UNIT]
    BAT1[CR2032 COIN CELL 225 MAH] --> PMU1
    PMU1 --> REG1[LDO 3V TO 1.8V]
    REG1 --> MCU1
    PMU1 --> REG2[DC DC BUCK FOR RF PA]
    REG2 --> RF1
    MCU1 --> DBG1[SWD 2 PIN DEBUG HEADER]
    PC1[HOST PC KEIL PYOCD] --> DBG1
```

This diagram maps directly to the **"real-world"** KTU viva question: *"Draw the block diagram of an ARM-based embedded system and explain each block."* The PMU + WIC combination is what enables the multi-year battery life characteristic of IoT edge nodes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Cortex-A with Cortex-M.** Cortex-A is for application processors (smartphones, Linux); Cortex-M is for **microcontroller-class** embedded systems with on-chip Flash and no MMU. Many students lose 2 marks by mixing them up.
> 2. **Always mention the NVIC** when explaining "interrupt handling in ARM." Generic "IRQ controller" is incomplete.
> 3. **Bit-banding is on Cortex-M3/M4**, NOT on M0/M0+ — get the family correct.
> 4. **Thumb-2 is the default ISA** for all Cortex-M cores; the old "ARM mode" / "Thumb mode" switch (CPSR.T bit) is not present in M-class.
> 5. In numerical problems, **always state units** and show the **clock-period conversion** explicitly — KTU award 1 mark for unit consistency.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "ARM" and explain why it is preferred for embedded applications.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ARM stands for **Advanced RISC Machine**, a family of 32-bit/64-bit **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** processor architectures designed and licensed by **Arm Holdings** (United Kingdom).

ARM is preferred for embedded applications because of the following reasons:

1. **Low Power Consumption** — The simple, pipelined, in-order design consumes ~32 % less energy per instruction than equivalent CISC cores, enabling battery-powered IoT nodes.
2. **Thumb-2 Code Density** — Mixed 16-bit/32-bit encoding reduces Flash footprint by ~30 %, critical for cost-sensitive MCUs with ≤ 256 KB Flash.
3. **Deterministic Interrupt Response** — The on-chip **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** gives fixed 12-cycle stacking and 6-cycle tail-chain, satisfying hard real-time deadlines in automotive and industrial systems.
4. **Scalable Ecosystem** — One ISA spans from Cortex-M0 (sensor nodes) to Cortex-A78 (smartphones), supported by free GCC, Keil, and IAR toolchains.
5. **Vendor Diversity** — Hundreds of licensees (ST, NXP, Microchip, Nordic, Renesas) ensure competitive pricing and abundant reference designs.

### **Q2. List the different operating modes of the ARM Cortex-M3 processor.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Cortex-M3 operates in **two modes** and **two privilege levels**:

| Mode | Privilege | Stack Pointer | Entry Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thread Mode** | Privileged or Unprivileged | MSP or PSP | On reset, on `BX LR` from Handler |
| **Handler Mode** | Always Privileged | MSP only | On any exception / interrupt |

The **CONTROL[0]** bit selects between **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** and **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** in Thread mode. The **CONTROL[1]** bit selects Unprivileged level in Thread mode (always 0 in Handler mode). The PSP is typically used by **RTOS tasks**, while the kernel uses MSP, allowing the **MPU** to enforce memory isolation between tasks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

#### **(a)** Explain the salient features of the ARM processor that make it suitable for embedded applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Load-Store RISC Architecture** — All data processing operations occur between CPU registers, not memory. This simplifies the pipeline and allows single-cycle execution of most instructions, leading to deterministic timing suitable for real-time systems.

2. **Thumb-2 Instruction Set** — Provides a mix of 16-bit and 32-bit instructions. The 16-bit instructions reduce code size (Flash footprint) by ~30 %, lowering silicon cost; the 32-bit instructions preserve full performance. The compiler can mix them transparently.

3. **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)** — Hardware support for up to 240 interrupt sources with configurable priority (3–8 bits). Provides automatic stacking of registers, vector fetch, tail-chaining, and late-arriving interrupts, giving worst-case interrupt latency of 12 clock cycles.

4. **Bit-Banding** — Atomic single-bit access to memory-mapped I/O registers in the 1-MB SRAM and peripheral bit-band regions. Eliminates read-modify-write hazards when sharing GPIO/control registers between ISRs and main code, without disabling interrupts.

5. **Wake-up Interrupt Controller (WIC)** — Allows the core to enter deep-sleep states (current < 1 µA) while remaining reactive to a small set of pre-defined external wake-up events, critical for coin-cell battery applications.

6. **MPU (Memory Protection Unit)** — Optional but typical on M3/M4/M7; allows partitioning of memory into 8 or 16 regions with separate access permissions, enabling RTOS task isolation and stack-overflow protection.

7. **Low-Power Modes** — Sleep, Deep-Sleep, Standby, and Shutdown (in some vendors). Each mode trades off wake-up latency against quiescent current. Standby retains SRAM; Shutdown does not.

8. **Scalability & Toolchain** — One ISA family covers tiny Cortex-M0 (8 KB Flash) up to Cortex-M7 (2 MB Flash, 480 MHz). Free GCC, paid Keil/IAR, and CMSIS HAL libraries make the platform extremely portable.

#### **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an ARM-based embedded system. Explain the role of the AMBA bus. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram** — see **Section 4.1** of these notes for the full Mermaid rendering. The key blocks are:

1. **ARM Cortex-M Core** — executes Thumb-2 instructions.
2. **NVIC** — handles interrupts with deterministic latency.
3. **WIC** — allows wake from Deep-Sleep.
4. **Flash** — non-volatile program storage.
5. **SRAM** — volatile data / stack.
6. **AHB-Lite Bus Matrix** — multi-master crossbar.
7. **APB Bridges** — connect low-speed peripherals to AHB.
8. **Peripherals** — GPIO, USART, SPI, I²C, ADC, Timers.
9. **RCC** — clock tree and PLL.
10. **PMU** — voltage regulators and power gating.
11. **DAP** — debug interface (SWD/JTAG).

**Role of AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture):**

- AMBA is the **on-chip interconnect standard** that ties the CPU, memory, and peripherals together.
- **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** is a pipelined, multi-master bus used for CPU ↔ RAM ↔ DMA traffic. It supports burst transfers and split transactions.
- **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** is a simpler, non-pipelined bus for low-bandwidth peripherals. APB bridges from AHB provide clock-domain isolation and reduce peripheral logic complexity.
- **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)** is used when higher throughput is required (e.g., Cortex-A, GPUs, DDR controllers).
- AMBA standardizes **bus protocols, arbitration, and signal timing**, allowing IP blocks from different vendors to interoperate within the same SoC.
- The bus matrix allows **parallel master access** (e.g., CPU and DMA can each access different peripherals simultaneously), increasing throughput and reducing CPU stall time.

> **Mark Allocation Guidance:** 2 marks for the block diagram, 3 marks for block explanations, and 2 marks for AMBA description.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

#### **(a)** Explain the ARM Cortex-M exception model. Discuss the role of the vector table and the stacking sequence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 implements a **uniform exception model** in which interrupts, faults, and system exceptions all share the same handling mechanism.

1. **Exception Sources** — Reset, NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt), HardFault, MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault, SVCall, DebugMonitor, PendSV, SysTick, and up to 240 external interrupts (IRQ0…IRQn).

2. **Vector Table** — A table of 32-bit addresses located at the bottom of memory (default `0x0000_0000`, remappable via the `VTOR` register in SCB). Each entry is the start address of the corresponding exception handler. The first entry is the **initial Main Stack Pointer (MSP) value**, loaded into SP on reset.

3. **Stacking Sequence on Exception Entry** — When an exception is accepted by the NVIC, the following registers are automatically pushed onto the active stack (MSP or PSP):
   - **R0, R1, R2, R3** — argument/scratch registers.
   - **R12** — Intra-procedure-call scratch register.
   - **R14 (LR)** — Link register, with a special `EXC_RETURN` value `0xFFFF_FFFX`.
   - **PC** — Return address of the interrupted instruction.
   - **xPSR** — Program Status Register.
   - This takes **12 clock cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4.

4. **Handler Mode Entry** — The CPU enters Handler mode (privileged), reads the handler address from the vector table, and begins execution. The `EXC_RETURN` value in LR encodes which stack pointer (MSP/PSP) to use on return.

5. **Exception Return** — The `BX LR` or `POP {…, PC}` instruction triggers the NVIC to unstack the saved frame and resume Thread mode.

6. **Tail-Chaining** — When a higher-priority interrupt arrives while a lower-priority ISR is executing, the NVIC skips the unstacking and re-stacking sequence, achieving a 6-cycle re-entry.

7. **Late-Arriving Interrupts** — If a higher-priority interrupt arrives during the first 12 cycles of stacking, the NVIC aborts the current stacking and re-vectors directly to the higher-priority ISR.

#### **(b)** A Cortex-M4 microcontroller runs at 80 MHz. An external interrupt is configured with priority 2. Calculate the worst-case interrupt latency and the maximum number of interrupts it can service per second assuming each ISR takes 5 µs to execute. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f_{CPU} = 80 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$
- $T_{clk} = 1/f_{CPU} = 12.5\,\text{ns}$
- Each ISR execution time: $t_{ISR} = 5\,\mu\text{s}$
- Interrupt priority = 2 (used only for arbitration, not for latency)

**Step 1 — Compute the worst-case interrupt latency**

Worst-case latency = stacking time + vector fetch time = 12 + 2 = 14 cycles.

$$
t_{lat} \;=\; 14 \times T_{clk} \;=\; 14 \times 12.5\,\text{ns} \;=\; 175\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total time to service one interrupt (latency + ISR execution)**

$$
t_{svc} \;=\; t_{lat} + t_{ISR} \;=\; 175\,\text{ns} + 5\,\mu\text{s} \;=\; 5.175\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum number of interrupts per second**

$$
N_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{t_{svc}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{5.175 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 193{,}237\,\text{IRQ/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the CPU load**

$$
\text{Load \%} \;=\; N_{max} \times t_{svc} \times 100 \;=\; 100\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Practical limit**

In practice, we reserve 20 % CPU headroom for background tasks (RTOS scheduler, low-priority polling). The sustainable interrupt rate is:

$$
N_{sustainable} \;\approx\; 0.8 \times 193{,}237 \;\approx\; 154{,}589\,\text{IRQ/s} \;\approx\; 154.6\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Answer Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Clock period | 12.5 ns |
| Stacking cycles | 12 |
| Vector fetch cycles | 2 |
| Worst-case latency | 175 ns |
| Time per interrupt | 5.175 µs |
| Max IRQ rate (100 % load) | 193.2 kHz |
| Practical IRQ rate (80 % load) | 154.6 kHz |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before the exam):**

- [x] **ARM = Advanced RISC Machine** — designed by Arm Holdings, UK; licensed to hundreds of silicon vendors.
- [x] **Cortex-M classes** are microcontroller-class (on-chip Flash, no MMU); Cortex-A are application processors.
- [x] **Thumb-2 ISA** is the default for all Cortex-M cores; mix of 16-bit and 32-bit instructions.
- [x] **NVIC** — 12-cycle stacking, 6-cycle tail-chain, supports 8-bit priority (configurable).
- [x] **Bit-banding** is available on Cortex-M3/M4 (1 MB region aliased to 32 MB).
- [x] **MPU** is optional on M0/M0+, present on M3/M4/M7/M33.
- [x] **Two stack pointers** — MSP (kernel) and PSP (RTOS tasks).
- [x] **WIC** allows Deep-Sleep current < 1 µA while keeping a small set of interrupts alive.
- [x] **AMBA bus family** — AHB (high-speed, pipelined), APB (low-speed, simple), AXI (high-throughput).
- [x] **Low-power modes** — Sleep, Deep-Sleep, Standby, Shutdown; each trades wake-up latency for current.
- [x] **FPU** — single-precision on M4, double-precision on M7, no FPU on M0/M0+/M3.
- [x] **DSP extensions** (SIMD MAC) are present on M4, M7, and M33.
- [x] **Common peripherals on Cortex-M SoC** — GPIO, USART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, ADC, DAC, Timers, RTC.
- [x] **AMBA matrix** allows DMA + CPU concurrent peripheral access.
- [x] **Formula for IRQ latency:** $t_{lat} = 14 \times T_{clk}$ (worst case, single interrupt).
- [x] **Formula for CPU throughput:** $\text{MIPS} = f_{CPU} / (CPI \times 10^{6})$.
- [x] **Formula for battery life:** $t_{batt} = C_{mAh} / I_{avg}$ (in hours, after $I_{avg}$ converted to mA).
- [x] **Cortex-M3/M4** are the most exam-relevant; be ready to draw their block diagrams and explain NVIC stacking.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Classic ARM Programmer's Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Classic ARM Programmer's Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Classic ARM Programmer's Model** is the architectural abstraction that defines the complete set of registers, processor modes, and status flags visible and accessible to a programmer writing assembly or low-level firmware for an ARM processor (specifically targeting the **ARM7TDMI** core in the KTU 2024 syllabus). It is "classic" because it represents the original ARM architectural state that existed before the evolution to modern AArch64/A64, comprising **37 registers** grouped into **General-Purpose Registers**, **Program Status Registers (CPSR/SPSR)**, and **Banked Registers** that are mode-specific.

According to the **ARM Architecture Reference Manual**, the programmer's model provides the contract between the hardware state and the software, ensuring deterministic behavior across **7 processor modes**: User (USR), System (SYS), Supervisor (SVC), FIQ, IRQ, Abort (ABT), Undefined (UND).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Classic" model refers specifically to the **ARM7TDMI / ARMv4T** programmer's view (32-bit ARM + 16-bit Thumb states). This is mandatory reading for **PEBMT741 Module 3**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef working in a professional kitchen (the ARM processor)**:

- The **counters and worktops** are your **General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12)** — they hold the ingredients (data) you are actively chopping, mixing, and cooking.
- The **active recipe card** on your stand is the **Program Counter (R15/PC)** — it tells you exactly which step you are performing.
- The **notepad** where you jot down the recipe you came from is the **Link Register (R14/LR)** — it remembers where to return after a side-task (function call).
- The **recipe book with a bookmark** is the **Stack Pointer (R13/SP)** — the bookmark remembers the top of a stack of saved notepads (nested function calls).
- The **manager's clipboard (CPSR)** records the kitchen's current state: "Is the oven hot? (C flag)", "Is the dish burnt? (V flag)", "Is the salt missing? (Z flag)". When the head chef (FIQ) rushes in, the manager locks away the previous clipboard into a **locked drawer (SPSR)** so the original state can be restored.
