Show the assembly sequence used to call it from ARM state and return to ARM state.* **Step 1 — ARM caller sets up the target address with the LSB cleared (the processor ignores LSB=0/1, but the LSB **must** indicate the destination state).** > For Thumb target: `LR = 0x0000_2001` (LSB = 1 → Thumb state). **Step 2 — Branch exchange in ARM state:** $$ \texttt{ARM:}\quad \texttt{LDR\ R0, =func\_thumb + 1} $$ $$ \texttt{BX\ R0} $$ > (The `+1` sets bit 0 = 1 so the processor enters Thumb state at the branch target.) **Step 3 — Inside the Thumb function, return to ARM state using `BX LR` (where LR still holds the caller's return address with LSB = 0, i.e. ARM state).** $$ \texttt{Thumb:}\quad \texttt{BX\ LR} $$ > [!NOTE] > **General interworking rule (KTU 14-mark favourite):** > - LSB = **1** → switch to **Thumb** state. > - LSB = **0** → switch to **ARM** state. > The address bits [31:1] supply the actual target word/half-word address. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Thumb State within ARM7TDMI ```mermaid flowchart LR A["MEMORY BUS
16-bit fetch in Thumb state"] --> B["INSTRUCTION FETCH STAGE
reads 16-bit halfword"] B --> C["THUMB DECODER
decompresses 16-bit to 32-bit ARM op"] C --> D["INSTRUCTION DECODE STAGE
reads 32-bit format"] D --> E["EXECUTE STAGE
ALU, barrel shifter, multiplier"] E --> F["REGISTER BANK R0 to R15
shared between ARM and Thumb states"] F --> E E --> G["WRITE BACK STAGE
result to register file or memory"] G --> F H["APSR FLAGS N Z C V"] --- E I["STATE BIT CPSR T"] --- B I --- C I --- D I --- E I --- G ``` ## 4.2 State-Transition Flow — ARM ↔ Thumb Interworking ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["PROCESSOR RESET
CPSR T = 0
ARM state"] S1["ARM STATE EXECUTION
32-bit instructions"] S2{"BX or BLX or
pop PC instruction
with LSB check"} S3["THUMB STATE EXECUTION
16-bit instructions
decompressed on the fly"] S4{"BX LR or
pop PC with LSB = 0"} S0 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 -- "target address LSB = 1" --> S3 S2 -- "target address LSB = 0" --> S1 S3 --> S4 S4 -- "LR LSB = 0" --> S1 S4 -- "LR LSB = 1" --> S3 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Grouping of Thumb Instruction Classes ```mermaid flowchart TB R["THUMB INSTRUCTION SET
16-bit encoding"] R --> P1["DATA PROCESSING GROUP
ADD, SUB, MOV, CMP, AND, ORR, EOR, LSL, LSR, ASR, ADC, SBC, NEG, MUL"] R --> P2["BRANCH GROUP
B unconditional, B conditional, BL, BX, BLX"] R --> P3["LOAD STORE GROUP
LDR, STR, LDRB, STRB, LDRH, STRH, LDRSB, LDRSH"] R --> P4["STACK GROUP
PUSH, POP"] R --> P5["DATA TRANSFER MULTIPLE
LDMIA, STMIA used as POP PUSH"] R --> P6["SOFTWARE INTERRUPT
SVC former SWI"] R --> P7["HI REGISTER OPS
MOV hi-low, ADD hi-low, CMP hi-hi"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Reading Hint:** In Mermaid block diagrams, the **upper block** always represents the instruction class, and the **lower blocks** are the mnemonic families. Use this chart as a quick last-minute revision map for KTU viva questions such as *"List the categories of Thumb instructions"* (2 marks). ## 4.4 Pipeline Stage Behaviour in Thumb State (ARM7TDMI 3-Stage) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CYCLE 1
FETCH halfword
16-bit from memory"] B["CYCLE 2
DECODE 16-bit
decompress to 32-bit
read registers"] C["CYCLE 3
EXECUTE
ALU, shift, multiply, address gen"] D["CYCLE 4
WRITE BACK
or memory access for LDR STR"] A --> B --> C --> D ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** In the classic ARM7TDMI **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch / Decode / Execute), Thumb state does **not** add an extra pipeline stage. The decompression happens *within* the existing Decode stage, so the **CPI (cycles per instruction)** is comparable to ARM state. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define the Thumb instruction set. Mention any two of its key features.** **Model Answer (verbatim examiner expectations):** The Thumb instruction set is a **16-bit compressed re-encoding of a subset of the ARM 32-bit instruction set**. It is executed when the processor is in Thumb state (signalled by `CPSR[T] = 1`). **Key features (any two):** 1. **Improved code density** — Thumb instructions occupy 2 bytes versus 4 bytes for ARM, producing ~ 30 % smaller code size on average. 2. **Restricted operand range** — only 5-bit immediate constants, only low registers (R0–R7) in most data-processing forms, and no conditional execution outside branch instructions. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **How does the processor determine whether to enter ARM or Thumb state on a `BX` instruction?** **Model Answer:** The **least significant bit (LSB, bit 0)** of the value loaded into the program counter (PC) by the `BX` instruction determines the next state: - If `LSB = 1` → the processor enters **Thumb state** at the branch target. - If `LSB = 0` → the processor enters **ARM state** at the branch target. - Bits [31:1] of the value supply the actual target instruction address. Because all ARM/Thumb instructions are at least half-word aligned, the LSB is always free to be used as the state flag. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified] **Q. A (a)** Explain the **Thumb instruction format** with a neat diagram. Discuss how it differs from the standard ARM (32-bit) instruction format. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Thumb instruction format — 16-bit fixed length.** A Thumb instruction is exactly **2 bytes** wide. The bits are interpreted according to the format class selected by the **5-bit primary opcode** in bits [15:11] (most format groups) or the upper 3 bits [15:13] (for branch and load/store formats). The lower bits carry the **operands** — register specifiers, immediate data, or branch offsets. **Generic 16-bit Thumb instruction layout:** $$ \underbrace{b_{15}\,b_{14}\,b_{13}\,b_{12}\,b_{11}\,b_{10}\,b_{9}\,b_{8}}_{\text{opcode field (operation class)}} \; \underbrace{b_{7}\,b_{6}\,b_{5}\,b_{4}\,b_{3}\,b_{2}\,b_{1}\,b_{0}}_{\text{operands / register specifiers / immediate / offset}} $$ **Sample format — Format 1: Move Shifted Register (e.g. `LSLS Rd, Rm, #imm5`):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{bits 15:12} &= 000 \; (\text{opcode group}) \\ \text{bits 11:10} &= \text{shift type: 00=LSL, 01=LSR, 10=ASR} \\ \text{bits 9:6} &= \text{shift amount} = 0 \text{ to } 31 \\ \text{bits 5:3} &= \text{Rs or Rm (source register)} \\ \text{bits 2:0} &= \text{Rd (destination register)} \end{aligned} $$ **2. Key differences from ARM (32-bit) format.** | Aspect | ARM (32-bit) | Thumb (16-bit) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Width | **32 bits** (4 bytes) | **16 bits** (2 bytes) | | Number of formats | Many (~ 20) | Fewer (~ 10) | | Primary opcode size | Variable (4–8 bits) | 5 bits typical | | Register field width | 4 bits per register | 3 bits (R0–R7 only) for most formats | | Immediate width | 8-bit + 4-bit rotate = up to 32 bits | 5-bit only | | Barrel-shifter in operand2 | ✅ | ❌ | | Conditional field (bits [31:28]) | ✅ on every instruction | ❌ (branches only) | **3. Decompression behaviour.** The 16-bit Thumb instruction is **decompressed to its 32-bit ARM equivalent** inside the decode stage. This is transparent to the programmer. --- ### Q. A (b) **With a suitable example, explain the procedure to call a Thumb function from an ARM routine and return back. State the role of bit 0 in the BX instruction.** **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. ARM → Thumb call procedure.** To call a Thumb function, the ARM routine must place the **target address + 1** into a register and execute `BX`. The `+1` sets bit 0 = 1, telling the processor to switch to Thumb state at the target. ```text ; ARM state code (caller) LDR R0, =ThumbFunc + 1 ; load target with LSB = 1 MOV LR, PC ; save return address in LR BX R0 ; branch to Thumb function ``` **2. Thumb → ARM return procedure.** Inside the Thumb function, the saved LR contains the caller's return address (with LSB = 0 because the caller is in ARM state). Executing `BX LR` therefore returns to the ARM caller. ```text ; Thumb state code (callee) ... ; function body BX LR ; return to ARM caller ``` **3. Role of bit 0 in `BX`.** | Bit 0 of operand | Resulting state | Address bits used | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **0** | **ARM state** | [31:1] | | **1** | **Thumb state** | [31:1] | > This is because both ARM (4-byte) and Thumb (2-byte) instructions are at least 2-byte aligned, so bit 0 is always free to be repurposed as the **state flag** for the branch-and-exchange. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative ### [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified] **Q. B (a)** List and briefly explain **any six categories** of Thumb instructions with one example each. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) 1. **Data-processing (register) instructions** — e.g. `ADD Rd, Rn, Rm` adds Rm to Rn and stores in Rd. *Thumb provides a 3-register form for low registers.* 2. **Data-processing (immediate) instructions** — e.g. `SUB Rd, Rn, #imm5` subtracts a 5-bit immediate from Rn. 3. **Shift instructions** — e.g. `LSLS Rd, Rm, #imm5` performs logical shift left by 0–31. 4. **Move / Compare / Add / Subtract immediate** — e.g. `MOV Rd, #imm8` moves an 8-bit constant. 5. **Load/Store single register** — e.g. `LDR Rd, [Rn, #imm]` loads a word from base + offset. 6. **Stack operations** — `PUSH {reglist, LR}` and `POP {reglist, PC}` push/pop registers on the user stack, with LR/PC as the link register. 7. **Branch instructions** — `B label` (unconditional, ± 2 KB) and conditional branches (`BEQ`, `BNE`, etc., ± 256 bytes). 8. **Software interrupt** — `SVC #imm8` (formerly `SWI`) for OS service calls. --- ### Q. B (b) **Explain the architectural differences between ARM and Thumb states. Why does Thumb state improve code density but not necessarily improve performance?** **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (b) | Parameter | ARM State | Thumb State | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Instruction width | 32 bits | 16 bits | | Conditional execution | All instructions | Branch only | | Register access | All 16 registers | Mostly R0–R7 | | Immediate size | Up to 32 bits (8 + rotate) | 5 bits typical, 8 bits for MOV | | Barrel shifter as operand2 | Yes | No | | Decoding | Direct | Decompression step inside decode | | Code density | Baseline | ~ 30 % higher | | Performance (CPI) | Comparable | Comparable per decompressed instruction | **Why code density improves but not necessarily performance:** - **Code density** improves simply because the same algorithm occupies **fewer bytes** of memory — this is a direct consequence of halving the instruction width and re-encoding the most-used operations. - **Performance** is roughly the same because every Thumb instruction is *decompressed* into the **same 32-bit ARM operation** that would have executed in ARM state. The number of *internal* operations remains the same, and the pipeline depth is unchanged. So Thumb does **not** execute more operations per cycle than ARM. - In some cases, Thumb can be **slower** if a complex ARM operation (e.g. long multiply) must be expanded into 2 or 3 Thumb instructions, requiring more cycles overall. - The performance benefit of Thumb is therefore *indirect* — better cache utilisation, fewer memory fetches, lower bus traffic — which can speed up the system in memory-bound workloads, but the raw ALU throughput is unchanged. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > 1. **Do not** confuse the `BLX` (Branch with Link and eXchange) opcode with `BX`. `BLX` additionally saves the return address in LR *and* switches state — a common 2-mark KTU trap. > 2. **Do not** claim that Thumb is "faster" than ARM. Thumb improves *density* and *memory bandwidth*, not raw ALU throughput. > 3. **Always** state the LSB convention for `BX` in the answer: **LSB=1 → Thumb**, **LSB=0 → ARM**. > 4. Many students forget that the decompressed 32-bit form is *not* written back to memory; it is transient inside the decode stage — losing 1 mark for this omission. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Card — KTU 2024 PEBMT741, Module 3 (Thumb Instruction Set)** - **Definition:** Thumb is a **16-bit compressed, re-encoded subset of the ARM 32-bit ISA**, executed when `CPSR[T] = 1`. - **Width:** Every Thumb instruction is **2 bytes** wide; ARM instructions are **4 bytes** wide. - **Decompression:** Happens *inside* the decode stage of ARM7TDMI; the decompressed 32-bit form is never written back to memory. - **Code-density gain:** Typically **~ 30 %** smaller code; theoretical upper bound is 50 % (perfect 1-to-1 mapping). - **Performance:** Roughly the same CPI as ARM, because Thumb expands to the same internal 32-bit operations; Thumb is *not* inherently faster. - **Register access:** Most Thumb data-processing forms use **R0–R7** (low registers). R8–R15 are restricted to MOV/ADD/CMP/BX-class instructions. - **Condition codes:** Only **branch** instructions are conditional in Thumb state. **Data-processing** is unconditional in Thumb. - **Flag updates:** Only a small subset of instructions (`ANDS`, `ADCS`, `SBCS`, `EORS`, `TST`, `NEGS`, `CMP`, `CMN`) update the **APSR** flags. - **`BX` interworking rule:** `LSB = 0` → ARM; `LSB = 1` → Thumb. Address bits [31:1] hold the target. - **Branch range:** Unconditional `B` → **± 2048 bytes**; conditional `B` → **± 256 bytes**. - **No barrel shifter** in operand2 for Thumb data-processing. - **Stack:** `PUSH {reglist, LR}` and `POP {reglist, PC}` are the canonical Thumb stack idioms. - **`BL` in Thumb:** Two-instruction sequence (prefix + suffix) to obtain a 32-bit branch-with-link range. - **SVC** (formerly `SWI`) is the only supervisor-call style instruction in classic Thumb. - **Multiplication:** Only 32-bit `MUL Rd, Rm, Rs` exists; long-multiply (SMULL/UMULL) and MAC are not in classic Thumb — use an ARM routine. - **Core association:** The classic Thumb subset is most strongly associated with the **ARM7TDMI** core — the canonical KTU reference architecture for PEBMT741. - **PSA / safety check:** R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC. R13 must be **8-byte aligned** on public-function entry (AAPCS). - **Pipeline impact:** No extra pipeline stage is added for Thumb on ARM7TDMI's 3-stage pipeline (Fetch / Decode / Execute). - **Power benefit:** Fewer memory fetches per algorithm → **lower dynamic power**, a major reason Thumb is used in battery-powered and IoT devices. > **Last-Mantra for the KTU Board Hall:** *"Thumb = 16-bit width, 30 % density gain, low-register bias, branch-only conditional, decompress-not-store, LSB-of-BX-target chooses state."* — Memorise this line; it is worth 2–3 marks in any PEBMT741 question.

topics.title: Architectural Support for System Development - The ARM Memory Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architectural Support for System Development - The ARM Memory Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: ARCHITECTURAL SUPPORT FOR SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT
## The ARM Memory Interface

---

### 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

#### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ARM Memory Interface** refers to the standardized hardware protocol and signal bus structure that governs how an ARM processor communicates with external memory subsystems. It defines the timing, signaling, control logic, and data transfer conventions required to access volatile (RAM) and non-volatile (ROM/Flash) storage devices. The interface is built upon the **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** specification, providing a unified, scalable, and high-throughput communication channel between the CPU core, caches, MMU, write buffer, and off-chip memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The ARM Memory Interface provides the *architectural support* required to connect the ARM processor core to the external world. It is not just a "wire" — it is a **complete protocol layer** defining address, data, control signaling, and bus cycle types (Non-sequential, Sequential, and Idle).

---

#### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Royal Postal System

Imagine the ARM processor as a **King** who needs information (data and instructions) from his vast library (memory). The library is huge, the King is fast, and librarians (memory controllers) respond at different speeds.

| Real-World Element | ARM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The King | ARM CPU Core |
| The Royal Messengers | Address & Data Buses |
| The Library Building | External Memory (SRAM, DRAM, Flash) |
| Lead Time for Slow Books | **Wait States** |
| Stack of Pre-fetched Pages | **Cache Memory** |
| Secretary Holding Outgoing Mail | **Write Buffer** |
| Royal Postal Codebook | **Memory Map** |
| Priority Dispatch Letters | **Non-Sequential Access (N-cycle)** |
| Routine Consecutive Letters | **Sequential Access (S-cycle)** |

The ARM Memory Interface is essentially the **"Royal Postal Rules"** — a strict set of protocols that ensures information flows reliably between the King (CPU) and the Library (Memory), no matter how slow or fast the librarian responds.

---

#### 1.3 The Three Fundamental Memory Cycle Types

Every memory transaction the ARM processor issues belongs to one of **three canonical cycle types**:

> [!NOTE]
> **N-cycle (Non-Sequential)** → First access to a new memory region. The full address must be placed on the bus and a complete handshake is performed.
>
> **S-cycle (Sequential)** → Access to a memory location adjacent to the previous one. Faster because the upper address bits are already latched.
>
> **I-cycle (Internal)** → No external memory transaction is required (e.g., cache hit, register-only operation). Bus is idle.

---

#### 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Bus Bandwidth** — Maximum data transfer rate, measured in bytes per second.
- **Bus Latency** — Time delay between request and data delivery.
- **Wait State** — Extra clock cycle inserted to accommodate slow memory.
- **Memory Mapping** — Assignment of physical addresses to memory-mapped devices.
- **Little-Endian** — Least Significant Byte (LSB) stored at the lowest memory address (ARM default).
- **Big-Endian** — Most Significant Byte (MSB) stored at the lowest memory address.
- **AMBA** — Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture, the open-standard bus protocol used by ARM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 2.1 ARM Memory Interface — Signal Architecture

The ARM memory interface is composed of **three logical groups** of signals:

#### 2.1.1 Address Group
- **A[31:0]** — 32-bit address bus, capable of addressing **4 GB** of memory space.
- **nTRANS** — Not-TRANsfer / User mode indicator (distinguishes user from privileged accesses).
- **PROT[1:0]** — Protection bits for MMU/Memory Protection Unit (MPU).
- **LOCK** — Locked transaction signal for atomic read-modify-write operations.

#### 2.1.2 Data Group
- **D[31:0]** — 32-bit bidirectional data bus.
- **nWAIT** — Wait-state request from slow memory to extend bus cycle.
- **nRESET** — System reset.

#### 2.1.3 Control Group
- **nOE** — Output Enable (read strobe).
- **nWE** — Write Enable (write strobe).
- **nCS / nCS[3:0]** — Chip Select lines for bank selection.
- **MAS[1:0]** — Memory Access Size (8, 16, or 32-bit).
- **BL[3:0]** — Byte Lane enables for sub-word writes.

---

### 2.2 ARM Bus Cycle Types — Detailed Behavior

#### 2.2.1 Non-Sequential Cycle (N-cycle)
- The processor places a **new full address** on the bus.
- It requests a memory access by asserting the appropriate control signals.
- This is the **slowest** cycle because the full address must be latched externally.
- **Typical use:** Instruction fetch from a new cache line, jump targets, stack frame setup.

#### 2.2.2 Sequential Cycle (S-cycle)
- The processor **assumes** the upper address bits remain unchanged from the previous cycle.
- Only the lower address bits are updated (often incremented).
- **Typical use:** Linear instruction fetches, sequential array access.
- This is the **fastest** external cycle.

#### 2.2.3 Internal Cycle (I-cycle)
- No external bus activity at all.
- The processor uses this time for register operations, cache lookups, or branch resolution.
- **Critical for performance** — modern ARM cores hide latency behind I-cycles.

---

### 2.3 Memory Access Timing Model

A complete memory access transaction consists of **four distinct phases**:

1. **Address Phase** — Processor drives the address and control signals.
2. **Turn-Around Phase** — Bus drivers switch direction (read vs. write).
3. **Data Phase** — Actual data transfer occurs.
4. **Recovery Phase** — Bus returns to idle before the next cycle.

The ARM allows **wait states** to be inserted between phases to accommodate slow memory:

$$
T_{access} = T_{address} + T_{wait} + T_{data} + T_{recovery}
$$

Where:
- $T_{access}$ = total access time in clock cycles
- $T_{wait}$ = number of wait states inserted
- $T_{address}$, $T_{data}$, $T_{recovery}$ = fixed pipeline overheads

---

### 2.4 Memory Hierarchy in ARM-Based Systems

ARM systems employ a **multi-level memory hierarchy** to balance speed, cost, and capacity:

| Level | Component | Typical Size | Access Time | Cost/bit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | CPU Registers | 32 × 32-bit | ~1 ns | Very High |
| L2 | Cache (SRAM) | 8 KB – 64 KB | ~5 ns | High |
| L3 | Main Memory (DRAM) | 16 MB – 1 GB | ~50 ns | Low |
| L4 | Secondary Storage (Flash/SSD) | MB – TB | ~µs–ms | Very Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Principle:** The Memory Interface is the **bridge between L2 (Cache) and L3 (Main DRAM)**. The interface must be carefully designed so that the "memory wall" — the growing gap between CPU speed and DRAM speed — does not bottleneck overall system performance.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Address Space | $A_{space} = 2^{N_{addr}}$ | $N_{addr}$ = number of address bits |
| Bus Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{W \times f_{clk}}{N_{cycles}}$ | $W$ = bus width (bits), $f_{clk}$ = clock (Hz), $N_{cycles}$ = cycles per access |
| Memory Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{D_{bytes}}{T_{total}}$ | Useful bytes per total transaction time |
| Access Time (with wait states) | $T_{access} = T_{base} + N_{WS} \times T_{clk}$ | $T_{base}$ = base cycle count, $N_{WS}$ = wait states |
| Effective Clock for Memory | $f_{eff} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{1 + N_{WS}}$ | Slowed equivalent clock considering waits |
| Memory Size from Chip | $S = 2^{rows} \times 2^{cols} \times W_{bus}$ | For array-organized memory chips |
| Little-Endian Byte Address | $A_{byte} = A_{word} + i$ | Where $i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ for byte offset |
| Cache Hit Rate | $H = \dfrac{H_{hits}}{H_{hits} + H_{misses}}$ | Fraction of accesses served from cache |
| Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) | $T_{AMAT} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \times T_{mem}$ | $T_{cache}$ = cache access, $T_{mem}$ = main memory access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorize the **Bandwidth formula** and the **AMAT formula** — these are the two most frequently tested equations in Module 3 of PEBMT741.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ARM Memory Interface is not an academic abstraction — it underpins every embedded device:

- **Automotive ECUs** — Memory-mapped CAN controllers, sensor buffers
- **Smartphones** — Big.LITTLE heterogeneous cores with shared L3 cache interface
- **IoT Edge Devices** — Low-power flash interfaces for firmware
- **Industrial PLCs** — Dual-bank memory for fail-safe firmware updates
- **Medical Devices** — Deterministic memory access for real-time signal processing

The interface design determines **determinism, jitter, and worst-case latency** — all critical in safety-critical and real-time embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Bus Bandwidth

**Problem Setup:** An ARM7TDMI processor operates at a clock frequency $f_{clk}$ and has a 32-bit data bus. Memory access requires $N$ clock cycles per transfer. Derive the maximum sustainable bus bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Define bandwidth conceptually.**
Bandwidth is the rate at which useful data crosses the bus boundary:

$$
BW = \frac{\text{Data transferred per transaction}}{\text{Time per transaction}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express data per transaction.**
For a 32-bit bus, each transfer moves $W = 32$ bits = $4$ bytes.

$$
\text{Data per transaction} = W = 32 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Express time per transaction.**
Each transaction takes $N_{cycles}$ clock cycles, and each clock cycle lasts $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ seconds.

$$
\text{Time per transaction} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into bandwidth equation.**

$$
BW = \frac{W}{N_{cycles} \times T_{clk}} = \frac{W \times f_{clk}}{N_{cycles}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**
For $f_{clk} = 66 \text{ MHz}$, $W = 32$ bits, and $N_{cycles} = 2$ cycles (1 address + 1 data, no wait):

$$
BW = \frac{32 \times 66 \times 10^6}{2} = 1056 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} = 1056 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to MB/s.**

$$
BW = \frac{1056}{8} = 132 \text{ MB/s}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Impact of Wait States on Effective Performance

**Setup:** An ARM processor runs at $f_{clk} = 50 \text{ MHz}$. A base memory access takes $T_{base} = 2$ cycles. Slow ROM requires $N_{WS} = 4$ wait states. Compute the effective access time and the performance penalty compared to zero-wait-state memory.

**Step 1 — Total access time without wait states.**

$$
T_{fast} = T_{base} \times T_{clk} = 2 \times \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 40 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Total access time with wait states.**

$$
T_{slow} = (T_{base} + N_{WS}) \times T_{clk}
$$

$$
T_{slow} = (2 + 4) \times \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 6 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 120 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Performance penalty ratio.**

$$
\text{Penalty} = \frac{T_{slow}}{T_{fast}} = \frac{120}{40} = 3\times
$$

This means every ROM access is **3 times slower** than an ideal SRAM access. This single number justifies why **instruction caches** exist — to avoid repeatedly fetching from slow ROM.

**Step 4 — Effective bandwidth reduction.**

$$
BW_{fast} = \frac{32 \times 50 \times 10^6}{2} = 800 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
BW_{slow} = \frac{32 \times 50 \times 10^6}{6} = 266.67 \text{ Mbps} = 33.33 \text{ MB/s}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) with Cache

**Setup:** An ARM9 system has an L1 cache with $T_{cache} = 5$ ns and a hit rate $H = 0.92$. Main DRAM access time is $T_{mem} = 50$ ns. Compute the AMAT.

**Step 1 — Write the AMAT equation.**

$$
T_{AMAT} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \times T_{mem}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
T_{AMAT} = 5 + (1 - 0.92) \times 50
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the miss penalty component.**

$$
(1 - 0.92) \times 50 = 0.08 \times 50 = 4 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$
T_{AMAT} = 5 + 4 = 9 \text{ ns}
$$

This is the **single most important performance number** in the system — it tells you the *average* time the CPU spends waiting for memory per access.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Types vs. ARM Interface Characteristics

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM | SDRAM | Flash (NOR) | Flash (NAND) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Access Time | 5–10 ns | 50–70 ns | 7–10 ns | 70–100 ns | 25–50 µs |
| Volatile? | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Refresh Required? | No | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Read Bandwidth | Very High | Moderate | High | Moderate | High (burst) |
| Write Bandwidth | Very High | Moderate | High | Slow (program) | Slow (page prog.) |
| ARM Use Case | Cache, internal SRAM | Main memory | Main memory | Boot ROM, firmware | Mass storage |
| Interface Complexity | Low | High (RAS/CAS) | Moderate | Moderate | High (ECC) |
| Random Byte Access | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (page-based) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** An ARM-based system has the following specifications:
- Processor clock: $f_{clk} = 100 \text{ MHz}$
- 32-bit external data bus
- Memory access takes 1 N-cycle + 1 S-cycle for sequential accesses
- Sequential access takes 2 cycles total
- Non-sequential access takes 3 cycles total
- Cache hit rate $H = 0.85$
- Cache access time $T_{cache} = 10 \text{ ns}$

**Find:**
(a) Peak bus bandwidth
(b) Average access time considering cache hits/misses
(c) Total memory transactions per second in a sequential loop

#### Solution

**(a) Peak bus bandwidth** — Use the fastest access (sequential, 2 cycles):

$$
BW_{peak} = \frac{32 \times 100 \times 10^6}{2} = 1600 \text{ Mbps} = 200 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**(b) Average memory access time** — Use AMAT formula:

$$
T_{AMAT} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \times T_{mem}
$$

First, compute $T_{mem}$ (assume non-sequential DRAM access, 3 cycles):

$$
T_{mem} = 3 \times \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 30 \text{ ns}
$$

Then:

$$
T_{AMAT} = 10 + (1 - 0.85) \times 30 = 10 + 4.5 = 14.5 \text{ ns}
$$

**(c) Sequential loop transaction rate** — Each iteration of a sequential loop uses 1 S-cycle after the initial N-cycle. For a loop of $L$ iterations:

$$
N_{trans} = 1 + L \text{ transactions}
$$

Total time:

$$
T_{total} = T_{N-cycle} + L \times T_{S-cycle}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 30 + L \times 20 \text{ ns}
$$

For $L = 100$:

$$
T_{total} = 30 + 100 \times 20 = 2030 \text{ ns} = 2.03 \text{ µs}
$$

Effective throughput:

$$
\Theta = \frac{100 \text{ words}}{2.03 \text{ µs}} \approx 49.26 \text{ million words/sec}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

### 4.1 ARM Memory Interface — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpu["ARM CPU CORE"]
    cache["L1 CACHE UNIFIED"]
    wb["WRITE BUFFER"]
    mmu["MMU OR MPU"]
    busctrl["BUS CONTROL UNIT"]
    abu["ADDRESS BUS A31-A0"]
    dbu["DATA BUS D31-D0"]
    ctl["CONTROL SIGNALS nWE nOE nCS"]
    sram["EXTERNAL SRAM BANK 0"]
    dram["EXTERNAL DRAM BANK 1"]
    flash["EXTERNAL FLASH BANK 2"]
    periph["MEMORY MAPPED PERIPHERALS"]

    cpu --> cache
    cache --> wb
    cache --> mmu
    mmu --> busctrl
    busctrl --> abu
    busctrl --> dbu
    busctrl --> ctl

    abu --> sram
    abu --> dram
    abu --> flash
    abu --> periph

    dbu --> sram
    dbu --> dram
    dbu --> flash
    dbu --> periph

    ctl --> sram
    ctl --> dram
    ctl --> flash
    ctl --> periph
```

---

### 4.2 ARM Bus Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> NonSeq: New address needed
    NonSeq --> Seq: Address is consecutive
    NonSeq --> Wait: nWAIT asserted
    Seq --> Seq: Still consecutive
    Seq --> Wait: nWAIT asserted
    Wait --> Seq: nWAIT deasserted
    Wait --> NonSeq: New address boundary
    Seq --> Idle: Burst complete
    NonSeq --> Idle: Single access done
    Idle --> [*]
```

---

### 4.3 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["REGISTERS 32 X 32 BIT 1 NS"]
    L2["L1 CACHE 8 TO 64 KB 5 NS"]
    L3["MAIN MEMORY 16 MB TO 1 GB 50 NS"]
    L4["FLASH STORAGE 1 MB TO 1 GB 100 NS"]
    L5["EXTERNAL STORAGE SD CARD GB TO TB MS"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

---

### 4.4 Memory Read Transaction Timing (with Wait States)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as System Clock
    participant ARM as ARM Processor
    participant MEM as External Memory
    participant BUS as Data Bus

    CLK->>ARM: T1 Rising Edge
    ARM->>BUS: Address valid A31-A0
    ARM->>MEM: nOE asserted nCS active
    MEM-->>ARM: nWAIT low requesting wait
    CLK->>ARM: T2 Rising Edge
    Note over ARM,MEM: Wait state T2 inserted
    CLK->>ARM: T3 Rising Edge
    MEM-->>ARM: nWAIT released
    CLK->>ARM: T4 Rising Edge
    MEM->>BUS: Data D31-D0 driven
    ARM->>MEM: nOE deasserted read complete
    CLK->>ARM: T5 Rising Edge
    Note over ARM: Recovery cycle
```

---

### 4.5 Little-Endian vs Big-Endian Byte Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LE["LITTLE ENDIAN LSB AT LOW ADDR"]
        LE0["A+0 LSB"]
        LE1["A+1"]
        LE2["A+2"]
        LE3["A+3 MSB"]
        LE0 --> LE1 --> LE2 --> LE3
    end

    subgraph BE["BIG ENDIAN MSB AT LOW ADDR"]
        BE0["A+0 MSB"]
        BE1["A+1"]
        BE2["A+2"]
        BE3["A+3 LSB"]
        BE0 --> BE1 --> BE2 --> BE3
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Explain the three types of bus cycles used in the ARM memory interface.

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

The ARM memory interface uses **three types of bus cycles** to communicate with external memory:

1. **Non-Sequential Cycle (N-cycle):** Issued when a new memory address is required that is not adjacent to the previous one. The full 32-bit address is placed on the bus, and control signals are asserted. This is the **slowest** cycle because address latching and bus arbitration are required.

2. **Sequential Cycle (S-cycle):** Issued for accesses where the address is sequential to the previous one (e.g., the next word in a linear instruction stream or array). Only the lower address bits change, making it **faster** than an N-cycle.

3. **Internal Cycle (I-cycle):** No external bus activity occurs. The CPU uses this cycle for register operations, cache hits, or branch prediction. It effectively **hides memory latency** by overlapping computation with potential memory access.

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- Correct identification of all three cycle types — **1 mark**
- Clear distinction between them — **1 mark**
- Mention of performance/usage context — **1 mark**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Differentiate between Little-Endian and Big-Endian memory formats with a suitable example for a 32-bit value `0x12345678`.

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

In a **32-bit system**, the value `0x12345678` occupies four consecutive byte addresses (A, A+1, A+2, A+3).

| Address | Little-Endian | Big-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| A | **0x78** (LSB) | **0x12** (MSB) |
| A+1 | 0x56 | 0x34 |
| A+2 | 0x34 | 0x56 |
| A+3 | **0x12** (MSB) | **0x78** (LSB) |

- **Little-Endian** stores the **Least Significant Byte** at the **lowest** address.
- **Big-Endian** stores the **Most Significant Byte** at the **lowest** address.
- **ARM default** is Little-Endian (ARMv4 and later support both via the `B` (Big-endian) bit in the System Control Coprocessor).

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- Correct byte layout for either format — **1 mark**
- Correct byte layout for the second format — **1 mark**
- ARM-specific context — **1 mark**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the ARM memory interface. Discuss the role of address bus, data bus, control signals, and the bus control unit. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An ARM processor operates at 50 MHz with a 32-bit external data bus. Calculate the peak bandwidth when:
   (i) Memory requires 0 wait states
   (ii) Memory requires 2 wait states
   (iii) Memory requires 4 wait states
Comment on the design implications. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — ARM Memory Interface Architecture [7 marks]**

The ARM memory interface is a **layered hardware subsystem** that connects the CPU core to external memory and peripherals. It consists of the following components:

**1. Address Bus (A[31:0])**
A 32-bit unidirectional bus capable of addressing **4 GB** of memory space. The bus control unit drives the address during the address phase of each bus cycle.

**2. Data Bus (D[31:0])**
A 32-bit bidirectional bus for transferring data to/from memory. Bus turn-around logic is required between read and write cycles to prevent contention.

**3. Control Signals**
- `nOE` — Output Enable (asserted during reads)
- `nWE` — Write Enable (asserted during writes)
- `nCS[3:0]` — Chip Select lines for bank selection
- `MAS[1:0]` — Memory Access Size (byte, half-word, word)
- `BL[3:0]` — Byte Lane enables
- `nWAIT` — Wait state request from memory
- `LOCK` — Locked atomic transaction

**4. Bus Control Unit (BCU)**
Acts as the **master controller** — sequences address, control, and data phases, inserts wait states, and resolves bus arbitration between CPU, DMA, and external masters.

**5. Write Buffer**
Buffers write transactions so the CPU can continue executing without stalling on slow memory writes.

**6. MMU/MPU (optional)**
Translates virtual to physical addresses and enforces memory protection.

| Component | Function | Mark |
|---|---|---|
| Block diagram correctly drawn | 2 | 2 |
| Address bus explained | 1 | 1 |
| Data bus explained | 1 | 1 |
| Control signals listed and explained | 2 | 2 |
| BCU role explained | 1 | 1 |

---

**Part (b) — Bandwidth Calculations [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $f_{clk} = 50 \text{ MHz}$
- $W = 32$ bits

**Formula:**

$$
BW = \frac{W \times f_{clk}}{T_{base} + N_{WS}}
$$

Assume base access time $T_{base} = 2$ cycles (address + data phase, no wait).

**(i) Zero wait states ($N_{WS} = 0$):**

$$
BW_0 = \frac{32 \times 50 \times 10^6}{2 + 0} = 800 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**(ii) Two wait states ($N_{WS} = 2$):**

$$
BW_2 = \frac{32 \times 50 \times 10^6}{2 + 2} = 400 \text{ Mbps} = 50 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**(iii) Four wait states ($N_{WS} = 4$):**

$$
BW_4 = \frac{32 \times 50 \times 10^6}{2 + 4} = 266.67 \text{ Mbps} \approx 33.33 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Design Implications:**

| Wait States | Bandwidth | % of Peak | Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 100 MB/s | 100% | Ideal for caches, internal SRAM |
| 2 | 50 MB/s | 50% | Acceptable for moderate-speed Flash |
| 4 | 33.3 MB/s | 33% | Heavy penalty — needs caching |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to **add $T_{base}$** to $N_{WS}$ in the denominator. The correct formula uses the **total** cycles, not just the wait states. Mark deduction: **−1 mark** for each wrong substitution.

---

### QUESTION B
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the three memory cycle types (N-cycle, S-cycle, I-cycle) in the ARM architecture. Discuss how they affect system performance. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An ARM system uses an L1 cache with the following parameters:
   - Cache hit rate $H = 0.90$
   - Cache access time $T_{cache} = 8$ ns
   - Main memory access time $T_{mem} = 60$ ns
Calculate:
   (i) The Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)
   (ii) The performance improvement (speedup) over a system without cache
   (iii) The new AMAT if the hit rate is improved to 95% using a better algorithm **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Memory Cycle Types [7 marks]**

| Cycle Type | Behavior | When Used | Performance Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N-cycle (Non-Sequential)** | Full new address issued | Branch target, first array element | Slowest — full bus arbitration |
| **S-cycle (Sequential)** | Only low-order address changes | Linear code, sequential array elements | Faster — upper bits latched |
| **I-cycle (Internal)** | No external bus activity | Cache hit, register-only operations | Effectively free — hidden latency |

**Performance Implications:**

- The ARM processor's **instruction pipeline** attempts to overlap I-cycles with N/S-cycles of subsequent instructions.
- A program dominated by N-cycles (e.g., linked-list traversal, pointer chasing) suffers high memory latency.
- A program dominated by S-cycles (e.g., tight loops over arrays) achieves near-peak bus bandwidth.
- I-cycles allow the CPU to hide cache miss latency through **out-of-order execution** and **non-blocking loads**.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct description of all three cycles — **3 marks**
- Performance implications discussed — **4 marks**

---

**Part (b) — AMAT and Performance Calculations [7 marks]**

**Formula:**

$$
T_{AMAT} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \times T_{mem}
$$

**(i) AMAT at $H = 0.90$:**

$$
T_{AMAT} = 8 + (1 - 0.90) \times 60 = 8 + 0.10 \times 60 = 8 + 6 = 14 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) Speedup over no-cache system:**
Without cache, every access takes $T_{mem} = 60$ ns.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{no-cache}}{T_{AMAT}} = \frac{60}{14} \approx 4.29\times
$$

**(iii) New AMAT at $H = 0.95$:**

$$
T_{AMAT,new} = 8 + (1 - 0.95) \times 60 = 8 + 0.05 \times 60 = 8 + 3 = 11 \text{ ns}
$$

**Performance comparison:**

| Configuration | AMAT | Relative Speed |
|---|---|---|
| No cache | 60 ns | 1.00× (baseline) |
| Cache at 90% hit | 14 ns | 4.29× |
| Cache at 95% hit | 11 ns | 5.45× |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often compute **miss penalty** incorrectly. The miss penalty is $(1 - H) \times T_{mem}$, **not** $T_{mem}$ alone. Also, do not confuse **AMAT** with **miss penalty** — they are different quantities. Mark deduction: **−2 marks** for using $T_{mem}$ directly as miss penalty.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for ARM Memory Interface**

- [x] **Three Cycle Types:** Non-Sequential (N), Sequential (S), Internal (I) — know definitions cold.
- [x] **AMBA Protocol:** The bus standard underlying all ARM memory interfaces.
- [x] **Key Signals:** A[31:0], D[31:0], nWE, nOE, nCS, nWAIT, LOCK, nRESET.
- [x] **Bandwidth Formula:** $BW = \dfrac{W \times f_{clk}}{N_{cycles}}$ — most frequently tested equation.
- [x] **Wait States Impact:** Each wait state **linearly reduces** effective bandwidth.
- [x] **AMAT Formula:** $T_{AMAT} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \times T_{mem}$ — second most tested equation.
- [x] **Endianness:** ARM defaults to **Little-Endian**; Big-Endian selectable via System Control Coprocessor.
- [x] **Memory Hierarchy:** Registers → Cache → Main DRAM → Flash → Storage.
- [x] **Write Buffer Role:** Decouples CPU from slow write memory — improves write throughput.
- [x] **Cache Justification:** Without cache, slow ROM/DRAM would starve the fast CPU (memory wall problem).
- [x] **Pipeline + I-cycles:** The ARM pipeline hides memory latency by issuing I-cycles when external memory is busy.
- [x] **Address Space:** 32-bit ARM = **4 GB** addressable memory space.
- [x] **Atomic Operations:** The `LOCK` signal ensures indivisible read-modify-write for semaphores.
- [x] **Memory-Mapped I/O:** Peripherals are addressed in the same space as memory — no separate I/O instructions needed.
- [x] **Cache Coherency:** Important in multiprocessor ARM systems — DMA must snoop the cache.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — ARM Memory Interface**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBMT741 — Advanced Microprocessors and Microcontrollers*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** is an open-standard, on-chip interconnect specification originally developed by **ARM Holdings** in **1996**. It defines the methodology for connecting and managing functional blocks (cores, memory controllers, peripherals, DSPs, custom logic) within a System-on-Chip (SoC). The architecture prescribes a layered bus hierarchy optimized for high performance, low power, and design reusability. The AMBA specification is *bus-protocol-agnostic* in its philosophy, meaning it does not dictate a single wire format; instead, it standardizes the **handshake, arbitration, transfer types, and signal timing** between IP (Intellectual Property) blocks. The currently active revisions are **AMBA 2.0, AMBA 3.0, AMBA 4.0, and AMBA 5.0 (CHI Issue B)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module-3 emphasis is on the **AMBA bus family** — namely **ASB, AHB, and APB** — which together form the classic *backbone + local segment* SoC design pattern. AXI/ACE/CHI are treated as advanced extensions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "City Bus Network" Imagine a busy modern city: - **The CPU / Processor Core** → a high-speed **bullet train station** (extremely fast, but expensive tracks). - **The AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** → the **multi-lane expressway** connecting bullet-train stations, main airports, and downtown. Fast, multi-lane, but expensive to build and high in power consumption. - **The ASB (Advanced System Bus)** → the older **state highway** — single-lane, slightly slower, but fully compatible and easy to retrofit. - **The APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** → the **local feeder bus / auto-rickshaw lane** that connects peripheral offices (timers, UARTs, keypads, GPIOs). It is cheap, low-speed, and ultra-low-power. - **The Bus Bridge (AHB-to-APB Bridge)** → the **interchange** that allows expressway traffic to safely step down into the local lanes. - **Arbitration** → the **traffic police** at every interchange deciding which vehicle (master) gets the green light. > **Why this matters:** Without such a structured hierarchy, a CPU would be directly burdened by slow devices (a UART does not need GHz-speed signaling). AMBA formalizes this "expressway + local lane" design discipline. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants The following figures are *protocol-defined* and appear frequently in KTU questions: | Metric | Value (Standard) | Significance | |---|---|---| | Typical AHB data bus width | **32 / 64 / 128 / 256 bits** | High-throughput transport | | Typical APB data bus width | **8 / 16 / 32 bits** | Low-power peripheral interface | | AMBA 2.0 release | **1996** | Origin of AHB, ASB, APB | | AMBA 3.0 release | **2003** | Introduced AXI, ATB | | AMBA 4.0 release | **2011** | Introduced ACE, CHI, AXI4 | | AMBA 5.0 release | **2015** | Introduced CHI Issue B | | Max AHB frequency (typical SoC) | **~150–200 MHz** | Single-edge clocking | > [!NOTE] > AMBA is a **specification**, not a product. Royalty-free licensing makes it the de-facto industry standard in > **1 billion** shipped SoCs per year, used by **Samsung, Qualcomm, Apple, NXP, STMicroelectronics, Texas Instruments, and MediaTek**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Classic AMBA 2.0 AMBA 2.0 — the most commonly tested version in KTU — formalizes **three** distinct bus protocols that work together inside a typical SoC. ### 2.1.1 AHB — Advanced High-performance Bus The **AHB** is the high-bandwidth, high-clock-frequency system backbone. It is the *only* bus in classic AMBA that supports pipelined, burst, and split transactions. **Key Properties of AHB:** - Single-cycle **bus master handover** (zero wait-state arbitration). - **Burst transfers** of 4, 8, and 16 beats (incremental and wrapping bursts). - **Split transactions** to free the bus during long peripheral latencies. - **Non-tri-state** implementation (uses muxes — easier to synthesize on modern CMOS). - Wide data bus (32-bit to 256-bit), supports byte, half-word, word transfers. - Support for **locked transfers** (atomic read-modify-write). - Big-endian and little-endian byte ordering supported. **Typical AHB Master/Slave examples:** - *Masters:* ARM Cortex-A series CPU, DMA controller, LCD controller, Ethernet MAC, Cryptographic engine. - *Slaves:* External memory controller, internal SRAM, AHB-to-APB bridge. ### 2.1.2 ASB — Advanced System Bus The **ASB** is the *older* alternative to AHB, included for legacy and low-complexity designs. It uses a **tri-state bus** mechanism (shared wires with high-impedance states) and is therefore harder to synthesize on deep-submicron processes. **Key Properties of ASB:** - Pipelined, burst-supporting. - **Tri-state implementation** (unlike AHB). - Lower maximum performance than AHB. - Retained for backward compatibility in older ARM cores (ARM7TDMI used ASB). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse AHB and ASB. Remember: **AHB = Non-Tri-State (Mux-based) + Higher Performance**, while **ASB = Tri-State (Wire-shared) + Legacy**. ### 2.1.3 APB — Advanced Peripheral Bus The **APB** is the *low-power, low-complexity* peripheral bus. It is a **non-pipelined, non-burst** simple bus designed for register-mapped peripherals that have low-bandwidth requirements. **Key Properties of APB:** - **Non-pipelined**, low power. - Latched address/control — bus signal only changes at the start of a transfer. - Simple 4-phase protocol: **IDLE → SETUP → ACCESS (one cycle) → IDLE**. - Optimized for **peripherals that only need register reads/writes** (timers, UART, GPIO, I²C controllers, watchdogs). - Connected to the high-speed system via a single **AHB-to-APB bridge** (the "interchange"). - No arbitration needed — the bridge is the only master on APB. ### 2.1.4 The AHB-to-APB Bridge — "The Interchange" The **bridge** is the critical glue logic. It performs: 1. **Protocol translation** (AHB pipelined burst → APB non-pipelined single). 2. **Address decoding** — decides which APB peripheral is being accessed. 3. **Wait-state insertion** — APB slaves may insert wait states without affecting AHB masters. 4. **Data-width adaptation** (e.g., AHB 32-bit → APB 8-bit peripheral). ## 2.2 AMBA Version Evolution (Critical for KTU 2-Mark Questions) | Version | Year | Key Additions | |---|---|---| | **AMBA 1.0** | 1996 | Original ASB + APB | | **AMBA 2.0** | 1999 | Added **AHB**; de-facto industry baseline | | **AMBA 3.0** | 2003 | Added **AXI** (Advanced eXtensible Interface), **ATB** (Advanced Trace Bus) | | **AMBA 4.0** | 2011 | Added **AXI4**, **ACE** (Coherency), **CHI** (Coherent Hub Interface), **AXI4-Lite**, **AXI4-Stream** | | **AMBA 5.0** | 2015 | **CHI Issue B**; lower-power, larger systems | ## 2.3 Typical AMBA SoC Topology ``` +--------------------+ | ARM Cortex CPU | (AHB Master) +---------+----------+ | v +-----------------+------------------+ | AHB System Bus | (Backbone, 32-256 bit) +-----------------+------------------+ | | +---------v------+ +-----v--------+ | DMA Engine | | AHB-to-APB | +---------------+ | Bridge | +------+-------+ | +------v-------+ | APB Local | (Peripheral bus) +------+-------+ | +--------+--------+----+--------+ v v v v +-------+ +-------+ +--------+ +---------+ | UART | | TIMER | | GPIO | | WATCHDOG| +-------+ +-------+ +--------+ +---------+ ``` ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Hierarchy | Question | Engineering Reasoning | |---|---| | Why not connect all peripherals directly to AHB? | AHB is **expensive in silicon area and power**; peripherals are slow. | | Why not use APB for everything? | APB cannot serve CPU/DMA bandwidth needs (no burst, no pipelining). | | Why a bridge instead of direct connection? | Bridges **decouple clock domains** and **shield AHB masters** from slow APB wait states. | | Why muxes for AHB, tri-state for ASB? | Muxes scale better to **deep-submicron (≤ 65 nm)** processes; tri-state buses have wire-delay problems. | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are essential for any **Part B (14-mark)** numerical problem on AMBA bus performance. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Bus Bandwidth** | $BW = f_{clk} \times W_{bus} \times E$ | bits/sec (or $\times \frac{1}{8}$ for Bytes/sec) | $f_{clk}$ = bus clock, $W_{bus}$ = data width, $E$ = efficiency (0–1) | | 2 | **Bus Transfer Rate (Beat Rate)** | $R = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | transfers/sec | One transfer per clock edge | | 3 | **Cycle Time** | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds | Fundamental clock period | | 4 | **Burst Transfer Time** | $T_{burst} = N_{beats} \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | $N_{beats}$ = number of beats (e.g., 4, 8, 16) | | 5 | **Burst Data Volume** | $V_{burst} = N_{beats} \times W_{bus}$ | bits | Total payload per burst | | 6 | **Effective Throughput with Wait States** | $BW_{eff} = \dfrac{W_{bus} \times f_{clk}}{1 + N_{ws}}$ | bits/sec | $N_{ws}$ = avg wait states/transfer | | 7 | **Bridge Latency Overhead** | $L_{bridge} = 2 \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | APB bridge adds ~2 cycles per access | | 8 | **Address Decoding (Peripheral Select)** | $A_{decoded} = A_{addr} \;\&\; \;(\sim A_{mask})$ | index | Standard 4 KB boundary addressing | | 9 | **AMBA Power Ratio (typical)** | $P_{APB} \approx 0.3 \cdot P_{AHB}$ | normalized | APB ≈ **30% of AHB** power | > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute value / magnitude operators have been rendered using `\times` and `\approx` rather than the vertical bar `|`, to preserve markdown table integrity. In your own written exams, use standard `|x|` notation. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mobile SoCs (Apple A-series, Snapdragon)**: AMBA CHI/AXI interconnect at > 1 GHz clocks with > 1 TB/s aggregate bandwidth. - **Automotive MCUs (NXP S32K3, TI Jacinto)**: AHB + APB for safety-critical real-time systems. - **IoT Edge Devices (Cortex-M0/M4)**: AHB-Lite + APB for ultra-low-power sensor hubs. - **FPGA Soft-Cores (Xilinx MicroBlaze, Altera Nios II)**: AMBA bus fabric generated automatically by vendor tools. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section provides fully-worked engineering derivations of the formulas from Section 2, in the order most likely to be required in a KTU 14-mark Part-B question. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bus Bandwidth as a Function of Clock and Width ### Problem Setup A 32-bit AHB bus is clocked at $f_{clk} = 100 \; \text{MHz}$. The bus is **fully efficient** ($E = 1.0$, no wait states, no idle cycles). Compute the **peak bandwidth** in both **Mbps** and **MB/s**. ### Step 1: Identify Variables - $W_{bus} = 32 \; \text{bits}$ - $f_{clk} = 100 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Hz}$ - $E = 1.0$ - Units of $BW$ requested: $\text{Mbps}$ and $\text{MB/s}$ ### Step 2: Apply the Bandwidth Equation $$ \begin{aligned} BW &= f_{clk} \times W_{bus} \times E \\[4pt] &= (100 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Hz}) \times (32 \; \text{bits}) \times (1.0) \\[4pt] &= 3.2 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/sec} \\[4pt] &= 3{,}200 \; \text{Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ > *Conversion logic:* 1 bit/second = $10^{-6}$ Mbps, so $3.2 \times 10^{9}$ bits/sec $= 3.2 \times 10^{3}$ Mbps $= 3{,}200$ Mbps. ### Step 3: Convert to MB/s $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{MB/s} &= \frac{BW_{bps}}{8} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3.2 \times 10^{9}}{8} \; \text{Bytes/sec} \\[4pt] &= 4.0 \times 10^{8} \; \text{Bytes/sec} \\[4pt] &= 400 \; \text{MB/s} \end{aligned} $$ ### Result $$ \boxed{BW = 3{,}200 \; \text{Mbps} = 400 \; \text{MB/s}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Throughput with Wait States ### Problem Setup The same 32-bit AHB bus now experiences an **average of 2 wait states per transfer** due to slow memory. Compute the **effective bandwidth**. ### Step 1: Apply the Wait-State Formula $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{eff} &= \frac{W_{bus} \times f_{clk}}{1 + N_{ws}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{32 \times 100 \times 10^{6}}{1 + 2} \; \text{bits/sec} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3.2 \times 10^{9}}{3} \; \text{bits/sec} \\[4pt] &= 1.0667 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/sec} \\[4pt] &= 1{,}066.67 \; \text{Mbps} \\[4pt] &\approx 133.33 \; \text{MB/s} \end{aligned} $$ ### Result $$ \boxed{BW_{eff} \approx 1{,}066.67 \; \text{Mbps} \approx 133.33 \; \text{MB/s}} $$ ### Interpretation Wait states of 2 reduced throughput from 100% to **33.3%** — a 3× penalty. This is why AMBA prefers **burst transfers** (single address, multiple data beats) to amortize the wait-state cost. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Burst Transfer Time and Throughput ### Problem Setup AHB performs an **8-beat INCR4 burst** at $f_{clk} = 100 \; \text{MHz}$, $W_{bus} = 32 \; \text{bits}$, with **1 wait state per beat**. Compute: - (a) Total burst duration - (b) Effective data rate - (c) Bus utilization ### Step 3.3a: Burst Duration Each beat takes $(1 + N_{ws}) \times T_{clk}$ seconds: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{beat} &= (1 + 1) \times T_{clk} = 2 \times T_{clk} \\[4pt] &= 2 \times \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} \; \text{sec} \\[4pt] &= 2 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{sec} = 20 \; \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ > *Note:* The first beat of an AHB burst also requires **one address phase cycle** = 1 extra $T_{clk}$. So total = 1 (address) + 8 × 2 (data) = 17 cycles. Total burst: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{burst} &= T_{clk} + N_{beats} \times T_{beat} \\[4pt] &= 10 \; \text{ns} + 8 \times 20 \; \text{ns} \\[4pt] &= 10 \; \text{ns} + 160 \; \text{ns} \\[4pt] &= 170 \; \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3.3b: Effective Data Rate $$ \begin{aligned} V_{burst} &= 8 \times 32 = 256 \; \text{bits} = 32 \; \text{Bytes} \\[4pt] R_{eff} &= \frac{V_{burst}}{T_{burst}} = \frac{32 \; \text{Bytes}}{170 \; \text{ns}} \\[4pt] &= 1.882 \times 10^{8} \; \text{Bytes/sec} \\[4pt] &\approx 188.24 \; \text{MB/s} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3.3c: Bus Utilization $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{T_{data \; payload}}{T_{burst}} = \frac{8 \times 1 \times T_{clk}}{T_{burst}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{8 \times 10 \; \text{ns}}{170 \; \text{ns}} = \frac{80}{170} \\[4pt] &= 0.4706 \approx 47.06\% \end{aligned} $$ > *Engineering insight:* With 1 wait state, an 8-beat burst only achieves ~47% utilization. **In practice, AHB slaves are designed to be zero-wait-state** for the first 4 beats of INCR4 to maintain > 90% efficiency. ### Result Summary $$ \boxed{T_{burst} = 170 \; \text{ns} \quad ; \quad R_{eff} \approx 188.24 \; \text{MB/s} \quad ; \quad U \approx 47\%} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bridge Cross-Domain Latency ### Problem Setup An **AHB-to-APB bridge** is required to read a 16-bit register from a UART. The AHB runs at **100 MHz** and the APB runs at **50 MHz**. Compute: - (a) Bridge latency in AHB clock cycles - (b) Total time in nanoseconds for the entire transaction ### Step 1: Bridge Pipeline Stages A typical AHB-to-APB bridge operates in **two AHB clock cycles** for protocol conversion: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{bridge} &= 2 \times T_{AHB} = 2 \times \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} \; \text{sec} \\[4pt] &= 2 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{sec} = 20 \; \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: APB Setup + Access APB needs 1 SETUP cycle (APB clock) + 1 ACCESS cycle (APB clock): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{APB} &= 2 \times T_{APB\_clk} = 2 \times \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} \\[4pt] &= 2 \times 20 \; \text{ns} = 40 \; \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Total Transaction Time $$ \begin{aligned} T_{total} &= L_{bridge} + T_{APB} \\[4pt] &= 20 \; \text{ns} + 40 \; \text{ns} = 60 \; \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ ### Result $$ \boxed{T_{total} = 60 \; \text{ns for one 16-bit APB read via AHB}} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Feature Comparison Table | Feature | **AHB** | **ASB** | **APB** | **AXI** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Data Width | 32/64/128/256 | 8/16/32 | 8/16/32 | 32/64/128/256/512/1024 | | Pipelining | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes (multi-channel) | | Burst Transfers | Yes (4/8/16) | Yes (limited) | **No** | Yes (up to 256 beats) | | Split Transactions | Yes | No | **No** | Yes (out-of-order) | | Bus Wire Style | **Non-tri-state (mux)** | **Tri-state** | Latched | Channelized | | Out-of-Order Completion | No | No | **No** | **Yes** | | Max Frequency (typical) | 150 MHz | 75 MHz | 50 MHz | > 1 GHz | | Power (normalized) | 1.0 | 0.85 | **~0.30** | 1.1–1.4 | | Typical Use | CPU, DMA, Memory | Legacy CPU bridges | UART, GPIO, Timers | High-perf SoCs | | AMBA Version | **2.0 (1999)** | **1.0 (1996)** | **1.0 (1996)** | **3.0 (2003)** | | Channels / Interfaces | HRDATA, HWDATA | RDATA, WDATA | PRDATA, PWDATA | 5 independent | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Per the protocol safeguards, all node identifiers are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters), and quoted labels contain raw uppercase text only — no markdown formatting tags, no reserved keywords. ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AMBA-Based SoC ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef master fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef bus fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef bridge fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef slave fill:#374151,stroke:#374151,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef periph fill:#9333ea,stroke:#9333ea,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px CPU["ARM CORTEX CPU CORE
AHB MASTER"]:::master DMA["DMA CONTROLLER
AHB MASTER"]:::master ETH["ETHERNET MAC
AHB MASTER"]:::master AHB["AHB SYSTEM BACKBONE
32 64 128 256 BIT
100 TO 200 MHZ"]:::bus SRAM["INTERNAL SRAM
AHB SLAVE"]:::slave EMC["EXTERNAL MEMORY
CONTROLLER AHB SLAVE"]:::slave BRIDGE["AHB TO APB BRIDGE
GLUE LOGIC"]:::bridge APB["APB PERIPHERAL BUS
LOW POWER 50 MHZ"]:::bus UART["UART CONTROLLER
APB SLAVE"]:::periph TIMER["TIMER MODULE
APB SLAVE"]:::periph GPIO["GPIO BANK
APB SLAVE"]:::periph WDT["WATCHDOG TIMER
APB SLAVE"]:::periph CPU --> AHB DMA --> AHB ETH --> AHB AHB --> SRAM AHB --> EMC AHB --> BRIDGE BRIDGE --> APB APB --> UART APB --> TIMER APB --> GPIO APB --> WDT ``` ## 4.2 AMBA Version Evolution Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef v1 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef v2 fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef v3 fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef v4 fill:#9333ea,stroke:#9333ea,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef v5 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px V1["AMBA 1 DOT 0
YEAR 1996
ASB AND APB"]:::v1 V2["AMBA 2 DOT 0
YEAR 1999
ADDS AHB"]:::v2 V3["AMBA 3 DOT 0
YEAR 2003
ADDS AXI AND ATB"]:::v3 V4["AMBA 4 DOT 0
YEAR 2011
ADDS AXI4 ACE CHI"]:::v4 V5["AMBA 5 DOT 0
YEAR 2015
CHI ISSUE B LOW POWER"]:::v5 V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 ``` ## 4.3 AHB Master–Slave Interconnect with Arbiter & Decoder ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef master fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef infra fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef slave fill:#374151,stroke:#374151,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px M1["MASTER 1
CPU"]:::master M2["MASTER 2
DMA"]:::master M3["MASTER 3
ETHERNET"]:::master ARB["AHB ARBITER
GRANTS BUS ACCESS"]:::infra DEC["ADDRESS DECODER
SELECTS TARGET SLAVE"]:::infra MUX["AHB READ DATA MUX
NON TRI STATE"]:::infra S1["SLAVE 1
SRAM"]:::slave S2["SLAVE 2
EXT MEMORY CTRL"]:::slave S3["SLAVE 3
AHB APB BRIDGE"]:::slave M1 --> ARB M2 --> ARB M3 --> ARB ARB --> DEC DEC --> S1 DEC --> S2 DEC --> S3 S1 --> MUX S2 --> MUX S3 --> MUX MUX --> ARB ``` ## 4.4 APB State Machine — 4-Phase Protocol ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR classDef state fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef active fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px [*] --> IDLE IDLE --> SETUP : PSEL rises
PENABLE low SETUP --> ACCESS : PENABLE high
data transfer ACCESS --> IDLE : transfer done
PSEL falls ACCESS --> SETUP : next transfer
back to back class IDLE state class SETUP,ACCESS active ``` > **Diagram Note:** The APB protocol always returns to **IDLE** between *unrelated* transfers, but consecutive transfers to the *same* peripheral can transition directly from **ACCESS → SETUP**, which is why APB is called *quasi-static*. ## 4.5 Three-Bus Comparison — Signal-Level View ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef ahb fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef asb fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef apb fill:#9333ea,stroke:#9333ea,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px AHB_BOX["AHB
HCLK HADDR HRDATA HWDATA
HREADY HRESP HBURST HLOCK

BCLK BA BSDATA BWDATA
