TRI STATE WIRE SHARED"]:::asb APB_BOX["APB
PCLK PADDR PRDATA PWDATA
PSEL PENABLE PWRITE
NON PIPELINED SIMPLE"]:::apb AHB_BOX -.COMPATIBLE WITH.-> ASB_BOX AHB_BOX --> APB_BOX ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions are 3 marks each and test direct, factual recall. ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define AMBA. List the three bus types defined in the AMBA 2.0 specification.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** AMBA stands for **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture**. It is an open-standard on-chip bus protocol specification developed by **ARM Holdings** for connecting functional blocks (CPU, memory, peripherals) inside a System-on-Chip. The three bus types defined in **AMBA 2.0** are: 1. **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** — high-speed system backbone. 2. **ASB (Advanced System Bus)** — legacy alternative to AHB, tri-state. 3. **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** — low-power peripheral bus. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming AHB/APB, 1 mark for ASB.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between AHB and APB. Mention any three points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | S.No. | AHB | APB | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Pipelined** protocol | **Non-pipelined** protocol | | 2 | Supports **burst** transfers (4, 8, 16 beats) | **No burst** support | | 3 | Higher **power consumption** | **Low power** consumption | | 4 | Wide data bus (32–256 bits) | Narrow data bus (8/16/32 bits) | | 5 | Needs **arbitration** (multiple masters) | **No arbitration** (bridge is sole master) | *(Any 3 points × 1 mark each = 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part-B question offers an internal choice. Both choices must be attempted as **OR** options. ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Detailed AMBA Architecture `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]` **Q.A. (a) Explain the AMBA bus architecture in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the roles of AHB, ASB, and APB and the AHB-to-APB bridge. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Introduction (1 mark)** AMBA is an open on-chip bus protocol standard by ARM, enabling modular SoC design with re-usable IP blocks. **2. AHB – Advanced High-performance Bus (2 marks)** - The **backbone** of the SoC. - Pipelined, supports bursts, split transactions, and locked transfers. - Non-tri-state implementation using muxes — scales to deep-submicron. - Connects high-speed masters (CPU, DMA) to high-speed slaves (SRAM, memory controller). - Wide data bus (32–256 bit), single-edge clocking. **3. ASB – Advanced System Bus (1 mark)** - Legacy predecessor of AHB, used in older ARM cores (ARM7TDMI). - Tri-state implementation — simpler conceptually but harder to synthesize. - Lower performance, retained only for compatibility. **4. APB – Advanced Peripheral Bus (1 mark)** - Optimized for **low-speed, low-power peripherals** (UART, Timer, GPIO, WDT). - Non-pipelined, no bursts, no arbitration. - 4-state protocol: IDLE → SETUP → ACCESS → IDLE. - Latched address/control signals reduce switching power. **5. AHB-to-APB Bridge (1 mark)** - Performs **protocol translation**, address decoding, wait-state insertion, and data-width adaptation. - Single APB master; multiple APB slaves connected to it. **6. Block Diagram (1 mark)** — refer to Section 4.1 above. --- **Q.A. (b) Compare the features of AHB, ASB, and APB in a tabular form. Discuss the design rationale for using a layered bus architecture in modern SoCs. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Comparison Table (4 marks)** — refer to Section 3.5 (Analytical Matrix). **2. Design Rationale for Layered Bus Architecture (3 marks):** - **Performance Isolation** — high-bandwidth traffic (CPU↔Memory) is decoupled from low-bandwidth traffic (CPU↔GPIO), preventing peripheral slowdowns from stalling the CPU. - **Power Optimization** — AHB runs at 100–200 MHz on full bus width; APB runs at 50 MHz on narrow width. Power ratio is approximately **3.3 : 1** in favor of APB. - **Design Reusability** — IP vendors (ARM Synopsys, Cadence) ship pre-verified APB peripherals; integrators just attach them to the bridge. - **Clock Domain Isolation** — bridge allows APB to run at a different (lower) clock, saving power. - **Cost Scaling** — wide high-speed buses consume significant silicon area; restricting them to the backbone saves die cost. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]` **Q.B. (a) Describe the AMBA AHB protocol in detail. Explain its signal set, transfer types, and burst mechanisms. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. AHB Protocol Overview (1 mark)** AHB is the high-speed, pipelined, burst-capable bus in AMBA 2.0. It uses two distinct phases per beat — **address phase** (1 cycle) and **data phase** (1+ cycles, possibly with wait states). **2. AHB Signal Set (2 marks):** - **HCLK** — bus clock. - **HRESETn** — active-low reset. - **HADDR[31:0]** — 32-bit address bus. - **HTRANS[1:0]** — transfer type: IDLE, BUSY, NON-SEQ, SEQ. - **HBURST[2:0]** — burst type: SINGLE, INCR, WRAP4, INCR4, WRAP8, INCR8, WRAP16, INCR16. - **HSIZE[2:0]** — transfer size: 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024 bits. - **HWRITE** — read/write direction. - **HWDATA[31:0]** — write data bus. - **HRDATA[31:0]** — read data bus. - **HREADY** — high = data ready, low = insert wait state. - **HRESP[1:0]** — response: OKAY, ERROR, RETRY, SPLIT. - **HMASTLOCK** — locked transfer. - **HPROT[3:0]** — protection bits (user/privileged, bufferable, cacheable). - **HSELx** — slave select (one-hot). - **HMASTER[3:0]** — current master ID (for SPLIT/CONTINUE). **3. Transfer Types (2 marks):** - **IDLE** — no transfer; default when no master wants the bus. - **BUSY** — master is performing an internal operation; data phase delayed. - **NON-SEQ** — first beat of a burst (address unrelated to previous). - **SEQ** — subsequent beats of a burst (sequential address). **4. Burst Mechanisms (2 marks):** - **INCR (Unspecified Length)** — incrementing address, no pre-declared length; useful for streaming. - **INCR4 / INCR8 / INCR16** — fixed-length incrementing bursts (4, 8, 16 beats). - **WRAP4 / WRAP8 / WRAP16** — wrapping bursts that automatically wrap at a power-of-2 boundary; ideal for cache line fills (e.g., WRAP4 on 16-byte cache line = 4×32-bit beats). - **SINGLE** — single beat with HTRANS = NON-SEQ. - The address of beat $i$ = $A_{base} + i \times \text{HSIZE}$ (byte addressing). - For WRAP bursts, the boundary is $\text{WRAP\_size} \times N_{beats}$ (e.g., WRAP4 of HSIZE=word wraps at 16 bytes). --- **Q.B. (b) With a neat diagram, explain the AMBA APB protocol. Discuss the role and operation of the AHB-to-APB bridge. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. APB Protocol (3 marks):** APB is a non-pipelined, low-power, latched peripheral bus. Its state machine is: $$ \text{IDLE} \xrightarrow{\text{PSEL} \uparrow,\; \text{PENABLE}=0} \text{SETUP} \xrightarrow{\text{PENABLE}=1} \text{ACCESS} \rightarrow \text{IDLE} $$ - **IDLE** — bus is idle; PSEL=0, PENABLE=0. - **SETUP** — address (PADDR) and control signals (PWRITE, PSEL) are latched for 1 cycle. - **ACCESS** — PENABLE high; data is transferred on PRDATA / PWDATA. - APB slaves can hold **PREADY low** to insert wait states. **Signal set (1 mark):** PCLK, PRESETn, PADDR, PSEL, PENABLE, PWRITE, PWDATA, PRDATA, PREADY, PSLVERR. **2. AHB-to-APB Bridge (3 marks):** Refer to the state-machine diagram in Section 4.4. The bridge: - Decodes the AHB HADDR against an address map to select one APB peripheral. - Translates AHB pipelined burst reads/writes into APB non-pipelined single transfers. - Inserts appropriate wait states on HREADY so that AHB masters see a uniform protocol. - Performs data-width adaptation (e.g., AHB 32-bit ↔ APB 8-bit UART register). **3. Diagram (1 mark)** — refer to Section 4.1 / 4.3 above. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing AHB with ASB (–2 marks).** AHB is **non-tri-state, mux-based**; ASB is **tri-state, wire-shared**. Examiners specifically test this. > 2. **Forgetting the bridge (–2 marks).** Every APB answer must mention the **AHB-to-APB bridge** and its translation role. Many students draw APB peripherals connected *directly* to AHB — *this is incorrect*. > 3. **Skipping the calculation in numericals (–3 marks).** In bandwidth/throughput problems, you must show the substitution step **before** the final answer. Writing only the final number loses process marks. > 4. **Wrong units in bandwidth (–1 mark).** Always state whether the answer is in **Mbps, MB/s, or KB/s**. Examiners deduct for ambiguous units. > 5. **Skipping the address phase in burst calculations (–2 marks).** AHB bursts always have **1 address cycle + N data cycles**, not just N data cycles. > 6. **Using `|` (vertical bar) in markdown tables.** In written exams this is fine, but if your answer is structured in a table, use $\times$ for multiply to avoid parsing issues. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print and Pin to Your Wall** ### Must-Know Definitions - **AMBA** = Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (ARM, 1996). - **AHB** = Advanced High-performance Bus (AMBA 2.0, 1999). - **ASB** = Advanced System Bus (AMBA 1.0, 1996) — *legacy, tri-state*. - **APB** = Advanced Peripheral Bus (AMBA 1.0, 1996) — *low-power*. - **AXI** = Advanced eXtensible Interface (AMBA 3.0, 2003) — *pipelined, 5 channels*. - **ACE** = AXI Coherency Extension (AMBA 4.0, 2011). - **CHI** = Coherent Hub Interface (AMBA 4.0+, Issue B in 5.0). ### Must-Know Distinctions - AHB uses **muxes** (non-tri-state); ASB uses **tri-state** wires. - APB is **non-pipelined, no bursts, no arbitration**; AHB is fully pipelined with bursts and arbitration. - The **AHB-to-APB bridge** is the **only APB master**; the bridge performs address decoding, wait-state translation, and data-width adaptation. - AMBA 2.0 is the *industry baseline*; AMBA 3.0+ introduced AXI for high-performance out-of-order SoCs. ### Must-Know Formulas - Bandwidth: $BW = f_{clk} \times W_{bus} \times E$ (bits/sec) - Wait-state throughput: $BW_{eff} = \dfrac{W_{bus} \times f_{clk}}{1 + N_{ws}}$ - Burst duration: $T_{burst} = T_{clk} + N_{beats} \times (1 + N_{ws}) \times T_{clk}$ - Power rule of thumb: $P_{APB} \approx 0.3 \times P_{AHB}$ ### Must-Remember Numbers - AHB: 32–256 bit, up to ~200 MHz - APB: 8/16/32 bit, typically ≤ 50 MHz - AHB burst lengths: **4, 8, 16** beats - AHB transfer types: **IDLE, BUSY, NON-SEQ, SEQ** - AHB burst types: **SINGLE, INCR, INCR4/8/16, WRAP4/8/16** - APB states: **IDLE → SETUP → ACCESS → IDLE** (4-phase) ### Must-Remember Real-World Use - **AMBA AHB-Lite** is used in **ARM Cortex-M** microcontrollers (STM32, NXP Kinetis, Nordic nRF). - **AMBA AXI/CHI** is used in **ARM Cortex-A** application processors (mobile SoCs). - **AMBA APB** is used for nearly **all low-bandwidth peripherals** across the industry. > **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When you see *“Explain AMBA architecture”* in a question, **always draw the three-bus block diagram first** (1 mark free). Then proceed with definitions, then comparisons. Examiners reward visual structure.

topics.title: ARM Classic/Cortex Based SoC: Basic System-on-Chip (SoC) Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Classic/Cortex Based SoC: Basic System-on-Chip (SoC) Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System-on-Chip (SoC) Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> A **System-on-Chip (SoC)** is a highly-integrated, complex integrated circuit (IC) that consolidates virtually **all the functional components of a complete electronic system** — including one or more processor cores (CPU), on-chip memory blocks, dedicated hardware accelerators, I/O controllers, peripheral interfaces, and a sophisticated on-chip bus interconnect — onto a **single silicon die** using deep-submicron VLSI fabrication technology.

In the **ARM ecosystem** specifically, an *ARM-based SoC* refers to any SoC whose central processing core(s) are derived from the ARM architecture family — either the legacy **ARM Classic** cores (ARM7TDMI, ARM9, ARM11) or the modern **ARM Cortex** cores (Cortex-A/-R/-M).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Modern Smartphone as an "Electronic City"**
> Imagine a complete city built on a single island. Instead of having separate buildings for the city hall, the post office, the school, the hospital, the power plant, and the roads scattered across a huge landmass, **everything is squeezed into one compact, self-sufficient urban block**.
>
> * The **ARM CPU core** = City Hall (governs decisions)
> * **On-chip SRAM/Cache** = Mayor's Office desk (ultra-fast scratchpad)
> * **External Memory Controller (DDR/LPDDR)** = Highway entrance to the outer world
> * **AMBA Bus Matrix** = Internal road network connecting all blocks
> * **Peripherals (UART, SPI, I²C, USB, GPIO, ADC, Timers, DMA)** = Service departments
> * **GPU / DSP / Hardware Accelerators** = Specialized factories
> * **Clock & Power Management Unit (PMU)** = City power grid and traffic lights
>
> Just as the city operates from one compact place, an SoC operates as a **complete computing system on a single chip**.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics in SoC Design

The following standard metrics define the performance envelope of any ARM-based SoC:

| Metric | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Process Technology Node** | **65 nm → 28 nm → 16 nm → 7 nm → 5 nm → 3 nm** |
| **Clock Frequency (ARM Cortex-A)** | **600 MHz → 3.0 GHz** |
| **Clock Frequency (ARM Cortex-M)** | **50 MHz → 400 MHz** |
| **On-chip SRAM Cache** | **32 KB → 8 MB L2 / L3** |
| **Package Pin Count** | **64 pins → 1500+ pins** |
| **Power Consumption (Mobile SoC)** | **0.5 W → 5 W** |
| **Die Area** | **10 mm² → 600 mm²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ARM dominates the SoC market:** The ARM business model is a *licensing model*. ARM Holdings designs the processor IP (Intellectual Property) and licenses it to hundreds of semiconductor companies (Qualcomm, Apple, Samsung, MediaTek, NXP, Texas Instruments, STMicroelectronics, etc.). Each licensee combines the ARM core with their own custom peripherals, accelerators, and memory to build a **unique SoC product**. This is why nearly every smartphone SoC (Snapdragon, Apple A-series, Exynos, Dimensity) is ARM-based.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Generic ARM-Based SoC — The Major Functional Blocks

An ARM Classic / Cortex based SoC is not a single CPU; it is a **miniature motherboard etched in silicon**. Let us dissect its anatomy in a structured, layer-by-layer manner:

### Layer 1 — Processing Subsystem
* **ARM CPU Core(s):** The "brain" of the SoC. Either a single core or a **multi-core cluster** (e.g., octa-core Cortex-A53/A55 + Cortex-A77).
* **Cache Hierarchy:** *L1 instruction cache (I-cache) + L1 data cache (D-cache)* — typically 16 KB to 64 KB each, private to the core. *L2 cache* — shared, 128 KB to several MB.
* **Memory Management Unit (MMU):** Present in Cortex-A (Linux/Android). Performs virtual-to-physical address translation using page tables.
* **Memory Protection Unit (MPU):** Lightweight alternative to MMU, found in Cortex-M and Cortex-R for real-time systems.

### Layer 2 — On-Chip Bus Interconnect (The "Road Network")
* **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture):** The de-facto on-chip bus standard defined by ARM Ltd.
* Common AMBA variants in SoCs:
  * **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface):** High-performance, multi-master, burst-based — used to connect CPU, DMA, and high-bandwidth peripherals.
  * **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus):** Medium-speed system backbone.
  * **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus):** Low-speed, low-power peripheral bus (UART, timers, GPIO, keypad).
* **Bus Matrix / Interconnect:** Hardware crossbar switch that arbitrates concurrent master access to multiple slaves.

### Layer 3 — Memory Subsystem
* **On-chip SRAM:** Tightly-coupled memory (TCM) or general-purpose scratchpad, single-cycle access.
* **External Memory Controller:** Provides interface to off-chip DDR SDRAM, LPDDR, NAND/NOR Flash, eMMC, SD cards.
* **Boot ROM:** Tiny mask-programmed ROM holding the first instructions executed on reset (bootloader).

### Layer 4 — Peripheral Subsystem
* **Communication Peripherals:** UART, SPI, I²C, I²S, USB, CAN, Ethernet MAC.
* **Human-Machine Interface (HMI):** GPIO, LCD controller, touch-screen controller, camera interface (CSI).
* **Analog Mixed-Signal (AMS):** ADC, DAC, comparators, PLLs, oscillators.
* **Timers & Counters:** General-purpose timers, watchdog, real-time clock (RTC), PWM channels.
* **DMA Controllers:** Offload bulk data movement from the CPU.
* **Crypto / Security Accelerators:** AES, SHA, RSA, True Random Number Generator (TRNG) for secure boot and encryption.

### Layer 5 — System Control & Power Management
* **Clock Control Unit (CCU) / Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs):** Generate all internal clock frequencies.
* **Power Management Unit (PMU):** Implements DVS (Dynamic Voltage Scaling), DFS (Dynamic Frequency Scaling), and multiple **power domains** with retention/isolation.
* **Reset Controller & Interrupt Controller (GIC/NVIC):** Centralized or core-local interrupt priority management.
* **JTAG / SWD Debug Interface:** On-chip debug access (IEEE 1149.1 boundary scan).

## 2.2 ARM Classic vs. ARM Cortex — The Two Generations

| Feature | **ARM Classic Family** | **ARM Cortex Family** |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture Version** | ARMv4T → ARMv6 (32-bit) | ARMv6-M → ARMv8.2-A (32/64-bit) |
| **Example Cores** | ARM7TDMI, ARM9, ARM11 | Cortex-M0, M3, M4, M7, R4, R5, A7, A9, A53, A57, A72, A75, A76, A77, A78, X1 |
| **Pipeline** | 3-stage (ARM7) → 8-stage (ARM11) | 2-stage (M0) → 11+ stage out-of-order (A77) |
| **Target Domain** | Early mobile, embedded | IoT, MCU, automotive, smartphones, servers |
| **ISA Extensions** | Basic ARM/Thumb | Thumb-2, NEON, SIMD, SVE, SVE2, TrustZone, Crypto |
| **Notable Application** | Nokia 3310 (ARM7), iPhone 2G (ARM11) | STM32, Raspberry Pi, Snapdragon, Apple A-series |

## 2.3 The Three Cortex Profiles — Why They Exist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ARM explicitly split the Cortex family into **three distinct "profiles"** to target the radically different requirements of the market:

* **Cortex-A (Application profile):** High-performance, MMU-equipped, runs rich OS (Linux, Android, iOS). Used in smartphones, tablets, smart-TVs, edge-AI gateways.
* **Cortex-R (Real-time profile):** Deterministic, low-latency, MPU-equipped. Used in automotive ECUs, 5G baseband, hard-disk controllers, storage.
* **Cortex-M (Microcontroller profile):** Ultra-low-power, deterministic interrupt latency, MPU, no MMU. Used in IoT sensors, wearables, motor control, drones, STM32-class MCUs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Clock Period** | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds | $f_{clk}$ in Hz |
| **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million instr/sec | $\text{CPI}$ = Cycles Per Instruction |
| **AMBA AHB Bus Width** | $32$ or $64$ | bits | Standard in SoCs |
| **AMBA AXI Burst Length** | $1$ to $16$ beats | beats | Per single transaction |
| **APB Bus Width** | $32$ | bits | Low-power peripheral bus |
| **AMBA APB Frequency Ratio** | $f_{AHB} = N \times f_{APB}$ (typically $N=1$ or $N=2$) | ratio | Bridge handles the division |
| **Cache Read Latency (L1)** | $\approx 1$ to $4$ | clock cycles | Core-local |
| **Cache Read Latency (L2)** | $\approx 8$ to $20$ | clock cycles | Shared / unified |
| **Main Memory (DDR) Latency** | $\approx 50$ to $200$ | clock cycles | Off-chip DRAM access |
| **Dynamic Power (CMOS)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Watts | $\alpha$ = switching activity |
| **Static Power (Leakage)** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | Watts | Dominates in low-nm nodes |
| **SoC Area Estimate** | $A_{die} \approx \dfrac{A_{core} + A_{cache} + A_{peri}}{\text{Yield} \cdot \text{Util}}$ | $\text{mm}^{2}$ | Routing utilization included |
| **Memory Bandwidth Required** | $BW = N_{cores} \times \text{IPC} \times f_{clk} \times \text{Bytes/Inst}$ | bytes/sec | For streaming workloads |
| **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)** | $f \propto V_{dd}$ | linear approx | Used in DVFS governors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$ equation is the single most important formula in modern SoC design. The **quadratic dependence on $V_{dd}$** is why smartphone SoCs aggressively reduce voltage during idle periods (a Cortex-A53 running at 0.6 V draws $\approx 36\times$ less dynamic power than at 1.0 V). This is the foundation of modern **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** governors in Android/iOS.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relating SoC Performance to Clock, CPI, and ISA

Let us derive the relationship between **instructions per second**, **clock frequency**, and **cycles per instruction** as used by an SoC designer when benchmarking a target workload.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
* $I$ = total number of executed instructions for a benchmark program
* $N_{clk}$ = total number of clock cycles consumed by those instructions
* $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency of the ARM core in Hz
* $\text{CPI}$ = average cycles per instruction

**Step 1: Definition of CPI**

The average number of clock cycles consumed per executed instruction is, by definition:

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{N_{clk}}{I}
$$

**Step 2: Solving for total clock cycles**

Multiplying both sides by $I$:

$$
N_{clk} = I \times \text{CPI}
$$

**Step 3: Time to execute the program**

If the core runs at $f_{clk}$ cycles per second, the total execution time $T_{exec}$ in seconds is:

$$
T_{exec} = \frac{N_{clk}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{I \times \text{CPI}}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Step 4: Introducing MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second)**

By definition, MIPS counts the number of millions of instructions completed per second:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{I}{T_{exec} \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 5: Substituting $T_{exec}$**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{I}{\left(\dfrac{I \times \text{CPI}}{f_{clk}}\right) \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 6: Cancel $I$ from numerator and denominator**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}
$$

This is the **final KTU-standard formula** for the theoretical MIPS rating of an ARM core.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An ARM Cortex-A53 core in your SoC runs at $f_{clk} = 1.2 \text{ GHz}$ and achieves an average $\text{CPI} = 2$ on a JPEG decoding workload. Calculate the MIPS rating.

**Solution (each step shown):**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.2}{2} \times 10^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MIPS} = 0.6 \times 10^{3} = 600 \text{ MIPS}}
$$

The Cortex-A53 can therefore theoretically execute **600 million instructions per second** on this workload.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SoC Dynamic Power and the DVFS Energy Saving

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Standard CMOS dynamic power equation**

The dynamic power consumed by a CMOS gate (and, by extension, the entire SoC core) is:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

where:
* $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (fraction of transistors that toggle per cycle)
* $C$ = total switched capacitance (in Farads)
* $V_{dd}$ = supply voltage (in Volts)
* $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency (in Hz)

**Step 2: Relationship between $V_{dd}$ and $f_{clk}$**

In a deeply-pipelined ARM core, the maximum usable clock frequency is approximately linearly proportional to the supply voltage (ignoring short-channel effects for simplicity):

$$
f_{clk} \approx k \cdot V_{dd}
$$

where $k$ is a technology-dependent constant (in Hz/Volt).

**Step 3: Substituting into $P_{dyn}$**

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot (k \cdot V_{dd}) = \alpha \cdot C \cdot k \cdot V_{dd}^{3}
$$

**Step 4: Energy per instruction**

Since $E = P \times t$ and $t = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$, the energy consumed per instruction is:

$$
E_{inst} = \frac{P_{dyn}}{f_{clk}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Voltage reduction benefit**

If $V_{dd}$ is reduced by a factor of $x$ (e.g., $x = 0.7$ for a 30% voltage drop), the energy per instruction scales as $x^{2}$:

$$
E_{inst}^{reduced} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot (x \cdot V_{dd})^{2} = x^{2} \cdot E_{inst}^{original}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Cortex-M4 core in an IoT SoC is running at $V_{dd} = 1.2 \text{ V}$. The DVFS governor scales $V_{dd}$ down to $0.84 \text{ V}$ (i.e., $x = 0.7$). What is the **percentage energy savings per instruction**?

**Solution:**

Voltage scaling factor: $x = 0.7$

Energy per instruction at new voltage:

$$
E_{inst}^{new} = x^{2} \cdot E_{inst}^{old} = (0.7)^{2} \cdot E_{inst}^{old} = 0.49 \cdot E_{inst}^{old}
$$

Therefore the **energy saved** is:

$$
\text{Savings} = (1 - 0.49) \times 100\% = 51\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{A 30% voltage reduction yields a 51% energy reduction per instruction.}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This non-linear energy benefit is *the* reason why modern smartphone SoCs (Snapdragon, Apple A-series) deploy **dozens of discrete DVFS operating points** (P-states). The OS scheduler picks the lowest voltage that still meets the deadline, giving the user hours of extra battery life. The cubic power dependence ($V^{3}$) means even tiny voltage reductions produce massive power savings.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AMBA Bus Selection in SoC Design

| Design Requirement | Recommended AMBA Bus | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Connect CPU to L2 cache / DDR controller | **AXI** (or CHI for coherent) | Burst-mode, high bandwidth, out-of-order completion |
| Connect DMA to high-speed peripherals (Ethernet, USB) | **AXI** or **AHB** | Multi-master support, pipelined |
| Connect to simple peripherals (UART, GPIO, Timers) | **APB** | Low power, simple protocol, low pin count |
| Connect two SoC blocks requiring cache coherency | **ACE / CHI** | ARM Coherent Extensions for big.LITTLE |
| Ultra-low-power IoT SoC (Cortex-M) | **APB** + **AHB-Lite** | Minimal silicon area and power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a typical SoC uses ALL THREE buses simultaneously:** A modern SoC uses a **layered bus architecture** — AXI/AHB for the high-speed backbone, then a bridge to APB for the slow peripherals. This is because driving a heavy protocol (AXI handshakes) into a simple UART would waste 99% of its cycles in idle. The APB bridge is a small piece of hardware that converts one AHB/AXI transaction into the equivalent APB cycles.
