## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **forget to mention the process node and clock frequency** when describing a Pi generation. Always state: **Core, ISA, Clock, Process Node, RAM type.**
> - Many students wrongly state that the **GPU is separate from the SoC** — it is **integrated into the Broadcom SoC** as a VideoCore block. This single error costs 2 marks.
> - For Pi 5 architecture questions, students forget the **RP1 southbridge** and the **PCIe link** — explicitly mention them.
> - Always differentiate between **GPIO voltage (3.3 V)** and **GPIO current limit (16 mA per pin)** — these are independent values frequently mixed up.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the evolution of the Raspberry Pi family from Pi 1 to Pi 5, highlighting the key improvements in CPU, RAM, and connectivity in each generation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the internal architecture of the Broadcom BCM2711 SoC used in Raspberry Pi 4. Explain the role of the AXI bus and unified memory architecture.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The Raspberry Pi family has undergone significant evolution over a decade, with each generation delivering meaningful performance and feature upgrades.

> **Step 1 (1 mark): Pi 1 Model B (2012)**
> - **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2835.
> - **CPU:** Single-core **ARM1176JZF-S** @ 700 MHz (ARMv6, 32-bit).
> - **RAM:** 256 MB (later 512 MB) SDRAM.
> - **Connectivity:** 2 × USB 2.0, 10/100 Ethernet, HDMI 1.4, no Wi-Fi/BT.
> - **GPIO:** 26-pin header.

> **Step 2 (1 mark): Pi 2 Model B (2015)**
> - **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2836.
> - **CPU:** Quad-core **Cortex-A7** @ 900 MHz (ARMv7-A, 32-bit).
> - **RAM:** 1 GB LPDDR2.
> - **Connectivity:** 4 × USB 2.0, 10/100 Ethernet, no Wi-Fi/BT.
> - **GPIO:** Expanded to 40-pin header.

> **Step 3 (1 mark): Pi 3 Model B/B+ (2016/2018)**
> - **SoC:** BCM2837 / BCM2837B0.
> - **CPU:** Quad-core **Cortex-A53** @ 1.2 GHz (B) / 1.4 GHz (B+) — first **64-bit ARMv8-A** Pi.
> - **RAM:** 1 GB LPDDR2.
> - **Connectivity:** Added 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi and Bluetooth 4.2 (B+ improved to 5 GHz).
> - **Ethernet:** 10/100 (B), 10/100 (B+ with improved throughput).

> **Step 4 (2 marks): Pi 4 Model B (2019)**
> - **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2711 (28 nm process).
> - **CPU:** Quad-core **Cortex-A72** @ 1.5/1.8 GHz — massive jump in performance.
> - **RAM:** 1/2/4/**8 GB LPDDR4**.
> - **Connectivity:** Dual **micro-HDMI 2.0** (4K@60), **2 × USB 3.0 + 2 × USB 2.0**, **Gigabit Ethernet**, **dual-band Wi-Fi**, **Bluetooth 5.0**, **USB-C power**.

> **Step 5 (2 marks): Pi 5 (2023)**
> - **SoC:** Broadcom **BCM2712** (16 nm process).
> - **CPU:** Quad-core **Cortex-A76** @ 2.4 GHz (ARMv8.2-A).
> - **RAM:** 4/8 GB **LPDDR4X** at 4267 MT/s.
> - **Connectivity:** Dual **micro-HDMI 2.1** (4K@60), **RP1 southbridge** for I/O, dual USB 3.0, **PCIe 2.0 ×1** internal link.
> - **Power:** USB-C 5V/5A (27 W) with **PMIC** (Power Management IC).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the block diagram of BCM2711 from Section 4.1 of these notes (4 × Cortex-A72 → L2 cache → AXI fabric → GPU / VPU / Memory Controller / Peripherals).

> **Explanation (4 marks):**

The Broadcom **BCM2711** is built on a **28 nm CMOS process** and integrates the following key blocks:

1. **CPU Subsystem (1 mark):** Four **ARM Cortex-A72** cores, each with private L1 caches (48 KB instruction + 32 KB data) and sharing a **1 MB L2 cache**. The cores implement the **ARMv8-A 64-bit ISA** with full MMU, virtualization extensions, and NEON SIMD.

2. **AXI Interconnect Fabric (1 mark):** The **AMBA AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)** is the central on-chip bus. It is **high-bandwidth, low-latency**, and supports **multiple master/slave** simultaneous transactions. The four CPU cores, GPU, VPU, and all peripheral controllers connect through this fabric.

3. **VideoCore VI GPU (1 mark):** The GPU is a **dual-core VideoCore VI** running at **500 MHz**, supporting **OpenGL ES 3.2** and hardware **H.265 decode** (4K@60). It also drives the **two HDMI 2.0 transmitters**.

4. **Unified Memory Architecture (1 mark):** The **LPDDR4 memory controller** is shared between the CPU and GPU. This **UMA** design means:
   - No separate VRAM chip is required (lower BOM cost).
   - The GPU dynamically allocates the memory it needs from the system pool.
   - Trade-off: CPU and GPU memory bandwidth is contended under heavy load.

> **Conclusion:** The BCM2711's strength lies in its **balanced heterogeneous design** — powerful CPU cores for general-purpose code, a capable GPU for media and graphics, all tied together by the **AXI fabric** on a single die.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between a microcontroller and a Raspberry Pi single-board computer. Explain with examples why Raspberry Pi is preferred for IoT gateway applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the Raspberry Pi 5 architecture. Highlight the role of the RP1 southbridge and the PCIe link connecting it to the main SoC.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter (1 mark each) | Microcontroller (e.g., 8051, STM32) | Raspberry Pi (SBC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Von Neumann / Harvard | Modified Harvard with MMU |
| **CPU Power** | 8-bit / 32-bit, 1–2 cores, 16–180 MHz | 32 / 64-bit, 4 cores, up to 2.4 GHz |
| **Memory** | 2 KB – 2 MB Flash, 4 KB – 256 KB SRAM | 512 MB – 8 GB LPDDR4X |
| **Operating System** | Bare metal or RTOS (FreeRTOS) | Full OS (Raspberry Pi OS, Ubuntu) |
| **Task Complexity** | Single or a few hard real-time tasks | Multi-tasking with multi-user GUI |
| **Cost** | ₹50 – ₹2,000 | ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 |
| **Connectivity** | Limited (UART, SPI, I²C) | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, BT, USB, HDMI, MIPI |

**Why Raspberry Pi is preferred for IoT Gateways (3 marks):**

1. **Multi-Protocol Support:** A Pi can simultaneously act as a **Modbus master** (over UART), **MQTT publisher** (over Wi-Fi/Ethernet), and **HTTP REST server** — impossible on a bare microcontroller without external chips.
2. **High-Level Language Programming:** Python, Node.js, Java, C/C++ all run natively — drastically reducing IoT application development time.
3. **Edge Intelligence:** Pi 4/5 can run **on-device ML inference** (TensorFlow Lite, OpenVINO) for anomaly detection in sensor streams before forwarding to cloud.
4. **Storage & Local Caching:** microSD/SSD support enables **local data buffering** during network outages.
5. **GUI & Remote Management:** Web-based dashboards (Grafana, Node-RED) run natively on the Pi.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Diagram (2 marks):** Use the Pi 5 architecture diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes.

> **Explanation (5 marks):**

The **Raspberry Pi 5 (2023)** introduced a **partitioned SoC architecture**, a major design departure from all previous Pi generations.

1. **Main SoC — BCM2712 (1.5 marks):** Built on a **16 nm process**, integrating:
   - Four **Cortex-A76** cores @ 2.4 GHz (ARMv8.2-A).
   - **VideoCore VII GPU** supporting OpenGL ES 3.1 and 4K HEVC decode.
   - **LPDDR4X memory controller** (32-bit, 4267 MT/s).
   - **PCIe 2.0 ×1 root complex** — the **only external I/O path**.

2. **RP1 Southbridge (1.5 marks):** A custom **40 nm I/O controller chip** designed by Raspberry Pi (the first time the Foundation designed its own silicon for the SBC line). It integrates:
   - 2 × USB 3.0 ports
   - 2 × USB 2.0 ports
   - Gigabit Ethernet MAC + PHY
   - 40-pin GPIO controller
   - I²C, SPI, UART, PWM, I²S controllers
   - microSD / SDIO interface
   - MIPI CSI-2 (camera) and DSI-2 (display) interfaces

3. **PCIe 2.0 ×1 Link (1.5 marks):** The BCM2712 and RP1 are connected by a single **PCIe 2.0 lane** providing **5 Gbps raw bandwidth** (~4 Gbps effective). This single lane carries **all** peripheral traffic of the system — a potential bottleneck for applications that saturate multiple USB 3.0 ports simultaneously.

4. **Power Management (0.5 marks):** A dedicated **PMIC (DA9091 by Renesas)** provides multiple voltage rails (0.8V, 1.0V, 1.8V, 3.3V) required by the BCM2712 and LPDDR4X, replacing the simple linear regulators of older models.

> **Conclusion:** The Pi 5's two-chip design is a forward-looking architectural choice — it allows the Foundation to **swap out the RP1 with newer I/O silicon** in future revisions without redesigning the main compute SoC.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read before every KTU exam:**

- ⭐ Raspberry Pi = **SBC** (Single-Board Computer), NOT a microcontroller. (The **Pico** is the only microcontroller in the family.)
- ⭐ The Pi is built on a **Broadcom SoC** that integrates **CPU + GPU + peripherals** on one die.
- ⭐ **CPU generations** to remember: ARM1176 → Cortex-A7 → Cortex-A53 → Cortex-A72 → **Cortex-A76** (Pi 5).
- ⭐ **64-bit ARMv8-A** was introduced with the **Pi 3**.
- ⭐ The Pi uses **unified memory architecture (UMA)** — CPU and GPU share LPDDR RAM.
- ⭐ Standard **40-pin GPIO header** is **3.3 V logic**, **NOT 5 V tolerant** — applying 5 V destroys the SoC.
- ⭐ Max GPIO current per pin: **16 mA**; total GPIO bank current limit: **~50 mA**.
- ⭐ **Pi 4 introduced USB 3.0 and dual micro-HDMI 2.0 (4K)** — major jump from Pi 3.
- ⭐ **Pi 5 introduced the RP1 southbridge** connected via **PCIe 2.0 ×1** (5 Gbps).
- ⭐ **Pi 5 CPU is Cortex-A76 @ 2.4 GHz**, built on **16 nm** process, paired with **LPDDR4X RAM**.
- ⭐ **OS:** Pi runs **Raspberry Pi OS (Debian-Linux)**; Pico runs **bare-metal C/C++/MicroPython**.
- ⭐ The Pi is widely used in **IoT gateways, edge AI, robotics, digital signage, home automation, and education**.
- ⭐ **Form factor:** Standard Pi is **85.6 × 56.5 mm** (credit card).
- ⭐ **Power:** Pi 4 uses **USB-C 5V/3A**; Pi 5 uses **USB-C 5V/5A** (27 W recommended).
- ⭐ **Boot source:** microSD card (default); Pi 5 also supports **USB and NVMe SSD boot** via RP1.
- ⭐ Always quote **CPU core, ISA, clock, process node, RAM type, and GPU** when describing any Pi generation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accessories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ACCESSORIES — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accessories** in the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** refer to the **on-chip peripheral subsystems** (also called **embedded resources** or **internal peripherals**) that are integrated within the controller die to support timing, communication, I/O expansion, and event handling without requiring external support chips.

The 8051 family classifies its accessories into **four major functional blocks**:

| # | Accessory | KTU-Syllabus Name | Primary Function |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Four 8-bit I/O Ports | **P0, P1, P2, P3** | Bidirectional parallel I/O |
| 2 | Two 16-bit Timers/Counters | **T0, T1** | Timing delays, event counting, baud-rate generation |
| 3 | Full-Duplex UART | **Serial Port** | Asynchronous/synchronous serial communication |
| 4 | Five-Source Interrupt Engine | **Interrupt System** | Hardware/software event-driven control |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEBMT741** syllabus, *Accessories* (Module 3) is **synonymous** with the 8051's **on-chip peripheral architecture**. The CO mapping is typically **CO3** (Understand the architecture and programming model of advanced microcontrollers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen Mixer" Model

Imagine a chef who owns a **single kitchen mixer-grinder**. The motor is the **CPU (ALU + Registers)**, but the real *work* of cooking is done by the **attachments (jars, blades, dough hooks)** — each accessory performs one specific task that the motor alone cannot do.

The 8051's **CPU** is the motor. Its **accessories** are:
- **I/O Ports** → the *control knobs* that let the chef operate the appliance.
- **Timers** → the *automatic timer dial* that turns the motor ON/OFF at preset intervals.
- **Serial Port** → the *whistle on the pressure cooker* that signals a remote listener.
- **Interrupts** → the *safety cutoff switch* that halts everything in an emergency.

> Just as the chef cannot bake a cake using only the motor (no timer, no temperature sensor, no whistle), a bare CPU cannot interact with the real world without its accessories.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are **frequently used** in KTU exam problems and **must be memorized**:

- **Oscillator Frequency Range:** $f_{osc} = \mathbf{1.2 \ MHz\ to\ 12\ MHz}$ (typical **11.0592 MHz** for serial communication)
- **Machine Cycle Period:** $T_{mc} = \mathbf{12 / f_{osc}}$ (each machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**)
- **Timer Increment Rate:** $T_{timer} = \mathbf{1\ \mu s}$ (when $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz)
- **ROM Size (8051):** **4 KB** on-chip program memory
- **RAM Size (8051):** **128 bytes** on-chip data memory
- **SFR Space:** **128 bytes** (addresses **0x80 – 0xFF**)
- **Register Banks:** **4 banks × 8 registers** = 32 bytes (addresses 0x00 – 0x1F)
- **Bit-Addressable Space:** **16 bytes** (0x20 – 0x2F) = **128 bits**

---

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the SFR Map of P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, PCON, IE, IP** — KTU questions directly test the bit-level structure of these registers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Accessory #1 — Parallel I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)

The 8051 has **four 8-bit bidirectional ports** mapped in the **SFR** region.

### 2.1.1 Port Structure Comparison

| Port | SFR Address | Internal Pull-up | Dual Function | KTU-Exam Note |
|------|:-----------:|:----------------:|---------------|---------------|
| **P0** | **0x80** | ❌ No (open drain) | AD0–AD7 (External Memory Bus) | Must use external pull-ups in bus mode |
| **P1** | **0x90** | ✅ Yes | General purpose I/O only | Simplest port — favorite exam topic |
| **P2** | **0xA0** | ✅ Yes | A8–A15 (High Address Bus) | Acts as high-address latch in external memory mode |
| **P3** | **0xB0** | ✅ Yes | RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD | **Alternate functions are HIGH-YIELD** |

### 2.1.2 P3 Alternate Functions (Must Memorize for KTU)

| P3 Bit | Alt. Function | Signal Direction | Pin Number (DIP-40) |
|:------:|---------------|------------------|:-------------------:|
| P3.0 | **RxD** | Input | 10 |
| P3.1 | **TxD** | Output | 11 |
| P3.2 | **INT0̄** | Input | 12 |
| P3.3 | **INT1̄** | Input | 13 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Input | 14 |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Input | 15 |
| P3.6 | **WR̄** | Output | 16 |
| P3.7 | **RD̄** | Output | 17 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a P3 alternate function is invoked (e.g., serial reception uses RxD), the corresponding latch bit **must be set to 1** in software. KTU questions test this nuance.

## 2.2 Accessory #2 — Timers/Counters (T0, T1)

### 2.2.1 Conceptual Model

A timer is a **16-bit up-counter register pair**:
- **Timer 0** = `TH0` (high byte) + `TL0` (low byte)
- **Timer 1** = `TH1` (high byte) + `TL1` (low byte)

It increments every **machine cycle** (timer mode) or every **external transition** on T0/T1 pin (counter mode), and **overflows from FFFFh → 0000h** setting the **TFx flag** in `TCON`.

### 2.2.2 The Four Operating Modes (TMOD Bits)

| Mode | M1 M0 | Operation | Max Count | KTU Note |
|:----:|:-----:|-----------|:---------:|----------|
| **0** | 0 0 | 13-bit timer (8-bit TH + 5-bit TL) | 8192 | Legacy 8048 mode |
| **1** | 0 1 | **16-bit timer** | 65536 | **Most-used — KTU favorite** |
| **2** | 1 0 | 8-bit auto-reload (TH → TL) | 256 | **Used for baud-rate generation** |
| **3** | 1 1 | Split timers (T0 only) | 256 each | Special, T1 stops |

### 2.2.3 TMOD Register (SFR 0x89) Layout

```
Bit:    7       6       5       4       3       2       1       0
     ┌───────┬───────┬───────┬───────┬───────┬───────┬───────┬───────┐
     │  GATE │ C/T̄  │  M1   │  M0   │  GATE │ C/T̄  │  M1   │  M0   │
     │  (T1) │  (T1) │  (T1) │  (T1) │  (T0) │  (T0) │  (T0) │  (T0) │
     └───────┴───────┴───────┴───────┴───────┴───────┴───────┴───────┘
```
- **GATE = 1** → Timer is hardware-controlled by **INTx̄** pin (used for pulse-width measurement).
- **GATE = 0** → Timer is software-controlled by `TRx` bit only (used for delays).
- **C/T̄ = 0** → Timer mode (internal clock).
- **C/T̄ = 1** → Counter mode (external pin).

### 2.2.4 Delay Calculation (THE Most-Tested Formula)

$$\text{Delay} = (65536 - \text{Initial Value}) \times T_{mc}$$

where the **initial value** is loaded into `THx:TLx`.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **repeating delay** (e.g., toggling a pin at 1 kHz), KTU often asks for the **total time including loop overhead**. The complete formula is:
> $$\text{Total Delay} = \text{Loop Count} \times \left[ (65536 - \text{Initial Value}) \times T_{mc} + \text{Overhead} \right]$$

## 2.3 Accessory #3 — Serial Port (UART)

### 2.3.1 Frame Format (Mode 1, Most Common)

```
┌─────┬─────────┬─────────┬─────────┬─────────┬─────────┬─────────┬─────┬─────┐
│START│   D0    │   D1    │   D2    │   D3    │   D4    │   D5    │  D6 │  D7 │STOP│
│  0  │   LSB   │         │         │         │         │         │     │ MSB │  1 │
└─────┴─────────┴─────────┴─────────┴─────────┴─────────┴─────────┴─────┴─────┘
        ←──────── 8 data bits (LSB first) ────────→                ← 1 stop bit
```

Total = **10 bits/frame** in Mode 1.

### 2.3.2 SCON Register (SFR 0x98) Layout

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Name | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** |
| Reset | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate |
|:---:|:---:|:----:|-------------|:---------:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | $f_{osc}/12$ (Fixed) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **8-bit UART** | **Timer 1 Overflow Rate** |
| 1 | 0 | 2 | 9-bit UART | $f_{osc}/32$ or $f_{osc}/64$ |
| 1 | 1 | 3 | 9-bit UART | Timer 1 Overflow Rate |

### 2.3.3 Baud-Rate Formula (Mode 1, KTU Hot Topic)

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

For **SMOD = 0** (default) and **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crystal **11.0592 MHz** is chosen because it produces **exact integer baud rates** (9600, 4800, 19200, …) with **integer TH1 values** — eliminating cumulative drift. KTU loves this numerical problem.

## 2.4 Accessory #4 — Interrupt System

### 2.4.1 Interrupt Vector Table (THE Most-Memorized Table in KTU)

| Vector | Address | Source | Flag | Type |
|:------:|:-------:|--------|:----:|------|
| **Reset** | **0x0000** | Power-on / RST pin | — | Highest Priority |
| External Int 0 | **0x0003** | P3.2 (INT0̄) | IE0 | Edge/Level triggered |
| Timer 0 Overflow | **0x000B** | TF0 flag | TF0 | Internal |
| External Int 1 | **0x0013** | P3.3 (INT1̄) | IE1 | Edge/Level triggered |
| Timer 1 Overflow | **0x001B** | TF1 flag | TF1 | Internal |
| Serial Port (TX/RX) | **0x0023** | TI/RI flag | TI/RI | Internal |

> [!NOTE]
> Each vector is **8 bytes** apart. KTU exam trick: *"What is the address of the ISR for Timer 1?"* → **0x001B** (must be exact, 3-mark short answer).

### 2.4.2 IE Register (SFR 0xA8) and IP Register (SFR 0xB8)

```
IE  (Interrupt Enable):   EA - - ES ET1 EX1 ET0 EX0
IP  (Interrupt Priority):  - - - PS PT1 PX1 PT0 PX0
```
- **EA** = Global Enable (must be 1 to enable ANY interrupt).
- **EX0/EX1** = Enable external interrupts 0/1.
- **ET0/ET1** = Enable timer overflow interrupts 0/1.
- **ES** = Enable serial port interrupt.
- Setting a bit in `IP` makes the corresponding interrupt **high priority** (it can interrupt a low-priority ISR).

### 2.4.3 Interrupt Processing Sequence (Step-by-Step)

1. Source sets the flag (e.g., `TF0 = 1`).
2. Hardware checks `IE` and priority (`IP` + natural priority).
3. If accepted, current PC is **pushed onto stack**.
4. Program Counter is loaded with the vector address.
5. ISR executes.
6. **`RETI` instruction** pops PC and clears the in-progress flag — the CPU resumes the main program.

---

## 📋 KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET (Pin This to Your Wall)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Default Values |
|---|---------|---------|:-----:|----------------|
| 1 | Machine Cycle | $T_{mc} = 12 / f_{osc}$ | seconds | 1 µs @ 12 MHz |
| 2 | Timer Delay (Mode 1) | $D = (65536 - N) \times T_{mc}$ | seconds | N = initial value |
| 3 | Auto-Reload (Mode 2) | $D = (256 - N) \times T_{mc}$ | seconds | THx holds N |
| 4 | Timer Overflow Rate | $f_{ov} = 1 / [(256 - TH1) \times T_{mc}]$ | Hz | — |
| 5 | Baud Rate (Mode 1, SMOD=0) | $B = f_{osc} / [384 \times (256 - TH1)]$ | bps | 11.0592 MHz crystal |
| 6 | Baud Rate (Mode 2, SMOD=1) | $B = f_{osc} / 32$ | bps | — |
| 7 | Sampling Rate (RxD) | $f_{smp} = 16 \times B$ | Hz | Receiver oversamples |
| 8 | INT Vector Spacing | Always **8 bytes** | bytes | 0x00, 0x08, 0x10, … |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** 8051 accessories form the backbone of embedded products — washing machines (timers), microwave ovens (interrupts), GPS modules (UART), and LED display boards (I/O ports). The same theory is used in modern **ARM Cortex-M** microcontrollers, where 8051 concepts directly map to **NVIC (Nested Vector Interrupt Controller)** and **SysTick timer**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> All derivations below use **standard KTU 2024 numerical style**. Show your work in steps to secure full marks.

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Delay Generated by Timer 0 in Mode 1

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** The 8051 uses a **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Configure **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of exactly **50 ms**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Machine Cycle Period

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1.085\ \mu s$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Number of Timer Counts Required

$$\text{Number of Counts} = \frac{\text{Required Delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{Number of Counts} = 46080$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Initial Timer Value

The 16-bit timer counts up from the loaded value to **65536** (which is $2^{16}$, the overflow point).

$$N = 65536 - \text{Number of Counts} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456$$

### Step 4 — Split N into TH0 and TL0

Converting **19456** to hexadecimal:

$$19456 \div 16 = 1216\ \text{remainder}\ 0$$
$$1216 \div 16 = 76\ \text{remainder}\ 0$$
$$76 \div 16 = 4\ \text{remainder}\ 12\ (C)$$
$$4 \div 16 = 0\ \text{remainder}\ 4$$

Reading remainders bottom-up: **0x4C00**

Therefore:

$$\text{TH0} = \mathbf{0x4C},\quad \text{TL0} = \mathbf{0x00}$$

### Step 5 — TMOD Configuration

We need **Timer 0, Mode 1, Software-Start, Timer (not Counter)**:

| Bit | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | Value |
|:---:|:----:|:---:|:--:|:--:|:-----:|
| T0  | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **0x01** |

So **`TMOD = 0x01`** (T1 untouched, assumed off).

### Step 6 — Verification (Round-Trip Check)

$$D = (65536 - 19456) \times 1.085\ \mu s = 46080 \times 1.085\ \mu s$$

$$D = 49996.8\ \mu s \approx 50\ \text{ms}\ \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For exact 50 ms**, use **12 MHz** crystal: $T_{mc} = 1\ \mu s$ exactly, and 50,000 counts → N = 15536 = 0x3CB0. KTU often gives 12 MHz for clean numbers.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Baud-Rate Calculation for 9600 bps

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Determine **TH1** for **9600 baud** serial communication using **11.0592 MHz** crystal and **SMOD = 0**.

### Step 1 — Start from the Mode 1 Baud-Rate Formula

$$B = \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Known Values

$$9600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the Denominator

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3.6864 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = 3$$

### Step 4 — Solve for TH1

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \mathbf{0xFD}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TH1 = 0xFD = -3 (signed byte)** is the KTU textbook value. The negative-load is the standard way of writing auto-reload values in 8051 C/Assembly.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Generating 1 kHz Square Wave on P1.0

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Using **Timer 0 in Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)**, generate a **1 kHz square wave** on P1.0 with **f_osc = 12 MHz**. Compute TH0.

### Step 1 — Square-Wave Period

For a square wave of frequency $f$:

$$T_{square} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\ \text{ms}$$

The pin toggles **twice** per full period (once HIGH, once LOW), so each delay (HALF period) is:

$$T_{half} = \frac{T_{square}}{2} = 0.5\ \text{ms} = 500\ \mu s$$

### Step 2 — Machine Cycle

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\ \mu s$$

### Step 3 — Required Timer Counts

$$\text{Counts} = \frac{T_{half}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{500\ \mu s}{1\ \mu s} = 500$$

### Step 4 — Compute TH0 for Mode 2

$$\text{TH0} = 256 - 500$$

But **500 > 256** — the 8-bit timer cannot hold 500 counts in a single cycle.

### Step 5 — Use a Software Loop

If a single cycle delay of $256 - TH0$ is too short, wrap it in a **register-decremented loop**. The KTU exam approach:

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{\text{Required Counts}}{256 - \text{TH0}}$$

Choose **TH0 = 0x00** (256 counts per cycle = 256 µs delay):

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{500}{256} \approx 1.95$$

Choose **TH0 = 0x38 = 56** (200 counts per cycle = 200 µs delay):

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{500}{200} = 2.5 \quad (\text{not integer})$$

Choose **TH0 = 0x06 = 6** (250 counts per cycle = 250 µs delay):

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{500}{250} = 2 \quad (\text{integer, perfect})\ \checkmark$$

Therefore: **TH0 = 0x06**, **Loop Count = 2** (e.g., R7 = 2).

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Interrupt Latency Calculation

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** An external interrupt INT0̄ is asserted. Compute the **interrupt latency** (cycles from assertion to first ISR instruction) and the **maximum latency** for a 12 MHz crystal.

### Step 1 — Standard Latency

The 8051 samples interrupts at **S5P2 of every machine cycle**. If accepted:

- Remaining instruction completes (worst case: **MUL/DIV = 4 machine cycles**).
- LCALL to vector = **2 machine cycles**.
- Total: **4 + 2 = 6 machine cycles** (worst case).

### Step 2 — Compute Time at 12 MHz

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\ \mu s$$

$$\text{Latency}_{max} = 6 \times T_{mc} = 6\ \mu s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **level-triggered** interrupts, the external source must hold the pin LOW until the ISR begins executing. For **edge-triggered** (default), the edge is latched by hardware — no timing constraint on the pulse width (other than ≥ 1 machine cycle).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 8051 Block Diagram Highlighting Accessories

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_ENGINE["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
        ALU["ALU 8-bit"]
        ACC["Accumulator A"]
        B_REG["B Register"]
        PSW["Program Status Word PSW"]
    end

    subgraph PROG_MEM["PROGRAM MEMORY 4 KB ROM"]
        ROM["Internal ROM 0x0000 to 0x0FFF"]
    end

    subgraph DATA_MEM["DATA MEMORY 128 B RAM + 128 B SFR"]
        RAM_BANK["4 Register Banks 0x00-0x1F"]
        BIT_AREA["Bit Addressable 0x20-0x2F"]
        GEN_AREA["General Purpose 0x30-0x7F"]
        SFR_AREA["SFR Area 0x80-0xFF"]
    end

    subgraph ACC_IO["ACCESSORY: PARALLEL I/O PORTS"]
        P0["P0 0x80 AD0-AD7"]
        P1["P1 0x90 General I/O"]
        P2["P2 0xA0 A8-A15"]
        P3["P3 0xB0 RxD TxD INT0 INT1 T0 T1 WR RD"]
    end

    subgraph ACC_TIMERS["ACCESSORY: TIMERS / COUNTERS"]
        T0["Timer 0 TH0 TL0"]
        T1["Timer 1 TH1 TL1"]
        TCON["TCON 0x88 Control Flags"]
        TMOD["TMOD 0x89 Mode Select"]
    end

    subgraph ACC_SERIAL["ACCESSORY: SERIAL PORT UART"]
        SBUF["SBUF 0x99 Transmit + Receive"]
        SCON["SCON 0x98 Control"]
        PCON["PCON 0x87 SMOD Bit"]
    end

    subgraph ACC_INT["ACCESSORY: INTERRUPT ENGINE"]
        IE_REG["IE 0xA8 Enable Mask"]
        IP_REG["IP 0xB8 Priority"]
        VEC_TBL["Vector Table 0x0000 to 0x0023"]
    end

    CPU_ENGINE --- PROG_MEM
    CPU_ENGINE --- DATA_MEM
    CPU_ENGINE --- ACC_IO
    CPU_ENGINE --- ACC_TIMERS
    CPU_ENGINE --- ACC_SERIAL
    CPU_ENGINE --- ACC_INT
    ACC_TIMERS -.Uses.-> P3
    ACC_SERIAL -.Uses.-> P3
    ACC_INT -.Uses.-> P3
```

## 4.2 Timer Subsystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC["f_osc 12 MHz Crystal"] --> DIV12["Divide by 12"]
    DIV12 --> TMC["Machine Cycle Clock 1 MHz"]
    TMC --> SW1{"GATE 0 or 1"}
    INT_PIN["INT0̄ Pin"] --> SW1
    SW1 --> AND_G["AND with TR0"]
    TR0["TR0 Software Bit"] --> AND_G
    AND_G --> MODE["Mode Select M1 M0"]
    MODE_CT["Counter Pin T0"] --> CTSC{"C/T̄"}
    TMC --> CTSC
    CTSC --> MODE
    MODE --> CNT["16-bit Up Counter TH0 TL0"]
    CNT --> OVF{"FFFFh Overflow"}
    OVF -- Yes --> TF0["Set TF0 Flag in TCON"]
    TF0 --> INT_ENG["Interrupt Engine IE0"]
    OVF -- No --> CNT
```

## 4.3 Serial Port Frame Transmitter (Mode 1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["8051 CPU writes SBUF"] --> SHIFT["Transmit Shift Register 10-bit"]
    SHIFT --> TX_LINE["TxD Pin P3.1 Wire"]
    TX_LINE -.-> RX_LINE["RxD Pin P3.0"]
    RX_LINE --> RX_SHIFT["Receive Shift Register"]
    RX_SHIFT --> SMPL["Sampling at 16x Baud Rate"]
    SMPL --> RX_BUF["Receive SBUF 0x99"]
    RX_BUF --> CPU_READ["CPU reads SBUF"]
    SMPL --> RI_FLAG["Set RI Flag"]
    A --> TI_FLAG["Set TI Flag after last bit"]
```

## 4.4 Interrupt Engine — Priority Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC["Interrupt Source 5 sources: INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial"] --> MASK{"IE Mask Bit EX0 ET0 EX1 ET1 ES"}
    MASK -- Disabled --> DROP["Ignored, continue main program"]
    MASK -- Enabled --> PRIO{"IP Bit + Natural Priority"}
    PRIO --> HIGH["High Priority or Higher Source"]
    PRIO --> LOW["Low Priority and Lower Source"]
    HIGH --> STK["Push PC onto Stack"]
    LOW --> CHK_BUSY{"Is a Higher-Priority ISR Active"}
    CHK_BUSY -- Yes --> WAIT["Wait until it RETIs"]
    CHK_BUSY -- No --> STK
    WAIT --> CHK_BUSY
    STK --> LCALL["LCALL to Vector Address"]
    LCALL --> ISR["Execute ISR Routine"]
    ISR --> RETI["RETI Pops PC Clears Priority Latch"]
    RETI --> MAIN["Resume Main Program"]
```

## 4.5 Port-3 Pin Multiplexing (Alternate Functions)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P3_PINS["P3 SFR 0xB0"]
        P30["P3.0 RxD Input"]
        P31["P3.1 TxD Output"]
        P32["P3.2 INT0̄ Input"]
        P33["P3.3 INT1̄ Input"]
        P34["P3.4 T0 Input"]
        P35["P3.5 T1 Input"]
        P36["P3.6 WR̄ Output"]
        P37["P3.7 RD̄ Output"]
    end

    P30 --> UART_MOD["Serial Port Module"]
    P31 --> UART_MOD
    P32 --> INT_ENG["Interrupt Engine"]
    P33 --> INT_ENG
    P34 --> TMR_MOD["Timer Subsystem"]
    P35 --> TMR_MOD
    P36 --> EXT_MEM["External Memory Write"]
    P37 --> EXT_MEM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the function of the TMOD register in the 8051. List the four timer modes with their M1 and M0 bit values.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (Point-Wise, 3-Mark Standard):

1. **TMOD (SFR 0x89)** is a non-bit-addressable register that controls the **operating mode** and **function** of Timer 0 and Timer 1. It is **not bit-addressable** and must be loaded as a full byte.

2. **Upper nibble (bits 7-4)** controls **Timer 1**; **lower nibble (bits 3-0)** controls **Timer 0**. Each nibble contains:
   - **GATE** → enables hardware control via INTx̄ pin.
   - **C/T̄** → selects timer (0) or counter (1) mode.
   - **M1, M0** → selects one of four modes.

3. **Four Operating Modes:**

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description |
|:--:|:--:|:----:|-------------|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit timer (THx 8-bit + TLx 5-bit) |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit timer |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split timer (T0 only) |

> Mode 1 is most commonly used for delay generation; Mode 2 is used for baud-rate generation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the role of the SCON register in the 8051 serial communication? List its bits and explain the function of SM0 and SM1.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3-Mark Standard):

1. **SCON (Serial Control, SFR 0x98)** configures the **serial port's operating mode**, controls **data reception/transmission**, and holds the **TI/RI interrupt flags**.

2. **Bit Layout:** `SM0 | SM1 | SM2 | REN | TB8 | RB8 | TI | RI`

3. **SM0 and SM1** together select one of **four serial modes**:

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate |
|:---:|:---:|:----:|-------------|:---------:|
| 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 8-bit shift register | $f_{osc}/12$ |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | **8-bit UART (most common)** | **Timer 1 overflow** |
| 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 9-bit UART | $f_{osc}/32$ or /64 |
| 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | 9-bit UART | Timer 1 overflow |

4. **REN** must be set to **1** to enable reception. **TI** and **RI** must be **cleared in software** by the ISR.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the 8051 serial port**. Describe the **transmit and receive operations** in **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)** with the frame format. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **12 MHz** crystal is connected to the 8051. The serial port must operate at **4800 baud** in **Mode 1** with **SMOD = 1**. Determine the value to be loaded into **TH1** and verify it. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a) — Serial Port Architecture (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
         8051 CPU
            │
            ▼
       ┌─────────┐
       │  SBUF   │  (0x99 — Two separate registers: one for TX, one for RX)
       └────┬────┘
            │
       ┌────┴────┐
       │  SCON   │  (0x98 — Mode select + flags)
       └────┬────┘
            │
   ┌────────┼────────┐
   ▼        ▼        ▼
TX Shift  RX Shift  Baud-Rate
Register  Register  Generator
(TxD)     (RxD)     (Timer 1)
```

**Explanation Points (5-Mark Body):**

1. The 8051's **serial port** is a **full-duplex UART** — it can transmit and receive simultaneously using two independent shift registers, both addressed through the single **SBUF** (SFR 0x99).

2. **Write** to SBUF → loads the **transmit register**; **Read** from SBUF → accesses the **receive register**. This dual-mapping is a hardware convenience.

3. **SCON** register controls the mode (SM0, SM1), enables reception (REN), and holds the **TI** (Transmit Interrupt) and **RI** (Receive Interrupt) flags.

4. **Mode 1 Frame Format:** `START (0) + 8 DATA BITS (LSB first) + STOP (1)` = **10 bits/frame**.

5. **Transmission:** CPU writes SBUF → data loaded into TX shift register → start bit transmitted → 8 data bits (LSB first) → stop bit transmitted → **TI flag set**.

6. **Reception:** The receiver oversamples RxD at **16× the baud rate** to detect the **start bit's falling edge** → samples each bit at the bit-center → assembles 8 data bits → checks stop bit → **RI flag set** → CPU reads SBUF.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b) — TH1 Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 12\ \text{MHz}$, Baud Rate $B = 4800$, SMOD = 1.

**Formula (Mode 1):**

$$B = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute SMOD = 1:**

$$B = \frac{2}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} = \frac{1}{16} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$4800 = \frac{1}{16} \times \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$4800 = \frac{10^{6}}{16 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for (256 − TH1):**

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{10^{6}}{16 \times 4800} = \frac{10^{6}}{76800} = 13.02$$

**Step 5 — Round and compute TH1:**

$$256 - \text{TH1} = 13$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 13 = 243 = \mathbf{0xF3}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$B = \frac{1}{16} \times \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 13} = \frac{10^{6}}{2496} = \mathbf{4807.7\ bps} \approx 4800\ \text{bps} \ \checkmark$$

**Answer:** **TH1 = 0xF3 = 243**, TMOD for Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload) = **0x20**.

---

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Describe the **8051 Interrupt System** with a neat diagram. Explain the **IE** and **IP** registers and the **natural priority order**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. **Timer 0 is configured in Mode 1** to generate a delay of **exactly 100 ms** that toggles **P1.7** continuously. Calculate the values to be loaded into **TH0 and TL0**, and write the **complete 8051 Assembly program**. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a) — Interrupt System (7 Marks)

**Architecture (textual diagram):**

```
         ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
         │    5 INTERRUPT SOURCES (8051)    │
         │  ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐  ┌─────┐ │
         │  │  INT0  │  │  T0    │  │ ... │ │
         │  │ (P3.2) │  │ (TF0)  │  │     │ │
         │  └────┬───┘  └────┬───┘  └──┬──┘ │
         └───────┼───────────┼─────────┼────┘
                 ▼           ▼         ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │    IE REGISTER (0xA8)    │  (Individual + Global Enable)
              └─────────────┬────────────┘
                            ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │    IP REGISTER (0xB8)    │  (Priority Selection)
              └─────────────┬────────────┘
                            ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │  HARDWARE PRIORITY ARBITER│
              └─────────────┬────────────┘
                            ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │  VECTOR TABLE → ISR JUMP │
              │  (8 bytes per vector)    │
              └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation Points (5-Mark Body):**

1. The 8051 supports **5 interrupt sources**: 2 external (INT0̄, INT1̄), 2 timer overflows (TF0, TF1), and 1 serial port (TI/RI).

2. **IE Register (0xA8) — Bit-Addressable:** `EA | – | – | ES | ET1 | EX1 | ET0 | EX0`
   - **EA** = Global enable (must be 1).
   - **EX0/EX1** = Enable external interrupts.
   - **ET0/ET1** = Enable timer interrupts.
   - **ES** = Enable serial port interrupt.

3. **IP Register (0xB8) — Bit-Addressable:** `– | – | – | PS | PT1 | PX1 | PT0 | PX0`
   - Setting any bit makes that interrupt **high priority** (can preempt a low-priority ISR).

4. **Natural Priority Order (highest → lowest):**
   - Reset → INT0 → Timer 0 → INT1 → Timer 1 → Serial Port

5. **Processing:** Flag set → Hardware checks IE → Priority resolved → Current PC pushed to stack → PC ← Vector address → ISR executes → **RETI** pops PC and resumes main program.

6. **Vector Addresses:** Reset = 0x0000, INT0 = 0x0003, T0 = 0x000B, INT1 = 0x0013, T1 = 0x001B, Serial = 0x0023.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(b) — 100 ms Delay Program (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$, Delay = 100 ms, Mode 1, Toggle P1.7.

**Step 1 — Compute Machine Cycle:**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\ \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Required Counts:**

$$\text{Counts} = \frac{100\ \text{ms}}{1.085\ \mu s} = 92160$$

**Step 3 — Compute Initial Value:**

$$N = 65536 - 92160 = \mathbf{-26624}$$

> 100 ms > 65.5 ms (max single-cycle delay in Mode 1) — **MUST use a software loop.**

**Step 4 — Choose TH0:TL0 and Loop Count:**

Choose **TH0:TL0 = 0xFE32 = 65186** → Counts per cycle = **65536 − 65186 = 350** → Time per cycle = **350 × 1.085 µs ≈ 380 µs**.

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{100\ \text{ms}}{380\ \mu s} \approx 263$$

But 350 × 263 = 92,050 ≠ 92,160. **Try TH0:TL0 = 0xFF00 = 65280** → 256 counts/cycle = 277.8 µs.

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{100\ \text{ms}}{277.8\ \mu s} \approx 360$$

Try **TH0:TL0 = 0xFF7C = 65404** → 132 counts/cycle = 143.3 µs.

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{100\ \text{ms}}{143.3\ \mu s} = 698$$

Since **132 × 698 = 92,136** ≈ 92,160 → **error = 24 µs ≈ 0.024%** (acceptable). Final choice: **TH0 = 0xFF, TL0 = 0x7C, Loop Count (R7) = 698 = 0x2BA**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exam solutions often **assume the loop overhead is negligible** and accept any integer answer that is "approximately" 100 ms. Students may present a 50 ms × 2 loop design for clarity.

**Simplified Exact Solution (50 ms twice):**

**Step 1:** Use the **derived value from Section 3.1** — for 50 ms at 11.0592 MHz, use **TH0 = 0x4C, TL0 = 0x00** with **Loop Count = 1**, but this gives 50 ms (not 100 ms). We need **two such cycles** — alternatively, scale counts directly.

**Final Clean Approach:** For 100 ms exactly:

$$N = 65536 - \left\lfloor \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = 65536 - 92160 = -26624$$

A cleaner KTU-style answer uses **12 MHz crystal** instead, where $T_{mc} = 1\ \mu s$:

$$N = 65536 - 100000 = -34464 = \mathbf{0x7780}$$

So **TH0 = 0x77, TL0 = 0x80**, with **software loop count = 2** (each cycle ≈ 50 ms), since 100 ms > 65.5 ms max. KTU accepts **either approach** if the calculation is clearly shown.

#### ✅ Complete 8051 Assembly Program (Using 12 MHz, 2 × 50 ms loop, KTU Standard):

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     000BH              ; Timer 0 ISR (not used here)
        RETI

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H        ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Start
        MOV     P1, #00H          ; Clear P1
        AGAIN: SETB   P1.7         ; Set P1.7 HIGH
        ACALL   DELAY_50MS
        CLR     P1.7              ; Set P1.7 LOW
        ACALL   DELAY_50MS
        SJMP    AGAIN             ; Repeat forever

DELAY_50MS:
        MOV     TH0, #4CH         ; Load TH0 for 50 ms
        MOV     TL0, #00H         ; Load TL0 for 50 ms
        SETB    TR0               ; Start Timer 0
WAIT:   JNB     TF0, WAIT         ; Wait for overflow
        CLR     TR0               ; Stop Timer
        CLR     TF0               ; Clear flag
        RET
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TF0 in software** after detection — leads to immediate re-entry. Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong SFR address quoted** (e.g., saying TMOD = 0x8A instead of 0x89) — loss: 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 3. **Neglecting loop overhead** in delay calculations — produces 5-10% timing error. KTU expects either an "approximate" answer with caveat OR an "exact" answer with full derivation. Loss: 1-2 marks if no explanation.
> 4. **Using 8051 `SETB P1.7` but writing C-style `P1_7 = 1`** in Assembly section — KTU examiners deduct heavily.
> 5. **Confusing TCON and TMOD bit positions** — e.g., writing "TR0 is in TMOD" — this is a very common error. Loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to set the P3 latch bit (e.g., `SETB P3.0`)** before using RxD — KTU tests this in **lab viva** and theory. Loss: 1 mark.
> 7. **Quoting the wrong vector address** for ISRs (e.g., "Timer 1 ISR is at 0x001A" instead of **0x001B**) — KTU examiners are strict here. Loss: 0.5 to 1 mark.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this checklist to your study board before the KTU exam.**

### ✅ I/O Ports
- [ ] P0 has **no internal pull-up** → needs external resistors in bus mode.
- [ ] P1 is the **simplest general-purpose** port.
- [ ] P2 carries **high address byte (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- [ ] **P3 has alternate functions** — must memorize all 8 pin functions.
- [ ] To use a P3 alt-function, the **latch bit must be set to 1** first.

### ✅ Timers/Counters
- [ ] **Mode 1** = 16-bit, used for **long delays** (max ≈ 65.5 ms at 12 MHz).
- [ ] **Mode 2** = 8-bit auto-reload, used for **baud-rate generation** (THx reloads TLx).
- [ ] **Mode 3** = split timers (only for T0); T1 stops in this mode.
- [ ] **GATE = 1** → hardware-controlled (pulse-width measurement).
- [ ] **C/T̄ = 1** → counter mode (uses T0/T1 pin).
- [ ] **Delay formula:** $D = (65536 - N) \times 12 / f_{osc}$.
- [ ] **TMOD is NOT bit-addressable**; **TCON IS bit-addressable**.
- [ ] **TFx flag MUST be cleared in software** by the ISR.

### ✅ Serial Port
- [ ] **Mode 1** = 8-bit UART, baud rate = Timer 1 overflow rate.
- [ ] **Frame** = Start(0) + 8 data + Stop(1) = **10 bits**.
- [ ] **SBUF is a dual register** — write for TX, read for RX.
- [ ] **SMOD bit is in PCON (0x87)**, NOT in SCON.
- [ ] **TI/RI must be cleared in software** (no auto-clear).
- [ ] **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred — produces exact integer baud rates.
- [ ] **REN = 1** to enable reception.
- [ ] **Baud rate formula:** $B = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12(256-TH1)}$.

### ✅ Interrupts
- [ ] **5 sources**: INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial.
- [ ] **Vector table** starts at **0x0000** with **8-byte** spacing.
- [ ] **Reset vector = 0x0000** (highest natural priority).
- [ ] **EA must be 1** for any interrupt to work.
- [ ] **RETI** instruction (not RET) exits an ISR — it also clears the priority latch.
- [ ] **Edge-triggered** (default) vs **Level-triggered** (set ITx = 0).
- [ ] **Natural priority**: Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial.
- [ ] **High-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority ISR** — but NOT another high-priority ISR.

### ✅ Numerical Mastery (For KTU Part B)
- [ ] Always quote **TMOD hex value** (e.g., 0x21 for T1 Mode 2).
- [ ] Always show **THx and TLx in hex**.
- [ ] Always perform a **verification step** (recompute the delay/baud).
- [ ] Always state the **assumption about loop overhead**.

> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, **14-mark questions often test TWO accessories together** (e.g., Timer + Serial, or Port + Interrupt). Practice *integrated* problems, not just isolated ones.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application and Use in Healthcare (Case Study: People's Ventilator Project)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application and Use in Healthcare (Case Study: People's Ventilator Project)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **People's Ventilator Project (PVP)** is an open-source, low-cost, rapidly-manufacturable mechanical ventilator system developed as a humanitarian engineering response to ventilator shortages during the **COVID-19 pandemic (2020-2021)**. It represents a benchmark case study in **embedded systems design for biomedical applications**, where a microcontroller-based architecture orchestrates real-time closed-loop control of pneumatic actuation, patient pressure monitoring, and alarm generation to deliver controlled positive-pressure ventilation to patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under Module 3 of PEBMT741, demonstrating how advanced microprocessors/microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M series, STM32, ESP32) integrate with sensors (pressure transducers, flow sensors), actuators (proportional solenoid valves), and HMI (touchscreen displays) in a **safety-critical medical embedded system**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blowing air into a balloon manually. Your brain continuously:
1. Feels the balloon's stretch (**sensing**)
2. Decides how much more air to push (**decision making**)
3. Adjusts your mouth and lungs (**actuation**)
4. Repeats this in a rhythmic cycle (**real-time loop**)

A ventilator does exactly this for a patient whose lungs cannot breathe on their own. The **microcontroller** is the "brain" of this system — it reads sensor data, computes required airflow using a **PID control algorithm**, and drives solenoid valves thousands of times per second to deliver precisely metered breaths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** The People's Ventilator Project was initiated by **MIT Lincoln Laboratory** in collaboration with clinicians from **Massachusetts General Hospital (MGH)**. It received **FDA Emergency Use Authorization (EUA)** consideration and became a global model for emergency medical device design.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following **bold metrics** define ventilator performance specifications in compliance with **ISO 80601-2-12:2020** (Medical electrical equipment — Particular requirements for critical care ventilators):

- **Tidal Volume (VT):** 200 – 800 mL
- **Respiratory Rate (RR):** 12 – 30 breaths/min
- **Inspiratory-to-Expiratory Ratio (I:E):** 1:2 (default)
- **Positive End-Expiratory Pressure (PEEP):** 5 – 20 cm H₂O
- **Peak Inspiratory Pressure (PIP):** 20 – 40 cm H₂O
- **Fraction of Inspired Oxygen (FiO₂):** 21% – 100%
- **Control Loop Sampling Rate:** ≥ 100 Hz (every 10 ms)
- **Hardware Watchdog Timeout:** ≤ 100 ms

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Why" and "How"

The People's Ventilator is built on a **closed-loop feedback control architecture** (see Section 4 for block diagram). The system can be decomposed into five functional blocks:

1. **Patient Interface Block**
   - *Why:* Provides the physical conduit between ventilator and patient lungs.
   - *How:* Uses a **dual-limb breathing circuit** — one limb for inspiratory airflow, one for expiratory exhaust, connected via a **HEPA-filtered HME (Heat and Moisture Exchanger)**.

2. **Sensor Acquisition Block**
   - *Why:* Converts physical quantities (pressure, flow, oxygen concentration) into electrical signals.
   - *How:* Uses **differential pressure transducers** (e.g., Honeywell HSC series) for airway pressure and **thermal mass flow sensors** (e.g., Sensirion SFM3000) for volumetric flow measurement. Signals are digitized via the microcontroller's **12-bit SAR ADC** at a sampling rate of **1 kHz**, then decimated to 100 Hz after **digital low-pass filtering**.

3. **Microcontroller Decision Block**
   - *Why:* Implements the **ventilation algorithm** (pressure-controlled, volume-controlled, or hybrid modes).
   - *How:* An **ARM Cortex-M4F** core (STM32F407 or equivalent) runs a **preemptive real-time scheduler (FreeRTOS)** with task priorities: Sensor Read (highest) → Control Compute → Valve Actuation → HMI Update → Telemetry (lowest).

4. **Actuator (Pneumatic) Block**
   - *Why:* Physically modulates the air/oxygen mixture delivered to the patient.
   - *How:* Uses **proportional solenoid valves** driven by **PWM signals at 25 kHz** with a 4–20 mA or 0–10 V linearized control input. The valve duty cycle is computed by the PID controller.

5. **Safety & Alarm Block**
   - *Why:* Medical devices must be **fail-safe** under all single-fault conditions.
   - *How:* Implements **hardware watchdog timer**, **independent watchdog (IWDG + WWDG)**, **redundant pressure sensors**, **battery backup with UPS transition < 20 ms**, and audible/visual alarms per **IEC 60601-1-8**.

### 2.2 Closed-Loop Control Theory

The ventilator employs a **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** to regulate airway pressure. The discrete-time PID algorithm is given by:

$$u[n] = K_p \cdot e[n] + K_i \sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k] \cdot T_s + K_d \cdot \frac{e[n] - e[n-1]}{T_s}$$

Where:
- $u[n]$ is the **valve control signal** (PWM duty cycle) at sample $n$
- $e[n] = P_{setpoint} - P_{measured}$ is the **pressure error**
- $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are the proportional, integral, and derivative gains
- $T_s$ is the **sampling period** (10 ms = 0.01 s)

To prevent **integral windup** (a critical safety concern), the algorithm uses **conditional integration with anti-windup clamping**:

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} u_{max} & \text{if } u_{raw}[n] > u_{max} \\ u_{raw}[n] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** | **Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tidal Volume | $V_T$ | $V_T = \int_0^{T_i} Q(t)\,dt$ | mL | 400 – 600 |
| Minute Ventilation | $\dot{V}_E$ | $\dot{V}_E = V_T \times RR$ | L/min | 5 – 10 |
| Airway Pressure | $P_{aw}$ | $P_{aw} = \rho g h$ (manometric) | cm H₂O | 15 – 30 |
| I:E Ratio | $I:E$ | $T_i / T_e$ | dimensionless | 1:2 |
| Cycle Period | $T_{total}$ | $T_{total} = 60 / RR$ | s | 2 – 5 |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | $f_s = 1 / T_s$ | Hz | ≥ 100 |
| ADC Resolution | $N$ | $N = \log_2(2^{bits})$ | bits | 12 |
| Pressure Resolution | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P = \frac{P_{FS}}{2^{N}}$ | cm H₂O | 0.029 |
| Flow Resolution | $\Delta Q$ | $\Delta Q = \frac{Q_{FS}}{2^{N}}$ | L/min | 0.0024 |
| Reynolds Number (gas) | $Re$ | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | < 2300 (laminar) |
| Valve Response Time | $\tau_v$ | $\tau_v = 1 / (2\pi f_{bw})$ | ms | < 5 |
| Battery Runtime | $t_{bat}$ | $t_{bat} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{sys}}$ | minutes | ≥ 30 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are directly applicable in **embedded firmware design**, **sensor calibration routines**, **closed-loop tuning experiments**, and **regulatory compliance testing** for any safety-critical medical device.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond ventilators, the same architecture is reused in:
- **Anesthesia machines** (Datex-Ohmeda, Dräger Perseus)
- **CPAP/BiPAP devices** (Philips DreamStation, ResMed AirSense)
- **Insulin pumps** (Medtronic MiniMed, Tandem t:slim)
- **Dialysis machines** (Fresenius Vmax)
- **Robotic surgery systems** (da Vinci, MAKO)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation: Tidal Volume from Flow Sensor Integration

The People's Ventilator measures volumetric flow $Q(t)$ at the airway opening and integrates over the inspiratory period $T_i$ to compute the **delivered tidal volume**:

$$V_T = \int_0^{T_i} Q(t) \, dt$$

Since the microcontroller can only perform **discrete numerical integration**, this integral is approximated using the **composite trapezoidal rule**:

**Step 1:** Discretize the inspiratory period $T_i$ into $N$ samples of width $T_s$:
$$T_i = N \cdot T_s \quad \text{where} \quad T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trapezoidal approximation:
$$V_T \approx T_s \left[ \frac{Q[0] + Q[N-1]}{2} + \sum_{k=1}^{N-2} Q[k] \right]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $T_s = 1/f_s$ and rearrange:
$$V_T \approx \frac{1}{f_s} \left[ \frac{Q[0] + Q[N-1]}{2} + \sum_{k=1}^{N-2} Q[k] \right]$$

**Step 4:** Convert from liters to milliliters:
$$V_T (\text{mL}) = 1000 \times V_T (\text{L})$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):**
Given: $f_s = 100$ Hz, $T_i = 1.0$ s, sensor readings $Q[0..9]$ (in L/min): {0, 30, 48, 52, 50, 48, 45, 42, 30, 0}.

**Step A:** Compute $N = f_s \times T_i = 100 \times 1.0 = 100$ samples (simplified to 10 representative samples for illustration).

**Step B:** Apply trapezoidal rule:
$$V_T = \frac{1}{100} \left[ \frac{0 + 0}{2} + (30 + 48 + 52 + 50 + 48 + 45 + 42 + 30) \right]$$
$$V_T = 0.01 \times 345 = 3.45 \text{ L/min} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step C:** Since $Q$ is in L/min, convert to L/s by dividing by 60:
$$V_T = \frac{3.45}{60} = 0.0575 \text{ L} = 57.5 \text{ mL}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Controller Stability (Z-Plane Analysis)

For the discrete PID controller to be stable, all poles of the closed-loop transfer function must lie **inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$ in the z-plane.

**Step 1:** Take the z-transform of the discrete PID controller:
$$C(z) = K_p + \frac{K_i \cdot T_s}{1 - z^{-1}} + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(1 - z^{-1})$$

**Step 2:** The plant (proportional valve + pneumatic circuit) is modeled as a first-order system:
$$G(z) = \frac{K_v (1 - e^{-T_s/\tau_v})}{z - e^{-T_s/\tau_v}}$$

**Step 3:** The open-loop transfer function is:
$$L(z) = C(z) \cdot G(z)$$

**Step 4:** The closed-loop transfer function is:
$$T(z) = \frac{L(z)}{1 + L(z)}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **Jury stability criterion** or use **MATLAB's `pzmap`** to verify stability. For the PVP's design with $K_p = 0.8$, $K_i = 12$, $K_d = 0.05$, $T_s = 0.01$ s, and $\tau_v = 0.05$ s, the dominant closed-loop pole is at $z = 0.847 + j0.214$, with magnitude:
$$\vert z_{pole} \vert = \sqrt{0.847^2 + 0.214^2} = \sqrt{0.717 + 0.046} = \sqrt{0.763} \approx 0.873$$

Since $0.873 < 1$, the controller is **stable** with a damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.4$ and natural frequency $\omega_n \approx 14.6$ rad/s.

### 3.3 Derivation: Watchdog Timer Threshold Calculation

The hardware watchdog must reset the microcontroller if the main loop hangs beyond a safe threshold $t_{WD}$.

**Step 1:** Maximum acceptable response latency for a critical alarm (per ISO 80601-2-12):
$$t_{alarm,max} = 100 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2:** Watchdog timer is clocked from the **Low-Speed Internal (LSI) oscillator** at $f_{LSI} = 32$ kHz.

**Step 3:** The prescaler and reload register values are computed as:
$$t_{WD} = \frac{(Prescaler + 1) \times (Reload + 1)}{f_{LSI}}$$

**Step 4:** Setting $t_{WD} = 50$ ms (half of $t_{alarm,max}$ for safety margin):
$$0.050 = \frac{(Prescaler + 1) \times (Reload + 1)}{32000}$$
$$(Prescaler + 1) \times (Reload + 1) = 1600$$

A valid choice: $Prescaler = 39$, $Reload = 39$, giving $(40)(40) = 1600$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state units explicitly and show intermediate steps. Marks are awarded for **methodology**, not just the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains 220V / Battery 24V Li-ion] --> B[Power Management Unit with UPS]
    B --> C[STM32F407 ARM Cortex-M4F MCU]
    C --> D[RTOS Scheduler - FreeRTOS]
    D --> E1[Task 1 - Sensor Read Priority 5]
    D --> E2[Task 2 - PID Compute Priority 4]
    D --> E3[Task 3 - Valve Actuation Priority 3]
    D --> E4[Task 4 - HMI Update Priority 2]
    D --> E5[Task 5 - Telemetry Priority 1]
    E1 --> F1[Differential Pressure Sensor 0 to 60 cmH2O]
    E1 --> F2[Mass Flow Sensor 0 to 200 L per min]
    E1 --> F3[Oxygen Sensor - Galvanic Cell]
    F1 --> G1[12-bit SAR ADC Channel 1]
    F2 --> G2[12-bit SAR ADC Channel 2]
    F3 --> G3[12-bit SAR ADC Channel 3]
    G1 --> E1
    G2 --> E1
    G3 --> E1
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> H1[Inspiratory Proportional Valve 24V DC]
    E3 --> H2[Expiratory Valve 12V DC]
    E3 --> H3[Oxygen Blender 0 to 100 percent FiO2]
    H1 --> I[Dual Limb Breathing Circuit with HEPA Filter]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Patient Airway Endotracheal Tube]
    E4 --> K1[5 inch Touchscreen TFT LCD]
    E4 --> K2[Buzzer 85 dB at 1m]
    E4 --> K3[Status LED Bar 3 colors]

    E5 --> L2[Bluetooth HC-05 for Clinician Tablet]
    C --> M[Independent Watchdog Timer IWDG]
    C --> N[Window Watchdog Timer WWDG]
    M --> C
    N --> C
```
