# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate** — Every algebraic and logical step is shown. **No skipped steps.**

## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating the Required Processor MIPS for a Given Application

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU-style numerical):**
A real-time motor-control application requires a **PID loop** that executes every **100 µs**. The loop contains:
- 200 integer ALU operations (1 cycle each on a Cortex-M4).
- 50 multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations (1 cycle each, hardware MAC).
- 30 load/store operations (2 cycles each).
- 20 branch operations (3 cycles each).
- Plus 100 cycles of overhead (interrupts, context switch).

Find the **minimum clock frequency** $f_{clk}$ required.

### Step 1: List all the operations and their cycle costs

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Integer ALU operations} &= 200 \text{ ops} \times 1 \text{ cycle/op} = 200 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{MAC operations} &= 50 \text{ ops} \times 1 \text{ cycle/op} = 50 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{Load/Store operations} &= 30 \text{ ops} \times 2 \text{ cycles/op} = 60 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{Branch operations} &= 20 \text{ ops} \times 3 \text{ cycles/op} = 60 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{Overhead cycles} &= 100 \text{ cycles} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the **Total Cycles per Loop** ($N_{cycles}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{cycles} &= 200 + 50 + 60 + 60 + 100 \\
N_{cycles} &= 470 \text{ cycles} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We sum all individual cycle costs because each category of instruction occupies the CPU pipeline for the stated number of clock cycles, and they execute sequentially in a single-threaded real-time control loop.

### Step 3: Compute the **Total Instructions per Loop** ($N_{instr}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{instr} &= 200 + 50 + 30 + 20 \\
N_{instr} &= 300 \text{ instructions} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the **Average CPI** for the loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{N_{instr}} \\
CPI_{avg} &= \dfrac{470}{300} \\
CPI_{avg} &\approx 1.567 \text{ cycles/instruction} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The average CPI is the total clock cycles divided by the total instructions, which gives the *mean* cost per instruction across mixed instruction types.

### Step 5: Apply the **loop execution time equation**

$$
t_{loop} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}
$$

For real-time, $t_{loop} \leq t_{deadline} = 100 \, \mu s$:

$$
\dfrac{470}{f_{clk}} \leq 100 \times 10^{-6}
$$

### Step 6: Solve for $f_{clk}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{clk} &\geq \dfrac{470}{100 \times 10^{-6}} \\
f_{clk} &\geq 4.7 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} \\
f_{clk} &\geq 4.7 \text{ MHz} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Rearranging the inequality gives the lower bound on the clock frequency. Any $f_{clk}$ above this value ensures the loop completes before the deadline.

### Step 7: Add a **30% design safety margin** (industry standard for real-time systems)

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{clk, design} &\geq 4.7 \times 1.3 \\
f_{clk, design} &\geq 6.11 \text{ MHz} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Practical Selection:** A **16 MHz Cortex-M0** or **72 MHz Cortex-M3** would be the typical KTU-recommended choice, as 4.7 MHz is rarely available as a standard oscillator frequency.

### Step 8: Compute the **achievable MIPS**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS}_{achievable} &= \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} \\
\text{MIPS}_{achievable} &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{1.567 \times 10^{6}} \\
\text{MIPS}_{achievable} &\approx 10.21 \text{ MIPS} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A 16 MHz Cortex-M0 (≈10 MIPS) is sufficient for this motor-control PID task. This is why **automotive ECUs** and **drones** widely use Cortex-M class MCUs rather than expensive Cortex-A application processors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power & Energy Estimation for an IoT Sensor Node

**Problem Setup:**
A wireless sensor node uses a Cortex-M0+ running at $f_{clk} = 32$ MHz, $V_{dd} = 3.3$ V, $C = 20$ pF, switching activity $\alpha = 0.1$, leakage $I_{leak} = 1 \, \mu A$. It is active for **1% of the time** (duty cycle).

### Step 1: Compute the **dynamic power** during active mode

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f_{clk} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.1 \times 20 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.3)^{2} \times 32 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.1 \times 20 \times 10^{-12} \times 10.89 \times 32 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 6.9696 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} \\
P_{dynamic} &\approx 696.96 \, \mu W \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The dynamic power formula captures the energy lost in charging and discharging the gate capacitance of CMOS transistors during state transitions.

### Step 2: Compute the **static (leakage) power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{static} &= I_{leak} \times V_{dd} \\
P_{static} &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.3 \\
P_{static} &= 3.3 \, \mu W \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Static power flows continuously due to sub-threshold leakage currents in MOSFETs, even when no switching occurs.

### Step 3: Compute the **total active-mode power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{active} &= P_{dynamic} + P_{static} \\
P_{active} &= 696.96 \, \mu W + 3.3 \, \mu W \\
P_{active} &= 700.26 \, \mu W \approx 0.7 \text{ mW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Assume **sleep-mode power** of $P_{sleep} = 5 \, \mu W$ (typical for Cortex-M0+ deep sleep)

### Step 5: Compute the **average power over one duty cycle**

With duty cycle D = 0.01 (1% active):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= D \cdot P_{active} + (1 - D) \cdot P_{sleep} \\
P_{avg} &= 0.01 \times 700.26 \, \mu W + 0.99 \times 5 \, \mu W \\
P_{avg} &= 7.0026 \, \mu W + 4.95 \, \mu W \\
P_{avg} &= 11.95 \, \mu W \approx 12 \, \mu W \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The average power is a duty-cycle weighted sum because the node alternates between the high-power active state and the low-power sleep state; the fraction of time in each state determines the long-term energy budget.

### Step 6: Compute the **battery lifetime**

For a CR2032 coin cell with capacity $C_{bat} = 220$ mAh at 3 V (energy $\approx 2400$ J):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{life} &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} \\
t_{life} &= \dfrac{2400}{11.95 \times 10^{-6}} \\
t_{life} &= 2.008 \times 10^{8} \text{ s} \\
t_{life} &\approx 6.37 \text{ years} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** By aggressive duty-cycling, an IoT sensor node can run for **>6 years** on a single coin cell — this is the design philosophy of LoRa, Zigbee, and BLE sensor nodes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Processor vs. Application Area

| Application Area | Required Throughput | Power Budget | Real-Time Strictness | Cost Target (₹/unit) | Recommended Processor |
|:-----------------|:-------------------:|:------------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------:|:----------------------|
| Digital Thermometer | < 1 MIPS | < 10 mW | Soft | < 30 | 8-bit MCU (PIC10F, ATtiny) |
| Washing Machine | 5–20 MIPS | 100–500 mW | Soft (100 ms) | 50–150 | Cortex-M0 / 8051 |
| Automotive ECU | 100–500 MIPS | 1–5 W | **Hard (1 ms)** | 200–1500 | Aurix TC2xx, Cortex-R5 |
| Drone Flight Controller | 500–2000 MIPS | 1–3 W | **Hard (1 ms)** | 1000–5000 | Cortex-M7 + DSP extensions |
| Smartphone AP | > 5000 MIPS (multi-core) | 3–8 W | Soft (16.6 ms / 60 fps) | > 5000 (chip) | Snapdragon, Dimensity, Apple A |
| Pacemaker | < 5 MIPS | < 30 µW avg | **Hard, safety-critical** | > 20000 (certified) | MSP430, Cortex-M0+ |
| 5G Baseband | > 10 GFLOPS | 20–50 W | **Hard (sub-ms)** | > 50000 | Multi-core DSP + FPGA |
| Satellite OBC | 50–200 MIPS | 5–15 W | **Hard, rad-hard** | > 1000000 | RAD750, LEON3FT |

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Matrix:** Notice the diagonal trend — as we move from consumer-grade to safety-critical/mission-critical applications, the **power budget, cost, and real-time strictness all rise simultaneously**, while the design margin tightens.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Application Area" in the context of advanced microprocessors and microcontrollers. List any four major application areas with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An *Application Area* refers to a specific engineering domain where a microprocessor or microcontroller is deployed to perform a dedicated computational, control, signal processing, or communication task. The choice of processor architecture is governed by the performance, power, real-time, and cost constraints of the application.

**Four Application Areas with examples (1.5 marks):**

1. **Embedded Control Systems** — Washing machine controller using 8051.
2. **Automotive Electronics** — Engine Control Unit (ECU) using Aurix TC2xx.
3. **IoT and Wireless Sensor Networks** — Smart agriculture soil-moisture node using ESP32.
4. **Medical Devices** — Patient heart-rate monitor using MSP430.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain why a DSP processor is preferred over a general-purpose MCU for audio signal processing applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** is preferred for audio applications for the following reasons:

1. **Harvard Architecture with separate program and data memories** (1 mark) — allows simultaneous fetch of instruction and operand, enabling single-cycle MAC operations essential for FIR/IIR filters.
2. **Hardware Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) unit** (1 mark) — executes `a = a + (b × c)` in a single cycle, which is the core operation of convolution and FFT used in audio codecs (MP3, AAC).
3. **Zero-overhead looping and circular buffering** (1 mark) — DSPs have dedicated hardware registers for loop counters and modulo arithmetic, allowing real-time audio sample processing with deterministic latency.

A general-purpose MCU lacks these dedicated features and would require 5–10× more clock cycles for the same audio task.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the major application areas of advanced microprocessors and microcontrollers. Discuss in detail the role of microcontrollers in **automotive electronics** and **IoT-based wireless sensor networks**.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *An embedded real-time system must execute a control loop every 50 µs. The loop contains 150 integer instructions (1 cycle each), 80 MAC instructions (1 cycle each), 40 load/store instructions (2 cycles each), 25 branch instructions (3 cycles each), and 80 cycles of interrupt overhead. Calculate (i) the total cycles, (ii) the average CPI, and (iii) the minimum clock frequency required, assuming a 25% safety margin.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the standard five-domain diagram (Input → Processing → Communication → Output → Power) as shown in Section 4.1 above. Label all major blocks (MCU, ADC, PWM, UART, Sensors, Actuators, Power supply).

**Application Areas Overview (1 mark):** Briefly list the 8 major application areas (Embedded Control, Automotive, Industrial, IoT, Consumer, Medical, Telecom, Aerospace) as in Section 1.1.

**Automotive Electronics (1.5 marks):**

- **Role:** ECUs (Electronic Control Units) manage engine, braking, steering, airbags, infotainment, ADAS.
- **Processors used:** Infineon Aurix (TC2xx/TC3xx), NXP S32K, Renesas RH850.
- **Standards:** ISO 26262 (functional safety), CAN/LIN/FlexRay communication, AEC-Q100 (automotive grade).
- **Key requirement:** Determinism — e.g., airbag must deploy within 10 ms of crash detection. The hard real-time constraint (Equation 6 in Section 2.3) is non-negotiable.

**IoT and WSN (1.5 marks):**

- **Role:** Battery-powered sensor nodes that measure physical quantities (temperature, humidity, motion) and transmit wirelessly.
- **Processors used:** ESP32, CC2538, STM32WL, nRF52840 — all are *Wireless SoCs* integrating MCU + radio + crypto on one die.
- **Key requirement:** Ultra-low average power (≈ µW range) to enable multi-year battery life.
- **Design technique:** Duty cycling (1% active, 99% sleep), as derived in Section 3.2.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Deadline: $t_{deadline} = 50 \, \mu s$
- Integer: 150 × 1 cycle = 150 cycles
- MAC: 80 × 1 cycle = 80 cycles
- Load/Store: 40 × 2 cycles = 80 cycles
- Branch: 25 × 3 cycles = 75 cycles
- Overhead: 80 cycles

**(i) Total Cycles (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{cycles} &= 150 + 80 + 80 + 75 + 80 \\
N_{cycles} &= 465 \text{ cycles} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Sum the cycle contributions of each instruction class and the overhead. Each class multiplies the number of instructions by its per-instruction cycle cost.

**(ii) Average CPI (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{instr} &= 150 + 80 + 40 + 25 = 295 \text{ instructions} \\
CPI_{avg} &= \dfrac{465}{295} \\
CPI_{avg} &\approx 1.576 \text{ cycles/instruction} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Total instructions = sum of instruction counts (excluding overhead cycles). Then average CPI = total cycles / total instructions.

**(iii) Minimum Clock Frequency (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{clk,min} &= \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{t_{deadline}} \\
f_{clk,min} &= \dfrac{465}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \\
f_{clk,min} &= 9.3 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} \\
f_{clk,min} &= 9.3 \text{ MHz} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

With 25% safety margin:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{clk,design} &\geq 9.3 \times 1.25 \\
f_{clk,design} &\geq 11.625 \text{ MHz} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Selection:** A standard **16 MHz Cortex-M0** or **48 MHz Cortex-M3** would satisfy this requirement with margin. KTU expects the **practical processor recommendation** as the final step.

---

### **Question B. [Alternative Choice for Internal Option – 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare and contrast the use of microcontrollers in **medical devices** and **consumer electronics**. Discuss the processor selection criteria, performance requirements, safety standards, and provide at least two real-world examples for each domain.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A wireless IoT sensor node uses a Cortex-M0+ MCU at $V_{dd} = 3.3$ V, $f_{clk} = 48$ MHz, $C = 15$ pF, $\alpha = 0.15$, $I_{leak} = 0.8 \, \mu A$. The node is active for 0.5% of the time and in deep sleep (1 µW) the rest. Compute (i) dynamic power, (ii) total active power, (iii) average power, and (iv) the battery life if powered by a 1200 mAh Li-Po battery at 3.7 V.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

| Criterion (3 marks) | Medical Devices | Consumer Electronics |
|:--------------------|:----------------|:---------------------|
| **Processor Class** | Ultra-low-power MCU (MSP430, Cortex-M0+) | High-performance AP (Cortex-A multi-core) |
| **Performance** | Low MIPS (1–50) but deterministic | High MIPS (thousands) with GPU/NPU |
| **Power** | µW average (battery-critical) | W range (mains-powered) |
| **Standards** | IEC 62304, ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR 820 | CE, RoHS, FCC, Bluetooth SIG |
| **Real-time** | Hard real-time (life-critical) | Soft real-time (UI latency) |
| **Cost per chip** | High (certification overhead) | Low (mass-produced) |
| **Reliability** | > 99.999% (5-nines) | > 99.9% (3-nines) |

**Medical Device Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Pacemaker** (Medtronic) — uses MSP430; must run 7+ years on a single cell.
2. **Portable ECG monitor** (Philips) — uses Cortex-M4 with 24-bit ADC for sub-µV signal detection.

**Consumer Electronics Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Smartphone** (Samsung Galaxy / iPhone) — uses multi-core AP (Snapdragon / Apple A) with 5G modem.
2. **Smart TV** (LG / Samsung) — uses quad-core Cortex-A55 with hardware H.265/HEVC decoder.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Given:** $V_{dd} = 3.7$ V, $f_{clk} = 48$ MHz, $C = 15$ pF, $\alpha = 0.15$, $I_{leak} = 0.8 \, \mu A$, $D = 0.005$ (0.5%), $P_{sleep} = 1 \, \mu W$, Battery = 1200 mAh at 3.7 V.

**(i) Dynamic Power (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f_{clk} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.15 \times 15 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.7)^{2} \times 48 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.15 \times 15 \times 10^{-12} \times 13.69 \times 48 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 1.4785 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \\
P_{dynamic} &\approx 1.478 \text{ mW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The dynamic power scales linearly with frequency, quadratically with voltage, and linearly with switching activity and load capacitance. Even small $\alpha$ values keep the dynamic component dominant in active mode.

**(ii) Total Active Power (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{static} &= I_{leak} \times V_{dd} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.7 = 2.96 \, \mu W \\
P_{active} &= 1.478 \text{ mW} + 0.00296 \text{ mW} \approx 1.481 \text{ mW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average Power (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= D \cdot P_{active} + (1 - D) \cdot P_{sleep} \\
P_{avg} &= 0.005 \times 1.481 \text{ mW} + 0.995 \times 0.001 \text{ mW} \\
P_{avg} &= 0.007405 \text{ mW} + 0.000995 \text{ mW} \\
P_{avg} &\approx 8.4 \, \mu W \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The duty cycle acts as a *time-weighted average* between the high active power and the near-zero sleep power. With only 0.5% active time, the sleep power — although tiny — still contributes ~12% of the average.

**(iv) Battery Life (2 marks):**

Battery energy:
$$
E_{bat} = 1200 \text{ mAh} \times 3.7 \text{ V} = 4.44 \text{ Wh} = 15{,}984 \text{ J}
$$

Lifetime:
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{life} &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{15984}{8.4 \times 10^{-6}} \\
t_{life} &= 1.903 \times 10^{9} \text{ s} \\
t_{life} &\approx 60.3 \text{ years} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** Such a node is **practically eternal** on a single charge — this is the *power budget design philosophy* behind LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, and BLE beacon devices. The 60-year theoretical life is of course limited by battery self-discharge (~2–5% per year) and component aging, giving a practical life of **~10–15 years**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Application-Area Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Application" with "Architecture"** — Students often list processor families (ARM, MIPS, x86) instead of *application areas* (automotive, IoT, medical). The examiner marks this as a **wrong interpretation** (lose 2–3 marks).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "Why" of processor choice** — A question asking *"Why is a DSP used in audio applications?"* requires the answer to mention **Harvard architecture, single-cycle MAC, and zero-overhead looping** — NOT just *"because it is fast"*. Generic answers get **partial credit only** (lose 1–2 marks).
>
> 3. **Numerical units mistakes** — In MIPS/power numericals, students frequently write **MHz instead of Hz** inside the formula. This single error propagates and gives a wrong answer that is **off by a factor of 10⁶** — losing **3–4 marks** instantly.
>
> 4. **Skipping the design safety margin** — Real-time numericals must include a **20–30% safety margin** on clock frequency. Skipping it costs **1 mark** and signals to the examiner that the student lacks industry awareness.
>
> 5. **No real-world example** — KTU 2024 marking scheme awards **1 mark for a real-world example** in every "Discuss the application" question. Writing only definitions without an example = **partial answer**.
>
> 6. **Not drawing a block diagram** — For a 7-mark question, a *neat, labelled block diagram* carries **at least 2 marks** in itself. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Application Areas of Advanced Processors" (Module 4)**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [x] **Application Area** = problem-specific engineering domain that dictates processor choice.
- [x] **Processor Classes** = GPP, MCU, MPU, DSP, FPGA/SoC.
- [x] **Three design constraints** = Performance, Power, Cost.
- [x] **Two real-time categories** = Hard (life/mission-critical) vs Soft (UI, multimedia).

### ✅ Eight Major Application Areas (must memorise in order)
- [x] **Embedded Control** → 8051 / PIC / Cortex-M0.
- [x] **Automotive** → Aurix, S32K, Cortex-R5; standards: ISO 26262, CAN.
- [x] **Industrial Automation & Robotics** → C2000 DSP, FOC, PID.
- [x] **IoT & WSN** → ESP32, nRF52, CC2538; duty cycling.
- [x] **Consumer Electronics** → Multi-core Cortex-A, Snapdragon, Apple A.
- [x] **Medical Devices** → MSP430, IEC 62304, FDA.
- [x] **Telecommunications** → TMS320C6xxx, SHARC, Blackfin.
- [x] **Aerospace & Defense** → RAD750, LEON, rad-hard.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorise
- [x] $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$
- [x] $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f + I_{leak} V_{dd}$
- [x] $t_{loop} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}} \leq t_{deadline}$
- [x] $P_{avg} = D \cdot P_{active} + (1-D) \cdot P_{sleep}$
- [x] $\text{SNR}_{ADC} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB
- [x] $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$

### ✅ Standards & Terminology
- [x] **AMBA** — bus standard from ARM.
- [x] **CAN/LIN/FlexRay** — automotive communication.
- [x] **ISO 26262 (ASIL A–D)** — automotive functional safety.
- [x] **IEC 62304** — medical device software lifecycle.
- [x] **FCC / CE / RoHS** — consumer electronics compliance.
- [x] **SEU / TID** — single-event upset / total ionizing dose (aerospace).

### ✅ Numerical Problem Pattern
- [x] Given deadline + instruction mix → compute $N_{cycles}$, CPI, $f_{clk}$, add safety margin.
- [x] Given $V_{dd}, f, C, \alpha$ → compute $P_{dynamic}, P_{avg}, t_{life}$.

### ✅ Common Block Diagram
- [x] Five domains: **Input → Processing → Communication → Output → Power**.

### ✅ Examiner's Pet Peeves to Avoid
- [x] Don't confuse *application* with *processor architecture*.
- [x] Don't forget units (Hz, W, s).
- [x] Always include a real-world example.
- [x] Always include a block diagram for 7-mark questions.
- [x] Always add 20–30% safety margin in real-time numericals.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPUs as Parallel Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GPUs as Parallel Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A **Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)** is a massively parallel, many-core processor originally designed to accelerate the rendering of computer graphics. In modern computing, GPUs are used as **general-purpose parallel processors** (GPGPU) to perform computationally intensive tasks by exploiting **data parallelism** and **task parallelism** through hundreds or thousands of smaller, efficient cores operating simultaneously.

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A GPU is a specialized electronic circuit designed to rapidly manipulate and alter memory to accelerate the creation of images in a frame buffer intended for output to a display device. From a parallel computing perspective, the GPU is a **throughput-oriented** processor — unlike the **latency-oriented** CPU, the GPU is optimized to execute a massive number of concurrent threads, prioritizing total work completed per unit time over individual task response time.

### 1.2 Key Terminology
- **Host:** The CPU and its main system memory (RAM).
- **Device:** The GPU and its dedicated memory (VRAM/global memory).
- **Kernel:** A function written to be executed in parallel by many threads on the GPU device.
- **Thread:** The smallest unit of execution in a GPU program.
- **Warp (NVIDIA) / Wavefront (AMD):** A group of threads (typically 32 for NVIDIA) that execute in **lockstep** (Single Instruction Multiple Thread - SIMT).
- **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads):** Execution model where a single instruction is issued across multiple threads in parallel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine a CPU as a single, highly skilled head chef who can prepare one complex, multi-course dish incredibly fast (low latency, high single-thread performance). Now imagine a GPU as a kitchen with **2,000 line cooks**, each of whom can only chop vegetables or stir a pot (simple operations). If you need 10,000 salads, the 2,000-cook kitchen will crush the head chef every time — even if each individual cook is 100× slower than the chef. The GPU trades **per-task sophistication** for **massive concurrent throughput**.

### 1.3 Why GPUs for Parallel Computing?
Modern scientific and engineering problems (deep learning, molecular simulation, fluid dynamics, image processing, cryptography) are dominated by **data-parallel kernels** — operations applied independently to many elements of large datasets (vectors, matrices, tensors). The GPU’s architecture maps naturally to this pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Parallelism Metrics (per KTU 2024 high-yield list):**
> - **Compute-to-Memory Ratio:** GPUs maintain a high ratio by hiding memory latency through thread-level parallelism.
> - **FLOPs (Floating Point Operations per Second):** Modern GPUs deliver **TeraFLOPs (10¹²)** to **PetaFLOPs (10¹⁵)** sustained throughput.
> - **CUDA Cores / Stream Processors:** GPU execution units (NVIDIA: thousands, AMD: thousands).
> - **Memory Bandwidth:** GPU VRAM bandwidth typically **>500 GB/s** (e.g., GDDR6, HBM2e).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GPU vs CPU — Architectural Philosophy
| Feature | **CPU** | **GPU** |
|---|---|---|
| Design Goal | **Latency** minimization | **Throughput** maximization |
| Core Count | 4–64 (complex, out-of-order) | 1,000–24,000+ (simple, in-order) |
| Cache Hierarchy | Large L1/L2/L3 (multi-MB) | Small L1/L2 (per SM, ~KB) |
| Branch Prediction | Aggressive, hardware-intensive | Minimal / per-warp uniform |
| Memory | DDR4/DDR5 system RAM | GDDR6 / HBM dedicated VRAM |
| Clock Speed | 3–5 GHz | 1–2.5 GHz |
| Best For | Sequential, control-heavy code | Data-parallel, arithmetic-heavy code |
| Energy Efficiency | Low (ops/watt for parallel) | High (ops/watt for parallel) |

### 2.2 GPU Architecture — Streaming Multiprocessor (SM)
A modern NVIDIA GPU is organized as an array of **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)**, each containing:
1. **CUDA Cores** (integer + floating-point ALUs).
2. **Special Function Units (SFUs)** for transcendentals (sin, cos, exp, sqrt).
3. **Warp Schedulers** that issue instructions to warps ready for execution.
4. **Register File** (~64K 32-bit registers per SM).
5. **Shared Memory / L1 Cache** (configurable, ~128 KB per SM).
6. **Load/Store Units** for memory operations.
7. **Tensor Cores** (in newer GPUs) for matrix-multiply-accumulate (MMA).

### 2.3 NVIDIA CUDA Thread Hierarchy
CUDA organizes threads in a three-level hierarchy (as per KTU 2024 syllabus):
1. **Thread:** Smallest execution unit; has its own registers and local memory.
2. **Block (CTA – Cooperative Thread Array):** A group of threads (max 1024) that can synchronize via `__syncthreads()` and share memory.
3. **Grid:** A collection of blocks executing one kernel.

Each thread has a unique **threadIdx**, **blockIdx**, **blockDim**, **gridDim** for indexing data.

### 2.4 Memory Hierarchy
| Memory Type | Scope | Speed | Size | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Registers | Per thread | Fastest | ~256/thread | Thread |
| Shared Memory | Per block | Very fast (on-chip) | ~48–100 KB/block | Block |
| L1/L2 Cache | Per SM / global | Fast | KBs / MBs | Kernel |
| Constant Memory | All threads | Cached, broadcast | ~64 KB | Application |
| Texture Memory | All threads | Cached, spatial | MBs | Application |
| **Global Memory (VRAM)** | All threads | Slow (hundreds of cycles) | GBs (8–80 GB) | Application |
| Local Memory | Per thread (spill) | Slow (off-chip) | KBs–MBs | Thread |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance & Occupancy Equations (Cheat Sheet for KTU 2024 ESE):**

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Speedup (Amdahl)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = parallel resources |
| **Theoretical Peak FLOPs** | $P = N_{cores} \times F_{clock} \times \text{FLOPS/cycle}$ | FLOPs/s |
| **Arithmetic Intensity (AI)** | $AI = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes Transferred}}$ | FLOPs/Byte — defines compute/memory regime |
| **Roofline Model** | $P_{att} = \min(P_{peak}, AI \times \beta_{mem})$ | $P_{att}$ achievable, $\beta_{mem}$ bandwidth |
| **Compute-Bound / Memory-Bound** | If $AI > AI_{ridge}$ → Compute-bound; else Memory-bound | $AI_{ridge} = P_{peak} / \beta_{mem}$ |
| **Occupancy** | $O = \dfrac{\text{Active Warps per SM}}{\text{Max Warps per SM}}$ | Ratio, ideal $\approx 1.0$ |
| **CUDA Memory Throughput** | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred}}{\text{Time}}$ | GB/s |
| **Coalesced Access Factor** | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Transactions}}{\text{Actual Transactions}}$ | Ideal $CF = 1$ |
| **Heterogeneous Speedup** | $S_{het} = \dfrac{T_{CPU}}{T_{GPU}}$ | Direct time ratio |
| **Threads per Block** | $1 \le T \le 1024$ | Power-of-two for coalescing |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Deep Learning / AI:** Training neural networks (backpropagation = massive matrix multiplies) on Tensor Cores.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** Solving Navier-Stokes equations via finite volume methods.
- **Medical Imaging:** CT/MRI reconstruction using filtered back-projection.
- **Cryptography:** AES, RSA, and hash brute-force key searching.
- **Genomics:** Smith-Waterman and BLAST sequence alignment.
- **Signal Processing:** FFTs, convolution, beamforming in radar.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time sensor fusion, SLAM, lane detection.
- **Scientific Simulation:** Molecular dynamics (GROMACS), weather (WRF), astrophysics (N-body).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl’s Law for GPU Parallel Speedup

**Statement:** Amdahl’s Law expresses the theoretical speedup of a task when only a fraction of it is parallelizable.

**Step 1 — Define the fractions.**
Let the total execution time of a sequential program be normalized to **1**.
- $f$ = fraction of the program that is **parallelizable** (i.e., can be executed on the GPU).
- $(1 - f)$ = fraction that is **intrinsically sequential** (host-side logic, I/O, control).

**Step 2 — Express the parallel execution time.**
On a GPU with $N$ parallel cores (or $N$ parallel workers), the parallel portion takes time $f / N$, and the sequential portion remains $(1 - f)$:

$$
T_{parallel} = (1 - f) + \frac{f}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the speedup.**
Speedup $S$ is the ratio of sequential time to parallel time:

$$
S(N) = \frac{T_{sequential}}{T_{parallel}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

**Step 4 — Examine the limit as $N \to \infty$.**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 - f}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with infinite parallel cores, the speedup is **bounded** by the sequential fraction. If 5% of the program is sequential, the **maximum achievable speedup is 20×**, regardless of GPU size.

**Step 5 — Heterogeneous CPU + GPU model.**
Modern systems use a CPU (host) executing the sequential part and a GPU (device) executing the parallel part. The total wall-clock time becomes:

$$
T_{total} = T_{CPU} + T_{kernel} + T_{transfer}
$$

where:
- $T_{CPU}$ = sequential host code + launch overhead.
- $T_{kernel}$ = GPU kernel execution time.
- $T_{transfer}$ = PCIe / NVLink data transfer (host $\leftrightarrow$ device).

The kernel time is itself:

$$
T_{kernel} \approx \max\!\left(\frac{\text{FLOPs}}{P_{peak}}, \frac{\text{Bytes}}{\beta_{mem}}\right)
$$

the maximum of compute-limited and memory-limited times.

### 3.2 Derivation: Roofline Model — Identify Bottleneck

The **Roofline model** plots attainable performance $P_{att}$ (GFLOPs/s) against arithmetic intensity $AI$ (FLOPs/Byte).

**Step 1 — Define Arithmetic Intensity.**

$$
AI = \frac{W}{Q}
$$

where $W$ = total floating-point operations, $Q$ = total DRAM bytes accessed.

**Step 2 — Memory-Bound Region.**
If the kernel is limited by memory bandwidth $\beta$ (GB/s), then:

$$
P_{att} = \beta \cdot AI
$$

This is a **linearly rising** region.

**Step 3 — Compute-Bound Region.**
If the kernel is limited by the peak hardware compute $P_{peak}$ (GFLOPs/s), then:

$$
P_{att} = P_{peak}
$$

This is a **horizontal ceiling**.

**Step 4 — The Ridge Point (transition).**

$$
AI_{ridge} = \frac{P_{peak}}{\beta}
$$

For a typical GPU: $P_{peak} = 15\,\text{TFLOPs/s} = 15000\,\text{GFLOPs/s}$, $\beta = 900\,\text{GB/s}$.

$$
AI_{ridge} = \frac{15000}{900} \approx 16.67\ \text{FLOPs/Byte}
$$

A kernel with $AI < 16.67$ is **memory-bound**; $AI > 16.67$ is **compute-bound**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Theoretical Peak FLOPs of a GPU

**Given:**
- $C$ = number of CUDA cores.
- $F$ = boost clock in GHz.
- Each core executes **2 FLOPs/cycle** (one FMA = fused multiply-add = 2 FLOPs).

**Step 1 — FLOPs per second per core.**

$$
\text{FLOPs/s per core} = F \times 10^9 \times 2
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate for $C$ cores.**

$$
P_{peak} = C \times F \times 2 \times 10^9\ \text{FLOPs/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to TFLOPs.**

$$
P_{peak}\,[\text{TFLOPs}] = \frac{C \times F \times 2}{1000}
$$

**Example (KTU-style numerical):**
A GPU has $C = 2{,}048$ CUDA cores, $F = 1.7$ GHz.

$$
P_{peak} = 2048 \times 1.7 \times 2 \times 10^9 = 6.963 \times 10^{12}\ \text{FLOPs/s} \approx 6.96\ \text{TFLOPs}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Kernel Optimization Checklist

| Optimization | Action | Effect on Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Coalesced memory access** | Threads in a warp read consecutive 4-byte words | Maximizes memory throughput |
| **Minimize divergent branches** | Restructure `if/else` to avoid per-thread divergence | Improves warp efficiency |
| **Increase occupancy** | More blocks/SM via `__launch_bounds__` | Hides memory latency |
| **Use shared memory** | Cache reused data in on-chip SRAM | Reduces global memory traffic |
| **Loop unrolling** | `#pragma unroll` | Hides pipeline latency |
| **Async copy (Ampere+)** | `cp.async` overlaps compute and load | Increases effective bandwidth |
| **Minimize host↔device transfers** | Keep data on GPU between kernels | Eliminates PCIe bottleneck |
| **Use FP16 / Tensor Cores** | Mixed precision via WMMA API | 2–16× peak FLOPs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GPU High-Level Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CPU Host - x86 ARM] -->|PCIe NVLink| B[GPU Device]
    B --> C1[SM 0]
    B --> C2[SM 1]
    B --> C3[SM N]
    C1 --> D1[Warp Scheduler 0]
    C1 --> D2[Warp Scheduler 1]
    C1 --> E1[CUDA Cores - 128 FP32]
    C1 --> E2[SFU - Sin Cos Exp]
    C1 --> E3[Tensor Cores - MMA]
    C1 --> F1[Register File 64K x 32b]
    C1 --> F2[Shared Memory 100KB L1]
    C1 --> F3[Load Store Units]
    C1 --> G[L2 Cache - 6 MB]
    G --> H[HBM2e GDDR6 VRAM - 24 GB]
```

### 4.2 CUDA Thread Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G[Grid - One Kernel Launch] --> B1[Block 0,0]
    G --> B2[Block 1,0]
    G --> B3[Block 0,1]
    G --> B4[Block 1,1]
    B1 --> W1[Warp 0 - Threads 0-31]
    B1 --> W2[Warp 1 - Threads 32-63]
    B1 --> T1[Thread 64]
    B1 --> T2[Thread 1023]
    W1 --> TH0[Thread 0]
    W1 --> TH1[Thread 1]
    W1 --> TH2[Thread 31]
```

### 4.3 Memory Hierarchy in GPU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Registers - Per Thread Fastest] --> SMEM[Shared Memory - Per Block On Chip]
    SMEM --> L1[L1 Cache - Per SM]
    L1 --> L2[L2 Cache - Global On Chip]
    C[Constant Texture Memory - Read Only Cached] --> L2
    L2 --> GM[Global Memory HBM GDDR - Off Chip Slowest]
    LM[Local Memory - Per Thread Spill] --> GM
```

### 4.4 Heterogeneous CPU-GPU Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start - Host CPU] --> B[Allocate Device Memory cudaMalloc]
    B --> C[Copy Input Host to Device cudaMemcpy]
    C --> D[Launch Kernel grid blocks threads]
    D --> E[GPU Executes Warps in Parallel]
    E --> F{Synchronize cudaDeviceSynchronize}
    F --> G[Copy Result Device to Host]
    G --> H[Free Device Memory cudaFree]
    H --> I[End - Host CPU]
```

### 4.5 Roofline Model — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low AI - Memory Bound Region] -->|Slope = Bandwidth| B[Ridge Point - Ppeak divided by Bandwidth]
    B -->|Horizontal Ceiling| C[High AI - Compute Bound Region]
    A --> A1[Kernel: Element-wise Add AI = 0.25]
    B --> B1[Kernel: Matrix Multiply AI = 100]
    C --> C1[Kernel: Dense Linear Algebra AI = 500]
```

### 4.6 SIMT Execution Model — Warp Lockstep

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    I[Single Instruction Fetched] --> D[Warp of 32 Threads]
    D --> P[Predicated Execution Active Mask]
    P --> R1[Thread 0 - Execute]
    P --> R2[Thread 1 - Execute]
    P --> R3[Thread 2 - Masked Off Idle]
    P --> R4[Thread 31 - Execute]
    R1 --> E[Coalesced Write to Memory]
    R2 --> E
    R4 --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a GPU and list three key architectural differences between a CPU and a GPU.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)** is a massively parallel, many-core processor designed to accelerate data-parallel workloads by executing thousands of threads simultaneously using the **SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Thread)** model.

**Three key differences (1 mark each):**
1. **Core count:** CPU has 4–64 complex cores optimized for sequential latency; GPU has thousands of small, simple cores optimized for throughput.
2. **Memory:** CPU uses large multi-level caches (L1/L2/L3) and DDR RAM; GPU uses small on-chip caches with high-bandwidth GDDR6/HBM dedicated VRAM.
3. **Control:** CPU has aggressive branch prediction, out-of-order execution, and speculation; GPU relies on hardware multithreading to hide latency and uses simple, in-order pipelines.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a "warp" in NVIDIA GPU architecture? Why must threads in a warp execute in lockstep?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **warp** is the fundamental unit of execution in an NVIDIA GPU, consisting of **32 threads** that execute a single instruction in **lockstep (SIMT — Single Instruction, Multiple Threads)**.

**Why lockstep (1+1+1 marks):**
1. **Hardware simplicity:** A single instruction decoder and PC can be shared across 32 ALUs, drastically reducing area and power per thread.
2. **Coalesced memory access:** Threads accessing consecutive memory addresses can be merged into a single transaction, maximizing memory bandwidth.
3. **Latency hiding:** When one warp stalls on memory, the warp scheduler can issue another ready warp in the same cycle, maintaining high utilization. Per-thread PC would make this impossible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse a warp with a *block*. A **warp = 32 threads** (hardware-defined); a **block = up to 1024 threads** (software-defined, contains multiple warps). Mixing these definitions will cost 1 mark.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **CUDA thread hierarchy** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **grid, block, warp, and thread** in parallel execution. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of the **Roofline model**, explain how to identify whether a GPU kernel is **compute-bound or memory-bound**. A GPU has a peak performance of 10 TFLOPs/s and memory bandwidth of 500 GB/s. Compute the **ridge point** and classify kernels with AI = 5 and AI = 50. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CUDA Thread Hierarchy (7 marks)**

CUDA organizes GPU threads in a 3-level hierarchy (1 mark per level, 2 marks for role/description, 2 marks for diagram logic):

1. **Thread:** The smallest execution unit. Each thread has its own registers, program counter, and local memory. Identified by `threadIdx.x/y/z` (0 to blockDim-1).

2. **Block (CTA – Cooperative Thread Array):** A group of up to **1024 threads** that execute concurrently on one SM. Threads in a block can:
   - Synchronize via `__syncthreads()`.
   - Communicate via fast **shared memory**.
   - Cooperate via atomic operations.
   Identified by `blockIdx.x/y/z`.

3. **Grid:** A collection of blocks executing one **kernel**. The total thread count is `gridDim × blockDim`. Blocks within a grid can run in any order on any SM — they cannot directly synchronize (must launch a second kernel).

4. **Warp (hardware level):** A block is partitioned into **warps of 32 threads** by the hardware. The warp scheduler issues instructions to warps; warps execute in SIMT lockstep.

**Execution model:** The host launches a kernel with a grid of blocks; the GPU SMs schedule warps onto CUDA cores; each warp executes one instruction at a time across all 32 threads (with predication for divergence).

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the standard KTU-style block diagram.)

---

**(b) Roofline Model (7 marks)**

The **Roofline model** (Williams, Waterman & Patterson, 2009) is a visual performance model with two regions: (2 marks for theory)

- **Memory-bound region (rising line, slope = $\beta$):** $P_{att} = \beta \cdot AI$.
- **Compute-bound region (horizontal ceiling):** $P_{att} = P_{peak}$.

**Ridge point computation (3 marks):**

$$
AI_{ridge} = \frac{P_{peak}}{\beta} = \frac{10 \times 10^{12}\ \text{FLOPs/s}}{500 \times 10^9\ \text{Bytes/s}} = \frac{10000}{500} = 20\ \text{FLOPs/Byte}
$$

**Kernel classification (2 marks):**
- Kernel 1, $AI = 5$: Since $5 < 20$, kernel is **memory-bound** — its performance is limited by DRAM bandwidth, not compute.
- Kernel 2, $AI = 50$: Since $50 > 20$, kernel is **compute-bound** — its performance is limited by the peak FLOPs of the GPU, not bandwidth.

**Engineering implication:** To speed up a memory-bound kernel, optimize memory access patterns (coalescing, shared memory, fewer loads). To speed up a compute-bound kernel, optimize arithmetic (FMA, Tensor Cores, loop unrolling, reduce FLOPs).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Describe the **GPU memory hierarchy** in detail. Differentiate between **global, shared, constant, and local memory** with respect to scope, lifetime, and access speed. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply **Amdahl’s Law** to compute the maximum speedup achievable on a GPU if 90% of a program is parallelizable. What happens if 99.9% is parallelizable? Comment on the implications for heterogeneous CPU+GPU programming. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GPU Memory Hierarchy (7 marks)**

The GPU memory hierarchy (refer to Section 2.4 table) is organized as a pyramid from fastest/smallest to slowest/largest (1 mark for diagram, 1 mark per memory type description):

| Memory | Scope | Lifetime | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Registers** | Per thread | Thread | Fastest (~1 cycle) |
| **Shared Memory** | Per block | Block | Very fast (on-chip, ~5 cycles) |
| **Constant Memory** | All threads | Application | Cached, broadcast (~5 cycles after first hit) |
| **L1/L2 Cache** | SM / global | Kernel | Fast (KBs of latency) |
| **Global Memory (VRAM)** | All threads | Application | Slow (400–800 cycles) |
| **Local Memory** | Per thread (register spill) | Thread | Slow (off-chip, in VRAM) |

**Key design rules (2 marks):**
- Use **shared memory** for data reused by multiple threads in a block (e.g., tiled matrix multiplication).
- Use **constant memory** for read-only data shared by all threads (e.g., coefficients, lookup tables).
- Use **registers** aggressively; spills to local memory are catastrophic for performance.
- Access **global memory** in coalesced patterns (consecutive threads → consecutive 32-bit words).
- **Texture memory** is best for 2D spatial locality (image processing).

---

**(b) Amdahl's Law Application (7 marks)**

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

**Case 1: $f = 0.90$, $N \to \infty$ (theoretical max):**

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10\times
$$

Even with infinite GPU cores, the speedup is bounded to **10×** because 10% of the program is sequential.

**Case 2: $f = 0.999$:**

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.999} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000\times
$$

**Comparative table (3 marks):**

| $f$ (parallel fraction) | $S_{max}$ (theoretical) |
|---|---|
| 0.50 | 2× |
| 0.90 | 10× |
| 0.99 | 100× |
| 0.999 | 1000× |

**Heterogeneous CPU+GPU implications (3 marks):**
1. **Sequential portions dominate:** Even a tiny sequential fraction $(1-f)$ can cripple speedup. Optimize host code aggressively.
2. **Transfer overhead matters:** PCIe/NVLink transfers $(T_{transfer})$ are sequential from the GPU's perspective. Minimize them by keeping data resident on the device.
3. **Heterogeneous partitioning is essential:** Identify the "10% sequential" carefully — it may include I/O, kernel launch latency, branchy code, or memory allocation. Use CUDA streams, graphs, and pinned memory to reduce $T_{CPU}$ and $T_{transfer}$.
4. **Use profilers (Nsight, nvprof)** to find the actual sequential fraction; theoretical $f$ rarely matches measured $f$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical for Full Marks):**
> 1. **Do not confuse $S_{max}$ with $S$ for a specific $N$.** The question asks for *maximum* speedup — always take $N \to \infty$. If a student uses $N = 1024$ cores, they will get ~9.9 instead of 10 and lose a mark.
> 2. **Always specify units in numerical answers** (×, GFLOPS, GB/s). A bare "10" without "10×" will be marked down.
> 3. **Do not write CUDA/C++ code** in Part B unless the question is from a CS elective. PEBMT741 is a *microprocessors and microcontrollers* course — answers must focus on **architecture, equations, and hardware analysis**, not source code.
> 4. **Roofline ridge calculation:** Common error is mixing units (TFLOPs vs. GFLOPs vs. FLOPs). Always convert to a common base (FLOPs/s and Bytes/s) before dividing.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire for KTU 2024):**

- **GPU** = throughput-oriented, massively parallel, many-core processor; **CPU** = latency-oriented, few-core, complex.
- **SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Threads)** = NVIDIA's execution model; **SIMD** = single instruction multiple data (CPU vector).
- **Warp = 32 threads** (NVIDIA); **Wavefront = 64 threads** (AMD). Both execute in lockstep.
- **Block ≤ 1024 threads**; **Grid** = collection of blocks running one kernel.
- **Grid** coordinates via `gridDim.x, blockIdx.x, threadIdx.x`.
- **Memory hierarchy (fast → slow):** Registers → Shared Memory → L1 → L2 → Global VRAM → Local (spill).
- **Shared memory** is per-block, on-chip, fast; ideal for tiling and inter-thread communication.
- **Global memory** requires **coalesced** 32-thread aligned accesses for full bandwidth.
- **Constant memory** broadcasts one value to all threads in a warp simultaneously.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$; bounded by $1/(1-f)$ as $N \to \infty$.
- **Roofline ridge point:** $AI_{ridge} = P_{peak} / \beta$. Below = memory-bound; above = compute-bound.
- **Peak FLOPs:** $P_{peak} = C \times F \times 2 \times 10^9$ FLOPs/s (2 FLOPs/cycle for FMA).
- **Occupancy** = active warps / max warps per SM; goal ≈ 1.0 to hide memory latency.
- **Branches in a warp cause divergence** — masked threads idle, wasting execution slots.
- **Heterogeneous model:** $T_{total} = T_{CPU} + T_{transfer} + T_{kernel}$. Minimize $T_{transfer}$ for real speedup.
- **Programming APIs:** NVIDIA **CUDA**, Khronos **OpenCL**, AMD **ROCm/HIP**, Apple **Metal**.
- **Modern GPU memory types:** **GDDR6** (consumer), **HBM2e / HBM3** (datacenter, on-package stacked).
- **Tensor Cores** (Volta+): matrix-multiply-accumulate units delivering 2–16× speedup for AI workloads.
- **Interconnects:** PCIe 4.0/5.0 (~32 GB/s), **NVLink** (~300 GB/s), **NVSwitch** for multi-GPU.
- **GPGPU frameworks** beyond CUDA: SYCL, oneAPI, Vulkan Compute, DirectCompute, HIP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of Graphics Pipelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evolution of Graphics Pipelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Graphics Pipeline** (also called the *rendering pipeline*) is a sequential, hardware-accelerated chain of processing stages that converts 3D geometric primitives, lighting models, textures, and viewpoint parameters into a 2D raster image (framebuffer) suitable for display on a screen.

In the context of modern GPUs and graphics controllers interfacing with advanced microprocessors, the graphics pipeline is the **logical data-flow architecture** of the GPU — the path traversed by every vertex, every fragment, and every pixel before it is written to display memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *The graphics pipeline describes the sequence of operations that transforms high-level scene descriptions into pixel data. Its evolution is tied to the transition from purely fixed-function hardware to fully programmable, massively parallel many-core architectures.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **car assembly line in a factory**:

1. Raw steel arrives (vertices & geometry).
2. Workers cut & shape the chassis (transformations).
3. Painters apply color based on lighting (lighting/shading).
4. Stamping machine adds decals & textures (texturing).
5. The finished car is photographed for a catalogue (rasterization to framebuffer).
6. Final picture is printed (display output).

> Early factories (1960s–80s) had **dedicated workers at each station** — each stage was hard-wired to do exactly one job. Modern smart factories have **robots that can be reprogrammed** to perform any task at any station. This is precisely the leap graphics hardware made: from **fixed-function pipeline** to **programmable / unified shader architecture**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Refresh Rate:** **60 Hz** (standard), up to **360 Hz** in gaming monitors.
- **Color Depth:** **24-bit** (8 bits per channel — R, G, B) standard; **30-bit / 36-bit** for professional.
- **Frame Latency Budget:** **16.67 ms** at 60 FPS.
- **Vertex Throughput:** Modern GPUs process **billions of vertices/sec**.
- **Pixel Fill Rate:** Measured in **GPixels/sec**.
- **Z-buffer precision:** Typically **24-bit** depth, **8-bit stencil**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Every modern 3D application — from CAD to gaming to scientific visualization — runs on a descendant of the original fixed-function graphics pipeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Generations of Graphics Pipeline Evolution

### **Generation 1 — Vector & Wireframe Era (1960s–1980s)**
- Devices: **IBM 2250, Tektronix 4010, Evans & Sutherland Picture System**.
- Display: **Vector (calligraphic) refresh displays** using CRT beams.
- Limitation: No filled polygons, flickering, no hidden surface removal.
- Representative math: simple 2D **line-drawing algorithms** (Bresenham, DDA).

### **Generation 2 — Fixed-Function Raster Pipeline (1990s)**
- Devices: **SGI RealityEngine, 3dfx Voodoo, NVIDIA NV1, RIVA 128**.
- Standards: **OpenGL 1.0 (1992), Direct3D 5.0 (1995)**.
- Stages were **hard-wired** in silicon — no programmability.
- Core stages: **T&L → Clipping → Rasterization → Texture Mapping → Blending**.
- Z-buffer became standard.

### **Generation 3 — Programmable Shader Pipeline (2001–2006)**
- Devices: **NVIDIA GeForce 3/4/5, ATI Radeon 8500–X800, Xbox GPU**.
- Standards: **OpenGL 2.0, Direct3D 8.0/9.0, HLSL, GLSL, Cg**.
- Introduced: **Vertex Shaders (2001)** and **Fragment/Pixel Shaders (2002)**.
- Assembly-level programmability, later high-level shading languages.

### **Generation 4 — Unified Shader Architecture (2006–2018)**
- Devices: **NVIDIA G80 (GeForce 8800), AMD R600, Intel HD Graphics**.
- Standards: **Direct3D 10/11, OpenGL 3.x/4.x, Vulkan, Metal**.
- Key innovation: **Single unified shader core** can run vertex, geometry, fragment, or compute workloads.
- Geometry Shader, Tessellation, Compute Shader (CUDA/OpenCL) introduced.

### **Generation 5 — Ray Tracing & Mesh Shading (2018–Present)**
- Devices: **NVIDIA RTX (Turing/Ampere/Ada/Blackwell), AMD RDNA 2/3, Intel Arc**.
- Standards: **Direct3D 12 Ultimate, Vulkan RT, OpenGL 4.6+**.
- Dedicated **RT cores** for BVH traversal; **Meshlet/Mesh shaders** for fine-grained geometry processing; **VRS (Variable Rate Shading)**, **AI Tensor cores** for DLSS/FSR.

## 2.2 The Canonical Stages of a Modern Graphics Pipeline

| **Stage** | **Function** | **Typical Math/Output** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Vertex Fetch | Read vertex attributes from VRAM | $(x,y,z, nx,ny,nz, u,v, r,g,b)$ |
| 2. Vertex Shader | Transform to clip space, per-vertex lighting | $v_{clip} = P \cdot V \cdot M \cdot v_{local}$ |
| 3. Tessellation | Subdivide patches into finer triangles | $u,v,w$ barycentric weights |
| 4. Geometry Shader | Per-primitive processing (emit/exclude) | Optional expansion |
| 5. Rasterization | Convert primitives to fragments | Screen-space pixels with barycentrics |
| 6. Fragment Shader | Per-pixel shading, texture sampling | $\text{color} = f(L,N,V,\text{tex})$ |
| 7. Per-Fragment Ops | Depth test, stencil, blend | $z \leq z_{buf}$, alpha blending |
| 8. Framebuffer Write | Final color to VRAM | RGB(A) $\rightarrow$ display |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Where Used** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model-View-Projection | $v_{clip} = P \cdot V \cdot M \cdot v$ | Vertex Shader | NDC coords |
| Perspective Projection | $P = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{r\tan(\theta/2)} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{\tan(\theta/2)} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -\frac{f+n}{f-n} & -\frac{2fn}{f-n} \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | Projection stage | $f, n$ in world units; $\theta$ in radians |
| Phong Diffuse | $I_d = k_d (L \cdot N) I_{light}$ | Lighting | Normalized vectors |
| Phong Specular | $I_s = k_s (R \cdot V)^{\alpha} I_{light}$ | Lighting | $\alpha$ = shininess exponent |
| Gouraud Shading | $I_{vert} = I_a + I_d + I_s$ interpolated | Per-vertex shading | Linear interpolation |
| Z-buffer Test | $z_{frag} \leq z_{buf}(x,y)$ | Per-fragment | $z \in [0,1]$ |
| Triangle Setup | Edge function $E(x,y) = (x_2-x_1)(y-y_1) - (y_2-y_1)(x-x_1)$ | Rasterization | Pixels covered |
| Mipmap Level | $D = \log_2 \max\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right\vert, \frac{dv}{dx}\right\vert, \frac{du}{dy}\right\vert, \frac{dv}{dy}\right\vert)$ | Texture filtering | Level integer |
| Framebuffer Rate | $F = P_f \times R$ | Performance | $P_f$ = pixels/frame, $R$ = Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The graphics pipeline evolution directly enables **GPGPU computing** (CUDA/OpenCL), **AI/ML inference** (Tensor cores), **autonomous vehicle perception**, and **medical imaging** — making GPU knowledge essential for modern microprocessor system designers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Model-View-Projection (MVP) Matrix

The complete vertex transformation chain is the foundation of every GPU. We derive it step by step.

### **Step 1: Model Matrix $M$ (Local → World Space)**

Each object exists in its own local coordinate system. We translate, rotate, and scale it to its position in the world.

$$
M = T \cdot R \cdot S
$$

Where:
- $T$ = translation matrix,
- $R$ = rotation matrix (composed of $R_x$, $R_y$, $R_z$),
- $S$ = scaling matrix.

For example, a rotation about the Y-axis by angle $\theta$:

$$
R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This takes a vertex at origin and places it in the scene.

### **Step 2: View Matrix $V$ (World → Camera Space)**

The camera has a position $C$ and looks toward a target $T$. The view matrix transforms world coordinates to be relative to the camera.

$$
V = R_{view} \cdot T_{view}
$$

Where:
- $T_{view} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & -C_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -C_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -C_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $R_{view}$ aligns the camera's $Z$-axis with $(C - T)$ (forward), the $X$-axis with world up $\times$ forward, and the $Y$-axis with forward $\times$ right.

### **Step 3: Projection Matrix $P$ (Camera → Clip Space)**

For a perspective camera with field of view $\theta$, aspect ratio $a$, near plane $n$, and far plane $f$:

The frustum maps to a cube of $x,y,z \in [-1, 1]$ (Normalized Device Coordinates). We derive $P$ from first principles.

$$
P_{33} = -\frac{f+n}{f-n}, \quad P_{34} = -\frac{2fn}{f-n}
$$

$$
P_{11} = \frac{1}{a \tan(\theta/2)}, \quad P_{22} = \frac{1}{\tan(\theta/2)}
$$

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{a\tan(\theta/2)} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{\tan(\theta/2)} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -\frac{f+n}{f-n} & -\frac{2fn}{f-n} \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### **Step 4: Perspective Division (Clip → NDC)**

The $-1$ in $P_{43}$ ensures $w = -z_{cam}$. After matrix multiply, we divide by $w$:

$$
x_{NDC} = \frac{x_{clip}}{w_{clip}}, \quad y_{NDC} = \frac{y_{clip}}{w_{clip}}, \quad z_{NDC} = \frac{z_{clip}}{w_{clip}}
$$

### **Step 5: Viewport Transform (NDC → Screen Space)**

Finally, NDC $\in [-1,1]$ maps to screen pixels:

$$
x_{screen} = (x_{NDC} + 1) \cdot \frac{W}{2}, \quad y_{screen} = (1 - y_{NDC}) \cdot \frac{H}{2}
$$

Where $W, H$ are the framebuffer resolution. This step is typically done by the **Rasterizer**, not the vertex shader.

### **Combined Final Equation**

$$
v_{clip} = P \cdot V \cdot M \cdot v_{local}
$$

This single matrix multiply is what every vertex undergoes in the vertex shader — a testament to the elegance of the graphics pipeline.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Phong Reflection Model (used in Fragment Shader)

The Phong model decomposes lighting into ambient, diffuse, and specular components.

### **Step 1: Ambient Term (constant base illumination)**

$$
I_a = k_a \cdot I_{light}
$$

Where $k_a \in [0,1]$ is the ambient coefficient and $I_{light}$ is the light intensity.

### **Step 2: Diffuse Term (Lambertian reflection)**

Light is scattered equally in all directions from a matte surface.

$$
I_d = k_d \cdot (N \cdot L) \cdot I_{light}
$$

$N$ is the surface normal, $L$ is the direction to the light. The dot product represents the cosine of the angle of incidence.

> **Logic:** If the surface faces the light head-on, $N \cdot L = 1$ (maximum). If perpendicular, $N \cdot L = 0$ (no diffuse contribution). If back-facing, $N \cdot L < 0$ (clamped to 0).

### **Step 3: Specular Term (shiny highlight)**

The reflection vector is:

$$
R = 2(N \cdot L)N - L
$$

Then the specular intensity depends on the alignment of the reflection vector with the view vector $V$:

$$
I_s = k_s \cdot (R \cdot V)^{\alpha} \cdot I_{light}
$$

$\alpha$ is the shininess exponent — large $\alpha$ creates a small, sharp highlight; small $\alpha$ creates a broad, soft highlight.

### **Step 4: Full Phong Equation**

$$
I_{total} = k_a I_{light} + k_d (N \cdot L) I_{light} + k_s (R \cdot V)^{\alpha} I_{light}
$$

This is the most commonly evaluated lighting equation in real-time fragment shaders.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Trade-offs Across Generations

| **Metric** | **Gen 2 (Fixed)** | **Gen 3 (Prog.)** | **Gen 4 (Unified)** | **Gen 5 (RT + Mesh)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Programmability | None | Vertex + Pixel | All stages | All + RT + Mesh |
| Geometry Throughput | $\sim 10$M tri/s | $\sim 100$M tri/s | $\sim 1$B tri/s | $\sim 10$B tri/s |
| Pixel Fill Rate | $1$ GP/s | $10$ GP/s | $100$ GP/s | $200+$ GP/s |
| Memory Bandwidth | $1$ GB/s | $20$ GB/s | $500$ GB/s | $1$ TB/s |
| Shader Cores | N/A (fixed) | $\sim 16$ | $\sim 5000$ | $\sim 18000$ |
| Power Efficiency | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Algorithm Flexibility | Rigid | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Real-time Ray Tracing | No | No | Limited (pre-RT) | Yes (dedicated HW) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timeline of Graphics Pipeline Evolution (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    g1[Gen 1: Vector Displays 1960s to 1980s] --> g2[Gen 2: Fixed Function Raster 1990s]
    g2 --> g3[Gen 3: Programmable Shaders 2001 to 2006]
    g3 --> g4[Gen 4: Unified Shader Architecture 2006 to 2018]
    g4 --> g5[Gen 5: Ray Tracing and Mesh Shading 2018 to Present]

    g1A[IBM 2250, Evans Sutherland, Tektronix 4010] --- g1
    g2A[SGI RealityEngine, Voodoo, RIVA 128] --- g2
    g3A[GeForce 3, Radeon 8500, DirectX 8 9] --- g3
    g4A[GeForce 8800 G80, Radeon R600, DirectX 10 11] --- g4
    g5A[RTX Turing, Ampere, RDNA 2, DirectX 12 Ultimate] --- g5
```

## 4.2 Modern GPU Pipeline Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuA[CPU and Application Layer] --> cmdA[Command Buffer and Draw Calls]
    cmdA --> vertA[Stage 1: Vertex Fetch]
    vertA --> vertS[Stage 2: Vertex Shader SM]
    vertS --> tessA[Stage 3: Tessellation and Hull Domain]
    tessA --> rastA[Stage 5: Rasterizer]
    rastA --> fragS[Stage 6: Fragment Shader SM]
    fragS --> depthA[Stage 7: Z Test and Stencil]
    depthA --> blendA[Stage 8: Blend and Output Merger]
    blendA --> fbA[Framebuffer and VRAM]
    fbA --> dispA[Display Output HDMI DP]

    uniA[Unified Shader Core Pool] -. serves .-> vertS
    uniA -. serves .-> fragS

    rtA[RT Core BVH Traversal] -. ray pipeline .-> fbA
    meshA[Mesh Shader Stage] -. replaces IA .-> rastA
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Fixed-Function vs Programmable Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fixed[Fixed Function Pipeline Gen 2]
        f1[Vertex Stream] --> f2[Transform and Light Fixed HW]
        f2 --> f3[Texture Stage Fixed Combiners]
        f3 --> f4[Rasterizer Fixed]
        f4 --> f5[Frame Buffer]
    end

    subgraph prog[Programmable Pipeline Gen 3 to 5]
        p1[Vertex Buffer] --> p2[Vertex Shader User Code]
        p2 --> p3[Rasterizer]
        p3 --> p4[Fragment Shader User Code]
        p4 --> p5[Output Merger]
    end

    f1 -. replaced by .-> p1
    f5 -. evolved to .-> p5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Mapping Rationale:** Since "graphics pipeline" is fundamentally a data-flow architecture, the Mermaid block diagrams above precisely capture the **stage-to-stage data flow** without requiring complex 3D physical drawings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain the five major stages of the fixed-function graphics pipeline that existed in the OpenGL 1.x era.**

**Model Answer:**

The five major stages of the fixed-function graphics pipeline (pre-shader era) are:

1. **Vertex Processing (Transform & Lighting — T&L):** Each vertex is transformed by the model-view-projection matrix and per-vertex lighting (Lambertian/Phong) is evaluated in fixed hardware.
2. **Primitive Assembly & Clipping:** Triangles are assembled from vertices; those outside the view frustum are clipped or culled.
3. **Rasterization:** Triangles are converted into a set of fragments (candidate pixels) using scanline or edge-function algorithms.
4. **Fragment Processing (Texture & Color):** Per-fragment operations like texture sampling, multi-texture blending, and fog are applied using fixed combiners.
5. **Framebuffer Operations:** Depth test, stencil test, alpha blending, and final write to color buffer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget **Primitive Assembly & Clipping** and jump directly to Rasterization. This is a 1-mark deduction in Part A. Always list the **clipping** stage explicitly.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the key architectural difference between a "fixed-function" GPU and a "unified shader" GPU?**

**Model Answer:**

In a **fixed-function GPU** (Gen 2, e.g., NVIDIA RIVA 128, 3dfx Voodoo), each pipeline stage is implemented as dedicated, hard-wired silicon. There are separate hardware units for vertex processing, texture sampling, and fragment operations. A vertex cannot be processed by a fragment unit — the data path is rigid.

In a **unified shader GPU** (Gen 4+, e.g., NVIDIA G80/GeForce 8800 and later), the various shader units are **merged into a single pool of identical ALU cores** (e.g., Streaming Multiprocessors). Any core can execute vertex, geometry, fragment, or compute workloads depending on the workload scheduler. This dramatically improves hardware utilization and allows dynamic load balancing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students sometimes confuse "programmable" with "unified." Programmability refers to **software control of operations**; unification refers to **hardware consolidation of pipeline stages**. Both are independent architectural concepts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **complete modern programmable graphics pipeline** of a unified shader GPU. Highlight the role of the Input Assembler, Vertex Shader, Rasterizer, Fragment Shader, and Output Merger. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **perspective projection matrix** $P$ from the frustum parameters: field of view $\theta$, aspect ratio $a$, near plane $n$, and far plane $f$. Show how a point at view-space coordinates $(x, y, z, 1)$ is mapped to clip space and then to Normalized Device Coordinates (NDC). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A modern unified-shader GPU pipeline consists of the following stages (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Input Assembler (IA):** Reads vertex/index buffers from VRAM; assembles primitives (points/lines/triangles). May invoke **mesh shaders** in Gen 5 hardware for GPU-driven geometry generation.
2. **Vertex Shader (VS):** A user-programmable stage that runs on the unified shader cores. Performs per-vertex transformations ($M$, $V$, $P$), vertex skinning, displacement, and outputs to the next stage.
3. **(Optional) Tessellation & Geometry Stages:** Subdivide patches into finer triangles or generate/exclude primitives. Common in D3D 11/12 and Vulkan.
4. **Rasterizer:** Hardware-fixed stage. Converts primitives into fragments. Performs **scan conversion** using edge functions, and **interpolates** vertex attributes (color, UVs, normals) across the triangle using barycentric coordinates.
5. **Fragment Shader (FS) / Pixel Shader:** User-programmable stage. Evaluates lighting (Phong/PBR), samples textures (with mipmapping and anisotropic filtering), and outputs a final color and depth per fragment.
6. **Output Merger (OM):** Performs **Z-test, stencil test, blending**, and writes the final color to the framebuffer. In Gen 5, the **RT cores** may run ray-tracing intersections in parallel.

The **unified shader core pool** (e.g., NVIDIA's SMs, AMD's CUs) dynamically allocates threads to VS, FS, compute, or RT workloads, achieving near-100% hardware utilization.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Goal:** Derive $P$ that maps view-space $(x_v, y_v, z_v, 1)$ to clip-space $(x_c, y_c, z_c, w_c)$ such that after $w$-division, all visible points lie in $[-1, 1]$.

**Setup:** A perspective frustum is symmetric about the Z-axis. We want:
- $x_v \in [-x_{max}, x_{max}] \rightarrow x_c \in [-w_c, w_c]$
- $y_v \in [-y_{max}, y_{max}] \rightarrow y_c \in [-w_c, w_c]$
- $z_v \in [n, f] \rightarrow z_c \in [-w_c, w_c]$ (using OpenGL convention)

**Step 1 — From similar triangles, the horizontal mapping:**

At $z_v = -n$ (OpenGL looks down $-Z$), the visible $x$ range is $\pm n \tan(\theta/2) \cdot a$. So we need:

$$
x_c = \frac{x_v}{-z_v} \cdot \frac{w_c}{a \tan(\theta/2)}
$$

Choosing $w_c = -z_v$:

$$
x_c = \frac{x_v}{a \tan(\theta/2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Same logic for y:**

$$
y_c = \frac{y_v}{\tan(\theta/2)}
$$

**Step 3 — For z, we need a linear map from $[n, f]$ to $[-1, 1]$** (in OpenGL):

$$
z_c = A z_v + B, \quad \text{with } w_c = -z_v
$$

After division, $z_{NDC} = A - B/z_v$. Solving $z_{NDC}(n) = -1$ and $z_{NDC}(f) = 1$:

$$
A = -\frac{f+n}{f-n}, \quad B = -\frac{2fn}{f-n}
$$

So:

$$
z_c = -\frac{f+n}{f-n} z_v - \frac{2fn}{f-n}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the matrix:**

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{a\tan(\theta/2)} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{\tan(\theta/2)} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -\frac{f+n}{f-n} & -\frac{2fn}{f-n} \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to a point:**

Given view-space point $p_v = (x_v, y_v, z_v, 1)^T$:

$$
p_c = P \cdot p_v = \left(\frac{x_v}{a\tan(\theta/2)}, \frac{y_v}{\tan(\theta/2)}, -\frac{f+n}{f-n} z_v - \frac{2fn}{f-n}, -z_v\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Perspective division to NDC:**

$$
x_{NDC} = \frac{x_v / (a\tan(\theta/2))}{-z_v}, \quad y_{NDC} = \frac{y_v / \tan(\theta/2)}{-z_v}, \quad z_{NDC} = \frac{-\frac{f+n}{f-n} z_v - \frac{2fn}{f-n}}{-z_v}
$$

These lie in $[-1, 1]$ for all points within the frustum, completing the derivation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. Forgetting the **$-1$ in $P_{43}$**, which is what *causes* the perspective division. (2 marks lost)
> 2. Mixing up OpenGL ($Z \in [-1,1]$, looking down $-Z$) and DirectX ($Z \in [0,1]$, looking down $+Z$) conventions. (1 mark lost)
> 3. Skipping the **w-divide step** — this is the entire point of the derivation. (2 marks lost)
> 4. Not specifying $w_c = -z_v$ explicitly. (1 mark lost)

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Compare the **evolution of graphics pipelines** across the five generations — from fixed-function (1990s) to ray-tracing capable GPUs (2020s). Highlight at least three key architectural innovations per generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Phong illumination model** in detail. Derive the reflection vector $R$ from the surface normal $N$ and light direction $L$. Show the complete equation used in a modern fragment shader. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| **Generation** | **Era** | **Three Key Innovations** |
|---|---|---|
| **Gen 1: Vector Era** | 1960s–1980s | 1. Vector (calligraphic) CRT displays  2. Wireframe models (no fill) 3. Early 2D line-drawing (Bresenham, DDA) |
| **Gen 2: Fixed-Function Raster** | 1990s | 1. Hardware T&L (Transform & Lighting)  2. Z-buffer hidden surface removal  3. Texture mapping with mipmaps |
| **Gen 3: Programmable Shaders** | 2001–2006 | 1. Vertex & Pixel Shaders (user code)  2. High-level shading languages (HLSL, GLSL, Cg)  3. Render-to-texture (MRT) |
| **Gen 4: Unified Shaders** | 2006–2018 | 1. Single shader core pool for all stages  2. Geometry/Tessellation/Compute shaders  3. GPGPU (CUDA, OpenCL) |
| **Gen 5: RT + Mesh Shading** | 2018–Present | 1. Dedicated RT cores (BVH traversal)  2. Mesh shaders (GPU-driven geometry)  3. Tensor/AI cores (DLSS, FSR) |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Phong Illumination Model — Full Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Ambient term:** A constant base brightness even where no direct light reaches.

$$
I_a = k_a \cdot I_L
$$

**Step 2 — Diffuse term (Lambert's cosine law):** Light $L$ hits a surface with normal $N$. The energy per unit area is proportional to $\cos\theta = N \cdot L$.

$$
I_d = k_d \cdot (N \cdot L) \cdot I_L
$$

This is the **matte** component — no shine, no view-dependence.

**Step 3 — Specular term:** For a shiny surface, the highlight depends on the view direction $V$ relative to the reflection $R$ of $L$ about $N$.

**Step 3a — Derive $R$:** The reflection of $L$ about $N$ is constructed by decomposing $L$ into components parallel and perpendicular to $N$:

$$
L = (L \cdot N) N + L_\perp
$$

Where $L_\perp$ is the component orthogonal to $N$. The reflection flips $L_\perp$:

$$
R = (L \cdot N) N - L_\perp
$$

Substituting $L_\perp = L - (L \cdot N) N$:

$$
R = (L \cdot N) N - [L - (L \cdot N) N] = 2(L \cdot N) N - L
$$

**Step 3b — Apply shininess exponent:**

$$
I_s = k_s \cdot (R \cdot V)^{\alpha} \cdot I_L
$$

$\alpha$ is typically $1$ to $256$; higher $\alpha$ → smaller, sharper highlight.

**Step 4 — Full Phong equation:**

$$
I_{total} = k_a I_L + k_d (N \cdot L) I_L + k_s (2(N \cdot L)N - L) \cdot V)^{\alpha} I_L
$$

This is evaluated **per-vertex** in Gouraud shading, or **per-fragment** in modern Phong shading. In contemporary PBR (Physically Based Rendering), it is replaced by the **Cook-Torrance** microfacet model, but Phong remains the pedagogical foundation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. Not explicitly deriving $R = 2(N \cdot L)N - L$ — students often quote it without proof, losing 2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting to **clamp $N \cdot L$ to $[0, 1]$** when the surface faces away from the light.
> 3. Confusing **Phong shading** (per-fragment, $R \cdot V$ computed per-pixel) with **Gouraud shading** (per-vertex, intensity interpolated).
> 4. Skipping the ambient term in the final equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Graphics Pipeline Evolution**

- **The graphics pipeline** is a **data-flow architecture** converting 3D scenes to 2D pixels via sequential stages.
- **Five Generations:** Vector → Fixed-Function → Programmable → Unified → RT/Mesh.
- **Gen 2 (Fixed-Function)** stages: **T&L → Clipping → Rasterization → Texture → Framebuffer**.
- **Gen 3 (Programmable)** introduced **Vertex Shaders (2001)** and **Fragment/Pixel Shaders (2002)** via DirectX 8/9 and OpenGL 2.0.
- **Gen 4 (Unified Shaders)** began with **NVIDIA G80 (2006)** — all stages share one **shader core pool** (Streaming Multiprocessors).
- **Gen 5 (RT/Mesh)** introduced **dedicated RT cores** (Turing 2018) and **mesh shaders** for GPU-driven geometry.
- **MVP Formula:** $v_{clip} = P \cdot V \cdot M \cdot v$ is the canonical vertex transformation.
- **Perspective Projection Matrix** has $P_{43} = -1$ to force perspective division by $-z$.
- **Phong Model:** $I = k_a + k_d (N \cdot L) + k_s (R \cdot V)^{\alpha}$.
- **Reflection Vector:** $R = 2(N \cdot L)N - L$.
- **Key Devices:** GeForce 3 (1st programmable), G80 (1st unified), RTX 2080 (1st RT cores), RTX 4090 (Ada Lovelace, modern flagship).
- **Z-buffer test:** $z_{frag} \leq z_{buf}(x,y)$; **stencil** enables per-pixel masking.
- **GPGPU** (General-Purpose computing on GPU) emerged directly from the unified shader architecture.
- **Modern APIs:** **DirectX 12 Ultimate, Vulkan 1.3, Metal 3** — explicit, low-overhead, multi-threaded command recording.
- **Performance metrics:** **Fill Rate (GP/s)**, **Triangle Throughput**, **Memory Bandwidth (GB/s)**, **TFLOPS (FP32/FP16/INT8)**.
- **Frame Time budget at 60 FPS** = **16.67 ms** end-to-end (CPU + GPU + display).
- **Difference between "Programmable" and "Unified"**: Programmability = software control; Unification = hardware consolidation of pipeline stages.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPU Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT741 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** GPU Computing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **GPU Computing** is a paradigm of high-performance parallel computing in which computationally intensive portions of an application are offloaded from the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to the Graphics Processing Unit (GPU), exploiting the GPU's massively parallel architecture to accelerate throughput-oriented workloads. The host CPU orchestrates program flow, while the device GPU executes thousands of lightweight threads concurrently using a **Single Instruction, Multiple Thread (SIMT)** execution model. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** *Heterogeneous computing = CPU (latency-optimized, sequential control) + GPU (throughput-optimized, data-parallel compute). The CPU is the **host**; the GPU is the **device**.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Metrics You Must Memorise for KTU Exams:** > - **FLOPS** = Floating-Point Operations Per Second > - **Memory Bandwidth (BW)** = GB/s transferred between memory and compute units > - **Occupancy** = Ratio of active warps to maximum supported warps per SM > - **Arithmetic Intensity (AI)** = FLOPs performed per byte of memory traffic --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large hospital** during an emergency. | Hospital Element | Computing Equivalent | |---|---| | **A few senior doctors** (highly trained, make complex decisions, treat one patient at a time) | **CPU cores** (latency-optimized, complex control logic, branch prediction) | | **Hundreds of nurses & paramedics** (follow standard protocols, treat many patients in parallel) | **GPU cores / CUDA cores** (throughput-optimized, simple in-order execution) | | **The triage nurse** who assigns cases to either doctors or the paramedic team | **The host runtime / CUDA driver** that partitions work | | **An emergency protocol book** every paramedic carries | **Warp-level SIMT instruction** (all 32 threads in a warp execute the same instruction) | > When a single patient needs a delicate surgery → send to the **doctor (CPU)**. > When 10,000 patients need a standard vaccination → send to the **paramedic team (GPU)** in parallel. This is the essence of GPU computing: **don't make one genius do 10,000 identical tasks; instead, use 10,000 average workers who all do the *same* simple task simultaneously.** --- ## 1.3 Why GPU Computing Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern applications are increasingly **data-parallel**: image processing, deep learning (CNN/RNN training), scientific simulation, molecular dynamics, cryptography, ray tracing. These naturally map to GPU hardware. Modern GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA A100) deliver **>19.5 TFLOPS (FP64)** and **>1.5 TB/s memory bandwidth**, dwarfing CPU capabilities for parallel workloads. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 CPU vs GPU: Architectural Philosophy | Parameter | **CPU** | **GPU** | |---|---|---| | Optimisation Goal | **Latency** (fast per-task completion) | **Throughput** (many tasks per unit time) | | Core Count | 4–64 (complex, out-of-order) | 1,000–20,000 (simple, in-order) | | Cache | Large L1/L2/L3 | Small L1, large L2, software-managed shared memory | | Control Logic | Heavy (branch predictor, speculative exec.) | Minimal | | Clock Speed | 3–5 GHz | 1–2.5 GHz | | Best For | Sequential, branchy, control-heavy code | Data-parallel, regular, arithmetic-heavy code | | Memory Model | Coherent caches | Explicit memory hierarchy (global / shared / registers) | --- ## 2.2 SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread) Execution - A **warp** = **32 threads** (NVIDIA) executing **one common instruction** in lock-step. - A **wavefront** = 64 threads on AMD GPUs. - The hardware fetches **one instruction per cycle** and broadcasts it to all 32 lanes. -
